topics.title: Definitions - Robots, Robotics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT302 - INTRODUCTION TO ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Definitions - Robots, Robotics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of a Robot

The most widely accepted and academically rigorous definition of a **Robot** is given by the **International Organization for Standardization (ISO 8373:2021)**:

> **"An automatically controlled, reprogrammable, multipurpose manipulator, programmable in three or more axes, which can be either fixed in place or mobile for use in industrial automation applications."**

For a more general, non-industrial definition (used in KTU 2024 syllabus and service robotics contexts):

> **"A robot is a reprogrammable, multifunctional, automatic or semi-automatic mechanical device designed to perform tasks with the help of sensors, actuators, and a controller, either in place of or in imitation of a human effort."**

The **Robotic Industries Association (RIA)** adds an important qualifier: *"A robot is a reprogrammable multifunctional manipulator designed to move material, parts, tools, or specialized devices through variable programmed motions for the performance of a variety of tasks."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Examiner Insight:** When writing the definition in exams, always include the **THREE KEY ATTRIBUTES** to score full marks:
> 1. **Reprogrammable** (software can be changed)
> 2. **Multipurpose/Multifunctional** (not single-task)
> 3. **Automatic or semi-automatic** (minimal human intervention)
>
> Missing even one attribute leads to a 1-mark deduction.

### 1.2 Definition of Robotics

**Robotics** is the **interdisciplinary branch of engineering and science** that deals with the design, construction, operation, and application of robots. It integrates:

- **Mechanical Engineering** (kinematics, dynamics, structure)
- **Electronics & Electrical Engineering** (sensors, actuators, drives)
- **Computer Science** (artificial intelligence, control algorithms, vision)
- **Cognitive Science & Biomechanics** (human-robot interaction, bio-inspired design)

> **Formal Definition:** *"Robotics is the science and technology of designing, manufacturing, controlling, and applying robots in various fields such as industry, medicine, defense, space exploration, and domestic service."*

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Robot as a "Smart Helper"**
>
> Imagine a **factory worker** who can:
> - Lift heavy objects all day without getting tired
> - Perform the **same welding task 10,000 times** with millimeter precision
> - Be **retrained** the next day to perform a completely different task (painting, packaging, cutting) simply by **updating the software** (not the body)
> - **Sense** its environment (detect obstacles, measure temperature) and **react** intelligently
>
> A **Robot** is essentially this "smart helper" — but built from **steel, sensors, and silicon** instead of flesh and bone. **Robotics** is the entire science of **designing, building, and managing** this smart helper.
>
> The word **"Robot"** itself comes from the Czech word *robota*, meaning **"forced labor" or "drudgery"**, coined by Karel Čapek in his 1920 play *R.U.R. (Rossum's Universal Robots)*.

### 1.4 Related Definitional Terms (KTU Board-Expected Vocabulary)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Manipulator** | The mechanical arm structure of a robot (the "body" that moves in space). |
| **End-Effector** | The tool or gripper attached to the end of the manipulator (welder, gripper, camera). |
| **Pedestal / Base** | The fixed or mobile platform on which the manipulator is mounted. |
| **Controller** | The "brain" of the robot (CPU, PLC, microcontroller) that executes the program. |
| **Sensor** | A device that measures physical quantities (position, force, vision) and feeds data to the controller. |
| **Actuator** | A device that converts energy (electrical/hydraulic/pneumatic) into mechanical motion (motors, pistons). |
| **Teleoperator** | A remotely controlled robot operated by a human at a distance (used in hazardous environments). |
| **Autonomous Robot** | A robot that performs tasks without continuous human intervention, using its own sensors and AI. |
| **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** | The number of independent movements a robot can perform. |
| **Payload** | The maximum weight a robot can carry while maintaining its rated performance. |
| **Workspace** | The 3D region in space that a robot's end-effector can reach. |
| **Repeatability** | The ability of a robot to return to a programmed position consistently. |
| **Accuracy** | The ability of a robot to move to a commanded position in absolute terms. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Laws of Robotics (Issac Asimov, 1942):** Although not engineering definitions, KTU often asks these in Part A:
> 1. A robot may not injure a human being or, through inaction, allow a human being to come to harm.
> 2. A robot must obey the orders given by human beings except where such orders conflict with the First Law.
> 3. A robot must protect its own existence as long as such protection does not conflict with the First or Second Law.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomical Breakdown of a Robot (Core Conceptual Architecture)

A robot, regardless of its type, is composed of **FOUR fundamental subsystems**. This is the *mental model* every KTU student must internalize:

1. **Mechanical Structure (The "Body")**
   - The skeletal framework: links, joints, and the manipulator arm.
   - Constructed from rigid materials (steel, aluminum alloys, composites).
   - Defines the robot's geometry, reach, and physical strength.
   - **Analogy:** Bones and muscles of a human body.

2. **Actuation System (The "Muscles")**
   - Provides the mechanical power to move the structure.
   - **Three primary types:**
     - **Electric motors** (DC servos, stepper motors, BLDC) — most common in industrial robots.
     - **Hydraulic actuators** (high power, used in heavy-payload robots).
     - **Pneumatic actuators** (compressed air, used for simple pick-and-place).
   - **Analogy:** Biceps, triceps, joints that produce force and movement.

3. **Sensory System (The "Nerves & Senses")**
   - Provides feedback about the internal state and external environment.
   - **Internal sensors (Proprioceptive):** Encoders, potentiometers, tachometers, force/torque sensors.
   - **External sensors (Exteroceptive):** Cameras (vision), ultrasonic sensors, LiDAR, infrared, tactile sensors.
   - **Analogy:** Eyes, ears, skin, proprioception (body awareness).

4. **Control System (The "Brain")**
   - The computational heart: microcontroller, PLC, or industrial PC.
   - Executes the programmed instructions, processes sensor data, and commands the actuators.
   - **Control levels:** Low-level servo control → Mid-level trajectory planning → High-level AI decision making.
   - **Analogy:** The human brain and nervous system working together.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This four-subsystem model is used in the **V-Model of Mechatronics Design** — a standard industry framework for developing robotic systems. Every robotic product from surgical robots (Da Vinci) to industrial arms (KUKA, ABB, FANUC) is built around these four pillars.

### 2.2 The Genesis of Robotics — A Brief Timeline

| Year | Milestone | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **1920** | Karel Čapek coins the term "Robot" in *R.U.R.* | Origin of the word in literature. |
| **1942** | Isaac Asimov formulates the **Three Laws of Robotics** | Ethical foundation of robotics. |
| **1954** | George Devol patents the first programmable robot (Unimate) | Birth of industrial robotics. |
| **1961** | Unimate installed at GM (New Jersey) | First industrial robot on a production line. |
| **1969** | **Stanford Arm** developed | First electrically powered, computer-controlled robot arm (revolutionary for research). |
| **1973** | **Cincinnati Milacron T3 robot** | First commercially available computer-controlled robot. |
| **1978** | **PUMA (Programmable Universal Machine for Assembly)** | Unimate + General Motors, became the standard research arm. |
| **1997** | NASA's **Sojourner** lands on Mars | First mobile autonomous robot on another planet. |
| **2000s** | Honda's **ASIMO**, Sony's **AIBO** | Humanoid and pet robots enter popular culture. |
| **2010s–Present** | Collaborative robots (cobots), surgical robots, AI-driven autonomous systems | Era of human-robot collaboration. |

### 2.3 The Difference Between a Robot, a Machine, and a Computer

This is a frequently asked **KTU Part A (3 marks)** question. The distinction is critical:

| Feature | Machine | Computer | Robot |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Reprogrammable?** | ❌ No (fixed function) | ✅ Yes (software-based) | ✅ Yes (software + hardware reconfigurable) |
| **Multipurpose?** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (computational tasks) | ✅ Yes (physical tasks) |
| **Physical interaction?** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (purely digital) | ✅ Yes (manipulates real world) |
| **Autonomy level** | None | High (computational) | Varies (teleoperated → fully autonomous) |
| **Has sensors?** | Usually no | Input devices only | Extensive sensor suite |
| **Has actuators?** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| **Moves in physical space?** | Often | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (3+ axes) |
| **Example** | Washing machine, lathe | Laptop, server | KUKA KR 16, ASIMO |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway:** A **robot is a mechatronic system** that combines the *physical capability of a machine* + the *intelligence of a computer* + the *adaptability of reprogramming*. It is **NOT** just a fancy machine, and it is **NOT** just a computer with arms.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

For a definitions topic, formulas are minimal, but the following **boundary conditions, metrics, and classification criteria** are essential for the board exam:

| Parameter / Metric | Definition / Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** | Number of independent joint motions | Dimensionless; typical industrial arm = 6 DOF |
| **Payload Capacity** | $P = m \cdot g$ (max mass under gravity) | kg or N |
| **Workspace (Reach)** | $R_{max} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} L_i$ (sum of link lengths) | meters |
| **Repeatability** | $\pm 3\sigma$ position variance over N cycles | mm or $\mu m$ |
| **Accuracy** | $\vert P_{commanded} - P_{actual} \vert$ | mm |
| **Resolution** | Smallest incremental motion achievable | mm or degrees |
| **Cycle Time** | $T_{cycle} = t_{move} + t_{process}$ | seconds |
| **Asimov's Laws** | 3 ethical constraints (textual, not mathematical) | Qualitative |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These metrics are used in **robot selection** for industrial automation. For example, choosing a robot for **arc welding** on a car chassis requires: 6 DOF, 8 kg payload, $\pm$ 0.1 mm repeatability, and reach $\geq$ 1.5 m. The formulas above are the **first filters** a robotics engineer applies.

### 2.5 Classification of Robots (KTU Syllabus Overview)

The KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 1) categorizes robots based on **four primary criteria**:

1. **By Application Domain**
   - **Industrial Robots** (manufacturing, assembly, welding)
   - **Service Robots** (domestic, medical, hospitality)
   - **Mobile Robots** (AGVs, drones, Mars rovers)
   - **Humanoid Robots** (ASIMO, Atlas)

2. **By Mechanical Structure**
   - **Cartesian / Gantry** (3 linear axes) — simple, precise.
   - **Cylindrical** (1 rotary + 2 linear) — good reach.
   - **Spherical / Polar** (2 rotary + 1 linear) — early industrial type.
   - **Articulated / Revolute** (all rotary joints, like a human arm) — most flexible.
   - **SCARA** (Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm) — fast, planar assembly.
   - **Parallel / Delta** (multiple arms converging) — ultra-high speed pick-and-place.

3. **By Control Method**
   - **Non-servo (Open-loop, pick-and-place)**
   - **Servo-controlled (Closed-loop, point-to-point, continuous path)**

4. **By Level of Autonomy**
   - **Manual / Teleoperated**
   - **Semi-autonomous**
   - **Fully Autonomous**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> **Note for the Student:** This section is a **definitions topic**, so there are no classical engineering derivations. Instead, we provide the **logical derivation of the robot definition** (how it was constructed), the **analytical taxonomy matrix** (how to classify any robot), and the **deductive proof of why a robot is not just a machine**.

### 3.1 The Logical Construction of the ISO Definition — Step-by-Step

The **ISO 8373:2021** definition is not arbitrary. It is the result of decades of standardization. Let us **logically derive** why each word is present:

**Step 1: Starting point — What is the simplest description?**
> "A device that moves objects."

This is too vague — it could describe a conveyor belt or a crane.

**Step 2: Add the attribute of "control"**
> "A **controlled** device that moves objects."

Now we have eliminated passive devices. The device must have a controller.

**Step 3: Specify the type of control — "automatically"**
> "An **automatically controlled** device that moves objects."

This excludes manually operated devices like a simple lever or pulley.

**Step 4: Add "reprogrammable"**
> "An automatically controlled, **reprogrammable** device..."

This is the **critical discriminator** between a *machine* and a *robot*. A washing machine is automatically controlled but **not reprogrammable for a fundamentally different task** (its core cycle is fixed). A robot can be reloaded with new software to perform welding instead of painting.

**Step 5: Add "multipurpose"**
> "...reprogrammable, **multipurpose** device..."

This eliminates single-task automated devices (e.g., a dedicated paint sprayer).

**Step 6: Specify the motion capability — "manipulator" with "three or more axes"**
> "...multipurpose **manipulator**, programmable in **three or more axes**..."

This sets the **minimum complexity threshold**. A 2-axis device (like a simple plotter) is not considered a robot.

**Step 7: Add the application context — "industrial automation"**
> "...for use in **industrial automation applications**."

This scopes the ISO definition to **industrial** robots specifically. The general robotics definition broadens this to all domains.

**Final Assembled Definition:**

$$\text{Robot} \equiv \text{Automatically Controlled} \cap \text{Reprogrammable} \cap \text{Multipurpose} \cap \text{Manipulator}_{3+ \text{axes}} \cap \text{Industrial}$$

In **set-theoretic notation:**

$$
R = \{ x \mid x \text{ is automatically controlled} \land x \text{ is reprogrammable} \land x \text{ is multipurpose} \land x \text{ has} \geq 3 \text{ axes} \}
$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — The "Robot Identification Test"

Use this **decision matrix** to determine if a given device qualifies as a robot under the ISO 8373 standard:

| Test # | Criterion | Required Answer | Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| **T1** | Is it **automatically controlled** (no continuous manual input)? | Yes ✅ | Mandatory |
| **T2** | Can its **task be changed by software** (not hardware modification)? | Yes ✅ | Mandatory |
| **T3** | Does it serve **multiple purposes** (not one fixed function)? | Yes ✅ | Mandatory |
| **T4** | Does it have a **manipulator with $\geq 3$ axes** of motion? | Yes ✅ | Mandatory |
| **T5** | Does it operate in an **industrial automation** context? | Yes ✅ (for ISO) | Contextual |
| **T6** | Does it use **sensors** to perceive its environment? | Recommended | Quality factor |
| **T7** | Does it possess **autonomy** in decision-making? | Optional | Autonomy factor |

**Decision Rule:**

$$
\text{IsRobot}(x) = \begin{cases} \text{True} & \text{if } T1 \land T2 \land T3 \land T4 = \text{Yes} \\ \text{False} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Examples for the Matrix:**
> - **Industrial robot arm (FANUC M-2000iA):** T1=✅, T2=✅, T3=✅, T4=✅ (6 axes) → **ROBOT**.
> - **CNC milling machine:** T1=✅, T2=❌ (program changes toolpath, not fundamental task type) → **MACHINE** (borderline; some sources argue it is a robot, but KTU convention: NO).
> - **Autonomous car (Waymo):** T1=✅, T2=✅, T3=✅, T4=✅ (steering, throttle, brake, gear = 4+ axes) → **MOBILE ROBOT**.
> - **Roomba vacuum:** T1=✅, T2=❌ (single purpose: vacuum) → **NOT a robot** by strict ISO; **service robot** by broader definition.

### 3.3 Deductive Proof — Why a Robot ≠ a Machine

**Premise 1:** A *machine* is defined as a device that uses energy to perform a specific task.
**Premise 2:** A *robot* is defined as a reprogrammable, multipurpose, automatically controlled manipulator with 3+ axes.
**Premise 3:** Reprogrammability implies that the input-output relationship of the device is **not fixed in hardware** but is **defined by software**.
**Premise 4:** Multipurpose means the same hardware can execute **fundamentally different tasks** (welding → painting → assembly).
**Conclusion:** Since machines are **hardware-defined** (changing task = changing hardware) and robots are **software-defined** (changing task = changing program), **a robot is a strict superset of a machine**. Formally:

$$
\text{Machine} \subset \text{Robot} \quad \text{(set-theoretic inclusion)}
$$

However, in **industrial practice**, the boundary is blurred, and KTU expects students to know the **conventional classification**, not the philosophical one.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Four-Subsystem Architecture of a Robot (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EXT[EXTERNAL ENVIRONMENT]
        env1[Objects, Obstacles, Human Operators]
    end

    subgraph SENS[SENSORY SYSTEM - NERVES]
        s1[Internal Sensors: Encoders, Potentiometers, Force Sensors]
        s2[External Sensors: Cameras, LiDAR, Ultrasonic, Tactile]
    end

    subgraph CTRL[CONTROL SYSTEM - BRAIN]
        c1[Microcontroller / PLC / Industrial PC]
        c2[Software: Control Algorithms, AI, Path Planning]
    end

    subgraph ACT[ACTUATION SYSTEM - MUSCLES]
        a1[Electric Motors: Servo, Stepper, BLDC]
        a2[Hydraulic Actuators]
        a3[Pneumatic Actuators]
    end

    subgraph MECH[MECHANICAL STRUCTURE - BODY]
        m1[Links and Joints - Manipulator Arm]
        m2[End-Effector: Gripper, Welder, Tool]
        m3[Base / Pedestal / Mobile Platform]
    end

    EXT --> s2
    s1 --> c1
    s2 --> c1
    c1 --> c2
    c2 --> c1
    c1 --> a1
    c1 --> a2
    c1 --> a3
    a1 --> m1
    a2 --> m1
    a3 --> m1
    m1 --> m2
    m1 --> m3

    m1 -. Feedback Loop .-> s1

    style EXT fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1E88E5
    style SENS fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#FB8C00
    style CTRL fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#43A047
    style ACT fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#E53935
    style MECH fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#8E24AA
    style c1 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32
    style m2 fill:#CE93D8,stroke:#6A1B9A
```

> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - The **External Environment** provides stimuli to the **Sensors**.
> - The **Control System** receives sensor data, processes it, and sends commands to the **Actuators**.
> - The **Actuators** drive the **Mechanical Structure**, which interacts with the environment via the **End-Effector**.
> - The **dashed feedback loop** from the mechanical structure back to the internal sensors represents **closed-loop control** (servo control).

### 4.2 The Robot Classification Taxonomy (Hierarchical Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[ROBOTS]

    start --> A[By Application Domain]
    start --> B[By Mechanical Structure]
    start --> C[By Control Method]
    start --> D[By Level of Autonomy]

    A --> A1[Industrial Robots]
    A --> A2[Service Robots]
    A --> A3[Mobile Robots]
    A --> A4[Humanoid Robots]

    B --> B1[Cartesian - 3 Linear Axes]
    B --> B2[Cylindrical - 1 Rotary plus 2 Linear]
    B --> B3[Spherical - 2 Rotary plus 1 Linear]
    B --> B4[Articulated - All Rotary Joints]
    B --> B5[SCARA - Selective Compliance]
    B --> B6[Parallel - Delta Configuration]

    C --> C1[Non-Servo - Open Loop, Pick and Place]
    C --> C2[Servo Controlled - Closed Loop]
    C2 --> C2a[Point to Point]
    C2 --> C2b[Continuous Path]
    C2 --> C2c[Controlled Path]

    D --> D1[Manual - Teleoperated]
    D --> D2[Semi-Autonomous]
    D --> D3[Fully Autonomous - AI Driven]

    style start fill:#FFD54F,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px
    style A fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#1565C0
    style B fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32
    style C fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#C62828
    style D fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#6A1B9A
    style B4 fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Note on Articulated Robots:** The articulated arm (4–6 rotary joints) is the most common configuration in KTU syllabus diagrams and is the **default image** students should draw when asked to "sketch a robot" in the exam.

### 4.3 The Asimov Three Laws — Logical Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[LAW 1: No Harm to Human]
    L2[LAW 2: Obey Human Orders]
    L3[LAW 3: Self-Preservation]

    L2 -->|Unless conflicts with| L1
    L3 -->|Unless conflicts with| L1
    L3 -->|Unless conflicts with| L2

    style L1 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:2px
    style L2 fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F9A825
    style L3 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32
```

> **Interpretation:** The arrows show the **hierarchy of priority**. Law 1 always supersedes Law 2, which always supersedes Law 3. This priority chain is the foundation of modern **robot ethics** and **AI safety research**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define a Robot as per ISO 8373 standard. List any two characteristics that distinguish a robot from a conventional machine.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Robot**, as defined by **ISO 8373:2021**, is *"an automatically controlled, reprogrammable, multipurpose manipulator, programmable in three or more axes, which can be either fixed in place or mobile for use in industrial automation applications."*

**Two distinguishing characteristics:**

1. **Reprogrammability:** A robot's task can be changed by modifying the software, without altering the hardware. A conventional machine (e.g., a dedicated drilling press) requires physical retooling to change tasks.

2. **Multipurpose Capability:** The same robot can perform fundamentally different operations (welding, painting, assembly, palletizing) simply by changing the program and the end-effector. A conventional machine is typically designed for one specific operation.

*(Other acceptable answers: 3+ axes of motion, integration of sensors for feedback, semi-autonomous/autonomous operation.)*

**Marks Distribution:** Definition (2 marks) + Two characteristics (0.5 + 0.5 marks) = **3 marks**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**What is Robotics? Name any four disciplines that contribute to the field of Robotics.**

**Model Answer:**

**Robotics** is the interdisciplinary branch of engineering and science that deals with the **design, construction, operation, and application of robots** to perform tasks traditionally done by humans or to automate processes in industry, medicine, exploration, and service sectors.

**Four contributing disciplines:**

1. **Mechanical Engineering** — design of manipulator arms, joints, linkages, and mechanical structures.
2. **Electrical/Electronics Engineering** — design of sensor circuits, motor drives, and power systems.
3. **Computer Science** — control algorithms, artificial intelligence, computer vision, and software development.
4. **Cognitive Science / Biomechanics** — study of human motion and behavior to design bio-inspired robots (humanoids, prosthetics).

**Marks Distribution:** Definition (1.5 marks) + Four disciplines (0.375 × 4 = 1.5 marks) = **3 marks**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question is worth **14 marks** with internal choice. Typically, part (a) carries **7 marks** and part (b) carries **7 marks**. The choice is between **Question A** and **Question B** (both covering the same module).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question aligned with Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** Define a Robot and Robotics. Explain the **four major subsystems** of a robot with a neat block diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast a **Robot, a Machine, and a Computer** based on at least six parameters. Explain why a CNC machine is **not classified as a robot** by the strict ISO definition. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A(a):**

**Definition of a Robot (2 marks):**
A robot is an automatically controlled, reprogrammable, multipurpose manipulator with three or more axes, used in industrial automation (ISO 8373:2021).

**Definition of Robotics (1 mark):**
Robotics is the interdisciplinary science and technology of designing, building, controlling, and applying robots.

**Four Major Subsystems (4 marks):**

| Subsystem | Function | Example Components |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Mechanical Structure** | The physical "body" that provides form and support. | Links, joints, base, manipulator arm. |
| **2. Actuation System** | Converts energy into mechanical motion. | DC servo motors, stepper motors, hydraulic/pneumatic cylinders. |
| **3. Sensory System** | Detects internal and external states for feedback. | Encoders, cameras, force sensors, LiDAR. |
| **4. Control System** | The "brain" that processes data and issues commands. | Microcontroller, PLC, industrial PC with control software. |

**Block Diagram (must be drawn; refer to Section 4.1 of these notes):**
The diagram should show: Sensors → Controller → Actuators → Mechanical Structure → End-effector, with a feedback loop from the structure back to the sensors.

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A(b):**

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Machine | Computer | Robot |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Reprogrammable** | No (fixed function) | Yes | Yes |
| **Multipurpose** | No | Yes (digital tasks) | Yes (physical tasks) |
| **Physical interaction** | Yes | No | Yes |
| **Actuators** | Yes | No | Yes |
| **Sensors** | Minimal | Input only | Extensive (proprio + extero) |
| **Autonomy** | None | High (logical) | Variable (teleop to full auto) |
| **Axes of motion** | 1–2 typically | 0 | 3+ |

**Why CNC is not classified as a Robot (3 marks):**

A **CNC (Computer Numerical Control) machine** is an automatically controlled, reprogrammable device, but it is **not a multipurpose manipulator**. Its primary function is **material removal** (milling, turning, drilling). Changing its task to a fundamentally different operation (e.g., welding) requires **hardware modification** (replacing the spindle with a torch), not just a software change. Furthermore, it has limited axes of motion in the "manipulator" sense (its axes move the tool, not a general-purpose arm), and it lacks the rich sensor feedback (vision, force) characteristic of a true robotic manipulator. Therefore, by strict ISO 8373, it remains a **machine**, not a robot.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question aligned with May 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** What are the **Three Laws of Robotics** as proposed by Isaac Asimov? Discuss their **relevance and limitations** in modern AI-driven autonomous systems. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a **neat classification diagram**, classify robots based on **(i) mechanical structure** and **(ii) control method**. Briefly describe the **SCARA robot** and explain why it is preferred for **printed circuit board (PCB) assembly**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B(a):**

**The Three Laws of Robotics (3 marks):**

1. **Law Zero (added later):** A robot may not harm humanity, or, by inaction, allow humanity to come to harm.
2. **First Law:** A robot may not injure a human being or, through inaction, allow a human being to come to harm.
3. **Second Law:** A robot must obey the orders given by authorized human beings, except where such orders conflict with the First Law.
4. **Third Law:** A robot must protect its own existence, as long as such protection does not conflict with the First or Second Law.

**Relevance in Modern AI (2 marks):**
- These laws form the **ethical foundation** of modern AI safety frameworks (e.g., Asilomar AI Principles, EU AI Act).
- They are used in **autonomous vehicles** (prioritize human safety over passenger convenience) and **medical robots** (do no harm).
- They inspired the field of **machine ethics** and **value alignment** in AI research.

**Limitations (2 marks):**
- **Ambiguity:** "Harm" is not precisely defined — is inconvenience "harm"? Is emotional distress "harm"?
- **Conflict resolution:** What happens when two humans give contradictory orders?
- **Self-driving dilemma:** The classic "trolley problem" — sacrificing one pedestrian to save five passengers has no clear answer under these laws.
- **Modern AI is not "obedient":** Modern AI (LLMs, neural networks) learns from data; it cannot be "commanded" like Asimov's fictional positronic robots.
- **No legal enforceability:** These are philosophical principles, not legal codes (though modern robot laws in EU, Japan, and South Korea are beginning to codify similar principles).

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B(b):**

**Classification by Mechanical Structure (3 marks):**
Refer to **Section 4.2** of these notes. The six types are:
1. Cartesian (3 linear axes)
2. Cylindrical (1 rotary + 2 linear)
3. Spherical/Polar (2 rotary + 1 linear)
4. **Articulated** (all rotary joints, 4–6 axes)
5. **SCARA** (Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm)
6. **Parallel/Delta** (multiple arms converging at the end-effector)

**Classification by Control Method (1 mark):**
- **Non-servo (Open-loop):** Pick-and-place robots with no feedback.
- **Servo-controlled (Closed-loop):**
  - Point-to-point (PTP)
  - Continuous path (CP)
  - Controlled path (between PTP and CP)

**SCARA Robot — Description and PCB Application (3 marks):**

**Description:** A SCARA robot has **two parallel rotary joints** providing compliance in the X-Y plane (horizontal) and a **linear joint** for Z-axis movement, plus a **rotational joint** about the Z-axis. It has **4 DOF** and is known for its **high speed** and **high precision** in horizontal-plane tasks.

**Why Preferred for PCB Assembly:**
- **High speed:** SCARAs can perform pick-and-place operations at **60–120 cycles/minute**, ideal for high-volume PCB component placement.
- **Rigidity in Z-axis:** The prismatic Z-axis provides **vertical stiffness** for **insertion tasks** (placing components into PCB holes without deflection).
- **Compliance in X-Y:** The horizontal compliance allows for **gentle insertion** of delicate components (resistors, ICs) without damaging them.
- **Compact footprint:** SCARAs have a small base, saving factory floor space.
- **High repeatability:** $\pm$ 0.01 mm is typical, essential for **surface-mount technology (SMT)** precision.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Losing Mistakes:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "3+ axes" criterion:** Writing just "a robot is a reprogrammable device" will lose 1 mark. Always include the axes requirement.
> 2. **Confusing Repeatability with Accuracy:** Many students interchange these terms. **Accuracy** is closeness to a commanded position; **Repeatability** is the ability to return to the same position consistently. Robots typically have **high repeatability but lower accuracy**.
> 3. **Spelling "Asimov" incorrectly:** Common misspellings — *Asimov, Azimov, Asimove*. The correct spelling is **Isaac Asimov** (Russian-American author).
> 4. **Drawing a humanoid robot when asked for an industrial robot:** In KTU exams, the default "robot diagram" is an **articulated arm**, not a humanoid. Read the question carefully.
> 5. **Omitting the comparison table in compare-type questions:** A **table is mandatory** for full marks in "compare and contrast" questions. Bullet points alone are insufficient.
> 6. **Writing the ISO definition without citing the standard number:** Always write "**ISO 8373:2021**" or "**ISO 8373**" to demonstrate precision.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1, Topic: Definitions of Robots and Robotics**

- ✅ **Robot (ISO 8373):** Automatically controlled + **Reprogrammable** + **Multipurpose** + **Manipulator with 3+ axes** + Industrial automation context.
- ✅ **Robotics:** The interdisciplinary science of designing, building, controlling, and applying robots.
- ✅ **Origin of the word "Robot":** Czech word *robota* = "forced labor," coined by **Karel Čapek** in **1920** in the play *R.U.R.*
- ✅ **First industrial robot:** **Unimate** by **George Devol**, installed at **General Motors** in **1961**.
- ✅ **Four subsystems of a robot:** Mechanical Structure, Actuation, Sensory, Control.
- ✅ **Three Laws of Robotics:** By **Isaac Asimov (1942)** — No harm > Obey orders > Self-preservation (in priority order).
- ✅ **Six mechanical configurations:** Cartesian, Cylindrical, Spherical, Articulated, SCARA, Parallel.
- ✅ **Two control methods:** Non-servo (open-loop) and Servo-controlled (closed-loop: PTP, CP, controlled path).
- ✅ **Robot ≠ Machine:** Robots are **software-defined and multipurpose**; machines are **hardware-defined and single-purpose**.
- ✅ **Key metrics to remember:** DOF, Payload, Workspace, Repeatability, Accuracy, Resolution.
- ✅ **SCARA = Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm** — best for PCB assembly due to high speed, Z-axis rigidity, and X-Y compliance.
- ✅ **Stanford Arm (1969)** and **PUMA (1978)** are the two most historically significant robots to remember for KTU exams.
- ✅ **Repeatability** ≠ **Accuracy** — this distinction is a guaranteed Part A question.
- ✅ **Always cite ISO 8373** when writing the formal robot definition in exams.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Robots - Manipulators, Mobile Robots - Wheeled & Legged Robots, Aerial Robots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBRT302 - INTRODUCTION TO ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Types of Robots - Manipulators, Mobile Robots - Wheeled & Legged Robots, Aerial Robots # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is a Robot? (KTU Syllabus Foundation) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Definition (ISO 8373:2021 Aligned):** An **industrial robot** is an *automatically controlled, reprogrammable, multipurpose manipulator, programmable in three or more axes, which can be either fixed in place or mobile*, for use in industrial automation applications. A **service robot** performs useful tasks for humans or equipment, excluding industrial automation applications. A robot, in the broadest engineering sense, is a **mechatronic system** — a tightly coupled integration of mechanical structure, electrical actuation, sensor feedback, and computational intelligence (the famous **Mechatronics Tetrad**: Mechanical + Electrical + Computer + Information). --- ## 1.2 The Three Primary Robot Taxonomies (KTU Module 1 Focus) For KTU Module 1, robots are classified along the dimension of **physical embodiment and locomotion strategy**. The four cardinal families are: | Family | Primary Function | Locomotion | KTU Canonical Example | |---|---|---|---| | **Manipulators** | Move objects/tools in space | Stationary base | Articulated robot arm in a car assembly line | | **Mobile Robots (Wheeled)** | Navigate flat/structured terrain | Wheels | AGV in a warehouse, Mars Rover | | **Mobile Robots (Legged)** | Traverse rough/unstructured terrain | Legs | Boston Dynamics Spot, humanoid ASIMO | | **Aerial Robots (Drones/UAVs)** | Fly through open 3D space | Rotor blades / fixed wings | Quadcopter surveying a farm | --- ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Robot Family Tree" 🧬 > [!IMPORTANT] > **Intuition Pump:** Think of robots as a family with very different personalities: > - The **Manipulator** is the *workshop craftsman* — chained to its workbench, but with extraordinary dexterity in its own little workspace (like a tailor at a sewing machine). > - The **Wheeled Mobile Robot** is the *delivery courier on a bicycle* — fast and energy-efficient, but only on smooth roads; stuck in a ditch or on stairs. > - The **Legged Robot** is the *mountain hiker* — slow, power-hungry, but can climb stairs, rocks, and rubble that defeat any wheel. > - The **Aerial Robot** is the *bird* — free to move in 3D without any ground at all, but consumes huge energy to stay aloft and cannot carry massive payloads. --- ## 1.4 Manipulators — The Industrial Backbone A **manipulator** is a serial or parallel chain of rigid links connected by joints, anchored to a fixed base. The end of the chain carries an **end-effector** (gripper, welding torch, paint sprayer, surgical instrument). > [!NOTE] > **Degrees of Freedom (DOF):** The number of *independent* parameters (joint variables) required to fully specify the configuration of the mechanism in space. For a serial manipulator in 3D, the **maximum practical DOF is 6** — 3 for *position* (x, y, z) and 3 for *orientation* (roll, pitch, yaw). **Architectural Sub-classes of Manipulators (KTU High-Yield):** 1. **Cartesian / Rectangular (PPP)** — 3 prismatic joints along X, Y, Z. Workspace is a rectangular box. Used in CNC pick-and-place, 3D printers. 2. **Cylindrical (RPP)** — Revolute base, then two prismatic. Workspace is a hollow cylinder. 3. **Spherical / Polar (RRP)** — Two revolute, one prismatic. Workspace is a spherical sector. 4. **Articulated / Revolute (RRR …)** — All revolute joints. Resembles a human arm. Workspace is a complex blob. **Industry workhorse** (e.g., KUKA KR 1000). 5. **SCARA (RRP with selective compliance)** — Two revolute + one prismatic, all vertical axes. Ideal for vertical assembly. 6. **Parallel / Delta** — Multiple kinematic chains connect the base to a single moving platform. Extremely fast and stiff (used in packaging). --- ## 1.5 Mobile Robots — The Wanderers A **mobile robot** has a *non-fixed* base. Its **configuration** is described by a *pose* in a 2D or 3D world frame, typically $(x, y, \theta)$ for ground robots, where $\theta$ is the heading angle. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pearl:** A wheeled robot gains mobility (the ability to *move from one place to another*) but loses the *dexterity* of a manipulator. This is a fundamental design trade-off in robotics. ### 1.5.1 Wheeled Mobile Robots (WMR) Wheels are the **most energy-efficient** locomotion mechanism on smooth ground (efficiency can exceed **98%** on hard surfaces, versus 20–40% for legs). **Standard Wheel Geometries (Holonomic vs Non-Holonomic):** - **Standard (fixed) wheel** — rolls in one direction, but the *steering* axis can rotate. Non-holonomic. - **Caster wheel** — swivels freely. Used for balance, not propulsion. - **Swedish / Mecanum wheel** — small passive rollers around the rim allow sideways motion. **Holonomic** — the robot can translate in any direction without first rotating. - **Omnidirectional wheel** — similar to mecanum, provides full planar holonomy. > [!NOTE] > **Holonomic vs Non-Holonomic:** A system is *holonomic* if the number of controllable DOF equals the total DOF of the system. A car is **non-holonomic** — it has 3 DOF $(x, y, \theta)$ but only 2 controls (steering, throttle); it cannot move sideways. A mecanum-wheeled robot is **holonomic** — 3 DOF, 3 independent controls. ### 1.5.2 Legged Mobile Robots Legs are used when the terrain is *unstructured* — stairs, rocks, rubble, sand, pipelines. Each leg is itself a small **serial manipulator** with its own hip, knee, and ankle joints. **Gait Patterns:** - **Static stability** — center of mass (CoM) always inside the support polygon. Possible with 4+ legs. - **Dynamic stability** — CoM is allowed outside the support polygon momentarily; the robot is stable through *controlled falling* (running). 2-legged humanoids *must* use dynamic stability. **Number of legs** (KTU classic question): 1 (unipod, hopping) → 2 (biped, human) → 4 (quadruped) → 6 (hexapod, very stable, insect-like) → 8 (octopod, spider-like). --- ## 1.6 Aerial Robots — The Sky-Bound > [!NOTE] > **Aerial Robot (Unmanned Aerial Vehicle — UAV) Definition:** A powered, aerial vehicle that does not carry a human operator, uses aerodynamic forces to provide vehicle lift, can fly autonomously or be piloted remotely, and can be expendable or recoverable. **Two Archetypes:** 1. **Rotary-wing (Multirotor):** Quadcopter, hexacopter, octocopter. **VTOL** (vertical take-off and landing), hover-capable. Most common for KTU applications (drone surveying, cinematography). 2. **Fixed-wing:** Airplane-style. Cannot hover, but extremely energy-efficient for long-range missions. Used in military surveillance, mapping large farms. 3. **Hybrid VTOL:** Combines both — vertical rotors for hover + fixed wing for cruise efficiency. **Key Performance Metric — Hover Thrust Equation:** A quadcopter must generate lift equal to its weight: $$T_{total} = 4 \cdot T_{motor} \geq M \cdot g$$ where $M$ is total mass, $g \approx 9.81 \, m/s^2$, and $T_{motor}$ is the thrust of one motor. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why So Many Robot Types? — The Underlying Design Drivers Every robot architecture is the result of optimising a constrained multi-objective problem. The four **primary drivers** are: 1. **Workspace geometry** — does the task require reaching into tight 3D cavities (favours articulated arms) or covering large flat floors (favours wheeled)? 2. **Terrain compliance** — smooth factory floor (wheels win) vs. disaster rubble (legs win) vs. open sky (rotors win). 3. **Payload-to-weight ratio** — heavy payloads favour parallel manipulators and fixed-wing aircraft; light payloads favour quadcopters. 4. **Energy efficiency** — wheels > tracks > legs > hover (per metre travelled on flat ground). --- ## 2.2 Manipulator Kinematics — The Math Spine The pose of a rigid body in 3D space is described by a **homogeneous transformation matrix** of size $4 \times 4$: $$ H = \begin{bmatrix} R & \mathbf{p} \\ \mathbf{0}^T & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ where $R \in SO(3)$ is the **rotation matrix** (3×3) and $\mathbf{p} \in \mathbb{R}^3$ is the position vector. For a serial chain of $n$ links, the end-effector pose is: $$ T_n = A_1(\theta_1) \cdot A_2(\theta_2) \cdots A_n(\theta_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} A_i(\theta_i) $$ where $A_i(\theta_i)$ is the $4 \times 4$ **Denavit–Hartenberg (DH) transform** for link $i$ (covered in detail in a later KTU module — included here for context). --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Robot Types Module > [!IMPORTANT] > The table below is the **only** formula reference most KTU students need for Module 1 questions. Memorise the **units** and the **sign conventions**. | # | Concept | Formula | Variable Glossary | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Degrees of Freedom (DOF) of a planar mechanism** (Grübler–Kutzbach) | $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | $n$ = total links, $j_1$ = 1-DOF joints, $j_2$ = 2-DOF joints | M = DOF. Apply to *single* closed loop. | | 2 | **DOF of a spatial mechanism** (Grübler) | $M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$ | $j_k$ = joint with $k$ DOF | Most KTU exam questions use spatial 6-DOF version. | | 3 | **6-DOF rule for a serial arm** | $DOF_{arm} = n$ (all revolute or prismatic) | $n$ = number of joints | 6-DOF needed for arbitrary 3D pose. | | 4 | **Wheel odometry (2D pose update)** | $x_{k+1} = x_k + v \Delta t \cos\theta_k$ | $v$ = linear velocity, $\Delta t$ = sample time | 3 such equations for $(x,y,\theta)$. | | 5 | **Differential drive kinematics** | $v = \dfrac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}, \quad \omega = \dfrac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}$ | $r$ = wheel radius, $L$ = wheelbase, $\omega_{R,L}$ = wheel angular speeds | m/s and rad/s. | | 6 | **Quadcopter hover thrust** | $4 T_{motor} = M g$ | $M$ = mass, $g \approx 9.81$ m/s² | Newtons. | | 7 | **Quadcopter roll/pitch torque** | $\tau = L \cdot T_{motor}$ | $L$ = distance from CoM to motor | N·m. | | 8 | **Static stability of legged robot** | $\vec{r}_{CoM} \in \text{Interior}(\text{Support Polygon})$ | Support polygon = convex hull of feet on ground | Boolean stability test. | | 9 | **Stride length vs stability** | $S_{static} \geq 0$ when $n_{legs} \geq 4$ (static walking possible) | — | For $n_{legs} \ge 4$, static gaits exist. | | 10 | **Workspace volume (Cartesian robot)** | $V = L_x \cdot L_y \cdot L_z$ | $L_i$ = travel along axis $i$ | m³. | --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Robot Type | Where You Will Find It in 2024–25 | Why This Architecture Was Chosen | |---|---|---| | Articulated 6-axis arm | Automotive body shop, Tesla Gigafactory | Dexterous welding around car body curves | | SCARA | PCB component placement | Fast, stiff vertical pick-and-place | | Delta (parallel) | Food packaging, pharma blister packs | 100+ picks/minute, hygienic | | AGV (wheeled) | Amazon fulfillment centres | Flat floor, 24×7 operation, long endurance | | Quadruped (Spot) | Construction site monitoring, oil rig inspection | Steps, stairs, muddy ground, autonomous patrol | | Humanoid (Optimus, Atlas) | Research, future warehouse labour | Human-shaped environments, human-tool compatibility | | Quadcopter | Aerial photography, precision agriculture | Hover capability, low cost, GPS-denied SLAM research | | Fixed-wing UAV | Border surveillance, crop mapping over km² | 2-hour endurance, large area coverage | --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation — Grübler–Kutzbach Formula for Planar DOF This is the single most important formula for KTU Module 1 problems. We will derive it from first principles so you can answer *any* variation the examiner throws at you. ### 3.1.1 Set-Up Consider a planar mechanism (all motion confined to the 2D plane, like a 4-bar linkage or a slider-crank). - Let $n$ = number of **links** (including the fixed ground link). - Let $j_1$ = number of **lower-pair joints** (1 DOF each: revolute R, prismatic P). - Let $j_2$ = number of **higher-pair joints** (2 DOF each: cam, gear pair contact). ### 3.1.2 Counting the DOF **Step 1 — Maximum DOF of a free body in the plane:** A free rigid link in 2D has **3 DOF** — 2 translations $(x, y)$ and 1 rotation $\theta_z$. So $n$ free links would have $3n$ DOF in total. **Step 2 — Subtract DOF removed by joints:** - A revolute joint removes **2 DOF** (allows 1 rotation, kills 2 translations). So each 1-DOF joint removes 2 DOF. - A cam contact (line/point contact) removes **1 DOF** (allows 2 DOF of relative motion). So each 2-DOF joint removes 1 DOF. **Step 3 — Subtract the ground link's DOF:** The ground link is fixed, so we subtract 3 DOF for it. Equivalently, we count only $(n-1)$ moving links. **Step 4 — Combine:** $$ M = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - 1 \cdot j_2 $$ $$ \boxed{\,M = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2\,} $$ This is the **Grübler–Kutzbach formula for planar mechanisms**. ### 3.1.3 Worked Example — 4-Bar Linkage A planar 4-bar linkage has $n = 4$ links and $j_1 = 4$ revolute joints. Substituting: $$ M = 3(4 - 1) - 2(4) - 0 $$ $$ M = 3(3) - 8 = 9 - 8 = 1 $$ **Interpretation:** $M = 1$ DOF — to control the entire mechanism, you only need to drive *one* joint (e.g., the input crank). The other three links follow kinematically. This matches our everyday experience with a bicycle or car door hinge mechanism. ### 3.1.4 Extension to Spatial (3D) Mechanisms In 3D, a free rigid body has **6 DOF** (3 translations + 3 rotations). A 1-DOF spatial joint (revolute) removes 5 DOF, a 2-DOF joint (universal) removes 4, etc. Generalising: $$ \boxed{\,M = 6(n - 1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5\,} $$ ### 3.1.5 Spatial Example — Standard Serial Robot Arm A 6-DOF industrial arm: $n = 7$ (6 links + ground), $j_1 = 6$ (all revolute). $$ M = 6(7 - 1) - 5(6) - 0 = 36 - 30 = 6 $$ Confirmed — the arm has the required 6 DOF for arbitrary spatial positioning. --- ## 3.2 Derivation — Differential Drive Kinematics This derivation appears in nearly every KTU university exam for the mobile-robot sub-topic. ### 3.2.1 Geometry A **differential-drive robot** has two independently driven wheels on a common axle. - $L$ = distance between the two wheels (wheelbase). - $r$ = radius of each wheel. - $\omega_R, \omega_L$ = angular velocities of right and left wheels (rad/s). - $v$ = linear velocity of the robot's center. - $\omega$ = angular velocity of the robot about its vertical axis. ### 3.2.2 Linear Velocity The point of contact of the right wheel with the ground moves at speed $r \omega_R$ (pure rolling). The left wheel moves at $r \omega_L$. The midpoint (the robot's center) moves at the *average*: $$ v = \frac{r \omega_R + r \omega_L}{2} = \frac{r (\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2} $$ ### 3.2.3 Angular Velocity The two wheels try to drag the robot in two different directions. The difference in their speeds creates a *rotation* of the whole chassis about an instantaneous center of curvature (ICC). The angular velocity is the velocity difference divided by the wheelbase: $$ \omega = \frac{r \omega_R - r \omega_L}{L} = \frac{r (\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L} $$ ### 3.2.4 Pose Update (Forward Kinematics in Time) Over a small time step $\Delta t$, the pose $(x, y, \theta)$ updates as: $$ x_{k+1} = x_k + v \, \Delta t \cos(\theta_k) $$ $$ y_{k+1} = y_k + v \, \Delta t \sin(\theta_k) $$ $$ \theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega \, \Delta t $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Special Cases (KTU favourite):** > - $\omega_R = \omega_L \implies \omega = 0$, the robot moves in a **straight line** (no rotation). > - $\omega_R = -\omega_L \implies v = 0$, the robot **spins in place** about its own center (zero turning radius). > - $\omega_L = 0$ and $\omega_R > 0$, the robot rotates about the **left wheel's contact point** (turning radius = $L$). --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Manipulator Architectures at a Glance | Architecture | Joint Sequence | DOF | Workspace Shape | Typical Application | Pros | Cons | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Cartesian | P-P-P | 3 | Rectangular box | 3D printing, pick-place | Simple kinematics, high accuracy | Large footprint | | Cylindrical | R-P-P | 3 | Hollow cylinder | Old machine loading | Good reach radially | Limited angular coverage | | Spherical (Polar) | R-R-P | 3 | Spherical sector | Welding | Long reach | Singularities | | Articulated | R-R-R-… | 4–6 | Spherical "donut" | Car assembly | Most dexterous | Complex kinematics | | SCARA | R-R-P (vertical axes) | 3–4 | Flat cylinder | PCB assembly | Very fast, stiff vertically | Limited 3D reach | | Delta (parallel) | 3 × (R-R-R) + platform | 3–4 | Dome / frustum | Food packaging | Ultra-fast (100+ Hz) | Limited workspace | > **KTU Mnemonic:** *"Cartesian = Cube, Cylindrical = Cylinder, Spherical = Sphere, Articulated = Anywhere-a-human-arm-can-reach."* --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Wheel Configurations | Drive Type | Wheels | DOF | Maneuverability | Control Complexity | Example | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Differential drive | 2 driven + 1 caster | 3 (2 ctrl) | High | Low | Roomba, Mars Sojourner | | Tricycle / Ackermann | 2 driven rear + 1 steered front | 3 (2 ctrl) | High (curved paths) | Medium | Autonomous car (simplified) | | Synchronous drive | 3 wheels, all steer + drive | 3 (2 ctrl) | High (omnidirectional in rotation) | Medium | Older hospital delivery robots | | Omnidirectional (Mecanum) | 4 mecanum wheels | 3 (3 ctrl) | Full holonomic | High | RoboCup SSL, warehouse robots | | Swedish 45° / 90° | 4 special wheels | 3 (3 ctrl) | Holonomic | Very high | Research prototypes | > **KTU Pearl:** The **number of independently driven wheels** is *not* the same as the **number of control inputs**. A car has 4 wheels but only 2 controls (steering + throttle), hence it is *non-holonomic*. --- ## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example — Quadcopter Hover > **Question:** A quadcopter has a total mass (with battery and payload) of $M = 1.5$ kg. The distance from the center of mass to each motor is $L = 0.25$ m. Find (a) the thrust per motor in hover, and (b) the maximum roll torque if each motor can produce up to $T_{max} = 6$ N. **Part (a) — Thrust per motor at hover:** In hover, total upward thrust = weight. $$ 4 T_{hover} = M g $$ $$ T_{hover} = \frac{M g}{4} = \frac{1.5 \times 9.81}{4} $$ $$ T_{hover} = \frac{14.715}{4} = 3.68 \, \text{N} $$ **Part (b) — Maximum roll torque:** To roll, the two motors on one side increase thrust, the two on the other side decrease by the same amount (the *differential* creates the torque, the *average* still balances gravity). $$ \tau_{roll,max} = L \cdot \Delta T_{max} $$ If the maximum swing is $\Delta T_{max} = T_{max} - T_{hover} = 6 - 3.68 = 2.32$ N per motor, then per side 2 motors contribute: $$ \tau_{roll,max} = L \cdot 2 \cdot \Delta T_{max} = 0.25 \times 2 \times 2.32 $$ $$ \tau_{roll,max} = 1.16 \, \text{N·m} $$ > [!NOTE] > This calculation underlies every quadcopter flight controller — it determines how *agile* the drone can be. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Master Classification Tree of Robots (Mermaid Mind-Map) ```mermaid graph TD A[ROBOTS
Module 1 KTU] --> B[Manipulators
Fixed Base] A --> C[Mobile Robots
Moving Base] A --> D[Aerial Robots
UAV Family] B --> B1[Cartesian PPP] B --> B2[Cylindrical RPP] B --> B3[Spherical RRP] B --> B4[Articulated RRRR] B --> B5[SCARA] B --> B6[Parallel Delta] C --> C1[Wheeled WMR] C --> C2[Legged LMR] C --> C3[Tracked] C1 --> C1a[Differential Drive] C1 --> C1b[Ackermann Steering] C1 --> C1c[Holonomic Mecanum] C1 --> C1d[Omnidirectional] C2 --> C2a[Uniped] C2 --> C2b[Biped Humanoid] C2 --> C3c[Quadruped] C2 --> C4d[Hexapod] C2 --> C5e[Octopod] D --> D1[Rotary Wing Multirotor] D --> D2[Fixed Wing] D --> D3[Hybrid VTOL] D1 --> D1a[Quadcopter] D1 --> D1b[Hexacopter] D1 --> D1c[Octocopter] classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef group fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef leaf fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000,color:#000 class A root class B,C,D group class B1,B2,B3,B4,B5,B6,C1,C2,C3,D1,D2,D3 leaf class C1a,C1b,C1c,C1d,C2a,C2b,C3c,C4d,C5e,D1a,D1b,D1c leaf ``` --- ## 4.2 Functional Architecture of a Mobile Robot System ```mermaid graph LR SENS[Sensors
Lidar, IMU, Camera, Wheel Encoders] --> PROC[Perception
SLAM Module] PROC --> PLAN[Planner
A-star, RRT, MPC] PLAN --> CTRL[Controller
PID / Model Based] CTRL --> ACT[Actuators
DC Motors, Servos] ACT --> MECH[Mechanical Base
Wheels Legs Rotors] MECH -->|Feedback| SENS USER[Operator / Mission Goal] --> PLAN classDef block fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656,stroke-width:2px classDef fb fill:#e2efda,stroke:#548235,stroke-width:2px class SENS,PROC,PLAN,CTRL,ACT,MECH block class USER fb ``` > [!NOTE] > This is the canonical **sense → plan → act** loop, valid for *every* robot family: manipulators, mobile, or aerial. Manipulators often compress planning and control into a single trajectory generator. --- ## 4.3 Quadcopter Dynamics — Functional Block Diagram ```mermaid graph TD POSE[Desired Pose
x y z roll pitch yaw] --> ATTC[Attitude Controller
PID on Euler angles] ATTC --> ALTC[Altitude Controller
PID on z] ATTC --> MIX[Control Mixer] ALTC --> MIX MIX --> M1[Motor 1 Front] MIX --> M2[Motor 2 Right] MIX --> M3[Motor 3 Back] MIX --> M4[Motor 4 Left] M1 --> THR[Thrust and Torque Generation] M2 --> THR M3 --> THR M4 --> THR THR --> BOD[6 DOF Rigid Body Dynamics] BOD --> IMU[IMU Gyroscope Accelerometer] IMU --> ATTC classDef loop fill:#fce4d6,stroke:#c00000 classDef act fill:#c5e0b4,stroke:#385723 class POSE,ATTC,ALTC,MIX,IMU loop class M1,M2,M3,M4,THR,BOD act ``` --- ## 4.4 Legged Robot Gait State Machine ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> StanceAllFour StanceAllFour --> TripodA: Lift legs 1 3 5 TripodA --> StanceAllFour: Place 1 3 5 StanceAllFour --> TripodB: Lift legs 2 4 6 TripodB --> StanceAllFour: Place 2 4 6 StanceAllFour --> Stop: Mission Complete Stop --> [*] note right of TripodA: Static Stability
CoM inside triangle of stance feet note right of TripodB: Three legs on ground form
a support triangle ``` --- ## 4.5 Wheel Configuration Comparison (Block-Level Schematic) ```mermaid graph TD subgraph DD[Differential Drive] DD1[Motor Right Wheel] --- DD2[Chassis] DD3[Motor Left Wheel] --- DD2 DD4[Passive Caster] --- DD2 end subgraph AC[Ackermann] AC1[Drive Motor] --- AC2[Chassis] AC3[Steered Front Wheel] --- AC2 end subgraph HO[Holonomic Mecanum] H1[Mecanum FL] --- H2[Chassis] H3[Mecanum FR] --- H2 H4[Mecanum RL] --- H2 H5[Mecanum RR] --- H2 end classDef arch fill:#ddebf7,stroke:#2e75b6 class DD,AC,HO arch ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Mark Distribution (Module 1 — Internal Choice Pattern):** > - Part A: 2 × 3 marks = **6 marks** (short answer / direct) > - Part B: 1 × 14 marks = **14 marks** (with internal choice between Q-A and Q-B) > - Total Module 1 weight in university exam: 20 marks per module. --- ## 5.1 PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` > **Differentiate between a manipulator and a mobile robot. Give one industrial example of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks — 1.5 each):** A **manipulator** is a robotic system with a *fixed base* whose primary function is to *manipulate* objects in its workspace through a serial/parallel chain of links. Its workspace is *stationary* and is defined by the reach of its links. **Example:** A 6-axis articulated robot arm (e.g., KUKA KR 16) welding a car body in an automotive assembly line. A **mobile robot** has a *movable base* whose primary function is *navigation* from one place to another. It trades off dexterous manipulation for the ability to cover large distances and re-locate itself. **Example:** An Automated Guided Vehicle (AGV) transporting palletised goods in an Amazon fulfillment warehouse. The two may be *combined* in a **mobile manipulator** (e.g., a wheeled humanoid carrying a robotic arm), but their core distinction is the *fixed vs. mobile* base. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` > **Explain the term "Degrees of Freedom" (DOF) of a robot. Why is 6 DOF considered the maximum practical value for a serial manipulator?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **DOF** is the number of independent parameters (position/orientation variables) required to completely specify the configuration of a robotic mechanism in space. For a **serial manipulator in 3D space**, a rigid body has 6 independent parameters — 3 for *position* $(x, y, z)$ and 3 for *orientation* (roll, pitch, yaw). A general task (e.g., placing a bolt and tightening it at an arbitrary angle) requires *all* six to be controllable independently. Therefore, a serial arm needs at least 6 joints. **More than 6 DOF** introduces *kinematic redundancy* — multiple joint configurations can achieve the same end-effector pose. This complicates control, increases cost, and is only justified for obstacle avoidance or dexterity in cluttered environments (e.g., the 7-DOF KUKA LWR). --- ## 5.2 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Before You Write:** > 1. **Do not** write generic "robot is a machine that works automatically" answers. Examiners award marks only for *syllabus-specific terminology* (e.g., "non-holonomic", "Grübler–Kutzbach", "support polygon"). > 2. In numerical questions, **always show units**. A "3.68" without "N" will lose 0.5 marks. > 3. For Grübler's formula problems, **write the formula first**, then substitute. A common mistake is forgetting to subtract 1 from $n$ to exclude the ground link. > 4. In a manipulator classification question, **draw a small sketch** of each architecture. 1 mark is reserved for the figure. > 5. Mobile robot DOF problems: students often count wheel *motors* as DOF — they are *actuators*, not DOF. Use Grübler. --- ### 📝 Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` > **(a)** Classify industrial manipulators based on their kinematic architecture. With neat sketches, explain **Cartesian**, **Cylindrical**, **Spherical**, **Articulated**, and **SCARA** configurations. State one industrial application of each. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Using Grübler–Kutzbach formula, calculate the degrees of freedom of: > (i) a planar 4-bar linkage, and > (ii) a 6-DOF spatial serial manipulator with all revolute joints. > Comment on the meaning of the result. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for Q-A **Part (a) — Manipulator Architectures (7 marks = 1.4 each, plus 0.5 for the comparison table)** | Architecture | Joint Type | DOF | Sketch Description | Industrial Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Cartesian** | P-P-P | 3 | 3 perpendicular sliders form a box-shaped workspace | 3D printing, CNC pick-and-place | | **Cylindrical** | R-P-P | 3 | Base rotates (R), then vertical slide (P), then horizontal reach (P) | Old machine-tool loading | | **Spherical (Polar)** | R-R-P | 3 | Two rotating joints, one prismatic reach | Welding, foundry pick-and-place | | **Articulated** | R-R-R-R-R-R | 4–6 | Resembles a human arm with shoulder, elbow, wrist | Automotive body assembly, painting | | **SCARA** | R-R-P (selective compliance) | 3–4 | Two horizontal revolute joints, vertical prismatic | PCB component placement, vertical assembly | > A small ASCII-style sketch description for each is acceptable in exams; the mark scheme allocates 0.5 marks for diagrams. **Comparison in tabular form (1 mark):** - Cartesian has the **simplest kinematics** and best accuracy; Articulated has the **largest dexterous workspace**; SCARA is **fastest** in the vertical plane; Spherical has limited dexterity but good reach; Cylindrical is mostly obsolete in modern industry. --- **Part (b) — Grübler–Kutzbach Calculations (7 marks = 3.5 each + 0 for commentary, but commentary is required for full marks)** **(i) Planar 4-bar linkage:** Given: $n = 4$ links, $j_1 = 4$ revolute joints, $j_2 = 0$. $$ M = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2 $$ $$ M = 3(4 - 1) - 2(4) - 0 $$ $$ M = 3(3) - 8 = 9 - 8 $$ $$ \boxed{\,M = 1 \text{ DOF}\,} $$ **Comment:** The 4-bar linkage has only 1 DOF, meaning a single input (e.g., the input crank) fully determines the configuration of all four links. This is why one motor is enough to drive an entire folding mechanism. **(ii) 6-DOF spatial serial manipulator:** Given: $n = 7$ links (6 moving + 1 ground), $j_1 = 6$ revolute joints. $$ M = 6(n - 1) - 5j_1 - 0 $$ $$ M = 6(7 - 1) - 5(6) $$ $$ M = 36 - 30 $$ $$ \boxed{\,M = 6 \text{ DOF}\,} $$ **Comment:** The arm can independently control 3 translations and 3 rotations, sufficient for *arbitrary* end-effector pose in 3D. Adding a 7th revolute joint would give $M = 7$, making the robot *kinematically redundant* — useful for singularity avoidance but harder to control. --- ### 📝 Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` > **(a)** Compare **wheeled** and **legged** mobile robots in terms of energy efficiency, terrain adaptability, and control complexity. Discuss the trade-offs involved in choosing one over the other, with examples. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A differential-drive mobile robot has wheel radius $r = 0.05$ m and wheelbase $L = 0.30$ m. If the right wheel rotates at $\omega_R = 4$ rad/s and the left wheel rotates at $\omega_L = 2$ rad/s, calculate: > (i) the linear velocity $v$ of the robot, > (ii) the angular velocity $\omega$ of the robot, > (iii) the radius of curvature $R$ of the path traced by the robot's center. > **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for Q-B **Part (a) — Wheeled vs Legged Comparison (7 marks)** | Parameter | Wheeled Mobile Robot | Legged Mobile Robot | |---|---|---| | **Energy efficiency on flat ground** | Excellent (>90%) | Poor (20–40%) due to impact losses | | **Terrain adaptability** | Limited to smooth, hard surfaces (with exceptions like Mars rovers with rocker-bogie) | Excellent — can traverse stairs, rocks, rubble, sand | | **Mechanical complexity** | Low (fewer actuators) | High (each leg has 2–3 actuators) | | **Control complexity** | Simple (kinematic models are tractable) | High (dynamic balance, gait planning) | | **Speed on flat ground** | High | Lower (except running humanoids in bursts) | | **Payload capacity** | High | Limited by actuator strength | | **Example** | Amazon Kiva AGV, Boston Dynamics Stretch (wheeled base) | Boston Dynamics Spot (quadruped), Cassie (biped) | **Trade-off discussion (2 marks):** Wheels are the *rational* choice for any environment that has been *engineered* for them (factory floors, roads, warehouse tiles). Legs become the *only* viable choice when the environment is *unstructured* or *natural* — disaster rubble, planetary exploration, military reconnaissance, or anywhere a human would have to walk. The hybrid concept of **wheel-legs** (e.g., Boston Dynamics Handle, ETH Zürich ANYmal with wheels) tries to capture the best of both worlds at the cost of mechanical and control complexity. **Stability principle (1 mark):** A legged robot with $n \ge 4$ legs can be **statically stable** (CoM inside support polygon at all times), while a biped *must* use **dynamic stability** (controlled falling). This is why most research platforms use 4 or 6 legs. --- **Part (b) — Differential-Drive Kinematics (7 marks = 2 + 2 + 3)** **Given:** $r = 0.05$ m, $L = 0.30$ m, $\omega_R = 4$ rad/s, $\omega_L = 2$ rad/s. **(i) Linear velocity $v$ (2 marks):** $$ v = \frac{r (\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2} = \frac{0.05 (4 + 2)}{2} $$ $$ v = \frac{0.05 \times 6}{2} = \frac{0.30}{2} $$ $$ \boxed{\,v = 0.15 \, \text{m/s}\,} $$ **(ii) Angular velocity $\omega$ (2 marks):** $$ \omega = \frac{r (\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L} = \frac{0.05 (4 - 2)}{0.30} $$ $$ \omega = \frac{0.05 \times 2}{0.30} = \frac{0.10}{0.30} $$ $$ \boxed{\,\omega = 0.333 \, \text{rad/s} \approx 19.1^\circ/\text{s}\,} $$ **(iii) Radius of curvature $R$ (3 marks):** The radius of curvature is the ratio of linear to angular velocity: $$ R = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{0.15}{0.333} = 0.45 \, \text{m} $$ **Alternative derivation (verify the answer is consistent):** The instant center of rotation (ICR) lies on the line connecting the two wheel contact points, offset from the left wheel by: $$ d_{left} = \frac{v}{\omega} \cdot \frac{\omega_L}{\omega_R - \omega_L} \cdot \text{(constant)} \quad \rightarrow \text{or simply } R = v/\omega $$ $$ \boxed{\,R = 0.45 \, \text{m}\,} $$ **Final remark (1 mark):** Since both wheels rotate in the *same* direction but at *different* speeds, the robot follows a *circular arc* of radius 0.45 m, curving *toward the slower* (left) side. If $\omega_L$ were 0, the robot would pivot around the left wheel — a common manoeuver for in-place turning of a differential drive. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌 > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1** ### ✅ Conceptual Vocabulary (must be in your active memory) - **Robot** (ISO 8373), **manipulator**, **end-effector**, **mobile robot**, **aerial robot (UAV)**, **UGV**, **AUV**. - **DOF** (degrees of freedom), **workspace** (reachable + dexterous), **payload**, **repeatability**, **accuracy**. - **Holonomic** vs **non-holonomic** systems. - **Static stability** vs **dynamic stability** for legged robots. - **Statically stable gait** is possible for $n_{legs} \ge 4$. - **VTOL** (vertical take-off and landing) — characteristic of multirotor UAVs. - **Forward kinematics**, **pose** $(x, y, \theta)$, **wheel odometry**. ### ✅ Architectural Sub-Types You Must Recognise Instantly - **Manipulators:** Cartesian, Cylindrical, Spherical, Articulated, SCARA, Delta (parallel). - **Wheeled:** Differential drive, Ackermann (car-like), Synchronous, Mecanum (holonomic). - **Legged:** Uniped → Biped → Quadruped → Hexapod → Octopod. - **Aerial:** Multirotor (quad/hex/octo), Fixed-wing, Hybrid VTOL. ### ✅ Formulas You Must Be Able to Write Cold, in Sleep, Under Exam Pressure - Planar Grübler: $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ - Spatial Grübler: $M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$ - Differential drive: $v = \tfrac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}$, $\omega = \tfrac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}$ - Quadcopter hover: $4 T = Mg$ - Turning radius: $R = v / \omega$ ### ✅ Common Exam Traps (Pitfalls to Avoid) - Forgetting to subtract the **ground link** in Grübler's formula (use $n-1$). - Confusing **holonomic** with "having many wheels" — it is about the *number of controllable DOF vs total DOF*. - Saying a manipulator has DOF = number of motors — **wrong**; DOF is about kinematics, not actuation. - Forgetting units (N, rad/s, m/s, m) in numerical answers. - Calling a SCARA "the same as an articulated arm" — SCARA has *selective compliance* and *vertical prismatic* axis, not the same as a fully articulated 6-axis arm. ### ✅ High-Yield Sample Question Stems for Self-Test 1. *"Using Grübler's formula, find the DOF of a 5-bar planar mechanism with 5 revolute joints."* (Answer: $3(4) - 2(5) = 2$ DOF) 2. *"Why is a 4-wheeled car non-holonomic even though it has 4 wheels?"* (Answer: only 2 controls for 3 planar DOF) 3. *"A hexapod robot walks using a tripod gait. Explain why this is statically stable."* (Answer: at any instant 3 feet on the ground form a support triangle; CoM can be kept inside it) 4. *"Why do quadcopters need an even number of rotors spinning in opposite directions?"* (Answer: to cancel net reaction torque from each motor) ---

topics.title: Anatomy of a Robotic Manipulator - Links, Joints, Actuators, Sensors, Controller
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT302 - INTRODUCTION TO ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Anatomy of a Robotic Manipulator - Links, Joints, Actuators, Sensors, Controller

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **robotic manipulator** is a programmable, multi-axis, mechanical-arm-type mechatronic system designed to perform industrial, medical, or exploratory tasks by mimicking the dexterity of a human arm. Its **anatomy** comprises five functionally integrated subsystems: **Links**, **Joints**, **Actuators**, **Sensors**, and the **Controller**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Standardized):**
> A robotic manipulator is an open or closed kinematic chain of rigid links connected by joints, powered by actuators, monitored by sensors, and orchestrated by a controller to execute programmed motion in a structured workspace.

| Subsystem | Functional Role | Human-Anatomy Analogy |
|-----------|----------------|-----------------------|
| **Link** | Rigid structural member | Bones |
| **Joint** | Constrained relative motion interface | Elbow / Knee / Shoulder |
| **Actuator** | Converts energy → mechanical motion | Muscles |
| **Sensor** | Measures internal & external state | Nerves & Skin (touch) |
| **Controller** | Decision-making & sequencing unit | Brain & Spinal Cord |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **human arm lifting a coffee cup**:

- The **upper arm and forearm** are the **links** — rigid, cannot bend.
- The **elbow and shoulder** are the **joints** — they allow pivoting/sliding.
- The **biceps and triceps** are the **actuators** — they generate force when commanded.
- The **stretch receptors in tendons and the eyes** are the **sensors** — they tell the brain where the hand is and whether it touched the cup.
- The **brain (motor cortex + cerebellum)** is the **controller** — it plans the trajectory, compares intention vs reality, and corrects errors.

A robot does **exactly the same thing**, but with motors instead of muscles, encoders instead of stretch receptors, and a microcontroller instead of neurons.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric for KTU:** The standard metric for measuring manipulator capability is **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** = total number of independent joint motions. A 6-DOF manipulator is considered the minimum for full spatial positioning + orientation.

## 1.3 Why These Five Subsystems?

Each subsystem addresses a fundamental engineering requirement:

1. **Links** → Structural integrity (must carry load without deflection).
2. **Joints** → Mobility (must permit the specific motions needed).
3. **Actuators** → Power (must deliver force/torque).
4. **Sensors** → Feedback (must close the control loop).
5. **Controller** → Intelligence (must execute the program).

Removing any one of these renders the manipulator **non-functional**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Links — The Skeletal Framework

A **link** is a rigid body that maintains a fixed geometric relationship between the joints it connects. In manipulator notation, link *i* connects joint *i* to joint *i+1*.

**Classifications:**
- **Ground link (Base):** Link 0 — the fixed anchor to the floor or platform.
- **End-effector link:** The terminal link that carries the gripper/tool.
- **Intermediate links:** All links between base and end-effector.

**Key link parameters** (used in Denavit–Hartenberg convention, Module 2):
- **Link length ($a_i$):** distance between joint axes.
- **Link twist ($\alpha_i$):** angle between joint axes.
- **Link offset ($d_i$):** distance along common normal.

**Structural constraints:**
- Stiffness must be high → typically **aluminum alloys, steel, carbon-fiber composites**.
- Mass must be low → high **strength-to-weight ratio** desired.
- Natural frequency should be **above control bandwidth** to avoid resonance.

## 2.2 Joints — The Mobility Interfaces

A **joint** is a constrained mechanical interface between two links that permits a specific, well-defined relative motion.

| Joint Type | DOF | Motion | Symbol | Industrial Use |
|------------|-----|--------|--------|----------------|
| **Revolute (R)** | 1 | Rotation about an axis | 🔄 | Shoulder, elbow |
| **Prismatic (P)** | 1 | Linear sliding | ↔ | Linear gantry |
| **Cylindrical** | 2 | Rotation + translation | Combination | Limited |
| **Spherical (Ball)** | 3 | Rotation in 3 axes | Multi-axial | Wrist (approximated) |
| **Universal** | 2 | Two orthogonal rotations | Hinge | Wrist |
| **Screw (Helical)** | 1 | Coupled rotation+translation | Twist | Rare |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The **n-DOF manipulator configuration is named by writing joint types from base to end-effector**. Example: A "RRR" manipulator is a 3-DOF arm with three revolute joints (typical of articulated arms).

**Joint Position Variables (Generalized Coordinates):**
- Revolute: $\theta_i(t)$ — angular position (rad).
- Prismatic: $d_i(t)$ — linear displacement (m).

## 2.3 Actuators — The Muscle Equivalent

An **actuator** converts stored energy (electrical, hydraulic, pneumatic, or chemical) into controlled mechanical motion.

**Three primary classes:**

### (a) Electric Actuators
- **DC Servo Motors, AC Servo Motors, Stepper Motors, BLDC motors.**
- Most common in industrial robots.
- **Advantages:** Precise control, easy interfacing, clean, low maintenance.
- **Disadvantages:** Lower power-to-weight ratio compared to hydraulics.

### (b) Hydraulic Actuators
- **Linear hydraulic cylinders, hydraulic rotary vane/piston motors.**
- **Advantages:** Very high force/torque, suitable for heavy payloads (e.g., 1000+ kg).
- **Disadvantages:** Leakage, maintenance-intensive, bulky.

### (c) Pneumatic Actuators
- **Compressed-air cylinders, air motors.**
- **Advantages:** Cheap, simple, clean, fast.
- **Disadvantages:** Compressibility → poor position accuracy; typically used in **pick-and-place** applications only.

**Actuator Selection Decision Matrix (real-world utility):**

| Parameter | Electric | Hydraulic | Pneumatic |
|-----------|----------|-----------|-----------|
| Precision | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ |
| Power density | ★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★ |
| Cost | ★★★★ | ★★ | ★★★★★ |
| Maintenance | ★★★★★ | ★★ | ★★★ |
| Cleanliness | ★★★★★ | ★★ | ★★★★ |

## 2.4 Sensors — The Nervous System

Sensors provide the controller with **information about the manipulator's internal state** and **the external environment**.

### Two Fundamental Categories:

#### (A) Proprioceptive (Internal) Sensors
Measure variables internal to the manipulator.

| Sensor | Measures | Typical Device |
|--------|----------|----------------|
| **Encoder (incremental/absolute)** | Joint angle | Optical/magnetic encoder |
| **Resolver** | Joint angle (robust) | Electromagnetic |
| **Potentiometer** | Joint angle | Resistive |
| **Tachometer** | Joint velocity | Back-EMF or optical |
| **Current sensor** | Motor torque | Hall-effect shunt |
| **Force/Torque sensor** | Wrist forces | Strain-gauge based |
| **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)** | Orientation/acceleration | MEMS accelerometer + gyroscope |

#### (B) Exteroceptive (External) Sensors
Measure variables in the workspace.

| Sensor | Function | Typical Application |
|--------|----------|---------------------|
| **Vision camera (CCD/CMOS)** | Image capture | Object recognition |
| **LiDAR** | Range measurement | Navigation, mapping |
| **Ultrasonic / IR proximity** | Distance | Obstacle detection |
| **Tactile sensor** | Contact detection | Gripper feedback |
| **Laser range finder** | Precise distance | Precision assembly |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In modern **closed-loop** robotic systems, sensors are **non-optional** — they are the only way the controller can verify that the command issued to the actuator was actually executed. The feedback signal closes the control loop.

## 2.5 Controller — The Brain

The **controller** is the digital (or hybrid digital-analog) system that:
1. Interprets the **user program / task description**.
2. Performs **kinematic and dynamic computations** (forward/inverse kinematics, trajectory planning).
3. Generates **joint-level reference commands**.
4. Implements **control algorithms** (PID, computed torque, adaptive control).
5. Closes the feedback loop by reading sensors and correcting errors.

**Typical controller hardware classes:**

| Type | Use Case | Key Features |
|------|----------|--------------|
| **Microcontroller (Arduino, STM32)** | Educational, small bots | Low cost, simple |
| **PLC (Programmable Logic Controller)** | Industrial automation | Rugged, deterministic |
| **Industrial Robot Controller (ABB, FANUC, KUKA)** | Production robots | High-speed, multi-axis, real-time OS |
| **PC-based + Real-Time OS (RTX, Xenomai)** | Research, advanced control | Flexible, high computation |
| **Embedded SoC (Raspberry Pi + RT extension)** | Hobbyist / research prototypes | Inexpensive, networked |

**Standard Control Loop Architecture:**

$$e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$$

$$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e(t)\,dt + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

where:
- $r(t)$ = reference (desired) joint position.
- $y(t)$ = actual (sensor-measured) joint position.
- $e(t)$ = tracking error.
- $u(t)$ = control signal sent to the actuator.
- $K_p, K_i, K_d$ = proportional, integral, derivative gains.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Table — Equations and Units (No Pipes in Cells)**

| Symbol/Equation | Meaning | Unit / Value |
|-----------------|---------|--------------|
| $\text{DOF} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i$ | Degrees of Freedom formula (Gruebler/Kutzbach) | dimensionless |
| $n$ | Number of links (excluding ground) | count |
