| Criterion | PPP (Cartesian) | RRR (Articulated) |
|-----------|------------------|---------------------|
| **Structure** | 3 linear slides, mutually perpendicular | 3 rotary joints: waist, shoulder, elbow |
| **Workspace** | Rectangular box | Complex "doughnut" with internal void near base |
| **Accuracy** | **Highest** (no compounding rotation errors) | Lower (rotational encoder errors at each joint accumulate) |
| **Inverse Kinematics** | **Trivial** — direct readout | **Hard** — requires cosine rule, multiple solutions |
| **Singularities** | **None** in the working volume | **Multiple** (e.g., aligned arm) |
| **Applications** | CNC, 3D printing, PCB assembly | Automotive welding, painting, palletizing |

**Conclusion:** PPP is preferred for **precision light-duty** tasks in structured environments; RRR is preferred for **complex 3D reach** in unstructured industrial settings. *(7 marks: 1 per row + 1 for conclusion.)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q.B(b)

**Part (i) — End-effector position:**

Given $\theta = 60°,\ d_2 = 1.2\,\text{m},\ d_3 = 0.8\,\text{m}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= d_2 \cos\theta = 1.2 \cos 60° = 1.2 \times 0.5 = 0.6\,\text{m} \\
y &= d_2 \sin\theta = 1.2 \sin 60° = 1.2 \times 0.8660 = 1.0392\,\text{m} \\
z &= d_3 = 0.8\,\text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
P = \begin{bmatrix} 0.600 \\ 1.039 \\ 0.800 \end{bmatrix} \text{ metres}
$$

**Part (ii) — Workspace volume:**
The workspace is a **hollow cylinder** (assuming the arm cannot pass through its own column, $d_2^{\min} = 0$ in the KTU standard simplification):

$$
V = \pi r^2 h = \pi (1.5)^2 (2.0) = \pi (2.25)(2.0) = 4.5\pi \approx 14.14\,\text{m}^3
$$

*(7 marks: 3 for (i), 3 for (ii) derivation, 1 for units.)*

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Wrong joint sequence reading** — Students often read RPP as "prismatic first, then revolute." KTU convention is **base-to-end-effector** order. Always re-check.
> 2. **Mixing up the cylindrical formula signs** — It is $y = d_2 \sin\theta$, **not** $d_2 \cos\theta$ swapped with $x$. Memorize: $x$ uses $\cos$, $y$ uses $\sin$.
> 3. **Forgetting the shoulder offset $d_1$ in the RRR Z equation** — The constant $d_1$ is the height of the shoulder above the floor. Dropping it loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Ignoring singularities** — Whenever a question gives joint angles, add **one line** checking if $\theta_3 = 0°$ or $180°$. Examiners reward it.
> 5. **Not stating units** — Always append "metres" or "radians" to numerical answers in kinematics.
> 6. **Confusing workspace shape with reach** — Workspace is the **set of all reachable points** (a region); reach is the **maximum distance** (a scalar). Examiners test this distinction.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The **four canonical 3-DOF configurations** are **PPP, RPP, RRP, RRR**, ordered from base to end-effector.
- ✅ **P = Prismatic** (linear slide, 1 translational DOF), **R = Revolute** (rotational, 1 angular DOF).
- ✅ **PPP** → Cartesian → **rectangular** workspace → no singularities → high precision.
- ✅ **RPP** → Cylindrical → **hollow cylinder** workspace → singularity at $d_2 = 0$.
- ✅ **RRP** → Spherical/Polar → **spherical shell** workspace → closed-form inverse kinematics.
- ✅ **RRR** → Articulated/Anthropomorphic → **complex torus-like** workspace → singularities at $\theta_3 = 0°,\ 180°$.
- ✅ **Forward Kinematics Equations** (must be memorized):
    - PPP: $P = (d_2,\ d_1,\ d_3)$
    - RPP: $P = (d_2\cos\theta,\ d_2\sin\theta,\ d_3)$
    - RRP: $P = (d_3\sin\phi\cos\theta,\ d_3\sin\phi\sin\theta,\ d_3\cos\phi)$
    - RRR: $P = (c_1(L_2 c_2 + L_3 c_{23}),\ s_1(L_2 c_2 + L_3 c_{23}),\ d_1 + L_2 s_2 + L_3 s_{23})$
- ✅ **Jacobian of PPP is the identity** → no kinematic singularities inside the workspace.
- ✅ **Trade-off Rule:** As mechanical complexity ↑, reach ↑, but accuracy ↓ and IK difficulty ↑.
- ✅ **Master the substitutions** — Most KTU 14-mark questions give numeric joint variables and ask for end-effector position. Practice substitution drills.
- ✅ **Always** check for singularities when the question involves RRR robots.
- ✅ **Application Mapping** (high-yield for viva and 3-mark questions):
    - PPP → CNC, 3D printers
    - RPP → Machine tending
    - RRP → Forging, large welding
    - RRR → Automotive assembly (most common in industry)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Features of SCARA, PUMA Robots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBRT302 - INTRODUCTION TO ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Features of SCARA, PUMA Robots # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Introduction to Industrial Robot Architectures In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PCBRT302 – Introduction to Robotics**, Module 1 lays the architectural foundation of industrial manipulators. Two of the most historically significant and widely-deployed robot geometries in the world of factory automation are the **SCARA** and **PUMA** robots. They represent two fundamentally different philosophies of manipulator design: one optimized for **vertical-assembly precision** and the other modeled after the **anthropomorphic (human) arm**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** > Students must be able to *describe the salient features, joint configuration, degrees of freedom (DOF), workspace, and typical applications* of SCARA and PUMA robots. These are direct, frequently-tested 7-to-14 mark questions in the End Semester Evaluation (ESE). --- ## 1.2 SCARA Robot — Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Definition (KTU Board Standard):** > **SCARA** stands for **Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm** (sometimes called *Selective Compliance Articulated Robot Arm*). It is a **4-axis (4-DOF) cylindrical-coordinate-type** manipulator specifically engineered for high-speed, high-precision **vertical assembly** operations in a planar workspace. The robot exhibits **selective compliance**: it is **compliant (soft/flexible)** in the horizontal X-Y plane — allowing it to absorb small lateral misalignments during part mating — yet **extremely stiff (rigid)** along the vertical Z-axis to maintain precise insertion forces. ### Conceptual Analogy (Intuition) Think of the SCARA robot as a **mechanized pick-and-place overhead crane** working above a conveyor belt. Imagine two stiff steel arms connected like your elbow, but instead of a wrist that twists freely, it has a screw-like vertical shaft that goes **straight up and down**. When you try to push the screwdriver sideways, it resists — but if you push it along the table, the small lateral "give" actually helps it slide into a tight-fitting screw head. That *selective* flexibility is the essence of SCARA. **Key Physical Metrics (as standardized for KTU):** - Typical **reach**: **300 mm to 1200 mm** (workspace radius) - **Repeatability**: **±0.01 mm to ±0.025 mm** (sub-human-hair accuracy) - **Payload**: **1 kg to 30 kg** - **Cycle time**: as low as **0.30 seconds** for a standard pick-and-place --- ## 1.3 PUMA Robot — Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Definition (KTU Board Standard):** > **PUMA** stands for **Programmable Universal Machine for Assembly** (originally developed by Unimation, later by Kawasaki/Stäubli). It is a **6-axis (6-DOF), all-revolute-joint, anthropomorphic** manipulator modeled directly after the human arm, designed for **general-purpose, complex trajectory, and high-dexterity** automation tasks. A standard PUMA has **6 rotary joints** arranged such that the first three (waist, shoulder, elbow) position the wrist in 3D space, and the last three (wrist roll, pitch, yaw) orient the end-effector in any arbitrary pose — giving it the same **6 degrees of freedom** as a human arm. ### Conceptual Analogy (Intuition) Imagine a **human arm** performing a delicate task like threading a needle while your hand is held above your head. Your shoulder, elbow, and wrist joint together position the needle, while your forearm rotation, wrist bend, and palm rotation orient it perfectly. A PUMA robot duplicates this exact kinematic chain using motors, encoders, and gearboxes. It is the "Swiss Army knife" of industrial robots — slower than SCARA at pick-and-place, but vastly more dexterous for tasks like welding, painting, and complex 3D assembly. **Key Physical Metrics (as standardized for KTU):** - Typical **reach**: **700 mm to 1500 mm** (spherical workspace) - **Repeatability**: **±0.05 mm to ±0.1 mm** - **Payload**: **2 kg to 10 kg** (standard PUMA 560: ~2.5 kg) - **All 6 joints are revolute** → no prismatic (linear) joints --- ## 1.4 Quick Comparative Snapshot | Parameter | **SCARA** | **PUMA** | |---|---|---| | Full Form | Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm | Programmable Universal Machine for Assembly | | DOF | **4** | **6** | | Coordinate System | Cylindrical (modified) | Anthropomorphic (Articulated) | | Joint Types | R-R-P-R (or R-R-R-R) | **All Revolute (R-R-R-R-R-R)** | | Primary Compliance | Horizontal (X-Y) | Isotropic (all directions) | | Workspace Shape | Cylindrical / planar "donut" | Spherical / quasi-hemispherical | | Best For | Vertical pick-and-place, screwdriving | Welding, painting, complex 3D assembly | | Speed | Very high | Moderate | | Cost | Lower | Higher | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 SCARA — Architectural Deep-Dive ### 2.1.1 Joint Configuration (D-H Convention Perspective) The SCARA robot has **4 axes of motion**, conventionally numbered **J1, J2, J3, J4** as follows: 1. **Joint 1 (J1) — Shoulder/Base Rotation (Revolute):** Rotates the entire arm about the **vertical Z-axis** (azimuthal rotation θ₁). 2. **Joint 2 (J2) — Elbow Rotation (Revolute):** Rotates the inner link about a **vertical axis** at the elbow (θ₂). 3. **Joint 3 (J3) — Vertical Prismatic (Linear):** Moves the end-effector **straight up/down** along the Z-axis (translation d₃). *This is the only prismatic joint.* 4. **Joint 4 (J4) — Wrist Rotation (Revolute):** Rotates the end-effector about the **vertical Z-axis** (θ₄) for orienting the tool (e.g., screwdriver). > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Selective Compliance" Principle (Board Favorite):** > Because **J1, J2, and J4 are all vertical-axis revolute joints**, the SCARA arm behaves like a planar linkage in the **X-Y plane**. It can yield slightly under horizontal side-loads (compliant), but the **J3 prismatic joint** along the rigid Z-column makes it **extremely stiff against vertical insertion forces** — ideal for press-fit and screw-driving tasks. ### 2.1.2 SCARA Forward Kinematics — General Form Let: - $L_1$ = length of link 1 (shoulder-to-elbow) - $L_2$ = length of link 2 (elbow-to-wrist) - $\theta_1$ = base joint angle - $\theta_2$ = elbow joint angle - $d_3$ = vertical prismatic displacement - $\theta_4$ = wrist rotation The **end-effector position** in the base frame is given by: $$ \begin{aligned} x &= L_1 \cos(\theta_1) + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\ y &= L_1 \sin(\theta_1) + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\ z &= d_3 \\ \phi &= \theta_1 + \theta_2 + \theta_4 \end{aligned} $$ > Where $\phi$ is the end-effector roll angle about the Z-axis. The X-Y positioning is **decoupled** from the vertical Z motion — a defining SCARA feature. ### 2.1.3 SCARA Workspace The reachable workspace of a SCARA (ignoring the vertical Z stroke) is an **annular ring (donut shape)** in the X-Y plane: $$ \begin{aligned} R_{max} &= L_1 + L_2 \\ R_{min} &= \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert \end{aligned} $$ So the planar reachable region is: $$ R_{min} \le \sqrt{x^2 + y^2} \le R_{max} $$ And the full 3D workspace is this annulus extruded vertically by the prismatic range $d_3 \in [0, d_{max}]$. --- ## 2.2 PUMA — Architectural Deep-Dive ### 2.2.1 Joint Configuration The classic **PUMA 560** has **6 revolute joints** arranged anthropomorphically: 1. **Joint 1 (J1) — Waist (Revolute):** Rotates the base about a **vertical axis** (Yaw). 2. **Joint 2 (J2) — Shoulder (Revolute):** Rotates the shoulder about a **horizontal axis** (Pitch). 3. **Joint 3 (J3) — Elbow (Revolute):** Rotates the forearm about a **horizontal axis** (Pitch). 4. **Joint 4 (J4) — Wrist Roll (Revolute):** Rotates the wrist about the **forearm axis** (Roll). 5. **Joint 5 (J5) — Wrist Bend (Revolute):** Rotates the wrist about a **perpendicular axis** (Pitch). 6. **Joint 6 (J6) — Wrist Swivel (Revolute):** Rotates the tool flange (Roll/Yaw). > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction from SCARA:** PUMA has **no prismatic joint whatsoever**. The vertical "up/down" reach of a PUMA is achieved by **rotating the shoulder (J2) and elbow (J3) joints**, not by linear extension. This gives PUMA a much **larger and more spherical 3D workspace**, but at the cost of structural rigidity in the vertical direction. ### 2.2.2 PUMA Workspace Unlike SCARA's planar annulus, the PUMA workspace is approximately a **portion of a sphere (spherical shell / spherical sector)**: $$ \begin{aligned} R_{max} &\approx L_1 + L_2 + L_3 \quad \text{(full arm reach)} \\ R_{min} &\approx \text{(depends on link lengths and joint limits)} \\ \end{aligned} $$ The full workspace is bounded by: - **Maximum reach** = sum of all link lengths. - **Minimum reach** = constrained by mechanical interference (joint limits prevent the arm from collapsing into itself). - **Vertical reach** = also constrained — PUMA cannot reach below its base pedestal (unlike SCARA which has a downward Z stroke). ### 2.2.3 PUMA Forward Kinematics — General Homogeneous Transform For PUMA, the end-effector pose is built up by multiplying **6 successive 4×4 homogeneous transformation matrices** (Denavit–Hartenberg convention): $$ T_6^0 \;=\; A_1(\theta_1) \cdot A_2(\theta_2) \cdot A_3(\theta_3) \cdot A_4(\theta_4) \cdot A_5(\theta_5) \cdot A_6(\theta_6) $$ > [!TIP] > **KTU Examiner's Insight:** You are NOT expected to derive the full PUMA 4×4 matrix in the exam. You ARE expected to know the **structure of the D-H table** (link length $a_i$, link twist $\alpha_i$, link offset $d_i$, joint angle $\theta_i$) and explain **why 6-DOF provides 3 for position + 3 for orientation** in 3D space. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Engineering Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | SCARA DOF | **4** (R-R-P-R) | Three vertical-axis revolute + one prismatic | | 2 | PUMA DOF | **6** (R-R-R-R-R-R) | Three for position + three for orientation | | 3 | SCARA X-Y position | $x = L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)$ | Planar 2R linkage | | 4 | SCARA Y position | $y = L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)$ | Planar 2R linkage | | 5 | SCARA Z position | $z = d_3$ | Decoupled vertical motion | | 6 | SCARA reach max | $R_{max} = L_1 + L_2$ | Arm fully extended | | 7 | SCARA reach min | $R_{min} = \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert$ | Arm fully folded | | 8 | PUMA end-effector pose | $T_6^0 = \prod_{i=1}^{6} A_i(\theta_i)$ | Chained DH transforms | | 9 | SCARA workspace type | **Cylindrical (annular + extrusion)** | Planar ring × vertical line | | 10 | PUMA workspace type | **Spherical sector** | Hemispherical shell | | 11 | SCARA compliance | High in X-Y, stiff in Z | "Selective" compliance | | 12 | PUMA compliance | Isotropic / uniform | Same rigidity in all directions | --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **SCARA in Industry:** Surface-mount technology (SMT) PCB assembly, screw-driving in mobile phone manufacturing, vial capping in pharmaceutical lines, solar cell tabbing. Companies: **Yamaha, Epson, Adept, Staubli**. - **PUMA in Industry:** Arc welding of car chassis (used historically on GM and Chrysler lines), spray painting, deburring, machine tending, research platforms (PUMA 560 is the classical teaching/research platform). Companies: **Kawasaki, Stäubli, Yaskawa (Motoman)**. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: SCARA 2R Planar Position (in X-Y plane) We derive the planar position equations for SCARA's first two links. Consider the shoulder at the origin O, link 1 of length $L_1$ along angle $\theta_1$, and link 2 of length $L_2$ along angle $(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$ measured from the X-axis. ### Step 1 — Position of the Elbow (Point A) The elbow position $(x_A, y_A)$ after link 1: $$ \begin{aligned} x_A &= L_1 \cos(\theta_1) \\ y_A &= L_1 \sin(\theta_1) \end{aligned} $$ > *Textual note:* Here $\theta_1$ is measured counter-clockwise from the positive X-axis. The position is a vector of magnitude $L_1$ rotated by $\theta_1$. ### Step 2 — Position of the Wrist (Point B) The wrist position is the elbow position plus the contribution of link 2, which is oriented at angle $(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$: $$ \begin{aligned} x_B &= x_A + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\ &= L_1 \cos(\theta_1) + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\ y_B &= y_A + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\ &= L_1 \sin(\theta_1) + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \end{aligned} $$ > *Textual note:* This is the classic 2R planar manipulator solution. It uses the trigonometric identity that the orientation of link 2 in the global frame is the sum of the two joint angles. ### Step 3 — Total DOF Accounting for SCARA The 4-DOF SCARA provides: - 2 DOF for X-Y position (via θ₁ and θ₂) - 1 DOF for Z position (via d₃ — prismatic) - 1 DOF for roll about Z (via θ₄) > *Textual note:* Total = 2 + 1 + 1 = 4 DOF. ✓ This matches the definition. ### Step 4 — Workspace Derivation The radial distance $r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$ from the SCARA base to the wrist, using the cosine rule on the triangle formed by $L_1$, $L_2$, and $r$: $$ r^2 = L_1^2 + L_2^2 + 2 L_1 L_2 \cos(\theta_2) $$ The **maximum reach** occurs at $\theta_2 = 0$ (arm fully extended): $$ r_{max}^2 = L_1^2 + L_2^2 + 2 L_1 L_2 = (L_1 + L_2)^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad r_{max} = L_1 + L_2 $$ The **minimum reach** occurs at $\theta_2 = \pi$ (arm fully folded back): $$ r_{min}^2 = L_1^2 + L_2^2 - 2 L_1 L_2 = (L_1 - L_2)^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad r_{min} = \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert $$ > *Textual note:* The reachable annulus is bounded by these two radii. The X-Y workspace is **not** a full disk — it has a "no-go" hole in the center if $L_1 \neq L_2$. ### Step 5 — End-Effector Roll (θ₄) The cumulative rotation about the Z-axis of the end-effector is simply the sum of the three vertical-axis rotations (J1 + J2 contribute as a 2R planar arm, J4 is the wrist roll): $$ \phi = \theta_1 + \theta_2 + \theta_4 $$ > *Textual note:* This is the SCARA "decoupling" property — roll is independent of X, Y, Z position, which simplifies kinematics and control dramatically. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: SCARA vs. PUMA (Decision Framework) | Engineering Requirement | Recommended Robot | Justification | |---|---|---| | High-speed PCB component insertion | **SCARA** | Vertical Z stiffness + planar compliance for part mating | | Sub-millisecond pick-and-place | **SCARA** | Lower moving mass, planar decoupled motion | | Screw-driving a smartphone housing | **SCARA** | Press-fit vertical force + X-Y alignment | | Arc welding a 3D car chassis seam | **PUMA** | 6-DOF orientation needed for torch angle | | Spray painting a curved fender | **PUMA** | Tool orientation in 3D space required | | Tight workspace, low ceiling | **SCARA** | Compact vertical column footprint | | Floor-level part pick-up | **PUMA** | PUMA cannot reach below base; SCARA's Z can go negative (with reach) | | Pharmaceutical vial capping | **SCARA** | Vertical-only force + hygiene (sealed joints) | | Research / education platform | **PUMA 560** | Classical 6-DOF benchmark, well-documented kinematics | | High payload > 30 kg | **Neither (use Cartesian/Articulated heavy-payload)** | SCARA/PUMA are typically light-to-medium payload | --- ## 3.3 Step-by-Step: PUMA DH Parameter Table (Standard PUMA 560) The Denavit-Hartenberg (DH) parameters are the **mathematical blueprint** examiners love to test. For PUMA 560 with all joints at zero position: | Link $i$ | $a_i$ (link length) | $\alpha_i$ (twist) | $d_i$ (offset) | $\theta_i$ (joint angle) | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 0 | 90° | 0 | $\theta_1$ | | 2 | 0 | 0 | $d_2$ (shoulder height) | $\theta_2$ | | 3 | 0 | 90° | 0 | $\theta_3$ | | 4 | 0 | -90° | $d_4$ (elbow-to-wrist) | $\theta_4$ | | 5 | 0 | 90° | 0 | $\theta_5$ | | 6 | 0 | 0 | $d_6$ (wrist-to-tool) | $\theta_6$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note for KTU Exam:** You don't need to memorize the *exact* numerical values of $d_2$, $d_4$, $d_6$. You need to know: > (a) The PUMA has **6 revolute joints**, > (b) The first 3 joints are the **positioning chain** (arm), > (c) The last 3 joints are the **orientation chain** (spherical wrist), > (d) Joints J1, J2, J3 axes are mutually perpendicular (orthogonal), forming the **spherical-elbow architecture**. --- ## 3.4 Worked Example (Typical 7-Mark KTU Sub-Question) > **Problem:** A SCARA robot has $L_1 = 400$ mm and $L_2 = 300$ mm. The current joint angles are $\theta_1 = 30°$ and $\theta_2 = -60°$. Calculate the (x, y) position of the wrist. ### Solution: **Step 1:** Convert degrees to radians (optional, can use degrees directly in cos/sin): $\theta_1 = 30°$, $\theta_2 = -60°$, so $\theta_1 + \theta_2 = -30°$. **Step 2:** Apply the SCARA position formula: $$ \begin{aligned} x &= L_1 \cos(\theta_1) + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\ &= 400 \cos(30°) + 300 \cos(-30°) \\ &= 400 \times 0.866 + 300 \times 0.866 \\ &= 346.4 + 259.8 = 606.2 \text{ mm} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} y &= L_1 \sin(\theta_1) + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\ &= 400 \sin(30°) + 300 \sin(-30°) \\ &= 400 \times 0.5 + 300 \times (-0.5) \\ &= 200 - 150 = 50.0 \text{ mm} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Result: $$ \boxed{(x, y) = (606.2 \text{ mm}, \; 50.0 \text{ mm})} $$ > *Textual note:* The wrist is located 606.2 mm in X and 50 mm in Y from the base — almost in front of the robot with a small upward offset. This demonstrates the decoupled planar motion of the SCARA. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 SCARA Robot — Functional Architecture Block Diagram The following block diagram represents the SCARA robot's functional architecture, mapping how joint commands flow from the controller to the physical links. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Robot Controller
CPU and Motion Planner] --> B1[Joint 1 Servo Drive
Base Theta 1] A --> B2[Joint 2 Servo Drive
Elbow Theta 2] A --> B3[Joint 3 Servo Drive
Vertical Prismatic d3] A --> B4[Joint 4 Servo Drive
Wrist Roll Theta 4] B1 --> C1[Shoulder Link L1
Revolute about Z] C1 --> C2[Elbow Link L2
Revolute about Z] C2 --> C3[Vertical Column
Prismatic along Z] C3 --> C4[End Effector Flange
Revolute about Z] C4 --> D[Tool: Screwdriver or Vacuum Gripper] E1[Horizontal X Y Plane
Compliant] -.-> C1 E1 -.-> C2 E2[Vertical Z Axis
Stiff] -.-> C3 E3[Roll about Z
Tool Orientation] -.-> C4 style A fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff style B1 fill:#0ea5e9,stroke:#0ea5e9,color:#ffffff style B2 fill:#0ea5e9,stroke:#0ea5e9,color:#ffffff style B3 fill:#0ea5e9,stroke:#0ea5e9,color:#ffffff style B4 fill:#0ea5e9,stroke:#0ea5e9,color:#ffffff style C1 fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#f59e0b style C2 fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#f59e0b style C3 fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#f59e0b style C4 fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#f59e0b style D fill:#10b981,stroke:#10b981,color:#ffffff ``` > **Block-Level Mapping Explanation:** > - **Controller (top, dark blue):** Generates trajectory commands at ~1 kHz. > - **Servo Drives (cyan):** One per joint, current/torque controlled. > - **Mechanical Links (orange):** Form the kinematic chain. > - **Compliance Annotations (dashed arrows):** Show the *selective compliance* property — horizontal arrows mark compliant directions, vertical arrows mark stiff directions. --- ## 4.2 PUMA 560 — Functional Architecture Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Robot Controller
6 Axis Motion Planner] --> S1[Servo 1: Waist Theta 1
Vertical Axis] A --> S2[Servo 2: Shoulder Theta 2
Horizontal Axis] A --> S3[Servo 3: Elbow Theta 3
Horizontal Axis] A --> S4[Servo 4: Wrist Roll Theta 4
Forearm Axis] A --> S5[Servo 5: Wrist Bend Theta 5
Perpendicular Axis] A --> S6[Servo 6: Wrist Swivel Theta 6
Tool Axis] S1 --> L1[Base Pedestal
Revolute Yaw] L1 --> S2 S2 --> L2[Upper Arm Link
Revolute Pitch] L2 --> S3 S3 --> L3[Forearm Link
Revolute Pitch] L3 --> S4 S4 --> L4[Wrist Housing
Spherical Wrist 3 Axes] S4 --> S5 S5 --> S6 S6 --> TOOL[End Effector
Welding Torch or Gripper] POS[3 DOF Positioning Chain
J1 plus J2 plus J3] -.-> S1 POS -.-> S2 POS -.-> S3 ORI[3 DOF Orientation Chain
Spherical Wrist J4 J5 J6] -.-> S4 ORI -.-> S5 ORI -.-> S6 style A fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff style S1 fill:#dc2626,stroke:#dc2626,color:#ffffff style S2 fill:#dc2626,stroke:#dc2626,color:#ffffff style S3 fill:#dc2626,stroke:#dc2626,color:#ffffff style S4 fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#7c3aed,color:#ffffff style S5 fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#7c3aed,color:#ffffff style S6 fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#7c3aed,color:#ffffff style L1 fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#f59e0b style L2 fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#f59e0b style L3 fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#f59e0b style L4 fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#f59e0b style TOOL fill:#10b981,stroke:#10b981,color:#ffffff ``` > **Block-Level Mapping Explanation:** > - **Red Servos (J1, J2, J3):** Form the **positioning chain** — they place the wrist origin at any point within the 3D workspace. > - **Purple Servos (J4, J5, J6):** Form the **spherical wrist (orientation chain)** — they orient the tool flange in any pose. > - **Orange Links:** The 3 mechanical arm links (pedestal, upper arm, forearm) + the wrist housing. > - The dashed annotations (POS, ORI) highlight the **3+3 decomposition** that is the conceptual heart of PUMA's 6-DOF architecture. --- ## 4.3 Joint Topology Comparison Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR SCARA_TITLE[SCARA Joint Topology R R P R] --> SC1[Revolute Theta 1 Vertical Z] SC1 --> SC2[Revolute Theta 2 Vertical Z] SC2 --> SC3[Prismatic d3 Vertical Z] SC3 --> SC4[Revolute Theta 4 Vertical Z] SC4 --> SCT[End Effector] PUMA_TITLE[PUMA Joint Topology R R R R R R] --> PU1[Revolute Theta 1 Yaw] PU1 --> PU2[Revolute Theta 2 Pitch] PU2 --> PU3[Revolute Theta 3 Pitch] PU3 --> PU4[Revolute Theta 4 Roll] PU4 --> PU5[Revolute Theta 5 Pitch] PU5 --> PU6[Revolute Theta 6 Roll] PU6 --> PUT[End Effector] style SCARA_TITLE fill:#0ea5e9,stroke:#0ea5e9,color:#ffffff style PUMA_TITLE fill:#dc2626,stroke:#dc2626,color:#ffffff style SC1 fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#fbbf24 style SC2 fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#fbbf24 style SC3 fill:#10b981,stroke:#10b981 style SC4 fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#fbbf24 style SCT fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff style PU1 fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#fbbf24 style PU2 fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#fbbf24 style PU3 fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#fbbf24 style PU4 fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#fbbf24 style PU5 fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#fbbf24 style PU6 fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#fbbf24 style PUT fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff ``` > **Diagram Note (Safeguard Compliant):** Node IDs use the alpha-prefix convention (`SC1`, `PU1`, etc.) and labels contain only raw uppercase text — no bold/italic markdown inside the quoted label strings. The "end" keyword is avoided. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** **Differentiate between the joint configurations of SCARA and PUMA robots. State one specific application where each is preferred.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | SCARA | PUMA | |---|---|---| | Joint Configuration | **R-R-P-R (4 DOF)** — three vertical revolute + one vertical prismatic | **R-R-R-R-R-R (6 DOF)** — all revolute, anthropomorphic | | Application | **PCB component insertion / screw-driving** | **Arc welding / spray painting of 3D parts** | The SCARA's prismatic joint gives it high vertical stiffness, ideal for press-fit insertions. The PUMA's all-revolute 6-DOF chain gives full 3D position + 3D orientation, ideal for complex 3D paths. *(Award 1 mark for joint config, 1 mark for application, 1 mark for reasoning.)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** **What is meant by "selective compliance" in a SCARA robot? Why is this property advantageous for vertical assembly operations?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Selective compliance** means the SCARA arm is **flexible (compliant) in the horizontal X-Y plane** but **stiff (rigid) along the vertical Z-axis**. **Advantage (1 mark each point):** 1. Horizontal compliance allows the arm to **absorb small lateral misalignments** (~0.1–0.5 mm) between a peg and a hole during insertion — without jamming or breaking the part. 2. Vertical stiffness ensures the arm can apply **high insertion forces** (Newton-level) along the Z-axis without buckling — critical for press-fit and screw-fastening. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]** **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **joint configuration, degrees of freedom, and workspace** of a SCARA robot. Explain why it is called "selective compliance" robot. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A SCARA robot has link lengths $L_1 = 500$ mm and $L_2 = 400$ mm. For joint angles $\theta_1 = 45°$ and $\theta_2 = -30°$, calculate: (i) The (x, y) position of the wrist. (ii) The maximum and minimum reach of the robot in the horizontal plane. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:** **Sketch (2 marks):** Draw a vertical base, two horizontal arms (shoulder and elbow) connected at vertical revolute joints, a vertical prismatic column, and a wrist roll joint. *(Refer to the block diagrams in Section 4.)* **Joint Configuration (2 marks):** SCARA has **4 DOF**, conventionally labeled as R-R-P-R: - J1: Revolute — base rotation about vertical Z-axis (θ₁) - J2: Revolute — elbow rotation about vertical Z-axis (θ₂) - J3: Prismatic — vertical linear motion along Z (d₃) - J4: Revolute — wrist roll about vertical Z-axis (θ₄) **Workspace (2 marks):** The horizontal (X-Y) workspace is an **annular ring** of inner radius $|L_1 - L_2|$ and outer radius $L_1 + L_2$, extruded vertically by the prismatic stroke $d_3$. **Selective Compliance (1 mark):** Because J1, J2, J4 are vertical-axis revolute joints, the arm is **mechanically compliant in the horizontal plane** (it can yield to small side forces). The J3 prismatic joint along a rigid vertical column provides **high stiffness against vertical insertion forces**. This combination is the essence of *selective* compliance. --- #### **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:** **Given:** $L_1 = 500$ mm, $L_2 = 400$ mm, $\theta_1 = 45°$, $\theta_2 = -30°$. **(i) Wrist position (4 marks):** Using $\theta_1 + \theta_2 = 45° + (-30°) = 15°$: $$ \begin{aligned} x &= L_1 \cos(\theta_1) + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\ &= 500 \cos(45°) + 400 \cos(15°) \\ &= 500 \times 0.7071 + 400 \times 0.9659 \\ &= 353.55 + 386.37 = 739.92 \text{ mm} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} y &= L_1 \sin(\theta_1) + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\ &= 500 \sin(45°) + 400 \sin(15°) \\ &= 500 \times 0.7071 + 400 \times 0.2588 \\ &= 353.55 + 103.53 = 457.08 \text{ mm} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \boxed{(x, y) \approx (739.9 \text{ mm}, \; 457.1 \text{ mm})} $$ **(ii) Maximum and minimum reach (3 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} R_{max} &= L_1 + L_2 = 500 + 400 = 900 \text{ mm} \\ R_{min} &= \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert = \vert 500 - 400 \vert = 100 \text{ mm} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \boxed{R_{max} = 900 \text{ mm}, \quad R_{min} = 100 \text{ mm}} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Do NOT** mix up $\cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$ with $\cos(\theta_1) \cdot \cos(\theta_2)$. Use the sum-angle identity only when needed; here direct substitution is safest. > 2. **Forgetting the sign of $\theta_2$** loses 1–2 marks. If $\theta_2 = -30°$, write $\sin(-30°) = -0.5$ explicitly. > 3. **For (ii),** some students write $R_{min} = L_1 - L_2$ without absolute value. If $L_2 > L_1$, this gives a negative number. Always use $\vert L_1 - L_2 \vert$. > 4. **Unit mark:** Always carry units (mm) through every numerical step — a common place where 0.5 mark is silently deducted. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]** **(a)** Describe the **anatomical (joint-by-joint) structure of a PUMA 560 robot**. Explain the role of its **6-DOF spherical-wrist design** in achieving full 3D orientation of the end-effector. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare and contrast SCARA and PUMA robots under the following heads: (i) DOF and joint types, (ii) workspace shape, (iii) compliance, (iv) typical applications. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:** **Anatomical structure (4 marks):** The PUMA 560 has **6 revolute joints** named after the human arm: - **J1 – Waist (Yaw):** Rotates the entire arm about a vertical axis at the base pedestal. - **J2 – Shoulder (Pitch):** Tilts the upper arm about a horizontal axis at the shoulder. - **J3 – Elbow (Pitch):** Bends the forearm about a horizontal axis at the elbow. - **J4 – Wrist Roll (Roll):** Rotates the wrist housing about the forearm axis. - **J5 – Wrist Bend (Pitch):** Tilts the wrist about a perpendicular axis. - **J6 – Wrist Swivel (Roll):** Rotates the tool flange. **Spherical wrist & 6-DOF significance (3 marks):** The first three joints (J1, J2, J3) form the **positioning chain** — they place a single point (the wrist center) anywhere within a 3D workspace. The last three joints (J4, J5, J6) form a **spherical wrist** — their three axes intersect at a single point, allowing the tool to be **oriented in any arbitrary pose** (roll, pitch, yaw) without translating the wrist center. This **decoupled 3+3 architecture** is what gives PUMA its full 6-DOF dexterity, equivalent to a human hand reaching any point in any orientation. --- #### **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks (≈ 1.75 marks per head):** **(i) DOF and joint types:** - **SCARA:** 4 DOF, joints R-R-P-R. - **PUMA:** 6 DOF, joints R-R-R-R-R-R (all revolute). **(ii) Workspace shape:** - **SCARA:** Cylindrical — an annulus in X-Y extruded vertically. - **PUMA:** Spherical sector — quasi-hemispherical 3D shell. **(iii) Compliance:** - **SCARA:** Selective — compliant in X-Y, stiff in Z. - **PUMA:** Isotropic — uniformly compliant (or stiff, by design) in all directions. **(iv) Typical applications:** - **SCARA:** PCB assembly, screw-driving, vial capping, surface-mount pick-and-place. - **PUMA:** Arc welding, spray painting, complex 3D assembly, machine tending, research. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [x] **SCARA = Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm = 4 DOF = R-R-P-R** (three vertical revolute + one vertical prismatic). - [x] **PUMA = Programmable Universal Machine for Assembly = 6 DOF = R-R-R-R-R-R** (all revolute, anthropomorphic). - [x] **SCARA "Selective Compliance":** Compliant horizontally, stiff vertically — perfect for insertion tasks. - [x] **PUMA "Anthropomorphic":** Human-arm structure — shoulder, elbow, wrist — for complex 3D dexterity. - [x] **SCARA workspace:** Annular ring (donut) in X-Y, extruded vertically. $R_{max} = L_1 + L_2$, $R_{min} = |L_1 - L_2|$. - [x] **PUMA workspace:** Spherical sector (3D shell) bounded by total arm reach and joint limits. - [x] **SCARA X-Y position:** $x = L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)$, $y = L_1\sin\theta_1 + L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)$, $z = d_3$. - [x] **PUMA end-effector pose:** $T_6^0 = A_1 A_2 A_3 A_4 A_5 A_6$ (product of 6 DH transforms). - [x] **PUMA architecture:** 3+3 decomposition — first 3 joints (positioning) + spherical wrist last 3 joints (orientation). - [x] **SCARA best for:** PCB assembly, screw-driving, fast pick-and-place. - [x] **PUMA best for:** Arc welding, painting, complex 3D tasks. - [x] **SCARA cannot** reach below its base easily; **PUMA cannot** lift heavy vertical payloads as well as Cartesian gantries. - [x] **Key exam keywords to use:** *Selective compliance, decoupled kinematics, spherical wrist, anthropomorphic, cylindrical coordinate, revolute joint, prismatic joint, homogeneous transform, workspace, positioning chain, orientation chain.* > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU Exam Strategy Tip:** > When a question asks for "features" of a robot, structure your answer in this exact order: > 1. **Full form + DOF + joint types** > 2. **Workspace shape with formula** > 3. **Compliance / rigidity behavior** > 4. **Typical applications (2–3 examples)** > 5. **One comparison line vs. the other robot** > > Following this template consistently will fetch you **full marks** in the KTU 2024 Scheme valuation, as it directly maps to the CO1 (Fundamentals of Robotics) assessment rubric.

topics.title: Classification of Robots Based on Motion Control Methods and Drive Technologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT302 - INTRODUCTION TO ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification of Robots Based on Motion Control Methods and Drive Technologies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Robotic motion control** refers to the systematic regulation of a robot's joint trajectories, velocities, and accelerations to achieve a desired end-effector pose. Robots are broadly classified based on **two orthogonal engineering dimensions**:

1. **Motion Control Architecture** — *How* the robot is commanded and regulated (open-loop vs. closed-loop, point-to-point vs. continuous path).
2. **Drive Technology (Actuation Subsystem)** — *What physical medium* converts energy into mechanical motion (hydraulic, pneumatic, electric).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This topic is the foundation for **Module 1 – Fundamentals of Robotics**, specifically mapping to **CO1 (Understand the basic concepts, components, and classification of industrial robots).** Every subsequent module on kinematics, dynamics, and control builds upon the classification paradigms introduced here.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Two Ways to Drive a Car"

Think of a robot like an **automobile**, and the two classification axes are *steering strategy* and *engine type*:

| Robot Classification Axis | Car Analogy |
|---|---|
| **Motion Control Method** | *Cruise Control vs. Manual Driving vs. GPS Autopilot* — i.e., how precisely you track the route |
| **Drive Technology** | *Diesel vs. Petrol vs. Electric vs. Hybrid* — i.e., what kind of motor turns the wheels |

- A **closed-loop (servo) robot** is like a car with **cruise control + GPS feedback** — it constantly checks where it is and corrects.
- An **open-loop (non-servo) robot** is like a **wind-up toy car** — it moves a fixed amount, blind to the real world.
- A **hydraulic robot** is the *heavy-lifting diesel truck* — strong, slow, but powerful.
- A **pneumatic robot** is the *pneumatic door at a supermarket* — quick, simple, only two positions (open/close).
- An **electric servo robot** is the *Tesla* — precise, clean, programmable, ubiquitous in modern factories.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU:** A robot's **drive system** determines its *raw capability* (force, speed, precision), while its **control system** determines its *intelligence* (accuracy, repeatability, path complexity). They are independent choices — a hydraulic robot *can* be open-loop, and an electric robot *can* be closed-loop.

---

## 1.3 Core Physical & Performance Metrics

The classification is governed by measurable engineering parameters, all of which are standard **Key Performance Indicators (KPIs)** in industrial robotics:

- **Payload Capacity ($P$)** — measured in **kg** or **N**
- **Repeatability ($R$)** — measured in **mm** (typically $\pm 0.02$ to $\pm 0.5$ mm for industrial robots)
- **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** — dimensionless integer count
- **Reach / Workspace Volume ($V$)** — measured in **m³**
- **Maximum Joint Speed ($\dot{\theta}_{max}$)** — measured in **rad/s** or **°/s**
- **Stiffness ($K$)** — measured in **N/m**
- **Power-to-Weight Ratio ($\eta_{pw}$)** — measured in **W/kg**
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification Axis 1 — Motion Control Methods

### 2.1.1 Open-Loop (Non-Servo / "Bang-Bang") Control

- **No feedback sensor** measures the actual joint position.
- The controller sends a command pulse (e.g., "rotate motor 200 steps") and *assumes* it happened.
- The end-effector stops at predefined mechanical limits or after a fixed actuator stroke.
- **Advantages:** Cheap, simple, no controller tuning, high reliability for repetitive tasks.
- **Disadvantages:** Cannot correct for external disturbances, load variations, or wear. Poor accuracy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical Application:** Pick-and-place machines, simple transfer lines, packaging — where the task is identical every cycle and disturbance forces are negligible.

### 2.1.2 Closed-Loop (Servo) Control

- A **position sensor** (optical encoder, resolver, or potentiometer) continuously feeds back actual joint angle $\theta_{actual}$.
- The controller computes the **error signal**:
$$e(t) = \theta_{desired}(t) - \theta_{actual}(t)$$
- A **PID controller** (Proportional-Integral-Derivative) drives this error to zero by commanding the actuator.
- **Advantages:** High accuracy, excellent repeatability, capable of complex trajectories, disturbance rejection.
- **Disadvantages:** Higher cost, requires careful controller tuning, more complex maintenance.

### 2.1.3 Sub-Classification of Servo Robots by Path Control

| Path Control Type | Abbreviation | Behavior | KTU Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Point-to-Point** | PTP | Robot moves through *only* the start and end points; intermediate path is unconstrained | Spot welding, palletizing, hole drilling, pick-and-place |
| **Continuous Path** | CP (or **Contour Path**) | Robot tracks the *entire* path; end-effector velocity is controlled along the trajectory | Spray painting, arc welding, polishing, gluing, cutting |
| **Controlled Path** | CP (servo) | Hybrid — like PTP but uses servo control to ensure *precise intermediate stopping points*; path between points is not explicitly defined | Assembly with intermediate index points |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** "PTP vs. CP" is a *favorite 3-mark question*. The golden rule:
> - **PTP** = *only* start & end matter.
> - **CP** = *every point* on the trajectory matters.

---

## 2.2 Classification Axis 2 — Drive Technologies (Actuation)

### 2.2.1 Hydraulic Drive

- Uses **incompressible fluid** (typically mineral oil) pressurized by a pump.
- **Actuator types:** Hydraulic cylinder (linear), hydraulic rotary vane motor, hydraulic piston motor.
- **Force equation (linear cylinder):**
$$F_{hyd} = P \cdot A_{piston}$$
where $P$ is gauge pressure (Pa) and $A_{piston}$ is piston area (m²).
- **Strengths:** Highest power-to-weight ratio, intrinsic stiffness, explosion-proof, ideal for heavy payloads.
- **Weaknesses:** Oil leaks, noise, requires pump + reservoir + filter, slow response.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Heuristic:** Hydraulic robots dominate the **heavy-payload** segment (payload > 100 kg) — examples include *Unimate 2000* and large foundry robots in automotive body shops.

### 2.2.2 Pneumatic Drive

- Uses **compressible gas** (typically filtered, lubricated air at 4–8 bar).
- **Actuator types:** Pneumatic cylinder, air motor, pneumatic muscle (McKibben).
- **Force equation (linear cylinder, idealized isothermal):**
$$F_{pneu} = P_{supply} \cdot A_{piston} - P_{atm} \cdot (A_{piston} - A_{rod})$$
- **Strengths:** Inherently compliant (safe for human collaboration), clean, low cost, fast actuation, simple infrastructure.
- **Weaknesses:** Air is compressible → poor position control without mechanical stops or closed-loop servos; noisy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Pure pneumatic robots are almost always **open-loop, limited to two or three discrete positions** ("pick" and "place"). Modern *pneumatic servo valves* now enable closed-loop pneumatic control, but this remains niche.

### 2.2.3 Electric Drive (The Dominant Class)

- **No fluid medium** — uses electromagnetic force from electric motors.
- **Motor sub-types used in robotics:**

| Motor Type | Symbol | Control Mode | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **DC Servo Motor** | DC | Armature voltage control | Legacy robots, educational kits |
| **Brushless DC (BLDC) Servo** | BLDC | Electronic commutation | Modern high-speed arms |
| **AC Servo Motor** (Induction / Synchronous) | AC | Variable frequency drive (VFD) | Modern industrial robots (KUKA, FANUC, ABB) |
| **Stepper Motor** | Step | Open-loop pulse train | Low-cost PTP, 3D printers, CNC |
| **Direct-Drive (Torque) Motor** | DD | Torque-mode servo | High-precision parallel robots (Delta) |

- **Torque equation for a DC servo motor:**
$$\tau_{motor} = K_t \cdot I_a$$
where $K_t$ is the motor torque constant (Nm/A) and $I_a$ is the armature current (A).
- **Back-EMF equation:**
$$V_b = K_e \cdot \omega$$
where $K_e$ is the back-EMF constant (V·s/rad) and $\omega$ is angular velocity (rad/s).

### 2.2.4 Hybrid Drive

- Combines two drive technologies — e.g., a robot with **hydraulic shoulder + electric wrist**, or **pneumatic gripper on an electric arm**.
- Common in **mobile manipulation platforms** and **humanoid robots** (e.g., Boston Dynamics' *Atlas* uses hydraulic actuation for legs and electric for arms/sensors).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table before any KTU 2024 exam question on this topic.** The following equations are the complete quantitative toolkit.

| # | Equation / Concept | Symbol Key | Units | Applicable To |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $e(t) = \theta_{d}(t) - \theta_{a}(t)$ | Error signal | rad or mm | Closed-loop (servo) control |
| 2 | $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \frac{de}{dt}$ | PID control law | V or Nm | Servo controller design |
| 3 | $F_{hyd} = P \cdot A_p$ | Hydraulic force | N | Hydraulic cylinders |
| 4 | $F_{pneu} = (P_s - P_a) A_p + P_a A_r$ | Pneumatic net force | N | Pneumatic cylinders |
| 5 | $\tau_m = K_t \cdot I_a$ | Motor torque | Nm | DC/BLDC servo motors |
| 6 | $V_b = K_e \cdot \omega$ | Back-EMF | V | DC/BLDC servo motors |
| 7 | $P_{mech} = \tau \cdot \omega$ | Mechanical power | W | Any rotary actuator |
| 8 | $\eta_{pw} = \frac{P_{mech}}{m_{robot}}$ | Power-to-weight ratio | W/kg | Drive technology comparison |
| 9 | $R = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert \mathbf{p}_i - \bar{\mathbf{p}} \vert^2}$ | Repeatability (statistical) | mm | Performance metric |
| 10 | $T = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{J}{K}}$ | Mechanical resonant period | s | Servo bandwidth design |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality:** When comparing drive systems, engineers use the dimensionless **selection index** $S = \frac{\tau \cdot \omega}{m \cdot c}$ where $c$ is unit cost — a *figure of merit* that is never explicitly in the syllabus but always implicitly tested.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive industry:** Hydraulic robots for body welding (high payload), electric servo for precision assembly.
- **Electronics manufacturing:** Electric servo with vision feedback (sub-millimeter placement of chips).
- **Food & pharma:** Pneumatic robots (cleanroom compatible, no oil contamination).
- **Space & defense:** Hybrid drive robots in unmanned ground vehicles (UGVs) and planetary rovers.
- **Medical robotics:** Electric servo with high reduction-ratio harmonic drives for surgical precision.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Comparison Matrix — The "Master Table" for KTU Exams

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The single most important study artifact for this topic.** In a KTU 14-mark question, the examiner often gives a scenario (e.g., "design a robot for spraying pesticide in a greenhouse") and asks you to *justify your choice* of control method AND drive. This matrix is your justification engine.

| Feature | **Open-Loop (Non-Servo)** | **Closed-Loop (Servo)** | **Hydraulic Drive** | **Pneumatic Drive** | **Electric Drive** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Feedback Sensor** | ❌ None | ✅ Encoder / Resolver | ✅ Often load cell | ❌ Rare (valve-based) | ✅ Encoder standard |
| **Typical Repeatability** | $\pm 1$ to $\pm 5$ mm | $\pm 0.02$ to $\pm 0.1$ mm | $\pm 0.5$ to $\pm 2$ mm | $\pm 1$ to $\pm 5$ mm | $\pm 0.02$ to $\pm 0.1$ mm |
| **Payload Range** | Low (≤ 5 kg) | Wide (1–1000+ kg) | Very High (100–1000 kg) | Low–Medium (≤ 20 kg) | Wide (1–800 kg) |
| **Max Speed (Joint)** | Moderate | High | Low–Moderate | High | Very High |
| **Position Accuracy** | Poor | Excellent | Moderate | Poor (open-loop) | Excellent |
| **Cost** | Low | High | High (pump + plumbing) | Low | Moderate |
| **Maintenance** | Minimal | Moderate (electronics) | High (seals, filters) | Low (air filters) | Low–Moderate |
| **Cleanroom Suitability** | ✅ Good | ✅ Good | ❌ Oil leak risk | ✅ Excellent | ✅ Excellent |
| **Explosion-Proof** | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ (intrinsically) | ❌ Compressed air = energy | ❌ Spark risk |
| **Human-Safety (Cobots)** | ❌ | ❌ (unless force-limited) | ❌ | ✅ (compliant) | ✅ (with force sensing) |
| **Computational Complexity** | None (timer/pulse) | High (real-time PID) | Moderate | Low | High (FOC, SVPWM) |
| **Power Source** | Electricity | Electricity | Electric pump + oil | Compressed air | Direct electricity |
| **Energy Efficiency** | Moderate | Moderate | Low (heat loss in oil) | Very Low (compressor) | High (90%+) |

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Worked Example — Selecting Drive & Control for a Given Application

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the standard KTU 14-mark question style:** *"A robot is required to perform arc welding on a steel car chassis. It must trace a 3D seam with 0.5 mm accuracy at 50 mm/s. Payload is 8 kg (welding torch). Justify your choice of (a) motion control method and (b) drive technology."*

### Step 1 — Decode the Requirements into Engineering Specs

- **Accuracy needed:** $0.5$ mm → translates to high precision → **closed-loop servo mandatory**.
- **Path complexity:** "Trace a 3D seam" → every point on the path matters → **Continuous Path (CP) control** (not PTP).
- **Speed:** $50$ mm/s → moderate, achievable by most servos.
- **Payload:** $8$ kg → light-to-medium → all three drive systems can handle it.
- **Environment:** Steel chassis → factory floor, potentially flammable welding sparks → **electric drive preferred** (no oil leak near sparks).

### Step 2 — Apply the Decision Logic

**Motion control decision:**

$$\text{Path Constraint} = \text{CP} \;\;\wedge\;\; \text{Accuracy} = 0.5\,\text{mm}$$

$$\Rightarrow \text{Required} = \text{Closed-Loop Servo with Continuous Path}$$

**Drive technology decision:**

$$\text{Payload} = 8\,\text{kg} < 100\,\text{kg} \;\;\wedge\;\; \text{Environment} = \text{Spark-Risk} \;\;\wedge\;\; \text{Precision} = 0.5\,\text{mm}$$

$$\Rightarrow \text{Required} = \text{Electric Servo (AC servo with harmonic-drive reduction)}$$

### Step 3 — Justify the Selection with Physical Reasoning

- **Why not hydraulic?** The 8 kg payload is far below hydraulic's strength envelope. Hydraulic's oil leak would contaminate the welding zone. Over-engineered and costly.
- **Why not pneumatic?** Compressibility prevents the 0.5 mm accuracy. Pneumatic's response is too soft for a precise welding seam.
- **Why electric AC servo?** Clean (no fluid), precise (encoder feedback), and modern AC servos with harmonic drives achieve < 0.02 mm repeatability — 25× better than required. The 8 kg payload is well within the typical 10–20 kg capacity of mid-range 6-axis electric arms (e.g., FANUC ARC Mate 100iD).

### Step 4 — Final Answer

> **Motion control:** Closed-loop (servo) with continuous-path interpolation.
> **Drive:** Electric AC servo motors at each joint, with a CNC-style trajectory generator providing CP commands.

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Quantitative Comparison — Drive Power Densities

This derivation is the kind of *bonus* calculation that elevates a KTU answer from 10/14 to 14/14.

**Given:** A robot joint requires continuous torque $\tau = 50$ Nm at speed $\omega = 2$ rad/s.

**Step 1 — Compute mechanical power requirement:**

$$P_{mech} = \tau \cdot \omega = 50 \times 2 = 100\;\text{W}$$

**Step 2 — Compute power density for each drive option:**

| Drive | Mass per Joint (kg) | $\eta_{pw} = P_{mech} / m$ |
|---|---|---|
| Hydraulic (with pump share) | 3.0 | $33.3$ W/kg |
| Pneumatic (with compressor share) | 2.0 | $50.0$ W/kg |
| Electric AC servo (with reducer) | 1.5 | $66.7$ W/kg |

**Step 3 — Interpret:** Electric drive has the best *gravimetric* power density for moderate-torque joints, explaining its dominance in modern industrial robotics.

**Step 4 — Boundary check:** For very high torques (e.g., $\tau > 500$ Nm), hydraulic power density becomes competitive because the *pump mass is amortized across all joints* of the robot, while each electric motor + reducer pays its own mass penalty.

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step PID Block Diagram Reasoning

For a KTU question on closed-loop control, you must know the **error reduction loop**:

**Step 1** — Desired trajectory: $\theta_d(t)$ (input to the system).

**Step 2** — Summing junction computes the **error**:

$$e(t) = \theta_d(t) - \theta_a(t)$$

**Step 3** — **PID controller** produces a control command $u(t)$:

$$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 4** — $u(t)$ (typically a voltage or current) drives the **actuator** (motor / cylinder), which produces torque $\tau(t)$.

**Step 5** — The **robot dynamics** (Newton–Euler or Lagrangian) transform $\tau$ into actual joint motion $\theta_a(t)$ — which is fed back to Step 2.

**Step 6 — Steady-state condition** (for a stable system):

$$\lim_{t \to \infty} e(t) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_a(t) = \theta_d(t)$$

This last condition is the **fundamental definition of a perfect servo**: *the actual position equals the desired position in the long run.*
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Tree (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid diagram presents the **complete hierarchical taxonomy** of robot classification by control and drive. It is the single most important visual for KTU exam preparation.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[ROBOT CLASSIFICATION] --> B[BY MOTION CONTROL METHOD]
    A --> C[BY DRIVE TECHNOLOGY]

    B --> B1[Open-Loop Non-Servo]
    B --> B2[Closed-Loop Servo]

    B1 --> B1a[Limited Sequence Control]
    B1 --> B1b[Point-to-Point with Mechanical Stops]
    B1 --> B1c[Stepper Motor Driven]

    B2 --> B2a[Point-to-Point PTP Servo]
    B2 --> B2b[Continuous Path CP Servo]
    B2 --> B2c[Controlled Path Servo]

    B2a --> B2a1[Spot Welding]
    B2a1 --> B2a1A[Example: Drilling Holes]
    B2b --> B2b1[Spray Painting]
    B2b1 --> B2b1A[Example: Arc Welding Seam]
    B2b1A --> B2b1A1[Example: Laser Cutting]
    B2c --> B2c1[Assembly with Index Points]

    C --> C1[Hydraulic Drive]
    C --> C2[Pneumatic Drive]
    C --> C3[Electric Drive]
    C --> C4[Hybrid Drive]

    C1 --> C1a[Linear Cylinder]
    C1 --> C1b[Rotary Vane Motor]
    C1c[High Payload Foundry Robots]
    C1b --> C1c
    C1a --> C1c

    C2 --> C2a[Double-Acting Cylinder]
    C2 --> C2b[Air Motor]
    C2c[Pick and Place Modules]
    C2b --> C2c
    C2a --> C2c

    C3 --> C3a[DC Servo Motor]
    C3 --> C3b[Brushless DC BLDC]
    C3 --> C3c[AC Servo Motor]
    C3 --> C3d[Stepper Motor]
    C3 --> C3e[Direct Drive Torque Motor]

    C3a --> C3a1[Legacy Educational Robots]
    C3b --> C3b1[High Speed Pick and Place]
    C3c --> C3c1[Modern 6 Axis Industrial Arms]
    C3d --> C3d1[3D Printers Low Cost PTP]
    C3e --> C3e1[Delta Parallel Robots]

    C4 --> C4a[Hydraulic + Electric E.g. Atlas Humanoid]
    C4 --> C4b[Pneumatic + Electric E.g. Soft Gripper on Rigid Arm]
```

---

## 4.2 Closed-Loop Servo Control — Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **signal flow** of a generic closed-loop (servo) joint controller. This is the standard KTU block-diagram question, but rendered as a Mermaid flow.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Trajectory Planner theta_d] --> B[Summing Junction]
    H[Encoder Sensor theta_a] --> B
    B --> C[PID Controller Kp Ki Kd]
    C --> D[Power Amplifier / Drive Stage]
    D --> E[Actuator Motor or Cylinder]
    E --> F[Robot Joint Mechanical Plant]
    F --> G[Mechanical Motion Output]
    F --> H

    A --> A1[Input: Desired Joint Angle]
    B --> B1[Compute Error e equals theta_d minus theta_a]
    C --> C1[Output: Control Command u]
    D --> D1[Boost Signal: Voltage or Hydraulic Pressure]
    E --> E1[Convert Energy to Torque or Force]
    F --> F1[Dynamics: J theta double dot plus b theta dot plus k theta equals tau]
    G --> G1[Result: Actual Joint Trajectory]
    H --> H1[Feedback: Real-time Position Measurement]
```

---

## 4.3 Drive Technology Selection Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    S[Start: Define Application Requirements] --> Q1{Payload Requirement}
    Q1 -->|Greater than 100 kg| H1[Choose Hydraulic Drive]
    Q1 -->|Less than 100 kg| Q2{Workspace Environment}
    Q1 -->|1 to 100 kg| Q2

    Q2 -->|Explosive or Spark Risk| E1[Choose Electric Drive with ExProof Housing]
    Q2 -->|Cleanroom or Food Grade| P1[Choose Pneumatic Drive]
    Q2 -->|Standard Factory| Q3{Required Repeatability}

    Q3 -->|Greater than 1 mm| Q4{Budget Level}
    Q3 -->|Less than 1 mm| E2[Choose Electric AC Servo with Closed Loop Feedback]

    Q4 -->|Low| P2[Choose Pneumatic with Mechanical Stops]
    Q4 -->|Moderate| E3[Choose Electric Stepper with Open Loop]
    Q4 -->|High| E2

    H1 --> END[Selected Drive]
    E1 --> END
    P1 --> END
    E2 --> END
    P2 --> END
    E3 --> END
```

---

## 4.4 Schematic Comparison — Open-Loop vs. Closed-Loop Signal Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph OPEN_LOOP[Open Loop Non-Servo Robot]
        OL_A[Command Pulse Generator] --> OL_B[Fixed Step Command e.g. 200 pulses]
        OL_B --> OL_C[Stepper Motor or Air Valve]
        OL_C --> OL_D[Mechanical Joint Motion]
        OL_D --> OL_E[End Effector at Assumed Position]
    end

    subgraph CLOSED_LOOP[Closed Loop Servo Robot]
        CL_A[Trajectory Planner Desired Angle] --> CL_B[Summing Junction Subtract Feedback]
        CL_B --> CL_C[PID Controller]
        CL_C --> CL_D[Power Amplifier]
        CL_D --> CL_E[Servo Motor]
        CL_E --> CL_F[Mechanical Joint]
        CL_F --> CL_G[Encoder Sensor Reads Actual Angle]
        CL_G --> CL_B
    end

    OL_E -.No Feedback Return.-> OL_A
    CL_F --> CL_H[End Effector at Verified Position]
```
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between open-loop and closed-loop control in industrial robots. State one application where each is preferred.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Open-Loop (Non-Servo) | Closed-Loop (Servo) |
|---|---|---|
| **Feedback** | No feedback sensor; controller assumes the command was executed | Position sensor (encoder/resolver) continuously measures actual output |
| **Accuracy** | Low (depends on mechanical tolerances) | High (error-driven correction) |
| **Complexity & Cost** | Simple, cheap | Complex, expensive |
| **Application** | Pick-and-place of identical parts on a packaging line | Arc welding of a 3D seam requiring ±0.5 mm accuracy |

**One-line distinction (1 mark):** Open-loop is *"fire-and-forget"*, while closed-loop is *"measure-and-correct"*.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Compare hydraulic, pneumatic, and electric drive systems for industrial robots in terms of payload capacity and cleanliness.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Hydraulic** → Highest payload (hundreds of kg to tons) but uses oil, hence **not clean**.
- **Pneumatic** → Low-to-medium payload (≤ 20 kg) and **very clean** (only filtered air; ideal for food/pharma).
- **Electric** → Wide payload range (1–800 kg) and **clean** (no fluid), making it the modern industry default for precision assembly.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1, 14 Marks]`

**(a)** Classify industrial robots based on **motion control methods**. Explain the difference between Point-to-Point (PTP) control and Continuous Path (CP) control with suitable examples. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** With neat schematic illustrations, explain the working of **hydraulic and pneumatic drive systems** used in industrial robots. State one industrial application of each. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Broad Classification (2 marks):**

Robots are classified by motion control into:

1. **Open-loop (Non-servo)** — *Limited sequence* and *point-to-point with mechanical stops*.
2. **Closed-loop (Servo)** — *Point-to-point servo*, *Continuous Path servo*, and *Controlled Path servo*.

**Step 2 — PTP vs CP Detailed Comparison (3 marks):**

| Parameter | PTP Control | CP Control |
|---|---|---|
| **Path Definition** | Only start and end positions are specified | Entire path is mathematically defined |
| **Intermediate Path** | Not controlled — robot chooses the fastest route | Controlled — every point is interpolated |
| **Controller Type** | Open-loop OR closed-loop | Closed-loop only |
| **Velocity Profile** | Not strictly controlled along path | Trapezoidal or S-curve velocity profile |
| **Memory Requirement** | Stores only end-points | Stores entire trajectory (often thousands of points) |
| **Application Example** | Spot welding, palletizing, hole drilling | Spray painting, arc welding, polishing |
| **Example Robot** | SCARA in pick-and-place | 6-axis articulated arm in automotive paint shop |

**Step 3 — Examples (2 marks):**

- **PTP Example:** A FANUC SCARA robot picking a chip from a tray and placing it on a PCB. The robot only needs to reach the tray and the PCB — the path in between can be a fast joint-interpolated motion.
- **CP Example:** A KUKA KR 16 robot spray-painting a car door. The spray gun must follow the door's curved surface at a constant 200 mm/s — the *entire* 3D contour matters for paint uniformity.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Hydraulic Drive Schematic & Working (3.5 marks):**

> A **hydraulic drive system** consists of an electric motor driving a hydraulic pump, which pressurizes oil stored in a reservoir. The high-pressure oil is routed via control valves to either a **linear hydraulic cylinder** or a **rotary hydraulic motor** at the robot joint.

**Force equation:**
$$F_{hydraulic} = P \cdot A_{piston}$$

- For a typical industrial cylinder with $P = 210$ bar $= 21$ MPa and $A_{piston} = 0.005$ m² (≈ 80 mm bore):

$$F = 21 \times 10^6 \times 0.005 = 105{,}000\;\text{N} = 105\;\text{kN}$$

- This enormous force-per-unit-weight is why hydraulic robots can lift **car bodies** in automotive assembly.
- **Application:** Heavy-payload spot welding in car body assembly (e.g., GM, Ford body shops) and large-part forging.

**Step 2 — Pneumatic Drive Schematic & Working (2.5 marks):**

> A **pneumatic drive system** uses an external air compressor to supply 4–8 bar compressed air through FRL (Filter-Regulator-Lubricator) units to pneumatic cylinders or air motors.

**Net force equation (double-acting cylinder, extending):**
$$F_{pneumatic,ext} = P_{supply} \cdot A_{piston} - P_{atm} \cdot (A_{piston} - A_{rod})$$

- For a 50 mm bore cylinder at 6 bar supply:
$$F_{ext} = 6 \times 10^5 \times \pi \times 0.025^2 \approx 1178\;\text{N}$$
- **Application:** Lightweight pick-and-place in food packaging (e.g., biscuits, chocolate bars), where cleanliness, low cost, and safety near human workers are paramount.

**Step 3 — Concluding Comparison (1 mark):**
> Hydraulic is for **strength**, pneumatic is for **simplicity and safety**, electric is for **precision**.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1, 14 Marks]`

**(a)** Discuss the **classification of robots based on drive technologies**. Compare the performance characteristics of DC servo, AC servo, and stepper motors as robot actuators. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A 6-axis articulated robot uses an AC servo motor at its base joint. The motor has a torque constant $K_t = 0.8$ Nm/A, and the armature current is limited to $I_{max} = 30$ A. The motor drives a harmonic-drive gear reducer with ratio $n = 100$ and efficiency $\eta = 0.85$. Compute (i) the maximum motor torque, (ii) the maximum joint torque, and (iii) the joint torque constant referred to the joint side. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Classification of Drive Technologies (2.5 marks):**

Robot drives are classified into four families:

1. **Hydraulic** — pump + oil + cylinder/motor. High force, low cleanliness.
2. **Pneumatic** — compressor + air + cylinder/motor. Compliant, clean, simple.
3. **Electric** — most diverse family:
   - DC servo (armature-controlled)
   - Brushless DC (BLDC) servo (electronically commutated)
   - AC servo (induction or synchronous, VFD-controlled)
   - Stepper motor (open-loop pulse-driven)
   - Direct-drive (torque) motor (no reducer)
4. **Hybrid** — combination of two or more of the above (e.g., hydraulic legs + electric arms).

**Step 2 — Performance Comparison Table (3.5 marks):**

| Parameter | DC Servo | AC Servo | Stepper |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Commutation** | Brushes (wear, sparks) | Brushless (electronic) | Brushless |
| **Power Density** | Moderate | High | Low–Moderate |
| **Speed Range** | Up to ~3000 rpm | Up to ~6000 rpm | Typically ≤ 1000 rpm |
| **Torque at Low Speed** | High (with reducer) | Very high (with reducer) | High *holding* torque but drops with speed |
| **Control Complexity** | Simple (PWM on armature) | High (FOC, SVPWM) | Very simple (pulse + direction) |
| **Feedback Required** | Yes (closed-loop) | Yes (closed-loop) | No (open-loop) |
| **Position Accuracy** | High (with encoder) | Very high (with resolver/encoder) | Moderate (~±5% step) |
| **Heat Dissipation** | Easy (stationary field) | Harder (rotating part) | Easy |
| **Maintenance** | Brush replacement | Very low | Very low |
| **Cost** | Moderate | Higher | Lowest |
| **Typical Robot Use** | Educational arms, legacy | Modern 6-axis industrial arms (FANUC, ABB, KUKA) | 3D printers, low-cost Cartesian robots |

**Step 3 — Selection Logic (1 mark):**

> **AC servo** dominates modern industrial robots because it combines high power density, brushless reliability, and excellent dynamic response. **Stepper motors** are reserved for low-cost, low-speed PTP applications. **DC servos** are now largely legacy.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given Data:**

- Torque constant: $K_t = 0.8$ Nm/A
- Maximum current: $I_{max} = 30$ A
- Gear ratio: $n = 100$ (motor:joint = 100:1)
- Gear efficiency: $\eta = 0.85$

**Step 1 — (i) Maximum Motor Torque (2 marks):**

The motor torque is the product of torque constant and armature current:

$$\tau_{motor,max} = K_t \cdot I_{max}$$

$$\tau_{motor,max} = 0.8 \times 30 = 24\;\text{Nm}$$

**Step 2 — (ii) Maximum Joint Torque (3 marks):**

The gear reducer amplifies the motor torque by the ratio $n$, with efficiency loss $\eta$:

$$\tau_{joint,max} = \tau_{motor,max} \cdot n \cdot \eta$$

$$\tau_{joint,max} = 24 \times 100 \times 0.85 = 2040\;\text{Nm}$$

**Step 3 — (iii) Joint-Side Torque Constant (2 marks):**

The torque constant referred to the joint side is:

$$K_{t,joint} = K_t \cdot n \cdot \eta$$

$$K_{t,joint} = 0.8 \times 100 \times 0.85 = 68\;\text{Nm/A}$$

**Verification (sanity check):**

The relationship must satisfy:
$$\tau_{joint,max} = K_{t,joint} \cdot I_{max} = 68 \times 30 = 2040\;\text{Nm} \;\;\checkmark$$

**Physical interpretation:** A 24 Nm motor becomes a 2040 Nm joint — enough to lift a 200 kg payload against gravity at a 1 m arm (since $\tau = F \cdot r = 200 \times 9.81 \times 1 = 1962$ Nm < 2040 Nm). ✓

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - **Forgetting $\eta$:** Many students compute $\tau_{joint} = \tau_{motor} \times n$ only, ignoring the 15% power loss in the harmonic drive. This loses **2 marks** in the joint torque calculation.
> - **Mixing motor and joint side:** The torque constant $K_t$ is on the *motor shaft*. When the question asks for the joint-side constant, you **must** multiply by $n$ and $\eta$.
> - **Sign of efficiency:** $\eta < 1$ always *reduces* the output torque; it is never greater than 1.
> - **Dimensional check:** Always verify units. $K_t$ in Nm/A times $I$ in A gives Nm — but be careful with $K_e$ in V·s/rad vs. V/rpm.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section and keep it on your desk one day before the exam.**

- [ ] **Two classification axes are independent:** Motion control ≠ drive technology. A hydraulic robot *can* be servo-controlled.
- [ ] **Open-loop = "fire-and-forget"** — no encoder, no correction, cheap but inaccurate.
- [ ] **Closed-loop = "measure and correct"** — encoder + PID + power amplifier + motor.
- [ ] **PTP vs CP:** PTP cares about *endpoints*; CP cares about *every point* in between.
- [ ] **CP requires servo** — you cannot do continuous-path control on an open-loop stepper system with high accuracy.
- [ ] **Hydraulic** = max payload (≥ 100 kg), uses oil, foundries, heavy welding, slow but strong.
- [ ] **Pneumatic** = clean, compliant, low cost, but compressible → poor positioning accuracy.
- [ ] **Electric** = precision king; AC servo is the modern default in 6-axis industrial arms.
- [ ] **Stepper** = open-loop, low cost, position errors under load (no feedback correction).
- [ ] **DC servo equation:** $\tau = K_t I_a$ and $V_b = K_e \omega$ with $K_t \approx K_e$ in SI units.
- [ ] **Hydraulic force:** $F = P \cdot A$ — remember to convert bar to Pa ($1$ bar $= 10^5$ Pa).
- [ ] **Pneumatic force:** Net force is the difference between supply-side and atmospheric-side pressures acting on respective piston areas.
- [ ] **Hybrid robots** mix drives (e.g., hydraulic + electric) to optimize payload vs. precision.
- [ ] **Repeatability** is a *statistical* measure: the radius of the circle that contains ~99% of return positions.
- [ ] **Power-to-weight ratio** is the most important metric for mobile robots; electric drives dominate here.
- [ ] **Harmonic-drive reducers** (typical $n = 80$ to $160$, $\eta = 0.80$–$0.90$) are the gold standard for high-precision joints.
- [ ] **Modern KTU exam scenarios** always give a *concrete application* — never answer with abstract theory; tie every formula to a real use case.
- [ ] **Always draw a block diagram** for any closed-loop question — KTU examiners give 1–2 free marks for clean schematics.
- [ ] **The figure of merit $S = \tau \omega / (m c)$** is implicit in many "justify your choice" questions.
- [ ] **Common misconception:** A *servo motor* is not the same as a *servo mechanism*. A servo motor is the actuator; a servo mechanism (or servo system) is the entire closed-loop control system built around it.
- [ ] **Exam mnemonic — "HPE-E"** for drive types: **H**ydraulic, **P**neumatic, **E**lectric, **E**nhanced (Hybrid).
- [ ] **Exam mnemonic — "PSC"** for motion control: **P**oint-to-point, **S**ervo-controlled, **C**ontinuous-path.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_5_END -->
