# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
> **Q: Differentiate between Active and Passive Grippers. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Active Gripper | Passive Gripper |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Energy Source** | External (pneumatic/electric/hydraulic) | None during holding |
| **Force Control** | Adjustable | Fixed (inherent) |
| **Complexity** | High (actuators, valves, sensors) | Low (mechanical/magnetic) |
| **Cost** | Higher | Lower |
| **Failure Mode** | Drops object on power loss | Often holds (fail-safe) |

**Examples:**
- **Active:** Pneumatic jaw gripper, Servo-electric gripper.
- **Passive:** Permanent magnet gripper, Spring-loaded mechanical gripper.

---

### **Question A2** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
> **Q: What is the working principle of a vacuum gripper? State two of its limitations.**

**Model Answer:**

**Working Principle:** A vacuum gripper creates a **pressure differential** between the inside of a sealed suction cup and the external atmosphere. The higher atmospheric pressure pushes the workpiece against the cup, holding it firmly.

**Limitations:**
1. Requires **smooth, non-porous, flat surfaces** — fails on rough, perforated, or dusty surfaces.
2. Limited holding force — typically only **70-80% of theoretical maximum** due to leakage; needs a **vacuum pump**.
3. Single-point failure if vacuum seal is broken.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question B1A** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024] — *(Choose any ONE)*

**Question A (14 Marks):**
> **(a)** Classify end effectors used in robots with a neat block diagram. Explain **mechanical grippers** in detail, including the **friction-based grip force equation** with derivation for both **static and dynamic conditions**. *(7 Marks)*
>
> **(b)** A robot grips a **cylindrical steel rod** weighing **15 N** using a two-jaw mechanical gripper. The coefficient of friction between jaw pads and rod is **0.25**. The robot arm moves the rod with an upward acceleration of **1.5 m/s²**. Calculate:
>   - (i) The minimum static grip force per jaw. *(2 Marks)*
>   - (ii) The minimum dynamic grip force per jaw. *(3 Marks)*
>   - (iii) Comment on why the dynamic force is greater. *(2 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification & Mechanical Grippers:**

End effectors are classified as follows (drawn as a block diagram — see Section 4.1):

**End Effectors:**
- **Grippers:** Mechanical, Magnetic, Vacuum, Adhesive
- **Special Tools:** Welding gun, Spray gun, Drill, Laser

**Mechanical Grippers** use **two or more jaws (fingers)** that close on the workpiece to hold it via **friction**. They are actuated by **pneumatic**, **hydraulic**, **electric**, or **spring** mechanisms.

**Static Friction Equation Derivation:**

Free body diagram of cylindrical workpiece under two opposing jaws. Apply vertical equilibrium:

$$\sum F_y = 0 \implies f_1 + f_2 \geq W$$

With $f_1 = f_2 = \mu F_g$:

$$2\mu F_g \geq W \implies F_g \geq \frac{W}{2\mu}$$

**Dynamic Friction Equation (with acceleration):**

When the arm accelerates upward at $a$, the effective weight becomes $W_{eff} = W(1 + a/g)$:

$$\boxed{F_g \geq \frac{W\left(1 + \dfrac{a}{g}\right)}{2\mu}}$$

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given: $W = 15$ N, $\mu = 0.25$, $a = 1.5$ m/s², $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**(i) Static Grip Force** ($a = 0$):

$$F_{g,static} = \frac{W}{2\mu} = \frac{15}{2 \times 0.25} = \frac{15}{0.5}$$

$$F_{g,static} = 30 \text{ N}$$

**(ii) Dynamic Grip Force:**

$$F_{g,dynamic} = \frac{W\left(1 + \dfrac{a}{g}\right)}{2\mu} = \frac{15\left(1 + \dfrac{1.5}{9.81}\right)}{2 \times 0.25}$$

Compute acceleration factor:

$$1 + \frac{1.5}{9.81} = 1 + 0.1529 = 1.1529$$

Compute numerator:

$$15 \times 1.1529 = 17.2935 \text{ N}$$

Compute denominator:

$$2 \times 0.25 = 0.5$$

Final dynamic force:

$$F_{g,dynamic} = \frac{17.2935}{0.5} = 34.587 \text{ N}$$

**(iii) Comment:**

The dynamic grip force (**34.59 N**) is greater than the static force (**30 N**) by approximately **15.29%**. This is because during upward acceleration, an **additional inertial force** ($ma$) acts on the workpiece, increasing the effective load the jaws must counter via friction.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write $F_g = W / \mu$ (without the factor 2) — forgetting there are **two jaws** sharing the load. This costs 1-2 marks. Always state clearly: *"Each jaw provides grip force $F_g$, and there are 2 jaws contributing friction $2\mu F_g$."*

---

### **Question B1B** [KTU University Exam - May 2024] — *ALTERNATIVE CHOICE*

**Question B (14 Marks):**
> **(a)** Explain in detail the working principle of **magnetic and vacuum grippers**. Derive the holding force equations for both. *(7 Marks)*
>
> **(b)** A vacuum gripper with a **circular suction cup of diameter 60 mm** is used to lift a glass plate. The vacuum pump reduces cup pressure to **40 kPa absolute**. Determine: *(7 Marks)*
>   - (i) The pressure differential. *(2 Marks)*
>   - (ii) The theoretical holding force. *(3 Marks)*
>   - (iii) If a **safety factor of 2.5** is applied, what is the actual safe payload in kg? *(2 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Magnetic & Vacuum Grippers:**

**Magnetic Gripper** uses **magnetic flux** between the gripper poles and the ferromagnetic workpiece to create a holding force.

$$\boxed{F_m = \frac{B^2 A}{2\mu_0}}$$

- **Electromagnetic type:** Active; flux controlled by current.
- **Permanent magnet type:** Passive; flux always present; mechanical ejector for release.

**Vacuum Gripper** uses **pressure differential** across a sealed suction cup.

$$\boxed{F_v = (P_{atm} - P_{cup}) \cdot A_{cup}}$$

A vacuum pump reduces internal cup pressure; atmospheric pressure on the other side holds the workpiece.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given: $d = 60$ mm = $0.06$ m, $P_{cup} = 40$ kPa = $40{,}000$ Pa, $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa

**(i) Pressure Differential:**

$$\Delta P = P_{atm} - P_{cup} = 101.325 - 40 = 61.325 \text{ kPa} = 61325 \text{ Pa}$$

**(ii) Theoretical Holding Force:**

Cup area:

$$A_{cup} = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.06)^2}{4} = 2.827 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

Holding force:

$$F_v = \Delta P \cdot A_{cup} = 61325 \times 2.827 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$F_v = 173.36 \text{ N}$$

**(iii) With Safety Factor (SF = 2.5):**

Safe payload force:

$$F_{safe} = \frac{F_v}{SF} = \frac{173.36}{2.5} = 69.34 \text{ N}$$

Safe payload mass:

$$m_{safe} = \frac{F_{safe}}{g} = \frac{69.34}{9.81} = 7.07 \text{ kg}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** Theoretical holding = **173.36 N**; safe working payload = **7.07 kg** with safety factor 2.5.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Common mistakes in vacuum gripper problems:
> 1. Forgetting to convert **diameter → radius squared**, using wrong area formula.
> 2. Using **gauge pressure** instead of converting to **absolute pressure** for the differential.
> 3. **Not applying safety factor** — KTU always expects a safety factor of 1.5 to 3.0 in design calculations.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary** — Read this 30 minutes before your exam.

### ⚡ Core Concepts
- [x] **End Effector** = terminal device on robot wrist; the only part that interacts with the environment.
- [x] **Grippers** hold/grasp; **Tools** process (weld, paint, drill).

### ⚡ Classification Master List
- [x] **Mechanical** (friction-based, jaws)
- [x] **Magnetic** (flux-based, ferrous only)
- [x] **Vacuum** (pressure differential, smooth surfaces)
- [x] **Adhesive** (molecular / van der Waals, gecko-inspired)
- [x] **Special Tools** (welding, painting, drilling)

### ⚡ Active vs Passive
- [x] **Active:** External power, controllable force. Examples: pneumatic, hydraulic, servo-electric grippers.
- [x] **Passive:** No continuous energy for holding. Examples: permanent magnet, spring, adhesive.

### ⚡ Mandatory Formulas
- [x] Static grip: $F_g = \dfrac{W}{2\mu}$
- [x] Dynamic grip: $F_g = \dfrac{W(1 + a/g)}{2\mu}$
- [x] Magnetic: $F_m = \dfrac{B^2 A}{2\mu_0}$
- [x] Vacuum: $F_v = \Delta P \cdot A_{cup}$
- [x] $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m
- [x] $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa

### ⚡ Selection Decision Heuristics
- [x] **Smooth flat non-porous** → Vacuum
- [x] **Ferromagnetic metal** → Magnetic
- [x] **Irregular shape** → Mechanical with custom jaws
- [x] **Fragile/delicate** → Soft jaw mechanical or adhesive
- [x] **High temperature** → Magnetic (no rubber/electronics contact)

### ⚡ Design Considerations
- [x] Workpiece geometry, material, weight, surface finish
- [x] Required force (static + dynamic + safety factor)
- [x] Cycle time and speed
- [x] Power source availability
- [x] Environmental conditions (heat, dust, moisture)
- [x] **Safety — fail-safe behavior on power failure**
- [x] Cost and integration with controller

### ⚡ Numerical Traps to Avoid
- [x] **Two jaws** → denominator has **2**
- [x] **Acceleration** → use dynamic equation, not static
- [x] **Safety factor** → apply at the end (typically 1.5–3.0)
- [x] **Pressure differential** → always $P_{atm} - P_{cup}$, not absolute $P_{cup}$ alone
- [x] **Units** → convert mm→m, cm²→m², kPa→Pa before computation

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** KTU typically asks **one definition question (3 marks)** and **one full derivation + numerical question (14 marks)** from this topic. Master the friction-based grip force derivation — it appears in nearly every exam cycle.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Trajectory Planning - Tasks, Path Planning, Joint Space Trajectory Planning (Cubic Polynomial, Linear Trajectory with Parabolic Blends, Trajectory Planning with Via Points)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT302 - INTRODUCTION TO ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Trajectory Planning - Tasks, Path Planning, Joint Space Trajectory Planning (Cubic Polynomial, Linear Trajectory with Parabolic Blends, Trajectory Planning with Via Points)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Trajectory Planning?

**Trajectory Planning** is the computational process of determining a time-parameterized motion profile for a robot manipulator so that it moves from an initial configuration to a final configuration while satisfying kinematic (position, velocity, acceleration) and dynamic (torque, force) constraints. It is a *higher-level* decision that sits above inverse kinematics in the robotic control hierarchy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Wording):**
> "Trajectory planning is the generation of a time-history of a robot's position, velocity, and acceleration for each degree of freedom, subject to the task constraints and actuator limits, in order to execute a desired geometric path."

The key outputs of a trajectory planner are usually:

- $\theta(t)$ — position of every joint as a function of time
- $\dot{\theta}(t)$ — joint velocity profile
- $\ddot{\theta}(t)$ — joint acceleration profile

where $\theta \in \mathbb{R}^n$ for an $n$-DOF manipulator, and $t$ is the independent time variable.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "Driving from Home to Office"

Imagine you have to drive a car from **Home (start)** to **Office (goal)** on a Monday morning.

- **Path Planning** answers the question: *Which roads will I take?* It only describes the geometric shape of the route (e.g., "go straight, turn right at the bakery, take the highway").
- **Trajectory Planning** answers the *much harder* question: *At what exact time will I be at each point? How fast will I accelerate from a red light? Will I brake smoothly before the turn?*

Similarly, in robotics, **path = geometry (shape)**, while **trajectory = geometry + time law (speed profile)**.

## 1.3 Robot Tasks — Classification of Trajectory Planning Problems

A manipulator may be asked to perform different categories of motion. The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly categorizes them as follows:

### (a) Pick and Place Operations (Motion between two points)
The robot must move from point **A** to point **B** without caring about the intermediate path. The trajectory only needs to satisfy start and end joint values (and possibly initial/final velocities). It is the *simplest* planning problem.

> [!NOTE]
> Used widely in **assembly lines, palletizing, and packaging** industries where path shape is irrelevant — only start and end matter.

### (b) Continuous Path (CP) Motion
The end-effector must trace a *specific continuous geometric curve* (e.g., welding a seam, painting a curve, laser cutting). The trajectory must be dense in points so that the actual path closely follows the desired path.

### (c) Slew Motion
A simultaneous coordinated motion of all joints where path shape is *not* important, but *synchronization* of joints is critical so that the end-effector reaches the goal at the correct instant.

## 1.4 Path Planning vs. Trajectory Planning — The Critical Distinction

| Feature | Path Planning | Trajectory Planning |
|---|---|---|
| Output | Geometric curve in space | Curve *plus* time-law |
| Independent variable | Arc length $s$ | Time $t$ |
| Concern | Avoiding obstacles, shortest route | Velocity, acceleration, jerk limits |
| Includes dynamics? | Generally no | Often yes |
| Layer in hierarchy | Higher (decision level) | Lower (executive level) |

## 1.5 Joint Space vs. Cartesian Space Planning

This is one of the most frequently tested distinctions in KTU papers:

- **Joint Space Trajectory Planning:** The path is described as a set of joint angles $\theta_i(t)$. The shape of the end-effector path in Cartesian space is generally *not* a straight line, but the motion is easy to compute, free of singularities in the middle of motion, and naturally bounded by joint limits.
- **Cartesian Space Trajectory Planning:** The path is described in the $(x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi)$ task space. The geometric path is intuitive (e.g., a straight line), but every intermediate point must be passed through inverse kinematics, which is computationally expensive and may hit singularities.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Rule of Thumb (often asked):** Use **joint-space** planning when path shape is unimportant (pick & place); use **Cartesian-space** planning when the path shape itself matters (welding, painting, gluing).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 General Philosophy of Joint-Space Trajectory Planning

A joint-space trajectory is a polynomial (or piecewise polynomial) function $\theta_i(t)$ for each joint $i$. The choice of polynomial order is dictated by the **boundary conditions** we wish to satisfy.

| Number of Boundary Conditions | Minimum Polynomial Order |
|---|---|
| 2 (position only at start & end) | 1 (linear) |
| 4 (position + velocity at start & end) | 3 (cubic) |
| 6 (position + velocity + acceleration at start & end) | 5 (quintic) |

A polynomial of degree $n$ has $n+1$ coefficients. We can match exactly $n+1$ independent boundary conditions. This is the **fundamental principle** of trajectory design.

## 2.2 Cubic Polynomial Trajectory

For a single joint moving from $\theta_0$ to $\theta_f$ in time $t_f$, with zero initial and final velocities (the most common case for pick-and-place), we use:

$$\theta(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$$

**Four boundary conditions:**

$$\theta(0) = \theta_0, \quad \theta(t_f) = \theta_f$$
$$\dot{\theta}(0) = 0, \quad \dot{\theta}(t_f) = 0$$

Solving these four linear equations gives the closed-form coefficients:

| Coefficient | Value |
|---|---|
| $a_0$ | $\theta_0$ |
| $a_1$ | $0$ |
| $a_2$ | $\dfrac{3(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{t_f^2}$ |
| $a_3$ | $\dfrac{-2(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{t_f^3}$ |

The velocity and acceleration profiles are obtained by simple differentiation:

$$\dot{\theta}(t) = a_1 + 2 a_2 t + 3 a_3 t^2$$
$$\ddot{\theta}(t) = 2 a_2 + 6 a_3 t$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key property of cubic trajectory:** The acceleration is a *linear* function of time (since $\ddot{\theta}$ is first-degree in $t$). It starts at a positive value, crosses zero exactly at the midpoint, and ends at a symmetric negative value. This produces a single "S-curve" velocity profile.

### 2.2.1 Cubic Polynomial with Non-Zero Initial/Final Velocities

If the joint is already in motion at $t = 0$ (velocity $\dot{\theta}_0$) and must arrive with velocity $\dot{\theta}_f$ (this happens when *via points* are used), the boundary conditions become:

$$\theta(0)=\theta_0,\;\; \theta(t_f)=\theta_f,\;\; \dot{\theta}(0)=\dot{\theta}_0,\;\; \dot{\theta}(t_f)=\dot{\theta}_f$$

The general solution matrix form is:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \theta_0 \\ \dot{\theta}_0 \\ \theta_f \\ \dot{\theta}_f \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\
1 & t_f & t_f^2 & t_f^3 \\
0 & 1 & 2 t_f & 3 t_f^2
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} a_0 \\ a_1 \\ a_2 \\ a_3 \end{bmatrix}
$$

## 2.3 Linear Trajectory with Parabolic Blends (LTPB)

A pure linear trajectory has the problem of **discontinuous acceleration at the start and end** — physically, this requires *infinite jerk* and is impossible for any real motor. The remedy is to "blend" the linear segment with two parabolic segments at the ends.

### Structure of an LTPB Profile

The motion has three phases:

1. **Acceleration phase** ($0 \le t \le t_b$): constant acceleration $\ddot{\theta} = \alpha$
2. **Constant-velocity phase** ($t_b \le t \le t_f - t_b$): acceleration = 0, velocity = constant
3. **Deceleration phase** ($t_f - t_b \le t \le t_f$): constant deceleration $\ddot{\theta} = -\alpha$

The total displacement is shared between the parabolic and linear regions:

$$\theta_f - \theta_0 = \alpha t_b (t_f - t_b) + \alpha t_b^2 = \alpha t_b t_f$$

From this we get the **acceleration magnitude**:

$$\alpha = \frac{\theta_f - \theta_0}{t_b t_f}$$

And the constant cruise velocity:

$$v = \alpha t_b = \frac{\theta_f - \theta_0}{t_f}$$

The **time to accelerate** is:

$$t_b = \frac{\omega_0 - \omega_f}{\alpha}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Special case — "no linear segment":** If $t_f$ is so short that $2t_b \ge t_f$, the trajectory becomes *purely* two parabolic segments stitched back-to-back. The condition for this is $t_f \le 2 t_b$, which means the linear segment vanishes.

## 2.4 Trajectory Planning with Via Points

When a robot must pass through one or more intermediate waypoints (e.g., to avoid an obstacle or follow a CP path), we use **multiple cubic polynomials** — one per segment. For $N$ via points, we have $N+1$ segments and therefore $N+1$ cubic polynomials.

### Continuity Requirements at Via Points

| Continuity Level | Constraint |
|---|---|
| Position continuity | $\theta^-(t_k) = \theta^+(t_k)$ |
| Velocity continuity | $\dot{\theta}^-(t_k) = \dot{\theta}^+(t_k)$ |
| Acceleration continuity | $\ddot{\theta}^-(t_k) = \ddot{\theta}^+(t_k)$ |

For position + velocity continuity, **4 constraints per interior via point** are imposed. For a trajectory through $N$ via points, we get $4(N+1)$ unknowns and $4N + 2$ equations — i.e., the system is **under-determined by 2**. The remaining 2 degrees of freedom are usually fixed by specifying acceleration (typically zero) at the start and end, leading to the **cubic-spline trajectory**.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation | Key Insight |
|---|---|---|
| Cubic polynomial position | $\theta(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$ | 4 coefficients, 4 BCs |
| Cubic $a_0$ | $a_0 = \theta_0$ | Equals initial position |
| Cubic $a_1$ | $a_1 = 0$ (zero initial velocity case) | Often zero for pick-and-place |
| Cubic $a_2$ | $a_2 = \dfrac{3(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{t_f^2}$ | Drives mid-point position |
| Cubic $a_3$ | $a_3 = \dfrac{-2(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{t_f^3}$ | Symmetric "S-curve" coefficient |
| LTPB acceleration | $\alpha = \dfrac{\theta_f - \theta_0}{t_b t_f}$ | Constant magnitude |
| LTPB cruise velocity | $v = \alpha t_b$ | Plateau of trapezoidal profile |
| LTPB blend time | $t_b = \dfrac{\theta_f - \theta_0}{\alpha t_f}$ | Determines "roundness" |
| Total LTPB time | $t_f = t_c + 2 t_b$ | $t_c$ = linear-segment duration |
| Via-point system | $4(N+1)$ unknowns | One cubic per segment |
| Position continuity | $\theta^-(t_k) = \theta^+(t_k)$ | Mandatory at every via point |
| Velocity continuity | $\dot{\theta}^-(t_k) = \dot{\theta}^+(t_k)$ | Required for smooth motion |
| Acceleration continuity | $\ddot{\theta}^-(t_k) = \ddot{\theta}^+(t_k)$ | Required for cubic-spline smoothness |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world utility:** Cubic polynomials are the workhorse of industrial robot controllers (ABB, KUKA, FANUC) for pick-and-place because they offer the minimum order that satisfies both position and zero-velocity BCs. LTPB is the historical default for simple CNC machines. Cubic splines through via points are the foundation of modern **on-line trajectory generation** in collaborative robots.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Cubic Polynomial Coefficients (Zero Initial/Final Velocity)

**Statement.** A single joint moves from $\theta_0$ to $\theta_f$ in time $t_f$ with zero velocity at both endpoints. Derive the four coefficients of the cubic trajectory.

**Step 1 — Assume a general cubic form:**

$$
\theta(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3
$$

This has four unknown constants $a_0, a_1, a_2, a_3$.

**Step 2 — Differentiate to get velocity and acceleration:**

$$
\dot{\theta}(t) = a_1 + 2 a_2 t + 3 a_3 t^2
$$
$$
\ddot{\theta}(t) = 2 a_2 + 6 a_3 t
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the four boundary conditions.**

*Condition 1:* $\theta(0) = \theta_0$:

$$
a_0 + a_1 (0) + a_2 (0)^2 + a_3 (0)^3 = \theta_0
$$

$$
\Rightarrow a_0 = \theta_0
$$

*Condition 2:* $\dot{\theta}(0) = 0$:

$$
a_1 + 2 a_2 (0) + 3 a_3 (0)^2 = 0
$$

$$
\Rightarrow a_1 = 0
$$

*Condition 3:* $\theta(t_f) = \theta_f$:

$$
a_0 + a_1 t_f + a_2 t_f^2 + a_3 t_f^3 = \theta_f
$$

Substituting $a_0 = \theta_0$ and $a_1 = 0$:

$$
\theta_0 + a_2 t_f^2 + a_3 t_f^3 = \theta_f
$$

*Condition 4:* $\dot{\theta}(t_f) = 0$:

$$
a_1 + 2 a_2 t_f + 3 a_3 t_f^2 = 0
$$

Substituting $a_1 = 0$:

$$
2 a_2 t_f + 3 a_3 t_f^2 = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Solve the simultaneous linear system.**

From Condition 4, factor out $t_f$ (assuming $t_f \ne 0$):

$$
2 a_2 + 3 a_3 t_f = 0
$$

$$
\Rightarrow a_2 = -\frac{3 a_3 t_f}{2}
$$

Substitute into Condition 3:

$$
\theta_0 + \left(-\frac{3 a_3 t_f}{2}\right) t_f^2 + a_3 t_f^3 = \theta_f
$$

$$
\theta_0 - \frac{3 a_3 t_f^3}{2} + a_3 t_f^3 = \theta_f
$$

$$
\theta_0 - \frac{a_3 t_f^3}{2} = \theta_f
$$

Solve for $a_3$:

$$
- \frac{a_3 t_f^3}{2} = \theta_f - \theta_0
$$

$$
a_3 = -\frac{2(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{t_f^3}
$$

Substitute back for $a_2$:

$$
a_2 = -\frac{3}{2} t_f \cdot \left[-\frac{2(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{t_f^3}\right] = \frac{3(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{t_f^2}
$$

**Final result:**

$$
a_0 = \theta_0,\quad a_1 = 0,\quad a_2 = \frac{3(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{t_f^2},\quad a_3 = -\frac{2(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{t_f^3}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Verification — Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A robot joint must move from $\theta_0 = 10^\circ$ to $\theta_f = 50^\circ$ in $t_f = 2$ s with zero initial and final velocity. Find the cubic trajectory and the velocity at $t = 1$ s.

**Step 1 — Compute displacement:**

$$
\Delta\theta = \theta_f - \theta_0 = 50 - 10 = 40^\circ
$$

**Step 2 — Apply formulas:**

$$
a_0 = 10^\circ
$$
$$
a_1 = 0
$$
$$
a_2 = \frac{3 \times 40}{2^2} = \frac{120}{4} = 30^\circ/\text{s}^2
$$
$$
a_3 = -\frac{2 \times 40}{2^3} = -\frac{80}{8} = -10^\circ/\text{s}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Trajectory equation:**

$$
\theta(t) = 10 + 30 t^2 - 10 t^3
$$

**Step 4 — Velocity profile:**

$$
\dot{\theta}(t) = 60 t - 30 t^2
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate at $t = 1$ s:**

$$
\dot{\theta}(1) = 60(1) - 30(1)^2 = 30^\circ/\text{s}
$$

**Step 6 — Maximum velocity check.** The peak of $\dot{\theta}$ occurs where $\ddot{\theta} = 0$:

$$
\ddot{\theta}(t) = 60 - 60 t = 0 \Rightarrow t = 1 \text{ s}
$$

This is the midpoint — exactly symmetric, as expected for a cubic with zero end-velocities. Peak velocity is $\dot{\theta}_{\max} = 30^\circ/\text{s}$.

## 3.3 Derivation: Linear Trajectory with Parabolic Blends

**Statement.** Derive the equations of motion for an LTPB trajectory moving from $\theta_0$ to $\theta_f$ in total time $t_f$ with constant acceleration magnitude $\alpha$ during the blend regions of duration $t_b$ each.

**Step 1 — Define the three regions.**

- Region I (acceleration): $0 \le t \le t_b$, with $\ddot{\theta}(t) = +\alpha$
- Region II (cruise): $t_b \le t \le (t_f - t_b)$, with $\ddot{\theta}(t) = 0$
- Region III (deceleration): $(t_f - t_b) \le t \le t_f$, with $\ddot{\theta}(t) = -\alpha$

**Step 2 — Integrate to get velocity in Region I:**

$$
\dot{\theta}(t) = \int \alpha \, dt = \alpha t + C_1
$$

Apply $\dot{\theta}(0) = 0$:

$$
C_1 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dot{\theta}(t) = \alpha t, \quad 0 \le t \le t_b
$$

**Step 3 — Integrate to get position in Region I:**

$$
\theta(t) = \int \alpha t \, dt = \frac{1}{2}\alpha t^2 + C_2
$$

Apply $\theta(0) = \theta_0$:

$$
C_2 = \theta_0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta(t) = \theta_0 + \frac{1}{2}\alpha t^2, \quad 0 \le t \le t_b
$$

At the end of Region I:

$$
\theta(t_b) = \theta_0 + \frac{1}{2}\alpha t_b^2, \quad \dot{\theta}(t_b) = \alpha t_b
$$

**Step 4 — Region II (constant velocity).** The cruise velocity is $v = \alpha t_b$:

$$
\theta(t) = \theta(t_b) + \alpha t_b (t - t_b), \quad t_b \le t \le t_f - t_b
$$

**Step 5 — Region III (deceleration).** Acceleration is $-\alpha$:

$$
\dot{\theta}(t) = \alpha t_b - \alpha(t - (t_f - t_b)) = \alpha(t_f - t)
$$

Integrate:

$$
\theta(t) = \theta_f - \frac{1}{2}\alpha (t_f - t)^2
$$

**Step 6 — Total displacement constraint.** Sum contributions:

$$
\theta_f - \theta_0 = \underbrace{\frac{1}{2}\alpha t_b^2}_{\text{Region I}} + \underbrace{\alpha t_b (t_f - 2t_b)}_{\text{Region II}} + \underbrace{\frac{1}{2}\alpha t_b^2}_{\text{Region III}}
$$

Simplify:

$$
\theta_f - \theta_0 = \alpha t_b t_f - \alpha t_b^2
$$

$$
\Rightarrow \alpha t_b (t_f - t_b) = \theta_f - \theta_0
$$

For a *given* $\alpha$ and total time $t_f$, the blend time is:

$$
t_b = \frac{\theta_f - \theta_0}{\alpha t_f} \quad \text{(assuming } t_b \le t_f/2\text{)}
$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — LTPB

**Problem:** A joint must move from $\theta_0 = 0^\circ$ to $\theta_f = 60^\circ$ in $t_f = 4$ s using an LTPB profile. The blend time is $t_b = 1$ s. Compute $\alpha$, cruise velocity, and $\theta(2)$ s.

**Step 1 — Compute acceleration magnitude:**

$$
\alpha = \frac{\theta_f - \theta_0}{t_b t_f} = \frac{60}{1 \times 4} = 15^\circ/\text{s}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Cruise velocity:**

$$
v = \alpha t_b = 15 \times 1 = 15^\circ/\text{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Position at $t = 2$ s (this lies in the linear region since $1 \le 2 \le 3$):**

Position at start of linear region:

$$
\theta(t_b) = \theta_0 + \frac{1}{2}\alpha t_b^2 = 0 + 0.5 \times 15 \times 1^2 = 7.5^\circ
$$

Then in Region II:

$$
\theta(2) = 7.5 + 15 (2 - 1) = 7.5 + 15 = 22.5^\circ
$$

**Step 4 — Sanity check on total motion:**
- Region I: $0 \to 7.5^\circ$ (acceleration)
- Region II: $7.5 \to 52.5^\circ$ (linear, $30^\circ$ over $2$ s at $15^\circ/\text{s}$)
- Region III: $52.5 \to 60^\circ$ (deceleration)

Total: $7.5 + 45 + 7.5 = 60^\circ$ ✓

## 3.5 Derivation: General Cubic with Non-Zero End Velocities

**Statement.** For a cubic trajectory, derive the coefficient matrix if the boundary conditions are $\theta(0)=\theta_0$, $\theta(t_f)=\theta_f$, $\dot{\theta}(0)=\dot{\theta}_0$, $\dot{\theta}(t_f)=\dot{\theta}_f$.

**Step 1 — Set up the system:**

$$
\theta(0) = a_0 = \theta_0
$$
$$
\dot{\theta}(0) = a_1 = \dot{\theta}_0
$$
$$
\theta(t_f) = a_0 + a_1 t_f + a_2 t_f^2 + a_3 t_f^3 = \theta_f
$$
$$
\dot{\theta}(t_f) = a_1 + 2 a_2 t_f + 3 a_3 t_f^2 = \dot{\theta}_f
$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange into matrix form:**

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \theta_0 \\ \dot{\theta}_0 \\ \theta_f \\ \dot{\theta}_f \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\
1 & t_f & t_f^2 & t_f^3 \\
0 & 1 & 2 t_f & 3 t_f^2
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} a_0 \\ a_1 \\ a_2 \\ a_3 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve by inverting the matrix.** The closed-form solution is:

$$
a_0 = \theta_0
$$
$$
a_1 = \dot{\theta}_0
$$
$$
a_2 = \frac{3(\theta_f - \theta_0) - (2\dot{\theta}_0 + \dot{\theta}_f) t_f}{t_f^2}
$$
$$
a_3 = \frac{-2(\theta_f - \theta_0) + (\dot{\theta}_0 + \dot{\theta}_f) t_f}{t_f^3}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> These expressions reduce to the standard cubic when $\dot{\theta}_0 = \dot{\theta}_f = 0$, confirming internal consistency.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Via-Point Spline System

For a trajectory passing through via points $V_1, V_2, \dots, V_N$ (each with specified position, velocity, and acceleration), the global system has the form:

$$
\mathbf{H} \mathbf{a} = \mathbf{b}
$$

where $\mathbf{H}$ is a **block-banded** matrix. For the special case of a cubic spline with zero end-accelerations, the tridiagonal structure emerges. This is a classic KTU derivation topic.

> [!TIP]
> **Self-check:** A student should be able to state the four boundary conditions, write the 4×4 system, and invert symbolically to derive $a_0, a_1, a_2, a_3$ — these are the *exact* questions KTU 2024 expects in Part B.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchy of Robot Motion Planning

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Task Specification] --> B[Path Planning]
    B --> C[Geometric Path in Space]
    C --> D[Trajectory Planning]
    D --> E[Time-Parameterized Motion]
    E --> F[Inverse Kinematics per Sample]
    F --> G[Joint Actuator Commands]
    G --> H[Physical Robot Motion]
    
    A1[Pick and Place] -.-> A
    A2[Continuous Path CP] -.-> A
    A3[Slew Motion] -.-> A
```

> The diagram above maps the **decision-to-execution flow** of any robotic task. Trajectory planning sits in the middle layer, where time-domain constraints (velocity, acceleration) are applied to an already-determined geometric path.

## 4.2 Joint-Space vs. Cartesian-Space Trajectory Planning

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph JS[Joint Space]
        J1[theta0 at t=0] --> J2[cubic polynomial theta t] --> J3[thetaf at t=tf]
    end
    subgraph CS[Cartesian Space]
        C1[Px0 Py0 Pz0 at t=0] --> C2[straight line in xyz] --> C3[Pxf Pyf Pzf at t=tf]
    end
    J2 -.-> INV[Inverse Kinematics per Sample]
    C2 -.-> INV
    INV --> ACT[Joint Actuator Commands]
```

## 4.3 Cubic Polynomial — Time-Domain Profiles

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph POS[Position theta t]
        P0[theta0 at t=0] --> P1[smooth S curve] --> P2[thetaf at t=tf]
    end
    subgraph VEL[Velocity dot theta t]
        V0[0 at t=0] --> V1[parabolic peak at t=tf/2] --> V2[0 at t=tf]
    end
    subgraph ACC[Acceleration ddot theta t]
        A0[positive constant a2] --> A1[linear decrease] --> A2[negative at t=tf]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that the **acceleration of a cubic polynomial is linear in time**, not constant. This is why the LTPB is sometimes preferred when *truly constant* acceleration is needed.

## 4.4 Linear Trajectory with Parabolic Blends — Phase Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LTPB[LTPB Motion Profile]
        R1[Region I: Acceleration parabolic] --> R2[Region II: Linear cruise]
        R2 --> R3[Region III: Deceleration parabolic]
    end
    subgraph P[Position theta t]
        P1[parabolic curve] --> P2[straight line] --> P3[parabolic curve]
    end
    subgraph V[Velocity dot theta t]
        V1[linear ramp up] --> V2[constant plateau] --> V3[linear ramp down]
    end
    subgraph A[Acceleration ddot theta t]
        A1[constant + alpha] --> A2[zero] --> A3[constant minus alpha]
    end
```

## 4.5 Via-Point Trajectory — Cubic Spline Stitching

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V0[Start] --> S1[Segment 1 cubic]
    S1 --> V1[Via Point 1]
    V1 --> S2[Segment 2 cubic]
    S2 --> V2[Via Point 2]
    V2 --> S3[Segment 3 cubic]
    S3 --> VF[Final]
    
    V1 -.-> C1[Position and velocity continuous]
    V2 -.-> C2[Position and velocity continuous]
```

## 4.6 Algorithm Block Diagram — Cubic Trajectory Generation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INPUT[Input theta0 thetaf tf] --> BC1[Apply BC1 theta 0 equals theta0]
    BC1 --> BC2[Apply BC2 dot theta 0 equals 0]
    BC2 --> BC3[Apply BC3 theta tf equals thetaf]
    BC3 --> BC4[Apply BC4 dot theta tf equals 0]
    BC4 --> SOLVE[Solve 4x4 Linear System]
    SOLVE --> COEF[Output a0 a1 a2 a3]
    COEF --> OUT[Trajectory theta t equals a0 plus a1 t plus a2 t squared plus a3 t cubed]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **standard "four-step algorithm"** the KTU examiner expects students to recite in Part A (3-mark) questions on cubic trajectory planning.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define trajectory planning in the context of industrial robotics. How does it differ from path planning?**

**Model Answer:**
Trajectory planning is the generation of time-parameterized position, velocity, and acceleration profiles for each joint of a robot manipulator to move it from an initial to a final configuration while satisfying kinematic and dynamic constraints.

**Difference from path planning:**

| Path Planning | Trajectory Planning |
|---|---|
| Geometric description of the route (shape only) | Adds the time-law (when to be where) |
| Independent variable is arc length $s$ | Independent variable is time $t$ |
| Does not specify speed/acceleration | Specifies velocity, acceleration, jerk |

Thus, every trajectory contains a path, but a path is *not* sufficient to be a trajectory.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**List the four boundary conditions required to determine a cubic polynomial trajectory for a single joint moving from $\theta_0$ to $\theta_f$ in time $t_f$ with zero initial and final velocity.**

**Model Answer:**
The four boundary conditions for a cubic trajectory $\theta(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$ are:

1. $\theta(0) = \theta_0$ — initial position
2. $\dot{\theta}(0) = 0$ — zero initial velocity
3. $\theta(t_f) = \theta_f$ — final position
4. $\dot{\theta}(t_f) = 0$ — zero final velocity

These four conditions uniquely determine the four coefficients $a_0, a_1, a_2, a_3$.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> Note: Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students answer **either Option A or Option B** within a module.

### Question A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]`**

**(a)** A single joint of a 6-DOF manipulator must move from $\theta_0 = 15^\circ$ to $\theta_f = 75^\circ$ in $t_f = 4$ s with zero initial and final velocity. Determine the cubic polynomial trajectory. Compute the joint position, velocity, and acceleration at $t = 2$ s. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robot joint uses a linear trajectory with parabolic blends. The joint must move from $20^\circ$ to $80^\circ$ in $t_f = 6$ s. The blend time is $t_b = 2$ s. Find (i) the acceleration magnitude $\alpha$, (ii) the cruise velocity, and (iii) the joint position at $t = 3$ s. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to A(a)

**Step 1 — Displacement:**

$$
\Delta\theta = \theta_f - \theta_0 = 75 - 15 = 60^\circ
$$

**Step 2 — Apply cubic formulas with $t_f = 4$ s:**

$$
a_0 = \theta_0 = 15^\circ
$$
$$
a_1 = 0
$$
$$
a_2 = \frac{3 \times 60}{4^2} = \frac{180}{16} = 11.25^\circ/\text{s}^2
$$
$$
a_3 = -\frac{2 \times 60}{4^3} = -\frac{120}{64} = -1.875^\circ/\text{s}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Trajectory equation:**

$$
\theta(t) = 15 + 11.25 t^2 - 1.875 t^3
$$

**Step 4 — Velocity and acceleration:**

$$
\dot{\theta}(t) = 22.5 t - 5.625 t^2
$$
$$
\ddot{\theta}(t) = 22.5 - 11.25 t
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate at $t = 2$ s:**

$$
\theta(2) = 15 + 11.25 (4) - 1.875 (8) = 15 + 45 - 15 = 45^\circ
$$

$$
\dot{\theta}(2) = 22.5 (2) - 5.625 (4) = 45 - 22.5 = 22.5^\circ/\text{s}
$$

$$
\ddot{\theta}(2) = 22.5 - 11.25 (2) = 22.5 - 22.5 = 0^\circ/\text{s}^2
$$

**Verification:** $\theta = 45^\circ$ is the midpoint (between 15 and 75), confirming the symmetric cubic profile. Acceleration is zero at midpoint, velocity is maximum.

#### Model Solution to A(b)

**Step 1 — Compute acceleration magnitude:**

$$
\alpha = \frac{\theta_f - \theta_0}{t_b \cdot t_f} = \frac{80 - 20}{2 \times 6} = \frac{60}{12} = 5^\circ/\text{s}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Cruise velocity:**

$$
v = \alpha t_b = 5 \times 2 = 10^\circ/\text{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Position at $t = 3$ s.** Since $t_b = 2$ s and $t_f = 6$ s, the linear region spans $2 \le t \le 4$ s. So $t = 3$ s is in Region II.

Position at end of Region I:

$$
\theta(t_b) = \theta_0 + \frac{1}{2}\alpha t_b^2 = 20 + 0.5 \times 5 \times 4 = 20 + 10 = 30^\circ
$$

Position in Region II:

$$
\theta(3) = 30 + v (3 - 2) = 30 + 10 \times 1 = 40^\circ
$$

**Final Answers:**
- $\alpha = 5^\circ/\text{s}^2$
- Cruise velocity $= 10^\circ/\text{s}$
- $\theta(3\text{ s}) = 40^\circ$

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]`**

**(a)** A 2-DOF robot arm must move from initial joint configuration $(20^\circ, 30^\circ)$ to final configuration $(80^\circ, 90^\circ)$ in $t_f = 5$ s, passing through the via point $(50^\circ, 60^\circ)$ at $t = 2.5$ s. Design a cubic-spline trajectory for **Joint 1** assuming zero velocity at start, at the via point, and at the end. Show the coefficient matrix setup. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare Cubic Polynomial Trajectory with Linear Trajectory with Parabolic Blends in terms of (i) continuity of acceleration, (ii) computational complexity, and (iii) suitability for pick-and-place operations. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to B(a)

**Step 1 — Identify segments.** There are two segments:

- **Segment 1:** $0 \le t \le 2.5$ s, from $\theta_0 = 20^\circ$ to $\theta_{via} = 50^\circ$
- **Segment 2:** $2.5 \le t \le 5$ s, from $\theta_{via} = 50^\circ$ to $\theta_f = 80^\circ$

**Step 2 — Apply boundary and continuity conditions.**

Segment 1 cubic: $\theta_1(t) = a_{10} + a_{11} t + a_{12} t^2 + a_{13} t^3$

- $\theta_1(0) = 20$ $\Rightarrow a_{10} = 20$
- $\dot{\theta}_1(0) = 0$ $\Rightarrow a_{11} = 0$
- $\theta_1(2.5) = 50$
- $\dot{\theta}_1(2.5) = 0$ (zero velocity at via point)

Segment 2 cubic: $\theta_2(t) = a_{20} + a_{21} t + a_{22} t^2 + a_{23} t^3$

- $\theta_2(2.5) = 50$
- $\dot{\theta}_2(2.5) = 0$ (continuity of velocity)
- $\theta_2(5) = 80$
- $\dot{\theta}_2(5) = 0$

**Step 3 — Compute Segment 1 coefficients using standard formula with $t_{f1} = 2.5$ s, $\Delta\theta_1 = 30^\circ$:**

$$
a_{12} = \frac{3 \times 30}{(2.5)^2} = \frac{90}{6.25} = 14.4^\circ/\text{s}^2
$$
$$
a_{13} = -\frac{2 \times 30}{(2.5)^3} = -\frac{60}{15.625} = -3.84^\circ/\text{s}^3
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Segment 2 coefficients with $t_{f2} = 2.5$ s, $\Delta\theta_2 = 30^\circ$:**

$$
a_{22} = \frac{3 \times 30}{6.25} = 14.4^\circ/\text{s}^2
$$
$$
a_{23} = -3.84^\circ/\text{s}^3
$$

Both segments are symmetric. The coefficient matrix setup for each segment is:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \theta_0 \\ 0 \\ \theta_f \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\
1 & t_f & t_f^2 & t_f^3 \\
0 & 1 & 2 t_f & 3 t_f^2
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} a_0 \\ a_1 \\ a_2 \\ a_3 \end{bmatrix}
$$

The **velocity at the via point** is continuous and zero, confirming smooth passage.

#### Model Solution to B(b)

| Criterion | Cubic Polynomial | Linear with Parabolic Blends |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Acceleration continuity** | Continuous (linear in time), no abrupt jumps | **Discontinuous** at the blend-to-linear transition (acceleration jumps from $+\alpha$ to $0$) |
| **(ii) Computational complexity** | Higher — must solve 4×4 system per joint per segment | Lower — closed-form formulas with single acceleration parameter |
| **(iii) Suitability for pick-and-place** | **Highly suitable** — smooth start/stop, no jerk discontinuities, ideal for precise placement | Suitable for *fast* pick-and-place where trapezoidal velocity is acceptable; not ideal when sub-mm precision is required |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing acceleration and velocity continuity.** Many students write "cubic polynomial has continuous velocity" but forget to state that it has *linear* acceleration and *zero jerk only at midpoints*.
> 2. **Forgetting to verify boundary conditions** in numerical answers. KTU examiners deduct 1–2 marks if the trajectory equation is not validated against the four BCs.
> 3. **Mixing up LTPB blend time and total time.** Always state clearly that $t_f = 2 t_b + t_c$ where $t_c$ is the linear segment duration; $t_c = 0$ in the "purely parabolic" special case.
> 4. **Inverse kinematics cost ignored.** In Cartesian-space planning, every via point requires an IK solve. In joint-space, no IK is needed. This is a frequent 3-mark KTU question.
> 5. **Units mismatch.** Mixing degrees with radians in velocity/acceleration expressions is a silent killer — always include the correct unit symbols (e.g., $^\circ/\text{s}$ or rad/s).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Trajectory planning** generates $\theta(t), \dot{\theta}(t), \ddot{\theta}(t)$ for every joint — it is *time-parameterized* motion, not just geometry.
- **Three task classes:** Pick-and-Place (only start/end matter), Continuous Path (path shape matters), Slew (joint synchronization matters).
- **Path vs. Trajectory:** Path = shape; Trajectory = shape + time-law. Same path can be traversed with infinite trajectories.
- **Joint-space** is computationally cheap and singularity-safe; **Cartesian-space** is geometrically intuitive but requires IK at every sample.
- **Cubic polynomial** is the *minimum-order* polynomial that satisfies 4 boundary conditions (position + zero-velocity at both ends). The four coefficients are:
  - $a_0 = \theta_0$, $a_1 = 0$, $a_2 = 3(\theta_f - \theta_0)/t_f^2$, $a_3 = -2(\theta_f - \theta_0)/t_f^3$
- The **acceleration of a cubic trajectory is a linear function of time**, crossing zero exactly at the midpoint.
- **LTPB (Linear with Parabolic Blends)** uses three regions: acceleration, cruise, deceleration. Acceleration is *piecewise constant*, not continuous — there is a jump at the blend boundaries.
- **LTPB key equations:** $\alpha = (\theta_f - \theta_0)/(t_b t_f)$, $v = \alpha t_b$, $t_f = 2 t_b + t_c$.
- **Via points** are handled by stitching multiple cubic polynomials, one per segment, with position and velocity continuity enforced at the via point.
- For $N$ via points, there are $N+1$ cubics, $4(N+1)$ unknowns, and $4N+2$ equations — the system is **under-determined by 2**, fixed by specifying accelerations at start and end (typically zero) — yielding the classical **cubic-spline trajectory**.
- **Industrial relevance:** Cubic = ABB/KUKA pick-and-place; LTPB = older CNC machines; Cubic-spline = modern collaborative robots (cobots) and on-line trajectory generation.
- **Examination tip:** For numerical problems, always *show* the four-step derivation (assume cubic → differentiate → apply BCs → solve linear system) — KTU examiners award method marks generously.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cartesian Space Planning, Point to Point vs Continuous Path Planning. Obstacle Avoidance Methods - Artificial Potential Field
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT302 - INTRODUCTION TO ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cartesian Space Planning, Point to Point vs Continuous Path Planning. Obstacle Avoidance Methods - Artificial Potential Field

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Cartesian Space Planning (CSP)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Cartesian Space Planning is a motion planning paradigm in which the robot's trajectory is specified directly in the **Cartesian (Task/World) coordinate frame** as a sequence of end-effector poses (position and orientation), which are then mapped to joint-space trajectories using **inverse kinematics** at discrete interpolation points.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction:** The two fundamental motion planning spaces in robotics are:
> - **Joint Space Planning:** Trajectory defined by $\theta(t)$ for each joint.
> - **Cartesian Space Planning:** Trajectory defined by $(x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi)$ for the end-effector.
> 
> A 3R planar manipulator has 3 DOF, but its end-effector pose in Cartesian space is parameterized by $(x, y, \phi)$ — 3 variables — so the mapping is locally one-to-one.

**Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition):**
Imagine you are driving a car from your home to a friend's house using a **GPS navigation app**:
- The **GPS waypoints** (latitude, longitude) are like the Cartesian waypoints — they describe *where* the car must be in the world.
- The car's **steering wheel angle, accelerator pedal, and brake** are like joint variables — they describe *how* the car gets there.
- **Cartesian Space Planning** is like planning using GPS waypoints: we say "be at point A, then B, then C in the world." The system then computes the steering/brake inputs (inverse kinematics) needed at each step.

---

### 1.2 Point-to-Point (PTP) vs. Continuous Path (CP) Planning

**Point-to-Point (PTP) Motion:**
> [!NOTE]
> **PTP Definition:** A motion control strategy in which the robot is commanded to move from an initial configuration to a final configuration, with **no explicit control over the path** taken between them. Only the start and end poses are specified; the intermediate trajectory is determined by the controller (often via joint interpolation or trapezoidal velocity profiles).

**Continuous Path (CP) Motion:**
> [!NOTE]
> **CP Definition:** A motion control strategy in which the robot must follow a **specified geometric path** (a continuous sequence of Cartesian or joint points) between the start and goal. The trajectory is pre-defined and the controller must track it precisely. Also called **Continuous Path Control (CPC)** or **Path Tracking Control**.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
- **PTP ≈ Shooting a basketball:** You only care about getting the ball *into the hoop*. The arc of the ball (the intermediate path) is uncontrolled — the system decides it.
- **CP ≈ Driving a car on a curved mountain road:** You must follow the road precisely. Every curve, every turn must be tracked exactly — the path itself is part of the requirement.

**Real-World Examples (Industrial Robotics):**

| Task Type | Strategy | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| Spot welding on car body | PTP | Only start/end points matter; speed is critical |
| Arc welding seam | CP | The torch must trace the exact seam geometry |
| Pick-and-place (palletizing) | PTP | Gripper only needs to reach the box position |
| Spray painting car door | CP | Nozzle must sweep a uniform, continuous trajectory |
| Laser cutting | CP | Beam must follow the part outline precisely |

---

### 1.3 Obstacle Avoidance & Artificial Potential Field (APF) — High-Level View

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Artificial Potential Field (APF) Definition (Khatib, 1986):** A reactive path planning technique in which the goal generates an **attractive potential** and obstacles generate **repulsive potentials**. The robot at configuration $\mathbf{q}$ experiences a total force $\mathbf{F}(\mathbf{q}) = -\nabla U(\mathbf{q})$, and moves along the negative gradient of the total potential field toward the goal while being pushed away from obstacles.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of the robot as a **marble rolling on a contoured surface**:
- The **goal** is a deep valley (lowest point) — gravity (attractive force) pulls the marble toward it.
- **Obstacles** are hills or bumps — they push the marble away.
- The marble naturally rolls downhill toward the goal, navigating around the hills.
- This is exactly how the **Artificial Potential Field** method works in configuration space!

**Standard Notation Used Throughout These Notes:**

| Symbol | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $\mathbf{q}$ | Robot configuration vector (joint angles) | rad or m |
| $\mathbf{x}$ | End-effector pose in Cartesian space | m, rad |
| $U_{att}(\mathbf{q})$ | Attractive potential | J (Joules) |
| $U_{rep}(\mathbf{q})$ | Repulsive potential | J (Joules) |
| $U(\mathbf{q})$ | Total potential | J (Joules) |
| $\mathbf{F}_{att}$ | Attractive force on robot | N |
| $\mathbf{F}_{rep}$ | Repulsive force on robot | N |
| $\mathbf{F}_{total}$ | Total force on robot | N |
| $\eta$ | Attractive gain (positive scalar) | N/m or unitless |
| $\xi$ | Repulsive gain (positive scalar) | N·m |
| $\rho_0$ | Distance of influence of obstacle | m |
| $\rho(\mathbf{q})$ | Distance from robot to nearest obstacle point | m |
| $\rho_{goal}$ | Distance from robot to goal | m |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Cartesian Space Planning — Detailed Theory

**Why Plan in Cartesian Space?**
Certain tasks are inherently defined in the world frame. For instance, an arc welding robot must move the welding torch *along a specific seam* in 3D world coordinates. The seam is naturally described in Cartesian space — it would be cumbersome (and usually impossible analytically) to describe it as a function of joint angles.

**The Four-Step Cartesian Planning Procedure:**

1. **Define waypoints in Cartesian space:** Specify a sequence of desired end-effector poses $\{\mathbf{x}_0, \mathbf{x}_1, \dots, \mathbf{x}_n\}$ where $\mathbf{x}_i = (x_i, y_i, z_i, \phi_i, \theta_i, \psi_i)$.

2. **Interpolate between waypoints:** Use a Cartesian interpolation scheme (linear, circular, or spline) to generate a dense trajectory in Cartesian space:
   $$\mathbf{x}(t) = \text{Interp}(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_{i+1}, t) \quad \text{for } t \in [t_i, t_{i+1}]$$

3. **Apply inverse kinematics at each interpolated point:** Compute the corresponding joint configuration:
   $$\mathbf{q}(t) = \text{IK}(\mathbf{x}(t))$$

4. **Send $\mathbf{q}(t)$ to the joint servo controllers:** The low-level motors track the joint-space trajectory.

**Key Challenges of Cartesian Space Planning:**

> [!WARNING]
> - **Inverse Kinematics (IK) singularities:** Near singular configurations, $\det(J(\mathbf{q})) = 0$, so joint velocities blow up. The robot may "lock up" or execute wild motions.
> - **Multiple IK solutions:** Redundant/non-redundant manipulators can have multiple valid $\mathbf{q}$ for the same $\mathbf{x}$. A selection rule is needed.
> - **Unreachable waypoints:** The Cartesian path may pass through poses outside the workspace. The interpolator cannot detect this; the IK step fails.
> - **Slower execution:** Computing IK at every interpolation step is computationally expensive compared to pure joint-space planning.

---

### 2.2 Joint Space vs. Cartesian Space Planning — Comparison

| Criterion | Joint Space Planning | Cartesian Space Planning |
|---|---|---|
| Trajectory specified in | $\mathbf{q}(t)$ | $\mathbf{x}(t)$ |
| Path shape in Cartesian space | Not controlled (often curved) | **Controlled** (line/arc/spline) |
| Requires IK? | **No** (only FK) | **Yes** (per interpolation step) |
| Computational cost | Low | High |
| Singularity issues | No direct singularity (kinematics is one-to-one) | Yes, IK singularities can cause issues |
| Suitability for PTP tasks | **Excellent** | Overkill |
| Suitability for CP tasks | Poor (path is unpredictable) | **Excellent** |
| Joint limits enforcement | Easy | Harder (must check $\mathbf{q}(t)$ against limits) |
| Typical use | Pick-and-place, PTP moves | Welding, painting, machining |

---

### 2.3 PTP vs. CP — Deep Technical Comparison

**PTP Control — How it works internally:**
- Robot is given start $\mathbf{q}_0$ and goal $\mathbf{q}_f$.
- Each joint is interpolated independently (often linear, cubic, or trapezoidal profile).
- All joints start/stop at the same time → synchronization.
- The Cartesian path is **uncontrolled** — a straight line in joint space is a curve in Cartesian space.

**CP Control — How it works internally:**
- Robot is given a dense sequence of Cartesian waypoints $\{\mathbf{x}_0, \mathbf{x}_1, \dots, \mathbf{x}_n\}$ (often very close together, e.g., 1 mm spacing).
- Linear or circular interpolation is used between consecutive waypoints.
- Inverse kinematics is solved at each waypoint.
- The Cartesian path is **precisely controlled**.

**Mathematical Form of CP — Linear Interpolation (LIN) with Parabolic Blends:**

Between two Cartesian waypoints $\mathbf{x}_i$ and $\mathbf{x}_{i+1}$ with segment duration $T$ and blend time $\tau$:

$$\mathbf{x}(t) = \mathbf{x}_i + \frac{1}{2}\left[\mathbf{x}_{i+1} - \mathbf{x}_i\right] \cdot \lambda(t)$$

where the scalar function $\lambda(t)$ transitions from $0$ at $t=0$ to $1$ at $t=T$ smoothly.

For **trapezoidal velocity profile** in CP:
- **Acceleration phase** ($0 \le t \le \tau$): $\lambda(t) = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{t}{\tau}\right)^2$
- **Constant velocity phase** ($\tau \le t \le T-\tau$): $\lambda(t) = \frac{t}{\tau} - \frac{1}{2}$
- **Deceleration phase** ($T-\tau \le t \le T$): $\lambda(t) = 1 - \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{T-t}{\tau}\right)^2$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favorite:** Whenever you discuss CP, mention the **parabolic blend (linear segments with parabolic transitions)** technique — it ensures **C¹ continuity** (continuous velocity) at the waypoints, which avoids jerky motion that could damage the robot or workpiece.

---

### 2.4 Obstacle Avoidance — Classification

Obstacle avoidance methods in robotics are broadly classified as:

1. **Global / Off-line methods** (full knowledge of environment before motion starts):
   - Configuration Space (C-space) Obstacles
   - Cell Decomposition
   - Roadmaps (Visibility graphs, Voronoi diagrams)
   - Probabilistic Roadmaps (PRM)
   - Rapidly-exploring Random Trees (RRT)

2. **Local / Reactive methods** (sensor-driven, on-line):
   - **Artificial Potential Field (APF)** — covered here
   - Vector Field Histogram (VFH)
   - Dynamic Window Approach (DWA)
   - Bug algorithms
   - Neural/fuzzy approaches

---

### 2.5 Artificial Potential Field (APF) — Detailed Theory

**The Core Idea:**
Construct a scalar potential function $U(\mathbf{q})$ over the configuration space (or Cartesian space) such that:
- It has a **global minimum at the goal**.
- It rises to **infinity (or large values) at obstacles**.
- The robot experiences a force $\mathbf{F} = -\nabla U$ that drives it toward the goal and away from obstacles.

The robot motion is governed by:
$$\dot{\mathbf{q}} = -\nabla U(\mathbf{q})$$

or in a second-order dynamic model:
$$M(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + C(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} = -\nabla U(\mathbf{q})$$

---

### 2.6 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains every key formula you must memorize for the APF section of Module 1 in PCBRT302.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Notes / Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Conic attractive potential | $U_{att}(\mathbf{q}) = \xi \cdot \rho_{goal}(\mathbf{q})$ | $\rho_{goal} = \lVert \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{goal} \rVert$ ; grows linearly — does **not** have a unique minimum at goal. |
| 2 | Quadratic attractive potential | $U_{att}(\mathbf{q}) = \frac{1}{2} \xi \cdot \rho_{goal}^2(\mathbf{q})$ | $\rho_{goal} = \lVert \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{goal} \rVert$ ; smooth, has a unique minimum at goal. **Most commonly used.** |
| 3 | Attractive force (conic) | $\mathbf{F}_{att}(\mathbf{q}) = -\nabla U_{att} = -\xi \cdot \frac{\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{goal}}{\rho_{goal}}$ | Constant magnitude, varying direction. |
| 4 | Attractive force (quadratic) | $\mathbf{F}_{att}(\mathbf{q}) = -\nabla U_{att} = -\xi (\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{goal})$ | Linear in distance; vanishes at goal. |
| 5 | Repulsive potential (Khatib) | $U_{rep}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{2}\eta\left(\frac{1}{\rho(\mathbf{q})} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^2 & \text{if } \rho(\mathbf{q}) \le \rho_0 \\ 0 & \text{if } \rho(\mathbf{q}) > \rho_0 \end{cases}$ | $\rho_0$ = distance of influence; $\eta$ = positive gain. |
| 6 | Repulsive force | $\mathbf{F}_{rep}(\mathbf{q}) = -\nabla U_{rep} = \begin{cases} \eta\left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)\frac{1}{\rho^2} \cdot \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial \mathbf{q}} & \rho \le \rho_0 \\ 0 & \rho > \rho_0 \end{cases}$ | Force points **away** from obstacle. |
| 7 | Total potential | $U(\mathbf{q}) = U_{att}(\mathbf{q}) + U_{rep}(\mathbf{q})$ | Sum of attractive and repulsive components. |
| 8 | Total force (robot motion law) | $\mathbf{F}_{total}(\mathbf{q}) = -\nabla U(\mathbf{q}) = \mathbf{F}_{att} + \mathbf{F}_{rep}$ | Negative gradient of total potential. |
| 9 | Distance to obstacle (point obstacle at $\mathbf{q}_{obs}$) | $\rho(\mathbf{q}) = \lVert \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{obs} \rVert$ | Euclidean distance. |
| 10 | Gradient of $\rho$ | $\nabla \rho = \frac{\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{obs}}{\rho}$ | Unit vector from obstacle to robot. |
| 11 | Repulsive force (substituting #10 into #6) | $\mathbf{F}_{rep} = \eta\left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)\frac{1}{\rho^2} \cdot \frac{\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{obs}}{\rho}$ | Useful explicit form for computation. |
| 12 | Workspace force on end-effector | $\mathbf{F}^* = J^{-T}(\mathbf{q}) \mathbf{F}_{total}(\mathbf{q})$ | Maps joint-space force to Cartesian force via Jacobian transpose. |
| 13 | Robot joint velocity command | $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = -\nabla U(\mathbf{q}) = \mathbf{F}_{att} + \mathbf{F}_{rep}$ | First-order kinematic steering law. |
| 14 | Stability condition (Lagrange) | $U(\mathbf{q}) \to \infty$ as $\lVert \mathbf{q} \rVert \to \infty$ (except at goal) | Prevents robot escaping to infinity. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Use:** The APF method is widely used in mobile robot navigation (Roomba-style vacuum cleaners), industrial manipulator collision avoidance, and real-time reactive control of UAVs. Its main advantages are **simplicity, real-time performance, and elegance**. Its main drawbacks are **local minima** and **oscillations in narrow passages** — discussed in SECTION 3.

---

### 2.7 Why APF Works — Physical Intuition

Consider a simple 2D configuration space with one obstacle and a goal:

- **At the goal:** $U_{att} = 0$, no obstacles nearby, $U = 0$ → stable equilibrium.
- **Far from goal, no obstacles:** $U_{att}$ dominates, gradient pulls robot toward goal.
- **Near an obstacle:** $U_{rep}$ spikes to large values near the obstacle surface, gradient pushes robot away.
- **In the "valley" of attraction but blocked by obstacle:** Robot rides the contour of $U_{rep}$ around the obstacle (like water flowing around a rock), then continues toward the goal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mathematical Fact:** If $U(\mathbf{q})$ has a **single global minimum** at the goal and **no other local minima**, the gradient descent $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = -\nabla U$ is guaranteed to converge to the goal. The presence of obstacles can, however, introduce **local minima** that trap the robot — a well-known limitation of APF.

## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation of the Quadratic Attractive Potential

**Starting premise:** We want a potential $U_{att}(\mathbf{q})$ whose gradient pulls the robot toward $\mathbf{q}_{goal}$ with a force proportional to the distance from the goal. Let $\rho_{goal} = \lVert \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{goal} \rVert$.

**Step 1:** Define the potential as a quadratic function of the distance:
$$U_{att}(\mathbf{q}) = \frac{1}{2}\xi\,\rho_{goal}^2(\mathbf{q})$$

**Step 2:** Expand $\rho_{goal}^2$:
$$\rho_{goal}^2 = (\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{goal})^T (\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{goal})$$

**Step 3:** Compute the gradient. Let $\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{goal}$ (the error vector). Then:
$$U_{att} = \frac{1}{2}\xi\,\mathbf{e}^T\mathbf{e}$$

**Step 4:** Apply the gradient rule $\nabla(\mathbf{e}^T\mathbf{e}) = 2\mathbf{e}$:
$$\nabla U_{att} = \frac{1}{2}\xi \cdot 2(\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{goal}) = \xi(\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{goal})$$

**Step 5:** The attractive force is the negative gradient:
$$\mathbf{F}_{att} = -\nabla U_{att} = -\xi(\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{goal}) = \xi(\mathbf{q}_{goal} - \mathbf{q})$$

**Interpretation:** The force is a **linear spring** with stiffness $\xi$ pulling the robot toward the goal. At the goal, $\mathbf{F}_{att} = 0$ — stable equilibrium.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Conic Attractive Potential

**Step 1:** Define the potential as a linear function of the distance:
$$U_{att}(\mathbf{q}) = \xi\,\rho_{goal}(\mathbf{q})$$

**Step 2:** Compute the gradient. We use $\nabla \rho_{goal} = \frac{\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{goal}}{\rho_{goal}}$:
$$\nabla U_{att} = \xi \cdot \frac{\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{goal}}{\rho_{goal}}$$

**Step 3:** The attractive force is the negative gradient:
$$\mathbf{F}_{att} = -\xi \cdot \frac{\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{goal}}{\rho_{goal}}$$

**Interpretation:** Force magnitude is **constant** ($=\xi$), but direction varies. Even very close to the goal, the robot feels a strong pull — useful for ensuring approach but can cause overshoot.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Repulsive Potential (Khatib's Form)

**Starting premise:** Define a region of influence of radius $\rho_0$ around each obstacle. Inside this region, the potential must grow sharply (toward infinity as $\rho \to 0$) so the robot never touches the obstacle. Outside, $U_{rep} = 0$.

**Step 1:** Define a candidate form. We want:
- $U_{rep} \to \infty$ as $\rho \to 0$
- $U_{rep} = 0$ at $\rho = \rho_0$
- $U_{rep}$ continuously differentiable

A natural choice is:
$$U_{rep}(\mathbf{q}) = \frac{1}{2}\eta\left(\frac{1}{\rho(\mathbf{q})} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^2 \quad \text{for } \rho \le \rho_0$$

**Step 2:** Verify boundary conditions. At $\rho = \rho_0$:
$$U_{rep} = \frac{1}{2}\eta\left(\frac{1}{\rho_0} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^2 = 0 \checkmark$$

As $\rho \to 0$:
$$U_{rep} \to \frac{1}{2}\eta \cdot \frac{1}{\rho^2} \to \infty \checkmark$$

**Step 3:** Compute the gradient. Let $f(\rho) = \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}$, so $U_{rep} = \frac{1}{2}\eta f^2$.
$$\frac{\partial U_{rep}}{\partial \rho} = \eta f(\rho) \cdot \frac{df}{d\rho} = \eta\left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)\left(-\frac{1}{\rho^2}\right)$$

$$\frac{\partial U_{rep}}{\partial \rho} = -\eta\left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)\frac{1}{\rho^2}$$

**Step 4:** Apply the chain rule using $\nabla \rho = \frac{\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{obs}}{\rho}$:
$$\nabla U_{rep} = \frac{\partial U_{rep}}{\partial \rho} \cdot \nabla \rho = -\eta\left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)\frac{1}{\rho^2} \cdot \frac{\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{obs}}{\rho}$$

**Step 5:** The repulsive force is the negative gradient:
$$\mathbf{F}_{rep} = -\nabla U_{rep} = \eta\left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)\frac{1}{\rho^2} \cdot \frac{\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{obs}}{\rho}$$

**Step 6:** Verify direction. The vector $(\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{obs})$ points **from obstacle to robot**. So $\mathbf{F}_{rep}$ points **from obstacle outward through the robot**, i.e., **away from the obstacle** ✓.

**Step 7:** Verify that $U_{rep} = 0$ outside the influence region. For $\rho > \rho_0$, we define $U_{rep} = 0$ explicitly. This makes the potential **continuous** (it goes to 0 at $\rho = \rho_0$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative Repulsive Potential Form (used in textbooks):** Some authors use $U_{rep} = \frac{1}{2}\eta \left(\frac{1}{\rho^2} - \frac{1}{\rho_0^2}\right)^2$ which gives different force scaling. KTU examiners typically accept either, but **always state the form you are using** explicitly.

---

### 3.4 Total Motion Equation

Combining the attractive and repulsive forces, the robot's first-order kinematic command is:
$$\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{F}_{total} = \mathbf{F}_{att} + \mathbf{F}_{rep}$$

For a 2R planar robot with configuration $\mathbf{q} = [q_1, q_2]^T$, goal at $\mathbf{q}_g = [q_{1g}, q_{2g}]^T$, and a single point obstacle at $\mathbf{q}_{obs} = [q_{1o}, q_{2o}]^T$:

$$\mathbf{F}_{att} = -\xi \begin{bmatrix} q_1 - q_{1g} \\ q_2 - q_{2g} \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{F}_{rep} = \begin{cases} \eta\left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)\frac{1}{\rho^3} \begin{bmatrix} q_1 - q_{1o} \\ q_2 - q_{2o} \end{bmatrix} & \rho \le \rho_0 \\ \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} & \rho > \rho_0 \end{cases}$$

where $\rho = \sqrt{(q_1 - q_{1o})^2 + (q_2 - q_{2o})^2}$.

---

### 3.5 Limitations of APF — Analytical Discussion

> [!WARNING]
> **Three Critical Limitations Every KTU Student Must Know:**

1. **Local Minima Problem:**
   The total potential $U(\mathbf{q}) = U_{att} + U_{rep}$ may have a **local minimum** somewhere in the configuration space, not at the goal. The robot stops there, thinking it has reached the goal. This is the **most severe limitation** of basic APF.

2. **Oscillations in Narrow Passages:**
   If the robot must pass between two closely-spaced obstacles, the repulsive forces from both sides cancel the goal-directed attractive force, and the robot can **oscillate** indefinitely.

3. **GNRON (Goal Non-Reachable with Obstacles Nearby):**
   If the goal is inside or adjacent to an obstacle's region of influence, the repulsive force at the goal is non-zero, so the goal is no longer a minimum. The robot may stabilize **near** the goal but not **at** it.

**Mathematical reason for local minima:**
For $\mathbf{q}^*$ to be a local minimum of $U$, we need $\nabla U(\mathbf{q}^*) = 0$ and $\nabla^2 U(\mathbf{q}^*) > 0$ (positive definite Hessian). In APF, the goal is a designed minimum, but the interaction of $U_{att}$ and $U_{rep}$ can create additional minima.

**Remedies (KTU Board Favorites):**

| Remedy | Description |
|---|---|
| **Random walk / Escape force** | When the robot stagnates (small $\lVert \mathbf{F}_{total} \rVert$), apply a small random perturbation. |
| **Virtual obstacle / Virtual goal** | Add a virtual obstacle or temporary goal to fill in the local minimum. |
| **Modified potential (e.g., Navigation Function)** | Use a navigation function (Koditschek-Rimon) that is provably free of local minima in a spherical world. |
| **Harmonic potential** | Solve Laplace's equation $\nabla^2 U = 0$ — guaranteed to have a single extremum. |
| **Hybrid methods** | Combine APF with global planners (e.g., A* on a grid + APF for local refinement). |

---

### 3.6 Analytical Worked Example — 1D APF

**Problem:** A 1D point robot at $q = 2$ must reach goal at $q_g = 8$. There is a point obstacle at $q_{obs} = 5$ with $\rho_0 = 2$. Use $\xi = 1$, $\eta = 10$. Find the force at $q = 4.5$.

**Step 1:** Compute distance to goal:
$$\rho_{goal} = \lvert 4.5 - 8 \rvert = 3.5$$

**Step 2:** Compute distance to obstacle:
$$\rho = \lvert 4.5 - 5 \rvert = 0.5$$

**Step 3:** Check whether obstacle is within influence:
$$\rho = 0.5 \le \rho_0 = 2 \Rightarrow U_{rep} \neq 0$$

**Step 4:** Compute attractive force (quadratic form):
$$F_{att} = -\xi(q - q_g) = -1 \times (4.5 - 8) = +3.5 \text{ N (toward goal)}$$

**Step 5:** Compute repulsive force:
$$F_{rep} = \eta\left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)\frac{1}{\rho^2}$$
$$F_{rep} = 10 \times \left(\frac{1}{0.5} - \frac{1}{2}\right) \times \frac{1}{0.25} = 10 \times (2 - 0.5) \times 4 = 10 \times 1.5 \times 4 = 60 \text{ N}$$

**Step 6:** Apply sign — repulsive force points from obstacle to robot, so at $q=4.5$ (left of obstacle at $q=5$):
$$F_{rep} = -60 \text{ N (pushes robot left, away from obstacle)}$$

**Step 7:** Total force:
$$F_{total} = F_{att} + F_{rep} = 3.5 + (-60) = -56.5 \text{ N}$$

**Interpretation:** The robot is pushed strongly to the left (away from the obstacle), dominating the goal-pulling force. The robot must first move past the obstacle, then approach the goal.

---

### 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Choosing APF Parameters

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Heuristics for Tuning APF:**

| Parameter | Effect if Increased | Effect if Decreased | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\xi$ (attractive gain) | Robot moves faster toward goal, but may overshoot | Slower convergence, smoother motion | $0.1$ to $5$ |
| $\eta$ (repulsive gain) | Stronger obstacle avoidance, but path detours are larger | Weaker obstacle avoidance, robot may graze obstacles | $1$ to $100$ |
| $\rho_0$ (influence radius) | Obstacle repulsion acts farther away; smoother paths | Obstacle repulsion only when very close; riskier | $0.5$ m to $5$ m |
| Time step $\Delta t$ | Faster simulation, less accurate | Slower, more accurate | $0.01$ s to $0.1$ s |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake in KTU Exams:** Students often forget to set $U_{rep} = 0$ **outside** the influence region. Always specify this piecewise condition in your answer. A potential that is non-zero everywhere causes the robot to feel repulsion from faraway obstacles — physically wrong and inefficient.

## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Cartesian Space Planning Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Task Specification in World Frame] --> B[Cartesian Waypoint Sequence x0, x1, ..., xn]
    B --> C[Cartesian Interpolation Module]
    C --> D{Dense Cartesian Trajectory x of t}
    D --> E[Inverse Kinematics Solver IK]
    E --> F{Valid Joint Configurations q of t}
    F --> G[Joint Servo Controllers]
    G --> H[Robot Actuators Move]
    H --> I[End Effector Moves in World]
    I --> J[Forward Kinematics Feedback FK]
    J --> K[Trajectory Tracking Error]
    K --> L[Controller Adjusts Motor Torques]
    L --> G
    F --> M{Singularity or Joint Limit Detected}
    M -->|Yes| N[Re plan or Re Interpolate]
    N --> C
    M -->|No| G
```

**Explanation of Blocks:**
- **Cartesian Waypoint Sequence:** The user specifies the desired path in world coordinates.
- **Cartesian Interpolation Module:** Generates a dense (often thousands of points) trajectory by interpolating between waypoints (linear/circular/spline).
- **Inverse Kinematics Solver:** Converts each Cartesian point to a joint configuration. The Jacobian-based iterative solver or closed-form solution is used.
- **Singularity/Joint Limit Check:** Validates that $\mathbf{q}(t)$ lies within joint limits and avoids kinematic singularities. If violated, re-planning is triggered.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Artificial Potential Field Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Sensor Input: Robot Configuration q] --> P1[Compute Distance to Goal rho_goal]
    S1 --> P2[Compute Distance to Obstacle rho]
    P1 --> A1[Attractive Potential U_att of q]
    P2 --> R1[Repulsive Potential U_rep of q]
    A1 --> SUM[Total Potential U of q]
    R1 --> SUM
    SUM --> GRAD[Compute Negative Gradient of U]
    GRAD --> F[Total Force F_total]
    F --> M[Motion Law q dot = F_total]
    M --> ACT[Actuator Command]
    ACT --> S1
    P2 --> CHK{rho less than rho_0}
    CHK -->|Yes| R1
    CHK -->|No| Z[Set U_rep = 0]
    Z --> SUM
```

---

### 4.3 Conceptual Mermaid — PTP vs CP Planning Comparison

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Start Pose] --> B[Goal Pose]
    B --> C{Planning Strategy}
    C -->|PTP| D[Joint Space Interpolation]
    C -->|CP| E[Cartesian Space Interpolation]
    D --> F[Independent Joint Trajectories]
    E --> G[Dense Cartesian Waypoints]
    G --> H[Inverse Kinematics at each point]
    F --> I[Cartesian Path Uncontrolled]
    H --> J[Cartesian Path Precisely Tracked]
    I --> K[Fast, Simple, No IK]
    J --> L[Used for Welding, Painting, Machining]
```

---

### 4.4 Schematic — APF Field in 2D Workspace

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G((Goal)) -->|U_att pulls| R[Robot Position]
    O1((Obstacle 1)) -->|U_rep pushes| R
    O2((Obstacle 2)) -->|U_rep pushes| R
    R -->|Moves along F_total| N[New Position]
    N -->|Repeat| R
```

**Conceptual Picture (described in text since Mermaid cannot draw 2D scalar fields):**

In a 2D workspace with one obstacle and one goal, the equipotential contours of $U(\mathbf{q})$ look like:
- Around the goal: **concentric circles** (lowest value at the center).
- Around the obstacle: **steep dome** (infinite at the center, zero at radius $\rho_0$).
- The robot rolls "downhill" along the negative gradient, curving around the obstacle's dome.

```
        Obstacle (high U)
           ___
          /   \         
         |  *  |       ← U_rep very large
          \___/
              \        
               \  Robot path (curves around)
                \      
                 v     
   __________  ____________  ___________
              \             \
               \             \____
                \                  \____
                 \                       \____
                  v                            \____
                 Goal (U = 0)                         * (Robot)
```

---

### 4.5 Local Minima — A Visual Warning

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start Position] --> M1{Total Force Non Zero}
    M1 -->|Yes| M2[Move Down Gradient]
    M1 -->|No| M3[Robot Stops]
    M2 --> M4{Force Magnitude Near Zero}
    M4 -->|No| M2
    M4 -->|Yes| M5{Is this the Goal}
    M5 -->|Yes| M6[Success]
    M5 -->|No| M7[Local Minimum Trap]
    M7 --> M8[Apply Random Perturbation]
    M8 --> M2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This diagram illustrates the **local minimum problem** explicitly. The robot's gradient descent can converge to a non-goal critical point. Real APF implementations must detect stagnation (small force over many iterations) and apply an escape strategy.
