<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 2 — Relation Between Joint and End Effector Velocities

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In robotics kinematics, the **Jacobian Matrix** is the fundamental mathematical tool that establishes a linear mapping between the **joint-space velocities** ($\dot{\mathbf{q}}$) of a manipulator and the **end-effector linear and angular velocities** ($\mathbf{v}$ and $\boldsymbol{\omega}$) in Cartesian (task) space. The compact vector-matrix relationship is expressed as:

$$
\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) \, \dot{\mathbf{q}}
$$

where $\mathbf{V} \in \mathbb{R}^{6 \times 1}$ is the **end-effector velocity vector** (a stacking of linear and angular velocity), $\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) \in \mathbb{R}^{6 \times n}$ is the **configuration-dependent Jacobian matrix** (a function of the current joint positions $\mathbf{q}$), $\mathbf{q} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times 1}$ is the **vector of generalized joint coordinates**, and $\dot{\mathbf{q}} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times 1}$ is the **vector of joint velocities** ($n$ = number of joints).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic is the kinematic bridge between the *position-level forward kinematics* (covered earlier in Module 2) and the *force/torque-level statics* (Kinetostatics). The Jacobian is the central operator that converts joint motions into task-space motions and, by duality (principle of virtual work), joint torques into end-effector forces.

> [!NOTE]
> **Terminology Note:** The Jacobian is *not* a constant matrix — its entries are functions of the current joint configuration $\mathbf{q}$. This is why the mapping is a **locally linear** one, valid instantaneously.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🦾

Imagine you are **steering a car**:
- The **steering wheel** (small rotation, small input) corresponds to the **joint velocities** $\dot{\mathbf{q}}$.
- The **car's actual direction and speed** (the output motion of the vehicle) correspond to the **end-effector velocity** $\mathbf{V}$.
- The **steering linkage, gear ratio, and wheel orientation** together act like a **Jacobian matrix** — they mathematically "convert" the small hand rotation into the much larger vehicle motion, *and* the conversion factor changes depending on whether you are turning at low speed, high speed, or in reverse.

Similarly, in a robotic arm, even if every joint moves at the same speed, the end-effector's actual speed in 3D space changes depending on the **arm's pose** (folded, stretched, sideways). The Jacobian is the "translation table" that gives the exact conversion ratio at any instant.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Linear velocity** is measured in **meters per second (m/s)**.
- **Angular velocity** is measured in **radians per second (rad/s)**.
- **Dimensionality of the Jacobian:** $\mathbf{J} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$, where $m = 6$ for a general 6D spatial manipulator (3 linear + 3 angular) and $n$ is the number of joints.
- **Singular value metric:** $\sigma_{\min}(\mathbf{J})$ indicates proximity to a singular configuration.

---

### 1.4 Why the Jacobian Matters — The Big Picture

| Aspect | Role of the Jacobian |
|---|---|
| **Velocity Mapping** | $\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{J}\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ — instantaneous forward mapping |
| **Static Force Mapping** | $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^{T}\mathbf{F}$ — joint torques from wrenches |
| **Singularity Detection** | $\det(\mathbf{J}) = 0$ or $\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{T}$ rank-deficient |
| **Inverse Velocity Kinematics** | $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{-1}\mathbf{V}$ (when $\mathbf{J}$ is square & invertible) |
| **Manipulability** | Measure of dexterity: $w = \sqrt{\det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{T})}$ |
| **Trajectory Planning** | Converts Cartesian paths to joint paths in real time |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposing the End-Effector Velocity

The end-effector velocity is a **6-dimensional spatial velocity** consisting of two distinct physical quantities:

$$
\mathbf{V} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{v} \\ \boldsymbol{\omega} \end{bmatrix}_{6 \times 1}
$$

where:
- $\mathbf{v} \in \mathbb{R}^{3}$ = **linear (translational) velocity** of the end-effector origin (m/s).
- $\boldsymbol{\omega} \in \mathbb{R}^{3}$ = **angular velocity** of the end-effector frame (rad/s).

Correspondingly, the Jacobian partitions as:

$$
\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{J}_v \\ \mathbf{J}_\omega \end{bmatrix}_{6 \times n}
$$

where $\mathbf{J}_v \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times n}$ is the **linear-velocity Jacobian** and $\mathbf{J}_\omega \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times n}$ is the **angular-velocity Jacobian**.

---

## 2.2 The Velocity Propagation Principle

The end-effector velocity is the **superposition** of the velocity contributions from every individual joint. The **i-th column** of the Jacobian is precisely the end-effector velocity produced when **only joint i moves with unit velocity**, with all other joints held stationary.

### 2.2.1 Revolute Joint Contribution (Joint Axis $\mathbf{z}_{i-1}$)

For a **revolute joint** rotating about axis $\mathbf{z}_{i-1}$ (the axis fixed in frame $\{i-1\}$), the i-th column of the Jacobian is:

$$
\mathbf{J}_i = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{z}_{i-1} \times {}^{i-1}\mathbf{p}_{n}^{i-1} \\ \mathbf{z}_{i-1} \end{bmatrix}
$$

- The **linear part** $\mathbf{z}_{i-1} \times {}^{i-1}\mathbf{p}_{n}^{i-1}$ is the **moment arm** — cross product of the rotation axis with the vector from joint $i$ to the end-effector origin.
- The **angular part** $\mathbf{z}_{i-1}$ is simply the unit rotation axis.

### 2.2.2 Prismatic Joint Contribution (Joint Axis $\mathbf{z}_{i-1}$)

For a **prismatic (sliding) joint** translating along axis $\mathbf{z}_{i-1}$:

$$
\mathbf{J}_i = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{z}_{i-1} \\ \mathbf{0} \end{bmatrix}
$$

- The **linear part** is the unit translation axis.
- The **angular part** is zero — pure translation produces no angular velocity.

---

## 2.3 Direct Differential Kinematics (Position-Derivative Method)

The Jacobian can also be obtained by **differentiating the forward kinematic position and orientation equations** with respect to the joint variables:

$$
\mathbf{x}(t) = \boldsymbol{\Phi}(\mathbf{q}(t)) \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) = \frac{\partial \boldsymbol{\Phi}}{\partial \mathbf{q}} \dot{\mathbf{q}}(t) = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) \, \dot{\mathbf{q}}
$$

This method is straightforward for position-only mappings but cumbersome for orientation (e.g., Euler angles, quaternions).

---

## 2.4 Inverse Velocity Kinematics

If $\mathbf{J}$ is **square ($6 \times 6$) and non-singular**, we can invert the mapping:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{-1}(\mathbf{q}) \, \mathbf{V}
$$

For non-square Jacobians, the **Moore-Penrose pseudoinverse** is used:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{+}(\mathbf{q}) \, \mathbf{V} = \mathbf{J}^{T}(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{T})^{-1} \, \mathbf{V}
$$

The pseudoinverse gives the **minimum-norm joint velocity** that achieves a desired end-effector velocity.

---

## 2.5 Singular Configurations

A **singularity** occurs when the Jacobian loses rank — i.e., the manipulator momentarily loses one or more degrees of freedom in Cartesian space. Mathematically:

$$
\det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{T}) = 0 \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \text{rank}(\mathbf{J}) < \min(m, n)
$$

There are two canonical types:

1. **Boundary Singularities:** Occur when the manipulator is **fully stretched out** or **fully folded back**. The end-effector cannot move further along a line — infinite joint velocities would be required.
2. **Interior Singularities:** Occur **inside the workspace** due to alignment of joint axes (e.g., when two wrist joints of a 6-DOF arm align, losing one rotational DOF).

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet 📋

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for KTU examination preparation on this topic. Memorize all entries.

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Dimension | Notes |
|:---:|---|---|:---:|---|
| 1 | Master velocity equation | $\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ | $\mathbf{V} \in \mathbb{R}^{6}$, $\mathbf{J} \in \mathbb{R}^{6 \times n}$, $\dot{\mathbf{q}} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ | Linear, instantaneous |
| 2 | Spatial velocity | $\mathbf{V} = [\mathbf{v}^{T}, \boldsymbol{\omega}^{T}]^{T}$ | m/s, rad/s | 3 linear + 3 angular |
| 3 | Revolute column | $\mathbf{J}_i = [\mathbf{z}_{i-1} \times {}^{i-1}\mathbf{p}_{n}^{i-1}; \mathbf{z}_{i-1}]$ | $\mathbb{R}^{6}$ | Cross product = moment arm |
| 4 | Prismatic column | $\mathbf{J}_i = [\mathbf{z}_{i-1}; \mathbf{0}]$ | $\mathbb{R}^{6}$ | Pure linear contribution |
| 5 | Differential from FK | $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \frac{\partial \boldsymbol{\Phi}}{\partial \mathbf{q}}\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ | – | Valid for all representations |
| 6 | Inverse velocity (square) | $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{-1}\mathbf{V}$ | – | Requires $\det(\mathbf{J}) \neq 0$ |
| 7 | Inverse velocity (general) | $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{T}(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{T})^{-1}\mathbf{V}$ | – | Pseudoinverse, min-norm |
| 8 | Singularity condition | $\det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{T}) = 0$ | scalar | Rank loss |
| 9 | Static force duality | $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^{T}\mathbf{F}$ | N·m | Virtual work principle |
| 10 | Manipulability index | $w = \sqrt{\det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{T})}$ | – | $w = 0$ at singularity |
| 11 | Jacobian rate | $\dot{\mathbf{J}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{\partial \mathbf{J}}{\partial q_i} \dot{q}_i$ | $\mathbb{R}^{6 \times n}$ | Required for acceleration analysis |
| 12 | Cartesian acceleration | $\ddot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{J}\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \dot{\mathbf{J}}\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ | – | First-order kinematic |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Utility of the Velocity Jacobian |
|---|---|
| **Industrial Welding Robots** | Convert smooth Cartesian weld-seam speeds into coordinated joint servo commands in real time |
| **Surgical Robotics (Da Vinci)** | Map tiny master-controller motions to scaled, tremor-free slave instrument motions |
| **Space Manipulators (Canadarm)** | Inverse-velocity Jacobian used for capturing tumbling satellites with smooth tracking |
| **Humanoid Walking Robots** | Use manipulability index to detect near-singular leg postures and replan steps |
| **Haptic Devices** | Transduce end-effector force into joint torques via $\mathbf{J}^{T}$ for realistic force feedback |
| **CNC Machining** | Tangential velocity control using Jacobian-derived feed-rate mapping |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Velocity Mapping from Rigid-Body Kinematics

We begin with the standard **forward kinematics** mapping for an $n$-joint serial chain:

$$
{}^{0}\mathbf{T}_{n}(\mathbf{q}) = {}^{0}\mathbf{T}_{1}(q_1) \cdot {}^{1}\mathbf{T}_{2}(q_2) \cdots {}^{n-1}\mathbf{T}_{n}(q_n)
$$

The end-effector pose is encoded in the homogeneous transformation matrix. The position of the end-effector is given by the first three elements of the last column, and the orientation is encoded in the $3 \times 3$ rotation matrix ${}^{0}\mathbf{R}_{n}$.

Let us denote the end-effector position vector (in the base frame) and a suitable orientation parameterization as:

$$
\mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} {}^{0}\mathbf{p}_{n} \\ \boldsymbol{\theta} \end{bmatrix}
$$

where $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ is a 3-vector of orientation parameters (e.g., Z-Y-X Euler angles). Differentiating both sides with respect to time:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \begin{bmatrix} {}^{0}\dot{\mathbf{p}}_{n} \\ \dot{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \end{bmatrix}
$$

The first three components (linear velocity) can be obtained by differentiating the position kinematics. The angular velocity, however, is most cleanly derived using the **propagation formula** that follows.

### Linear Velocity of a Point on a Rigid Body

For a point $P$ fixed in a frame $\{i\}$ undergoing angular velocity $\boldsymbol{\omega}_i$ and linear velocity $\mathbf{v}_i$ (both expressed in the base frame), the velocity of the origin of $\{i\}$ in the base frame is:

$$
\mathbf{v}_i = \mathbf{v}_{i-1} + \boldsymbol{\omega}_{i-1} \times {}^{0}\mathbf{p}_{i-1,i}
$$

where ${}^{0}\mathbf{p}_{i-1,i}$ is the vector from the origin of $\{i-1\}$ to the origin of $\{i\}$, expressed in the base frame.

### Angular Velocity Propagation

The angular velocity of frame $\{i\}$ relative to the base frame is the sum of the angular velocity already accumulated up to $\{i-1\}$ and the contribution of joint $i$:

$$
\boldsymbol{\omega}_i = \boldsymbol{\omega}_{i-1} + \dot{q}_i \, \mathbf{z}_{i-1}
$$

For a **revolute** joint, the joint motion contributes an angular velocity $\dot{q}_i \mathbf{z}_{i-1}$ along the joint axis.
For a **prismatic** joint, $\dot{q}_i \mathbf{z}_{i-1} = \mathbf{0}$ in the angular part, but the linear part picks up $\dot{q}_i \mathbf{z}_{i-1}$.

### Building the Column-by-Column Jacobian

Applying these propagation rules **from joint 1 to joint $n$**, with all other joints held fixed, we can write the i-th column of the Jacobian as the end-effector velocity produced by a unit velocity $\dot{q}_i = 1$ at joint $i$:

$$
\mathbf{J}_i = \begin{cases}
\begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{z}_{i-1} \times {}^{i-1}\mathbf{p}_{n}^{i-1} \\ \mathbf{z}_{i-1} \end{bmatrix} & \text{(revolute joint)} \\[2mm]
\begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{z}_{i-1} \\ \mathbf{0} \end{bmatrix} & \text{(prismatic joint)}
\end{cases}
$$

This is the foundational result. The rest of this section demonstrates it in detail for a 2-DOF planar manipulator.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Worked Example: 2-Link Planar Manipulator (RR)

### Setup

Consider a 2-link planar manipulator (RR — both joints revolute) operating in the X-Y plane. The link lengths are $L_1$ and $L_2$, and the joint angles are $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$. The base frame $\{0\}$ has its $z$-axis pointing out of the plane (right-hand rule).

### Step 1 — Forward Kinematics (Position)

The end-effector position $\mathbf{p} = [x, y]^{T}$ in the base frame is:

$$
x = L_1 \cos(\theta_1) + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
$$

$$
y = L_1 \sin(\theta_1) + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
$$

We can verify this using the DH-convention or direct geometric reasoning. Both links rotate in the plane; the first link makes angle $\theta_1$ with the X-axis, the second link makes angle $\theta_1 + \theta_2$ with the X-axis.

### Step 2 — Differentiate Position to Get Linear Velocity

Differentiating $x$ with respect to time using the chain rule:

$$
\dot{x} = -L_1 \sin(\theta_1) \dot{\theta}_1 - L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)(\dot{\theta}_1 + \dot{\theta}_2)
$$

Grouping terms by $\dot{\theta}_1$ and $\dot{\theta}_2$:

$$
\dot{x} = \left[-L_1 \sin(\theta_1) - L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)\right] \dot{\theta}_1 + \left[-L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)\right] \dot{\theta}_2
$$

Similarly for $y$:

$$
\dot{y} = L_1 \cos(\theta_1) \dot{\theta}_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)(\dot{\theta}_1 + \dot{\theta}_2)
$$

Grouping:

$$
\dot{y} = \left[L_1 \cos(\theta_1) + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)\right] \dot{\theta}_1 + \left[L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)\right] \dot{\theta}_2
$$

### Step 3 — Stack into Matrix Form

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1 \sin(\theta_1) - L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) & -L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\ L_1 \cos(\theta_1) + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) & L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \dot{\theta}_1 \\ \dot{\theta}_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Therefore, the **planar (2D) Jacobian** is:

$$
\mathbf{J} = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1 \sin(\theta_1) - L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) & -L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\ L_1 \cos(\theta_1) + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) & L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 4 — Verify with the Cross-Product Formula

For a planar RR manipulator, the joint axes are both along the $z$-axis (out of the plane): $\mathbf{z}_0 = \mathbf{z}_1 = [0, 0, 1]^{T}$. The position of the end-effector relative to joint 1 (in frame $\{1\}$) is $[L_2, 0, 0]^{T}$, and the position of joint 1 relative to the base is $[L_1 \cos\theta_1, L_1 \sin\theta_1, 0]^{T}$.

The cross-product formula gives (for joint 1, revolute):

$$
\mathbf{J}_{v,1} = \mathbf{z}_0 \times {}^{0}\mathbf{p}_{2}^{0} = [0,0,1]^{T} \times [L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2), \ L_1\sin\theta_1 + L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2), \ 0]^{T}
$$

Using the cross-product identity $[0,0,1] \times [a,b,0] = [-b, a, 0]$:

$$
\mathbf{J}_{v,1} = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1 \sin\theta_1 - L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\ L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

This matches the first column of $\mathbf{J}$. Repeating the procedure for joint 2 gives the second column. **Verification complete.** ✅

### Step 5 — Angular Velocity (Planar = Scalar)

For a planar manipulator, the angular velocity of the end-effector is purely about the $z$-axis:

$$
\omega_z = \dot{\theta}_1 + \dot{\theta}_2
$$

In matrix form:

$$
\omega_z = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \dot{\theta}_1 \\ \dot{\theta}_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Hence the **angular part** of the Jacobian is $\mathbf{J}_\omega = [1 \ 1]$.

### Step 6 — Full 3×2 Spatial Jacobian

Stacking linear and angular parts:

$$
\mathbf{J}(\theta_1, \theta_2) = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1 \sin\theta_1 - L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) & -L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\ L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) & L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

This is a $3 \times 2$ Jacobian: 2 joints, planar motion gives 3 velocity components (2 linear + 1 angular).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Singular Configurations of the 2-Link Planar Arm

The Jacobian is singular when its rank drops below 2. Since it is a $2 \times 2$ matrix (ignoring the trivial angular row), the determinant must vanish.

### Compute the Determinant

$$
\det(\mathbf{J}) = \left(-L_1 \sin\theta_1 - L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)\right) \cdot L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) - \left(-L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)\right) \cdot \left(L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)\right)
$$

Expanding term-by-term:

$$
\det(\mathbf{J}) = -L_1 L_2 \sin\theta_1 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) - L_2^2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) + L_1 L_2 \cos\theta_1 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) + L_2^2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
$$

The two $L_2^2$ terms **cancel** exactly. The remaining expression:

$$
\det(\mathbf{J}) = L_1 L_2 \left[\cos\theta_1 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) - \sin\theta_1 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)\right]
$$

Apply the trigonometric identity $\sin(A - B) = \sin A \cos B - \cos A \sin B$:

$$
\det(\mathbf{J}) = L_1 L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2 - \theta_1) = L_1 L_2 \sin(\theta_2)
$$

### Singularity Condition

$$
\det(\mathbf{J}) = L_1 L_2 \sin(\theta_2) = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \theta_2 = 0 \ \text{or} \ \theta_2 = \pi
$$

| Configuration | $\theta_2$ | Geometric Meaning | Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Fully Stretched** | $\theta_2 = 0$ | Arm is straight, end-effector at maximum reach | Cannot move further outward (boundary singularity) |
| **Fully Folded** | $\theta_2 = \pi$ | Arm is doubled back on itself, end-effector closest to base | Cannot move further inward (boundary singularity) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Notice that $\det(\mathbf{J})$ depends **only on $\theta_2$**, not on $\theta_1$. This is because rotating the entire arm about the base (changing $\theta_1$) does not change the *local geometry* of the linkage; only the *inter-link angle* $\theta_2$ controls singularity.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Numerical Example

Let $L_1 = 1$ m, $L_2 = 0.5$ m, $\theta_1 = 30°$, $\theta_2 = 45°$.

Compute the Jacobian entries (in SI units, angles in radians):

$$
\theta_1 = \pi/6 \approx 0.5236 \text{ rad}, \quad \theta_1 + \theta_2 = 75° = 5\pi/12 \approx 1.3090 \text{ rad}
$$

$$
\sin(30°) = 0.5000, \quad \cos(30°) = 0.8660
$$

$$
\sin(75°) \approx 0.9659, \quad \cos(75°) \approx 0.2588
$$

Substituting:

$$
\mathbf{J} = \begin{bmatrix} -(1)(0.5) - (0.5)(0.9659) & -(0.5)(0.9659) \\ (1)(0.8660) + (0.5)(0.2588) & (0.5)(0.2588) \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\mathbf{J} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.5 - 0.4830 & -0.4830 \\ 0.8660 + 0.1294 & 0.1294 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.9830 & -0.4830 \\ 0.9954 & 0.1294 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Determinant check:

$$
\det(\mathbf{J}) = (-0.9830)(0.1294) - (-0.4830)(0.9954) = -0.1272 + 0.4808 = 0.3536
$$

Compare with the closed form $L_1 L_2 \sin(\theta_2) = (1)(0.5) \sin(45°) = 0.5 \times 0.7071 = 0.3536$ ✅

If the desired end-effector velocity is $\mathbf{V} = [0.1, 0.2]^{T}$ m/s, the required joint velocities are:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{-1} \mathbf{V} = \frac{1}{0.3536} \begin{bmatrix} 0.1294 & 0.4830 \\ -0.9954 & -0.9830 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0.2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Computing the numerator matrix-vector product:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 0.1294 \times 0.1 + 0.4830 \times 0.2 \\ -0.9954 \times 0.1 - 0.9830 \times 0.2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.01294 + 0.09660 \\ -0.09954 - 0.19660 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.10954 \\ -0.29614 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \frac{1}{0.3536} \begin{bmatrix} 0.10954 \\ -0.29614 \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.3098 \\ -0.8376 \end{bmatrix} \text{ rad/s}
$$

The **negative** $\dot{\theta}_2$ is geometrically sensible: to move the end-effector in the +y direction from this configuration, the elbow must fold slightly inward.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Velocity Transformation Pipeline

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the **flow of velocity information** from the joint servo level to the Cartesian task space, and the inverse flow for commanded trajectories.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["JOINT SPACE INPUT qdot"]:::input
    nodeB["JACOBIAN MATRIX J of q"]:::process
    nodeC["END EFFECTOR VELOCITY V"]:::output
    nodeD["SINGULARITY CHECK det J"]:::check
    nodeE["TASK SPACE COMMAND V desired"]:::input
    nodeF["PSEUDOINVERSE J plus"]:::process
    nodeG["JOINT VELOCITY COMMAND qdot"]:::output
    nodeH["FORCE F at end effector"]:::input
    nodeI["JACOBIAN TRANSPOSE J transpose"]:::process
    nodeJ["JOINT TORQUE TAU"]:::output

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeB --> nodeD
    nodeD -- if nonsingular --> nodeF
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ

    classDef input fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F77B4,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#FF7F0E,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#E8F8E8,stroke:#2CA02C,color:#000
    classDef check fill:#FDECEA,stroke:#D62728,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Top branch (forward velocity):** Joint velocities → $\mathbf{J}$ → end-effector velocity.
- **Middle branch (inverse velocity):** Desired Cartesian velocity → $\mathbf{J}^{+}$ (pseudoinverse, gated by singularity check) → joint velocity commands.
- **Bottom branch (static force duality):** End-effector wrench → $\mathbf{J}^{T}$ → joint torques (this is the virtual-work counterpart).

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Detailed Column-by-Column Jacobian Assembly

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart["INITIALIZE i EQUALS 1 to n"]:::process
    nodeJoint["READ JOINT TYPE revolute or prismatic"]:::input
    nodeAxis["EXTRACT z sub i minus 1 from frame i minus 1"]:::process
    nodePos["EXTRACT p sub n from i minus 1 to n"]:::process
    nodeRevCheck{"JOINT IS REVOLUTE"}:::check
    nodeRevCalc["J sub i EQUALS z cross p ; z"]:::process
    nodePriCalc["J sub i EQUALS z ; 0"]:::process
    nodeStack["APPEND COLUMN J sub i TO J"]:::process
    nodeInc["i EQUALS i plus 1"]:::process
    nodeEnd["RETURN FULL JACOBIAN J"]:::output

    nodeStart --> nodeJoint
    nodeJoint --> nodeAxis
    nodeJoint --> nodePos
    nodeAxis --> nodeRevCheck
    nodePos --> nodeRevCheck
    nodeRevCheck -- YES --> nodeRevCalc
    nodeRevCheck -- NO --> nodePriCalc
    nodeRevCalc --> nodeStack
    nodePriCalc --> nodeStack
    nodeStack --> nodeInc
    nodeInc --> nodeJoint
    nodeInc -- i greater than n --> nodeEnd

    classDef input fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F77B4,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#FF7F0E,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#E8F8E8,stroke:#2CA02C,color:#000
    classDef check fill:#FDECEA,stroke:#D62728,color:#000
```

This represents the **column-wise construction algorithm** for the Jacobian starting from the DH parameters of any serial manipulator. Each column is computed independently based on the joint type and the current configuration.

---

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Kinematic Mapping Layers

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Layer1["LAYER 1 JOINT SPACE"]
        dirA["q sub 1 joint variable"]
        dirB["q sub 2 joint variable"]
        dirC["q sub n joint variable"]
    end
    subgraph Layer2["LAYER 2 DIFFERENTIAL OPERATOR"]
        jac["JACOBIAN J OF q"]
    end
    subgraph Layer3["LAYER 3 CARTESIAN TASK SPACE"]
        outA["v linear velocity m per s"]
        outB["omega angular velocity rad per s"]
    end
    subgraph Layer4["LAYER 4 CONTROL AND PLANNING"]
        ctlA["Inverse velocity control"]
        ctlB["Force torque mapping"]
        ctlC["Singularity avoidance"]
    end

    dirA --> jac
    dirB --> jac
    dirC --> jac
    jac --> outA
    jac --> outB
    outA --> ctlA
    outA --> ctlB
    outB --> ctlA
    outB --> ctlB
    outA --> ctlC
    outB --> ctlC

    classDef layerStyle fill:#F0F8FF,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000
    class Layer1,Layer2,Layer3,Layer4 layerStyle
```

**Interpretation of the layered architecture:**

| Layer | Function | Math Object |
|:---:|---|---|
| 1 — Joint Space | Configuration of the manipulator | Vector $\mathbf{q} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ |
| 2 — Differential Operator | Linear instantaneous mapping | Matrix $\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) \in \mathbb{R}^{6 \times n}$ |
| 3 — Task Space | Physical motion of the end-effector | Vector $\mathbf{V} = [\mathbf{v}, \boldsymbol{\omega}]^{T}$ |
| 4 — Control | Practical use of the mapping | Algorithms, control laws |

---

## 4.4 Geometric Schematic — Singular vs. Non-Singular Configurations (2-Link Arm)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["theta 2 equals 45 degrees NON SINGULAR det J equals 0.35 L1 L2"]:::state
    nodeB["theta 2 equals 0 STRETCHED SINGULAR det J equals 0"]:::state
    nodeC["theta 2 equals 180 FOLDED SINGULAR det J equals 0"]:::state

    nodeA -- "theta 2 approaches 0" --> nodeB
    nodeA -- "theta 2 approaches 180" --> nodeC
    nodeB -- "manoeuvrability lost at max reach" --> nodeA
    nodeC -- "manoeuvrability lost at min reach" --> nodeA

    classDef state fill:#FFF8DC,stroke:#8B7500,color:#000
    class nodeA,nodeB,nodeC state
```

This conceptual diagram illustrates how **the planar 2-link arm transitions between non-singular and singular configurations** purely as a function of the elbow angle $\theta_2$. The determinant $L_1 L_2 \sin(\theta_2)$ smoothly goes to zero at the two extremes.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Paper]

> **Define the Jacobian matrix of a robotic manipulator. Why is it called a "configuration-dependent" operator?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Jacobian matrix** $\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})$ of a robotic manipulator is a $6 \times n$ matrix (or $m \times n$ for a general task) that linearly maps the joint-space velocities $\dot{\mathbf{q}} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ to the end-effector spatial velocity $\mathbf{V} \in \mathbb{R}^{6}$ (linear + angular), according to the relation $\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) \dot{\mathbf{q}}$. **(1 mark)**

It is called a **configuration-dependent operator** because every entry of the Jacobian is a function of the **current joint positions** $\mathbf{q}$, not a constant. The same joint velocity $\dot{q}_i = 1$ rad/s produces **different end-effector velocities** at different arm postures (e.g., stretched vs. folded). **(1 mark)**

Mathematically, this dependence is explicit: $\mathbf{J}_i = \mathbf{z}_{i-1} \times {}^{i-1}\mathbf{p}_{n}^{i-1}$ for revolute joints — and the position vector ${}^{i-1}\mathbf{p}_{n}^{i-1}$ is a function of $\mathbf{q}$. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

> **What is a singular configuration? State the determinant condition and give one physical example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **singular configuration** of a manipulator is a joint-space pose $\mathbf{q}_s$ at which the Jacobian matrix $\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}_s)$ **loses full rank**, i.e., the manipulator loses one or more degrees of freedom in Cartesian space. **(1 mark)**

The mathematical condition is:

$$
\det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{T}) = 0 \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \text{rank}(\mathbf{J}) < \min(m, n)
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Physical example:** A 2-link planar arm with $\theta_2 = 0$ (fully stretched) — the determinant is $L_1 L_2 \sin(0) = 0$. The end-effector cannot translate along the arm's longitudinal direction; to do so would require **infinite joint velocities**, which is impossible for real actuators. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2]

> **(a) [7 marks]** Derive the **Jacobian matrix** for a 2-link planar (RR) manipulator with link lengths $L_1$ and $L_2$ and joint angles $\theta_1, \theta_2$, using the *direct differentiation* method.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** Determine the **singular configurations** of this manipulator and physically interpret each.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation of the Jacobian (7 marks):**

The forward kinematic position equations of the end-effector are:

$$
x = L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \quad (1 \text{ mark})
$$

$$
y = L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \quad (1 \text{ mark})
$$

Differentiating $x$ with respect to time using the chain rule:

$$
\dot{x} = -L_1 \sin\theta_1 \cdot \dot{\theta}_1 - L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)(\dot{\theta}_1 + \dot{\theta}_2)
$$

Grouping terms in $\dot{\theta}_1$ and $\dot{\theta}_2$:

$$
\dot{x} = \left[-L_1 \sin\theta_1 - L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)\right]\dot{\theta}_1 + \left[-L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)\right]\dot{\theta}_2 \quad (2 \text{ marks})
$$

Similarly for $y$:

$$
\dot{y} = \left[L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)\right]\dot{\theta}_1 + \left[L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)\right]\dot{\theta}_2 \quad (2 \text{ marks})
$$

Stacking in matrix form $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{J}\dot{\boldsymbol{\theta}}$:

$$
\mathbf{J} = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1 \sin\theta_1 - L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) & -L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\ L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) & L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \end{bmatrix} \quad (1 \text{ mark})
$$

**(b) Singular configurations (7 marks):**

The Jacobian determinant is computed by the standard $2 \times 2$ formula:

$$
\det(\mathbf{J}) = J_{11} J_{22} - J_{12} J_{21}
$$

$$
\det(\mathbf{J}) = \left[-L_1 \sin\theta_1 - L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)\right]\left[L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)\right] - \left[-L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)\right]\left[L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)\right] \quad (3 \text{ marks})
$$

Expanding and simplifying (using $\sin(A+B)\cos(A+B) - \cos(A+B)\sin(A+B) = 0$ for the $L_2^2$ terms):

$$
\det(\mathbf{J}) = L_1 L_2 \left[\cos\theta_1 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) - \sin\theta_1 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)\right]
$$

$$
\det(\mathbf{J}) = L_1 L_2 \sin(\theta_2) \quad (2 \text{ marks})
$$

Setting $\det(\mathbf{J}) = 0$:

$$
L_1 L_2 \sin(\theta_2) = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \theta_2 = 0 \ \text{or}\ \theta_2 = \pi \quad (1 \text{ mark})
$$

**Physical interpretation (1 mark):**

- **$\theta_2 = 0$** — The arm is **fully stretched out**, end-effector at maximum reach ($L_1 + L_2$ from the base). The end-effector cannot move further outward; any radial motion is blocked. This is a **boundary singularity**.
- **$\theta_2 = \pi$** — The arm is **fully folded back**, end-effector at minimum reach ($\vert L_1 - L_2 \vert$ from the base). The end-effector cannot move further inward. Also a **boundary singularity**.

At both configurations, the two link axes are collinear, so the manipulator effectively has only **1 DOF** in the plane — radial motion is lost, leaving only tangential motion.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2]

> **(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **general relationship** between joint-space velocities and end-effector velocities. With the help of clear diagrams, derive the i-th column of the Jacobian for (i) a **revolute joint** and (ii) a **prismatic joint**.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** For a 3-DOF planar manipulator (RRR) with link lengths $L_1 = L_2 = L_3 = 1$ m and current joint angles $\theta_1 = 30°$, $\theta_2 = 45°$, $\theta_3 = 60°$, **compute the Jacobian**, determine the **end-effector velocity** for $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = [0.5, 0.3, 0.2]^{T}$ rad/s, and check for **singularity**.

#### Model Solution

**(a) General relationship and column derivation (7 marks):**

The end-effector spatial velocity $\mathbf{V} \in \mathbb{R}^{6}$ (linear $\mathbf{v}$ + angular $\boldsymbol{\omega}$) is related to the joint velocity vector $\dot{\mathbf{q}} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ by the **Jacobian matrix**:

$$
\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) \dot{\mathbf{q}} \quad (1 \text{ mark})
$$

Since the mapping is linear at any fixed configuration, the i-th column of $\mathbf{J}$ is the end-effector velocity produced by **unit velocity at joint $i$** alone, with all other joints locked. This is a direct consequence of the **superposition principle** for linear transformations. **(1 mark)**

**(i) Revolute joint — i-th column derivation (2.5 marks):**

For a revolute joint rotating about axis $\mathbf{z}_{i-1}$ with unit angular velocity:
- The **linear velocity** of the end-effector is the rotational velocity at the moment arm: $\mathbf{v} = \boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{r} = \mathbf{z}_{i-1} \times {}^{i-1}\mathbf{p}_{n}^{i-1}$.
- The **angular velocity** of the end-effector is simply $\boldsymbol{\omega} = \mathbf{z}_{i-1}$.

Therefore:

$$
\mathbf{J}_i = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{z}_{i-1} \times {}^{i-1}\mathbf{p}_{n}^{i-1} \\ \mathbf{z}_{i-1} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**(ii) Prismatic joint — i-th column derivation (2.5 marks):**

For a prismatic joint translating along axis $\mathbf{z}_{i-1}$ with unit velocity:
- The **linear velocity** of the end-effector is $\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{z}_{i-1}$.
- The **angular velocity** of the end-effector is $\boldsymbol{\omega} = \mathbf{0}$ (pure translation produces no rotation).

Therefore:

$$
\mathbf{J}_i = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{z}_{i-1} \\ \mathbf{0} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**(b) Numerical computation for 3-DOF RRR planar arm (7 marks):**

The end-effector position is:

$$
x = L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) + L_3 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2 + \theta_3)
$$

$$
y = L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) + L_3 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2 + \theta_3)
$$

Differentiating:

$$
\dot{x} = -\sum_{k=1}^{3} L_k \sin(\theta_1 + \cdots + \theta_k) \cdot \sum_{j=1}^{k} \dot{\theta}_j
$$

$$
\dot{y} = \sum_{k=1}^{3} L_k \cos(\theta_1 + \cdots + \theta_k) \cdot \sum_{j=1}^{k} \dot{\theta}_j
$$

With $L_1 = L_2 = L_3 = 1$, $\theta_1 = 30°$, $\theta_2 = 45°$, $\theta_3 = 60°$:

| Cumulative Angle | Value | $\sin$ | $\cos$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $\theta_1$ | $30°$ | $0.5000$ | $0.8660$ |
| $\theta_1 + \theta_2$ | $75°$ | $0.9659$ | $0.2588$ |
| $\theta_1 + \theta_2 + \theta_3$ | $135°$ | $0.7071$ | $-0.7071$ |

The Jacobian (2 × 3) is:

$$
\mathbf{J} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.5000 - 0.9659 - 0.7071 & -0.9659 - 0.7071 & -0.7071 \\ 0.8660 + 0.2588 - 0.7071 & 0.2588 - 0.7071 & -0.7071 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -2.1730 & -1.6730 & -0.7071 \\ 0.4177 & -0.4483 & -0.7071 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**(2 marks for Jacobian)**

End-effector velocity:

$$
\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{J} \dot{\mathbf{q}} = \begin{bmatrix} -2.1730 & -1.6730 & -0.7071 \\ 0.4177 & -0.4483 & -0.7071 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.3 \\ 0.2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Component 1: $(-2.1730)(0.5) + (-1.6730)(0.3) + (-0.7071)(0.2) = -1.0865 - 0.5019 - 0.1414 = -1.7298$ m/s

Component 2: $(0.4177)(0.5) + (-0.4483)(0.3) + (-0.7071)(0.2) = 0.2089 - 0.1345 - 0.1414 = -0.0670$ m/s

**(2 marks)**

$$
\mathbf{V} = \begin{bmatrix} -1.7298 \\ -0.0670 \end{bmatrix} \text{ m/s}
$$

**Singularity check (2 marks):**

The Jacobian is $2 \times 3$ (more columns than rows). It is singular when its rows are linearly dependent, i.e., the two rows are scalar multiples. The ratio of corresponding entries:

$$
\frac{-2.1730}{0.4177} \approx -5.20, \quad \frac{-1.6730}{-0.4483} \approx 3.73, \quad \frac{-0.7071}{-0.7071} = 1.00
$$

Since the ratios are **not equal**, the rows are linearly independent — the Jacobian has rank 2. **The configuration is non-singular.** ✅

To be rigorously non-singular, the manipulator must have a configuration in which the 3 effective link vectors (the columns of $\mathbf{J}_v$) span the 2D plane, which is true here since $\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3$ are all nonzero and not aligned in a degenerate way.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the angular part:** For 3D (spatial) manipulators, students often compute only the linear part $\mathbf{J}_v$ and forget the angular part $\mathbf{J}_\omega$. For planar arms, $\mathbf{J}_\omega$ is a single row of all 1s — including it is mandatory.
> 2. **Sign errors in cross products:** $\mathbf{z} \times \mathbf{p} \neq \mathbf{p} \times \mathbf{z}$. Always use right-hand rule. A common mistake is reversing the cross-product order in the revolute column.
> 3. **Confusing position Jacobian with velocity Jacobian:** The Jacobian from differentiation gives the velocity mapping **only** if the position equations are differentiated in a globally non-singular orientation representation. For Euler angles, this fails near gimbal lock — ZYZ or quaternion representations are preferred for the analytical Jacobian.
> 4. **Singularity check on non-square Jacobians:** Students often incorrectly try to compute $\det(\mathbf{J})$ on a non-square Jacobian. The correct test is $\det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{T}) = 0$ (or equivalently, check rank).
> 5. **Wrong joint axis:** The axis $\mathbf{z}_{i-1}$ is the **axis of the i-th joint expressed in the base frame** — extracted from the rotation matrix ${}^{0}\mathbf{R}_{i-1}$, third column. Not the axis of frame $\{i\}$, and not the link axis.
> 6. **Units mismatch:** $\theta$ in radians when differentiating $\sin/\cos$ functions; angular velocity in rad/s; linear velocity in m/s. Mixing degrees and radians is a guaranteed mark deduction.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **The Jacobian $\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})$ is the velocity-level bridge** between joint space and task space. Master equation: $\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{J}\dot{\mathbf{q}}$.
- ✅ **Two equivalent ways to construct $\mathbf{J}$:** (1) column-by-column using the cross-product rules, (2) direct differentiation of forward kinematics. Cross-product method is preferred for 3D.
- ✅ **Revolute column:** $\mathbf{J}_i = [\mathbf{z}_{i-1} \times \mathbf{p}; \mathbf{z}_{i-1}]$ — moment arm and rotation axis.
- ✅ **Prismatic column:** $\mathbf{J}_i = [\mathbf{z}_{i-1}; \mathbf{0}]$ — translation axis only.
- ✅ **For a 2-link planar RR arm:** $\det(\mathbf{J}) = L_1 L_2 \sin(\theta_2)$ — depends **only** on $\theta_2$.
- ✅ **Singularities** occur at $\theta_2 = 0$ (stretched) and $\theta_2 = \pi$ (folded) for the 2-link arm.
- ✅ **Force-torque duality:** $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^{T}\mathbf{F}$ — virtual-work principle.
- ✅ **Manipulability** $w = \sqrt{\det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{T})}$ quantifies dexterity; $w = 0$ at singularities.
- ✅ **Inverse velocity:** $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{-1}\mathbf{V}$ (square case) or $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{+}\mathbf{V}$ (general case, minimum-norm).
- ✅ **Acceleration relation:** $\ddot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{J}\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \dot{\mathbf{J}}\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ — needed for dynamics.
- ✅ **Dimension rule:** For an $n$-DOF arm moving in 3D, $\mathbf{J} \in \mathbb{R}^{6 \times n}$ (3 linear + 3 angular). For planar: $\mathbf{J} \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times n}$ (2 linear + 1 angular).
- ✅ **Exam mantra:** "*Always state both the linear and angular parts. Always specify the reference frame. Always check for singularities.*"

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Manipulator Dynamics, Lagrange's Formulation - Kinetic Energy Expression
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT302 - INTRODUCTION TO ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Manipulator Dynamics, Lagrange's Formulation - Kinetic Energy Expression

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Manipulator Dynamics: Lagrangian Formulation

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor — Module 3**
> *Manipulator Dynamics: Lagrange's formulation — Kinetic Energy Expression, Potential Energy Expression, Equations of Motion, Generalized Forces.*

### 1.1 What is Manipulator Dynamics?

**Manipulator Dynamics** is the analytical study of the relationship between the **generalized forces** (joint torques/forces $\tau_i$) applied at the joints of a robotic manipulator and the resulting **motion** (joint positions $q_i$, velocities $\dot{q}_i$, accelerations $\ddot{q}_i$) of the manipulator, taking into account the **mass distribution, inertia, gravity, and coupling effects** between links.

Mathematically, the dynamic model is expressed as:

$$\tau = M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q)$$

where each term has a precise physical meaning that we will unpack in this lesson.

### 1.2 Why Use Lagrange's Formulation?

There are two classical ways to derive manipulator dynamics:

| Method | Strengths | Weaknesses |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Newton–Euler** | Recursive, computationally fast, ideal for real-time control. | Requires force/torque analysis at every joint; algebra explodes for high DOF. |
| **Lagrange–Euler** | Systematic, energy-based, clean closed-form equations. | Expensive $O(n^4)$ computation in raw form; not recursive. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Lagrangian / Euler–Lagrange Equation)**
> The **Lagrangian** $\mathcal{L}$ of a mechanical system is defined as the difference between the **total kinetic energy** $K$ and the **total potential energy** $P$ of the system:
> $$\mathcal{L}(q,\dot{q}) = K(q,\dot{q}) - P(q)$$
> The equations of motion are then obtained from the **Euler–Lagrange equation**:
> $$\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i, \quad i = 1,2,\dots,n$$

### 1.3 The Intuitive Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Acrobat on a Trampoline** 🤸
> Imagine an acrobat performing a routine. To choreograph the dive, the coach does **not** track every muscle and joint. Instead, the coach works with **energy**: *kinetic energy* (the speed of motion) and *potential energy* (the height above the trampoline). The **difference** between these energies — the Lagrangian — tells the coach how the acrobat's body will naturally evolve. Apply the same idea to a robot arm: instead of forces, we use **energy bookkeeping** to derive how every link will move. This is the soul of Lagrange's method.

### 1.4 Generalized Coordinates

For an $n$-DOF manipulator, we choose a set of $n$ independent **generalized coordinates**:

$$q = \begin{bmatrix} q_1 & q_2 & \cdots & q_n \end{bmatrix}^\top$$

For a revolute joint, $q_i = \theta_i$ (angle); for a prismatic joint, $q_i = d_i$ (linear displacement). Each generalized coordinate corresponds to one degree of freedom.

### 1.5 The Two-Stage Roadmap

To apply Lagrange's formulation to a manipulator, we proceed in two stages:

1. **Stage 1 — Energy Computation**: Express the **kinetic energy** $K$ and **potential energy** $P$ of every link in terms of $q$ and $\dot{q}$.
2. **Stage 2 — Equation Derivation**: Substitute $\mathcal{L} = K - P$ into the Euler–Lagrange equation to obtain $\tau_i$.

This module's focus is **Stage 1 — the Kinetic Energy Expression**, which is the most mathematically rich part.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of Kinetic Energy

A manipulator is a chain of $n$ rigid links. The **total kinetic energy** is the sum of the kinetic energies of all individual links:

$$K = \sum_{i=1}^{n} K_i$$

For any single rigid link $i$, kinetic energy splits into two physical contributions:

1. **Translational KE** of its center of mass: $\tfrac{1}{2} m_i v_{c_i}^\top v_{c_i}$
2. **Rotational KE** about its center of mass: $\tfrac{1}{2} \omega_i^\top I_i \omega_i$

Therefore:

$$K_i = \frac{1}{2} m_i v_{c_i}^\top v_{c_i} + \frac{1}{2} \omega_i^\top I_i \omega_i$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Symbols**
> - $m_i$ — total mass of link $i$ (**SI unit: kg**)
> - $v_{c_i}$ — linear velocity of the center of mass of link $i$ (**SI unit: m/s**)
> - $\omega_i$ — angular velocity of link $i$ (**SI unit: rad/s**)
> - $I_i$ — inertia tensor of link $i$ about its center of mass (**SI unit: kg·m²**)
> - $J_{v_i}$ — linear Jacobian mapping joint rates to $v_{c_i}$
> - $J_{\omega_i}$ — angular Jacobian mapping joint rates to $\omega_i$

## 2.2 Jacobian Velocity Mapping

A core result from kinematics is that the velocity of any point on link $i$ is a **linear function of joint rates** through the **Jacobian**. Specifically:

$$v_{c_i} = J_{v_i}(q) \, \dot{q}$$

$$\omega_i = J_{\omega_i}(q) \, \dot{q}$$

where:
- $J_{v_i} \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times n}$ is the **linear velocity Jacobian** of link $i$'s center of mass.
- $J_{\omega_i} \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times n}$ is the **angular velocity Jacobian** of link $i$.
- $\dot{q} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the **joint velocity vector**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Meaning of the Jacobian**
> The Jacobian acts like a *gearbox* between the actuator world (joint rates $\dot{q}$) and the physical world (link velocities). It is the **only** place where joint motion enters the energy equation.

## 2.3 The Mass (Inertia) Matrix

When you substitute the Jacobian relations into the kinetic energy expression and sum over all links, a beautiful structure emerges. The total kinetic energy takes the **quadratic form**:

$$K = \frac{1}{2} \dot{q}^\top \, M(q) \, \dot{q}$$

where $M(q) \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ is the symmetric, positive-definite **Manipulator Mass Matrix** (also called the **Inertia Matrix** or **Kinetic Energy Matrix**):

$$M(q) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( m_i \, J_{v_i}^\top(q) \, J_{v_i}(q) + J_{\omega_i}^\top(q) \, I_i \, J_{\omega_i}(q) \right)$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Properties of $M(q)$ — KTU Examiner's Favourite**
> 1. **Symmetric**: $M(q) = M^\top(q)$.
> 2. **Positive Definite**: $x^\top M(q) x > 0$ for all $x \ne 0$. This guarantees that $K \ge 0$ for all $\dot{q}$, with equality only at $\dot{q} = 0$.
> 3. **Configuration dependent**: $M$ is a function of $q$ because the Jacobians depend on the joint angles.
> 4. **Bounded**: There exist constants $m_{\min}, m_{\max} > 0$ such that $m_{\min} I \le M(q) \le m_{\max} I$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $K_i = \tfrac{1}{2} m_i v_{c_i}^\top v_{c_i} + \tfrac{1}{2} \omega_i^\top I_i \omega_i$ | KE of a single rigid link | J (Joule) |
| 2 | $v_{c_i} = J_{v_i}(q) \dot{q}$ | Linear velocity via Jacobian | m/s |
| 3 | $\omega_i = J_{\omega_i}(q) \dot{q}$ | Angular velocity via Jacobian | rad/s |
| 4 | $K = \sum_{i=1}^{n} K_i$ | Total KE (scalar) of the manipulator | J |
| 5 | $K = \tfrac{1}{2} \dot{q}^\top M(q) \dot{q}$ | Quadratic form of total KE | J |
| 6 | $M(q) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \big( m_i J_{v_i}^\top J_{v_i} + J_{\omega_i}^\top I_i J_{\omega_i} \big)$ | Mass matrix construction | kg·m² (or kg) |
| 7 | $\mathcal{L} = K - P$ | Lagrangian definition | J |
| 8 | $\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\tfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \tfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i$ | Euler–Lagrange equation | N·m or N |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Why does this matter in industry?**
> - **Robot Simulation**: Tools like MATLAB *Robotics Toolbox*, Gazebo, and PyBullet internally use the mass matrix $M(q)$ to simulate motion realistically.
> - **Model-Based Control**: Computed-Torque Control uses $M(q)\ddot{q} + C\dot{q} + G = \tau$ to *cancel* nonlinearities and behave like a simple linear system.
> - **Energy-Efficient Motion Planning**: Knowing $K = \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{q}^\top M \dot{q}$ lets engineers compute instantaneous power $P = \dot{q}^\top M \ddot{q} + \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{q}^\top \dot{M} \dot{q}$ and design minimum-energy trajectories.
> - **Safety & Collision Analysis**: $M(q)$ bounds the inertia the robot "feels" — critical for human–robot collaboration (cobots).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Individual Link KE to Quadratic Form

### Step 1 — Write KE for a Single Link

For rigid link $i$ with center of mass $c_i$:

$$K_i = \frac{1}{2} m_i \, v_{c_i}^\top v_{c_i} + \frac{1}{2} \omega_i^\top I_i \omega_i$$

**Logic**: A rigid body's KE is the sum of (a) translation of the center of mass treated as a point mass, and (b) rotation about the center of mass. This is **Konig's theorem** (also called the *parallel-axis form* of KE).

### Step 2 — Substitute the Jacobian Velocity Mappings

Insert $v_{c_i} = J_{v_i}(q) \dot{q}$ and $\omega_i = J_{\omega_i}(q) \dot{q}$ into $K_i$:

$$K_i = \frac{1}{2} m_i \, (J_{v_i} \dot{q})^\top (J_{v_i} \dot{q}) + \frac{1}{2} (J_{\omega_i} \dot{q})^\top I_i (J_{\omega_i} \dot{q})$$

**Logic**: We use the property $(A x)^\top = x^\top A^\top$ to bring the joint-rate vector to the front.

### Step 3 — Expand Using Transpose Property

$$K_i = \frac{1}{2} m_i \, \dot{q}^\top J_{v_i}^\top J_{v_i} \, \dot{q} + \frac{1}{2} \dot{q}^\top J_{\omega_i}^\top I_i J_{\omega_i} \, \dot{q}$$

**Logic**: $(J_{v_i} \dot{q})^\top (J_{v_i} \dot{q}) = \dot{q}^\top J_{v_i}^\top J_{v_i} \dot{q}$. This is a standard rule of matrix algebra.

### Step 4 — Factor Out the Common $\dot{q}$ Bracket

Both terms have $\dot{q}^\top (\cdot) \dot{q}$, so:

$$K_i = \frac{1}{2} \dot{q}^\top \Big( m_i J_{v_i}^\top J_{v_i} + J_{\omega_i}^\top I_i J_{\omega_i} \Big) \dot{q}$$

**Logic**: We group all matrix coefficients into a single inner bracket — this bracket is the **per-link contribution to the mass matrix**.

### Step 5 — Sum Over All Links

$$K = \sum_{i=1}^{n} K_i = \frac{1}{2} \dot{q}^\top \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{n} \Big( m_i J_{v_i}^\top J_{v_i} + J_{\omega_i}^\top I_i J_{\omega_i} \Big) \right] \dot{q}$$

**Logic**: Total KE is additive over non-interacting links.

### Step 6 — Define the Mass Matrix

$$M(q) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \Big( m_i J_{v_i}^\top J_{v_i} + J_{\omega_i}^\top I_i J_{\omega_i} \Big)$$

Therefore, in compact quadratic form:

$$K = \frac{1}{2} \dot{q}^\top M(q) \dot{q}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result — Kinetic Energy Expression**
> $$\boxed{\,K(q,\dot{q}) = \frac{1}{2} \dot{q}^\top M(q) \dot{q}, \quad M(q) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \Big( m_i J_{v_i}^\top J_{v_i} + J_{\omega_i}^\top I_i J_{\omega_i} \Big)\,}$$
> This is **the** KTU board-exam formula for KE in Lagrange's formulation.

## 3.2 Worked Example — 2R Planar Manipulator

> [!NOTE]
> **Setup**
> Consider a 2-revolute planar arm with link lengths $L_1, L_2$, point masses $m_1, m_2$ at the distal ends (a simplification valid when the link inertia tensors are negligible compared to point-mass translation).

### Step 1 — Joint Vector and Jacobians

Joint vector: $q = \begin{bmatrix} \theta_1 \\ \theta_2 \end{bmatrix}$, $\dot{q} = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{\theta}_1 \\ \dot{\theta}_2 \end{bmatrix}$

The linear Jacobians (mapping joint rates to the velocity of the **end of each link**) are:

$$J_{v_1} = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1 s_1 & 0 \\ L_1 c_1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad J_{v_2} = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1 s_1 - L_2 s_{12} & -L_2 s_{12} \\ L_1 c_1 + L_2 c_{12} & L_2 c_{12} \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

where $s_1 = \sin\theta_1$, $c_1 = \cos\theta_1$, $s_{12} = \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)$, $c_{12} = \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)$.

### Step 2 — Per-Link Mass Matrices

The per-link contribution is $M_i = m_i J_{v_i}^\top J_{v_i}$. For link 1:

$$J_{v_1}^\top J_{v_1} = \begin{bmatrix} L_1^2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

So $M_1 = m_1 \begin{bmatrix} L_1^2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$.

For link 2, after expanding (we skip the tedious algebra and present the result):

$$J_{v_2}^\top J_{v_2} = \begin{bmatrix} L_1^2 + L_2^2 + 2L_1 L_2 c_2 & L_2^2 + L_1 L_2 c_2 \\ L_2^2 + L_1 L_2 c_2 & L_2^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 3 — Total Mass Matrix

Summing both contributions:

$$M(q) = m_1 \begin{bmatrix} L_1^2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} + m_2 \begin{bmatrix} L_1^2 + L_2^2 + 2L_1 L_2 c_2 & L_2^2 + L_1 L_2 c_2 \\ L_2^2 + L_1 L_2 c_2 & L_2^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$M(q) = \begin{bmatrix} (m_1+m_2)L_1^2 + m_2 L_2^2 + 2 m_2 L_1 L_2 c_2 & m_2 L_2^2 + m_2 L_1 L_2 c_2 \\ m_2 L_2^2 + m_2 L_1 L_2 c_2 & m_2 L_2^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 4 — Total Kinetic Energy

$$K = \frac{1}{2} \dot{q}^\top M(q) \dot{q}$$

$$K = \frac{1}{2} m_1 L_1^2 \dot{\theta}_1^2 + \frac{1}{2} m_2 \Big[ (L_1^2 + L_2^2 + 2L_1 L_2 c_2)\dot{\theta}_1^2 + 2(L_2^2 + L_1 L_2 c_2)\dot{\theta}_1\dot{\theta}_2 + L_2^2 \dot{\theta}_2^2 \Big]$$

> [!TIP]
> **Physical Interpretation**
> - The term $m_2 L_1 L_2 c_2$ inside $M_{11}$ represents the **centrifugal coupling** between the two links — the elbow angle $\theta_2$ changes the effective inertia of the shoulder.
> - The off-diagonal terms $M_{12} = M_{21}$ represent the **Coriolis coupling** — moving one joint exerts inertial force on the other.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Kinetic Energy Computation Pipeline

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the full data flow from raw manipulator parameters to the final compact quadratic kinetic energy expression.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Robot Geometry"] --> B["DH Parameters"]
    B --> C["Forward Kinematics"]
    C --> D["Position and Orientation of Link i"]
    D --> E["Compute Linear Jacobian Jvi"]
    D --> F["Compute Angular Jacobian Jwi"]
    A --> G["Link Mass mi and Inertia Tensor Ii"]
    E --> H["Velocity Mapping vi = Jvi dq"]
    F --> I["Angular Velocity wi = Jwi dq"]
    H --> J["Per Link Kinetic Energy Ki"]
    I --> J
    G --> J
    J --> K["Substitute Jacobian into KE Expression"]
    K --> L["Per Link Mass Matrix Contribution mi JviT Jvi + JwiT Ii Jwi"]
    L --> M["Sum Over All Links i = 1 to n"]
    M --> N["Total Mass Matrix M of q"]
    N --> O["Final Quadratic KE Form K = 0.5 dqT M dq"]
```

### Reading the Diagram

| Block | Responsibility |
| :--- | :--- |
| `A → B → C → D` | Build the kinematic model of the manipulator. |
| `D → E` / `D → F` | Differentiate the forward kinematics to obtain the Jacobians. |
| `E → H` / `F → I` | Apply the linear mapping from joint rates to link velocities. |
| `H, I, G → J` | Compute the **scalar KE of link $i$** using König's theorem. |
| `J → K → L` | Convert the per-link KE into a per-link matrix contribution. |
| `L → M → N` | Sum contributions across all links to get $M(q)$. |
| `N → O` | Final closed-form scalar KE. |

## 4.2 Information-Theoretic View of the Mass Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Inputs
        dq["Joint Rates dq"]
    end
    subgraph Geometry
        Jv["Linear Jacobian Jv"]
        Jw["Angular Jacobian Jw"]
    end
    subgraph InertiaProperties
        m["Mass m"]
        Imat["Inertia Tensor I"]
    end
    Mblock["Mass Matrix M of q"]
    KEout["Kinetic Energy K"]
    dq --> KEout
    Mblock --> KEout
    Jv --> Mblock
    Jw --> Mblock
    m --> Mblock
    Imat --> Mblock
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this layered view matters for KTU**
> During the exam, if you forget the formula for $M(q)$, you can recover it by following the diagram top-down: *kinematics gives the Jacobians → inertia gives the weighting → quadratic form gives the KE*. This structural memory is the key to a confident answer.

## 4.3 Symbol Reference Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q["Generalized Coordinates q"] --> DQ["Joint Rates dq"]
    DQ --> K1["Translational KE 0.5 m vT v"]
    DQ --> K2["Rotational KE 0.5 wT I w"]
    K1 --> KI["Per Link KE Ki"]
    K2 --> KI
    KI --> SUM["Total KE K = sum Ki"]
    SUM --> FORM["K = 0.5 dqT M dq"]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **Q1.** State the general expression for the kinetic energy of an $n$-DOF robotic manipulator using generalized coordinates. Define each term in your expression.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The total kinetic energy of an $n$-DOF manipulator is the sum of the kinetic energies of all $n$ rigid links. For each link $i$:
> $$K_i = \frac{1}{2} m_i v_{c_i}^\top v_{c_i} + \frac{1}{2} \omega_i^\top I_i \omega_i$$
> Summing over all links and using the Jacobians $v_{c_i} = J_{v_i}\dot{q}$ and $\omega_i = J_{\omega_i}\dot{q}$:
> $$K = \frac{1}{2} \dot{q}^\top M(q) \dot{q}$$
> where:
> - $m_i$ = mass of link $i$,
> - $v_{c_i}$ = linear velocity of center of mass of link $i$,
> - $\omega_i$ = angular velocity of link $i$,
> - $I_i$ = inertia tensor of link $i$ about its center of mass,
> - $M(q)$ = configuration-dependent mass matrix. **(3 marks — full marks for formula + identification of all terms)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Q2.** List any **four** important properties of the manipulator mass matrix $M(q)$ in Lagrange's formulation.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks — $4 \times 0.75$):**
> 1. **Symmetric**: $M(q) = M^\top(q)$. **(0.75)**
> 2. **Positive Definite**: $\dot{q}^\top M(q) \dot{q} > 0$ for all $\dot{q} \ne 0$, guaranteeing non-negative KE. **(0.75)**
> 3. **Configuration Dependent**: $M(q)$ is a function of joint positions because Jacobians depend on $q$. **(0.75)**
> 4. **Bounded**: $m_{\min} I \le M(q) \le m_{\max} I$ for some positive constants, ensuring numerical stability. **(0.75)**

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**
> - Students commonly **forget the rotational KE term** $\tfrac{1}{2}\omega_i^\top I_i \omega_i$ and write only the translational part. **Cost: 2 marks.**
> - Students sometimes **omit the transpose on Jacobians** when forming $M(q)$. The correct form is $J_{v_i}^\top J_{v_i}$, **not** $J_{v_i} J_{v_i}$. **Cost: 1 mark.**
> - Students frequently write $M(q)$ as a *scalar* instead of an $n \times n$ matrix. **Cost: 1 mark.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the kinetic energy expression of a general $n$-DOF serial-chain manipulator using the Lagrangian approach. Clearly state the König's theorem step and the role of the Jacobian in your derivation.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Define generalized coordinates and KE of one link [1 mark]:**
The manipulator has $n$ DOF with generalized coordinates $q = [q_1, \dots, q_n]^\top$. For each rigid link $i$, by **König's theorem**, KE splits into translational and rotational parts:
$$K_i = \frac{1}{2} m_i v_{c_i}^\top v_{c_i} + \frac{1}{2} \omega_i^\top I_i \omega_i$$

**Step 2 — Apply Jacobian velocity relations [1 mark]:**
Using the **linear and angular velocity Jacobians** of the center of mass of link $i$:
$$v_{c_i} = J_{v_i}(q) \dot{q}, \qquad \omega_i = J_{\omega_i}(q) \dot{q}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the KE [1.5 marks]:**
$$K_i = \frac{1}{2} m_i \dot{q}^\top J_{v_i}^\top J_{v_i} \dot{q} + \frac{1}{2} \dot{q}^\top J_{\omega_i}^\top I_i J_{\omega_i} \dot{q}$$
$$K_i = \frac{1}{2} \dot{q}^\top \Big( m_i J_{v_i}^\top J_{v_i} + J_{\omega_i}^\top I_i J_{\omega_i} \Big) \dot{q}$$

**Step 4 — Sum over all links [1 mark]:**
$$K = \sum_{i=1}^{n} K_i = \frac{1}{2} \dot{q}^\top \underbrace{\left[ \sum_{i=1}^{n} \Big( m_i J_{v_i}^\top J_{v_i} + J_{\omega_i}^\top I_i J_{\omega_i} \Big) \right]}_{M(q)} \dot{q}$$

**Step 5 — Define the mass matrix [1.5 marks]:**
$$\boxed{M(q) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \Big( m_i J_{v_i}^\top J_{v_i} + J_{\omega_i}^\top I_i J_{\omega_i} \Big)}$$
$$K = \frac{1}{2} \dot{q}^\top M(q) \dot{q}$$
**Role of the Jacobian**: It linearly maps the **actuator space** (joint rates) to the **task space** (link velocities), allowing all link KE to be expressed in a single set of variables $\dot{q}$. **(1 mark for role of Jacobian, distributed as clarity throughout)**

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a 2-revolute planar manipulator with link lengths $L_1, L_2$ and point masses $m_1, m_2$ at the distal ends, compute the **mass matrix $M(q)$** using the formula derived in part (a). Hence write the explicit scalar kinetic energy expression.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Joint vector [0.5 marks]:**
$$q = \begin{bmatrix} \theta_1 \\ \theta_2 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad \dot{q} = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{\theta}_1 \\ \dot{\theta}_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Linear Jacobians [2 marks]:**
$$J_{v_1} = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1 s_1 & 0 \\ L_1 c_1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad J_{v_2} = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1 s_1 - L_2 s_{12} & -L_2 s_{12} \\ L_1 c_1 + L_2 c_{12} & L_2 c_{12} \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $J_{v_1}^\top J_{v_1}$ [1 mark]:**
$$J_{v_1}^\top J_{v_1} = \begin{bmatrix} L_1^2 s_1^2 + L_1^2 c_1^2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} L_1^2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $J_{v_2}^\top J_{v_2}$ [2 marks]:**
Using $s_1^2 + c_1^2 = 1$ and the standard trigonometric identities, the non-trivial terms simplify to:
$$J_{v_2}^\top J_{v_2} = \begin{bmatrix} L_1^2 + L_2^2 + 2 L_1 L_2 c_2 & L_2^2 + L_1 L_2 c_2 \\ L_2^2 + L_1 L_2 c_2 & L_2^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Assemble the mass matrix [1 mark]:**
$$M(q) = m_1 \begin{bmatrix} L_1^2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} + m_2 \begin{bmatrix} L_1^2 + L_2^2 + 2 L_1 L_2 c_2 & L_2^2 + L_1 L_2 c_2 \\ L_2^2 + L_1 L_2 c_2 & L_2^2 \end{bmatrix}$$
$$M(q) = \begin{bmatrix} (m_1+m_2)L_1^2 + m_2 L_2^2 + 2 m_2 L_1 L_2 c_2 & m_2(L_2^2 + L_1 L_2 c_2) \\ m_2(L_2^2 + L_1 L_2 c_2) & m_2 L_2^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 — Write scalar KE [0.5 marks]:**
$$K = \frac{1}{2} \dot{q}^\top M(q) \dot{q} = \frac{1}{2} m_1 L_1^2 \dot{\theta}_1^2 + \frac{1}{2} m_2 \Big[ (L_1^2 + L_2^2 + 2 L_1 L_2 c_2)\dot{\theta}_1^2 + 2(L_2^2 + L_1 L_2 c_2)\dot{\theta}_1\dot{\theta}_2 + L_2^2 \dot{\theta}_2^2 \Big]$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **physical significance** of the manipulator mass matrix $M(q)$. Discuss why $M(q)$ is **symmetric** and **positive definite**, and how these properties influence robot control.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Physical significance [2 marks]:**
$M(q)$ represents the **effective inertia** of the manipulator as seen at the joints. The diagonal entries $M_{ii}$ are the **moments of inertia** that joint $i$ experiences due to motion of all links, while the off-diagonal entries $M_{ij} = M_{ji}$ represent the **inertial coupling** — accelerating joint $j$ produces an inertial reaction torque on joint $i$ (and vice versa). Since the configuration of the arm changes with $q$, $M(q)$ varies during motion.

**Step 2 — Symmetry proof [2 marks]:**
$$M_{ij}(q) = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \big( m_k [J_{v_k}]_{:,i}^\top [J_{v_k}]_{:,j} + [J_{\omega_k}]_{:,i}^\top I_k [J_{\omega_k}]_{:,j} \big)$$
$$= M_{ji}(q)$$
because the inner products are commutative: $a^\top b = b^\top a$. Hence $M(q) = M^\top(q)$. **(2 marks)**

**Step 3 — Positive definiteness [2 marks]:**
For any non-zero $\dot{q}$:
$$\dot{q}^\top M(q) \dot{q} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \big( m_k \vert J_{v_k} \dot{q} \vert^2 + (J_{\omega_k} \dot{q})^\top I_k (J_{\omega_k} \dot{q}) \big) = 2K \ge 0$$
and equals zero **only** when $\dot{q} = 0$, because then every link has zero velocity. Hence $M(q)$ is **positive definite**. **(2 marks)**

**Step 4 — Influence on control [1 mark]:**
In **Computed-Torque Control**, the law $\tau = M(q)\big[\ddot{q}_d + K_p e + K_d \dot{e}\big] + C\dot{q} + G$ *linearizes* the closed-loop dynamics to a decoupled second-order system. The positive definiteness of $M(q)$ guarantees that the control law is well-defined (no division by zero) and that the closed-loop system is stable.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 2-DOF manipulator has a single mass $m$ concentrated at the end-effector. The end-effector position is given by $x = L \cos\theta_1$ and $y = L \sin\theta_1 + L \sin\theta_2$ where $L$ is a constant link length. **(i)** Find the linear Jacobian. **(ii)** Compute the mass matrix $M(q)$. **(iii)** Write the kinetic energy expression.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Position vector and its time derivative [1 mark]:**
$$\dot{x} = -L \sin\theta_1 \cdot \dot{\theta}_1$$
$$\dot{y} = L \cos\theta_1 \cdot \dot{\theta}_1 + L \cos\theta_2 \cdot \dot{\theta}_2$$

**Step 2 — Form the linear Jacobian [1.5 marks]:**
$$J = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial x}{\partial \theta_1} & \dfrac{\partial x}{\partial \theta_2} \\[4pt] \dfrac{\partial y}{\partial \theta_1} & \dfrac{\partial y}{\partial \theta_2} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -L \sin\theta_1 & 0 \\ L \cos\theta_1 & L \cos\theta_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $J^\top J$ [1.5 marks]:**
$$J^\top J = \begin{bmatrix} -L s_1 & L c_1 \\ 0 & L c_2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} -L s_1 & 0 \\ L c_1 & L c_2 \end{bmatrix}$$
$$= \begin{bmatrix} L^2 s_1^2 + L^2 c_1^2 & L^2 c_1 c_2 \\ L^2 c_1 c_2 & L^2 c_2^2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} L^2 & L^2 c_1 c_2 \\ L^2 c_1 c_2 & L^2 c_2^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the mass matrix [1 mark]:**
For a single point mass, $M(q) = m J^\top J$:
$$M(q) = m L^2 \begin{bmatrix} 1 & c_1 c_2 \\ c_1 c_2 & c_2^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Write the kinetic energy [1 mark]:**
$$\dot{q} = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{\theta}_1 \\ \dot{\theta}_2 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad K = \frac{1}{2} \dot{q}^\top M(q) \dot{q}$$
$$K = \frac{1}{2} m L^2 \big( \dot{\theta}_1^2 + 2 c_1 c_2 \, \dot{\theta}_1 \dot{\theta}_2 + c_2^2 \, \dot{\theta}_2^2 \big)$$

**Step 6 — Physical check [1 mark]:**
Verify dimensions: $[m L^2] = \text{kg} \cdot \text{m}^2$, $[\dot{\theta}^2] = \text{s}^{-2}$, so $[K] = \text{kg} \cdot \text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s}^{-2} = \text{J}$ ✔. The off-diagonal term $m L^2 c_1 c_2$ represents the **inertial coupling** between the two joints.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Kinet

topics.title: Velocity Jacobian and Potential Energy Expression
