> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBRT302 - INTRODUCTION TO ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Velocity Jacobian and Potential Energy Expression # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Velocity Jacobian — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Velocity Jacobian (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):** > The **Velocity Jacobian Matrix**, denoted **J(q)**, is a matrix function of joint positions that linearly maps the **joint-space velocity vector** $\dot{q} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ to the **task-space (end-effector) spatial velocity vector** $\xi \in \mathbb{R}^6$ (composed of linear and angular velocities). > > $$\xi = \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix} = J(q)\,\dot{q}$$ For an $n$-DOF manipulator, the Jacobian is a **6 × n** matrix (3 × n for planar manipulators), whose columns correspond to contributions from individual joints. The first three rows form the **linear velocity Jacobian** $J_v$, and the last three rows form the **angular velocity Jacobian** $J_\omega$. $$J(q) = \begin{bmatrix} J_v(q) \\ J_\omega(q) \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{(6 × n matrix)}$$ ## 1.2 Potential Energy — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Potential Energy of a Manipulator (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):** > The total **gravitational potential energy** $P(q)$ of a robotic manipulator is the scalar sum of the potential energies of all its links, evaluated at the height of each link's center of mass measured in the **base (inertial) frame**, under a uniform gravity field $\mathbf{g}$. > > $$P(q) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i\,\mathbf{g}^{T}\,{}^{0}\!r_{c_i}$$ > > where $m_i$ is the mass of link $i$, and ${}^{0}\!r_{c_i}$ is the position vector of the center of mass of link $i$ expressed in the base frame. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition **Velocity Jacobian — The "Steering Translator":** Imagine driving a car where the steering wheel and accelerator (the **joints**) are at the rear, but the car's body (the **end-effector**) at the front moves in a complex way. The **Velocity Jacobian is the "translation sheet"** that tells the driver: *"If I turn the steering wheel 1 radian per second, the car body will move forward at exactly 0.5 m/s and rotate at 0.1 rad/s."* It is a *first-order, linear, instantaneous* mapping — it does NOT tell you the position; it tells you only the **rate of change** at a particular joint configuration. **Potential Energy — The "Stored Climb":** Think of the manipulator as a chain of **mass-loaded springs hanging from the ceiling**. Each link is a weight suspended at a certain height. The **Potential Energy** simply adds up how much "gravitational work" each link is currently storing because of how high it is. **Lower the arm → less potential energy. Raise the arm → more potential energy.** In Lagrangian dynamics, this scalar function becomes the engine that produces the gravity term in the torque equation $\tau = \frac{d}{dt}\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}} - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q}$. ## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics in **Bold** - **J(q)**: 6 × n matrix for spatial manipulators; **3 × n for planar** manipulators. - **n**: Number of joints (degrees of freedom). - **m_i**: Mass of link $i$ — a **scalar** in kg. - **g**: Gravitational acceleration — **9.81 m/s²** along the vertical axis. - **${}^{0}\!r_{c_i}$**: Position of COM of link $i$ in the **base frame** — a 3 × 1 vector. - **Units of P(q)**: **Joules (J)**. - **Singularity**: Configuration where $\det(J J^{T}) = 0$ — Jacobian loses rank. > [!NOTE] > The Velocity Jacobian is derived from either the **Direct (Geometric) Method** using cross-products of DH-frame axis vectors and position vectors, or the **Differential (Analytical) Method** using partial derivatives of the forward kinematics. Both produce identical results. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Velocity Jacobian — Column-by-Column Logic The $i$-th column of $J(q)$ is the **end-effector spatial velocity contribution** when only joint $i$ moves with unit velocity while all others are locked. Its structure depends purely on the joint type. ### 2.1.1 For a **Revolute Joint** (Rotational) When joint $i$ rotates about axis $\hat{z}_{i-1}$: - Every point on link $i$ (and all downstream links) undergoes **circular motion** about that axis. - The end-effector's linear velocity = (axis) × (lever arm from axis to end-effector). - The end-effector's angular velocity = the axis itself. $$J_i = \begin{bmatrix} \hat{z}_{i-1} \times \left({}^{0}\!o_n - {}^{0}\!o_{i-1}\right) \\ \hat{z}_{i-1} \end{bmatrix} \quad (6 \times 1)$$ ### 2.1.2 For a **Prismatic Joint** (Linear) When joint $i$ translates along axis $\hat{z}_{i-1}$: - All points translate together — no rotation. - The end-effector's linear velocity = the axis direction. - The end-effector's angular velocity = zero. $$J_i = \begin{bmatrix} \hat{z}_{i-1} \\ 0_{3 \times 1} \end{bmatrix} \quad (6 \times 1)$$ ### 2.1.3 Why This Works — The "Why" Behind Each Step 1. **Why cross-product for revolute joints?** The instantaneous linear velocity of a point rotating about an axis is given by $\omega \times r$. Here $\omega = \dot{q}_i \hat{z}_{i-1}$ and $r$ is the vector from any point on the axis to the end-effector. Choosing ${}^{0}\!o_{i-1}$ (origin of frame $i-1$) is convenient because it lies on the axis. 2. **Why does the angular contribution for revolute joints equal $\hat{z}_{i-1}$?** Because a single rotating joint contributes a pure angular velocity vector along its own axis, regardless of downstream geometry. 3. **Why is angular velocity zero for prismatic joints?** A pure translation has no rotation, so $\omega$ is identically zero. 4. **Why evaluate in the base frame?** All end-effector velocities are reported with respect to the **stationary inertial (base) frame**, so every quantity must be expressed in frame $\{0\}$. ## 2.2 The Velocity Jacobian — Block Form $$J(q) = \begin{bmatrix} \hat{z}_{0} \times ({}^{0}\!o_n - {}^{0}\!o_{0}) & \hat{z}_{1} \times ({}^{0}\!o_n - {}^{0}\!o_{1}) & \cdots & \hat{z}_{n-1} \times ({}^{0}\!o_n - {}^{0}\!o_{n-1}) \\ \hat{z}_{0} & \hat{z}_{1} & \cdots & \hat{z}_{n-1} \end{bmatrix}$$ (With prismatic columns replaced by $[\hat{z}_{i-1};\ 0]$ instead.) ## 2.3 The Potential Energy Expression — Link-by-Link For each link $i$ with mass $m_i$ and center of mass at position ${}^{0}\!r_{c_i}$ (expressed in the base frame): $$P_i = m_i\, \mathbf{g}^{T}\, {}^{0}\!r_{c_i}$$ If we choose the gravity vector as $\mathbf{g} = [0,\ 0,\ -9.81]^T$ m/s² (i.e., the **force per unit mass is $-\mathbf{g}$ acting downward**, where $\mathbf{g}$ points downward), then: $$P_i = -m_i\, g\, h_i \quad \text{where } h_i = {}^{0}\!r_{c_i,z}$$ **Total potential energy** of the manipulator: $$\boxed{P(q) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i\, \mathbf{g}^{T}\, {}^{0}\!r_{c_i}(q)}$$ ### 2.3.1 Why This Expression? 1. **Why does $P$ depend on $q$?** The center of mass of each link moves in Cartesian space as the joints rotate/translate, so ${}^{0}\!r_{c_i}$ is an implicit function of $q$. 2. **Why is $P$ a scalar?** Energy is a scalar; it has magnitude but no direction. 3. **Why include all $n$ links?** The manipulator is a rigid multi-body system; gravitational energy of the entire chain must be summed. 4. **Why express in the base frame?** The gravitational field is uniform and vertical, referenced to the inertial (base) frame. 5. **Why is the sign sometimes written as $-mgh$?** It depends on the chosen sign convention for $\mathbf{g}$. The expression $m_i \mathbf{g}^T {}^{0}\!r_{c_i}$ is the most **coordinate-free** form, valid for any orientation of $\mathbf{g}$. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Dimensions | Notes | |---|----------|---------|------------|-------| | 1 | End-effector velocity | $\xi = J(q)\dot{q}$ | $6 \times 1$ | Linear + angular stacked | | 2 | Jacobian column (revolute) | $J_i = \begin{bmatrix} z_{i-1} \times (o_n - o_{i-1}) \\ z_{i-1} \end{bmatrix}$ | $6 \times 1$ | Axis $\times$ lever arm | | 3 | Jacobian column (prismatic) | $J_i = \begin{bmatrix} z_{i-1} \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$ | $6 \times 1$ | Pure translation | | 4 | Linear velocity Jacobian | $J_v = \dfrac{\partial x}{\partial q}$ | $3 \times n$ | Analytical alternative | | 5 | Angular velocity Jacobian | $J_\omega$ | $3 \times n$ | Sum of $z_{i-1}$ for active revolutes | | 6 | Static force relation | $\tau = J^{T}(q)\,F$ | $n \times 1$ | $F$ is end-effector wrench | | 7 | Singularity condition | $\det(J J^{T}) = 0$ | scalar | Loss of full rank | | 8 | Manipulability | $w = \sqrt{\det(J J^{T})}$ | scalar | Yoshikawa's measure | | 9 | Link $i$ potential energy | $P_i = m_i\, g^{T}\,{}^{0}\!r_{c_i}$ | J (Joules) | $r_{c_i}$ in base frame | | 10 | Total potential energy | $P(q) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i\, g^{T}\,{}^{0}\!r_{c_i}(q)$ | J (Joules) | Scalar function of $q$ | | 11 | Gravity torque vector | $g(q) = \left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial q}\right)^{T}$ | $n \times 1$ | Used in Euler-Lagrange | | 12 | Center of mass position | ${}^{0}\!r_{c_i} = {}^{0}\!T_{i}\,{}^{i}\!r_{c_i}$ | $3 \times 1$ | Local COM is constant | > [!NOTE] > The notation $z_{i-1}$ and $o_{i-1}$ refers to the $z$-axis unit vector and origin of frame $\{i-1\}$ expressed in the **base frame** $\{0\}$. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Velocity Jacobian**: Used in real-time **operational-space control**, **singularity avoidance algorithms**, **resolved-rate motion planning** ($\dot{q} = J^{+}\xi_{desired}$ where $J^{+}$ is the Moore-Penrose pseudo-inverse), and **bilateral teleoperation** (mapping master–slave velocities). - **Potential Energy**: Forms the **gravity compensation term** in the Lagrangian dynamic model $\tau = M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + g(q)$. Industrial robots like the KUKA KR-AGILUS use gravity-compensated control to reduce motor torque when the arm is fully extended. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Velocity Jacobian (Geometric / Direct Method) ### Step 1 — Begin from Forward Kinematics The end-effector pose is given by the forward kinematics function: $$X = \Phi(q)$$ where $X \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ ($m = 6$ for spatial, $3$ for planar) and $q \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$. ### Step 2 — Differentiate Both Sides with Respect to Time By the **chain rule of multivariable calculus**: $$\dot{X} = \frac{\partial \Phi}{\partial q}\,\dot{q} = J(q)\,\dot{q}$$ This defines the **Jacobian matrix** as the matrix of first-order partial derivatives of the forward kinematics. ### Step 3 — For a Single Revolute Joint Consider joint $i$ rotating about axis $\hat{z}_{i-1}$ with rate $\dot{q}_i$. The end-effector frame $\{n\}$ origin moves on a circle about the axis $\hat{z}_{i-1}$. The position of $o_n$ relative to any point on the axis (say $o_{i-1}$) is $r = o_n - o_{i-1}$. The instantaneous linear velocity of $o_n$ is: $$v_n = \dot{q}_i\,\hat{z}_{i-1} \times (o_n - o_{i-1})$$ The angular velocity of the end-effector frame is the same as the joint axis vector (since downstream links rotate together with the joint): $$\omega_n = \dot{q}_i\,\hat{z}_{i-1}$$ ### Step 4 — Stack into a Column Vector The $i$-th column of the Jacobian captures the contribution of unit $\dot{q}_i$: $$J_i = \begin{bmatrix} \hat{z}_{i-1} \times (o_n - o_{i-1}) \\ \hat{z}_{i-1} \end{bmatrix}$$ ### Step 5 — For a Single Prismatic Joint Joint $i$ translates along $\hat{z}_{i-1}$. There is no rotation, and the end-effector translates together: $$v_n = \dot{q}_i\,\hat{z}_{i-1}, \quad \omega_n = 0$$ $$J_i = \begin{bmatrix} \hat{z}_{i-1} \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$ ### Step 6 — Assemble the Full Jacobian By the **principle of superposition** (valid because the mapping $\dot{q} \mapsto \xi$ is linear): $$J(q) = \begin{bmatrix} \hat{z}_{0} \times (o_n - o_0) & \hat{z}_{1} \times (o_n - o_1) & \cdots & \hat{z}_{n-1} \times (o_n - o_{n-1}) \\ \hat{z}_{0} & \hat{z}_{1} & \cdots & \hat{z}_{n-1} \end{bmatrix}$$ (with prismatic columns appropriately modified). ## 3.2 Worked Example — 2R Planar Manipulator A 2R planar arm has link lengths $l_1, l_2$ with revolute joints at the base and the elbow. Forward kinematics: $$\begin{aligned} x &= l_1\cos q_1 + l_2\cos(q_1 + q_2) \\ y &= l_1\sin q_1 + l_2\sin(q_1 + q_2) \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 1 — Compute Partial Derivatives for Linear Velocity $$\frac{\partial x}{\partial q_1} = -l_1\sin q_1 - l_2\sin(q_1 + q_2)$$ $$\frac{\partial x}{\partial q_2} = -l_2\sin(q_1 + q_2)$$ $$\frac{\partial y}{\partial q_1} = l_1\cos q_1 + l_2\cos(q_1 + q_2)$$ $$\frac{\partial y}{\partial q_2} = l_2\cos(q_1 + q_2)$$ ### Step 2 — Compute Angular Velocity Contribution For a planar arm rotating about the base $z$-axis, $\hat{z}_0 = \hat{z}_1 = [0,\ 0,\ 1]^T$, so: $$J_\omega = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ ### Step 3 — Assemble the Full 3 × 2 Jacobian $$J(q) = \begin{bmatrix} -l_1\sin q_1 - l_2\sin(q_1 + q_2) & -l_2\sin(q_1 + q_2) \\ l_1\cos q_1 + l_2\cos(q_1 + q_2) & l_2\cos(q_1 + q_2) \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ ### Step 4 — Verify the Geometric Method Using the geometric method, ${}^{0}\!o_0 = [0,0,0]^T$, ${}^{0}\!o_1 = [l_1\cos q_1,\ l_1\sin q_1,\ 0]^T$, ${}^{0}\!o_2 = [l_1\cos q_1 + l_2\cos(q_1+q_2),\ l_1\sin q_1 + l_2\sin(q_1+q_2),\ 0]^T$: $$J_1 = \begin{bmatrix} \hat{z}_0 \times (o_2 - o_0) \\ \hat{z}_0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -l_1\sin q_1 - l_2\sin(q_1+q_2) \\ l_1\cos q_1 + l_2\cos(q_1+q_2) \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ $$J_2 = \begin{bmatrix} \hat{z}_1 \times (o_2 - o_1) \\ \hat{z}_1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -l_2\sin(q_1+q_2) \\ l_2\cos(q_1+q_2) \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Both methods agree**, confirming the correctness. ## 3.3 Derivation of the Potential Energy Expression ### Step 1 — Recall Definition of Gravitational Potential Energy For a point mass $m$ at position $r$ in a uniform gravitational field $\mathbf{g}$, the potential energy is the dot product of weight and height: $$P = -\mathbf{F}_g \cdot r = -(-m\mathbf{g}) \cdot r = m\,\mathbf{g}^{T} r$$ The negative sign on $m\mathbf{g}$ reflects that gravity **acts downward**; potential energy decreases as the mass descends. ### Step 2 — Extend to a Rigid Link A rigid link is a continuous distribution of mass. Integrating the elemental potential energy over the link volume $V_i$: $$P_i = \int_{V_i} \rho(\mathbf{r})\,\mathbf{g}^{T}\mathbf{r}\,dV$$ ### Step 3 — Use the Definition of Center of Mass The center of mass is defined as: $${}^{0}\!r_{c_i} = \frac{1}{m_i}\int_{V_i} \rho(\mathbf{r})\,\mathbf{r}\,dV$$ Substituting: $$P_i = m_i\,\mathbf{g}^{T}\!\left(\frac{1}{m_i}\int_{V_i}\rho(\mathbf{r})\,\mathbf{r}\,dV\right) = m_i\,\mathbf{g}^{T}\,{}^{0}\!r_{c_i}$$ ### Step 4 — Sum Over All Links The total potential energy of the chain is the sum of individual link contributions: $$P(q) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i\,\mathbf{g}^{T}\,{}^{0}\!r_{c_i}(q)$$ The dependence on $q$ arises because the center of mass of each link moves as joints rotate/translate. ## 3.4 Expressing COM Position from Forward Kinematics The COM of link $i$ has a constant position ${}^{i}\!r_{c_i}$ in its **own frame** $\{i\}$. The base-frame position is: $${}^{0}\!r_{c_i} = {}^{0}\!T_{i}(q)\begin{bmatrix} {}^{i}\!r_{c_i} \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ where ${}^{0}\!T_{i}$ is the cumulative DH transformation from base to frame $\{i\}$. This makes $P$ an explicit function of $q$: $$P(q) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i\,\mathbf{g}^{T}\,{}^{0}\!T_{i}(q)\begin{bmatrix} {}^{i}\!r_{c_i} \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ ## 3.5 Worked Example — 2R Planar Manipulator Assume each link is a uniform rod of mass $m_1, m_2$ with COM at mid-length: ${}^{1}\!r_{c_1} = [l_1/2,\ 0,\ 0]^T$, ${}^{2}\!r_{c_2} = [l_2/2,\ 0,\ 0]^T$. Gravity $\mathbf{g} = [0,\ -g,\ 0]^T$ (with $g = 9.81$ m/s² pointing down). ### Step 1 — COM of Link 1 in Base Frame Using ${}^{0}\!T_1$: $${}^{0}\!r_{c_1} = \begin{bmatrix} (l_1/2)\cos q_1 \\ (l_1/2)\sin q_1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$ ### Step 2 — COM of Link 2 in Base Frame $${}^{0}\!r_{c_2} = \begin{bmatrix} l_1\cos q_1 + (l_2/2)\cos(q_1+q_2) \\ l_1\sin q_1 + (l_2/2)\sin(q_1+q_2) \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$ ### Step 3 — Compute Each Potential Energy $$\begin{aligned} P_1 &= m_1\,[0,\ -g,\ 0]\,\begin{bmatrix} (l_1/2)\cos q_1 \\ (l_1/2)\sin q_1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = -\frac{m_1 g l_1}{2}\sin q_1 \end{aligned}$$ $$\begin{aligned} P_2 &= m_2\,[0,\ -g,\ 0]\,\begin{bmatrix} l_1\cos q_1 + (l_2/2)\cos(q_1+q_2) \\ l_1\sin q_1 + (l_2/2)\sin(q_1+q_2) \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \\ P_2 &= -m_2 g\,l_1\sin q_1 - \frac{m_2 g l_2}{2}\sin(q_1+q_2) \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 4 — Total Potential Energy $$P(q) = -\left(m_1 + 2m_2\right)\frac{g l_1}{2}\sin q_1 - \frac{m_2 g l_2}{2}\sin(q_1+q_2)$$ ### Step 5 — Gravity Torque Vector Differentiating with respect to $q$: $$g(q) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial q_1} \\[6pt] \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial q_2} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -\left(m_1 + 2m_2\right)\dfrac{g l_1}{2}\cos q_1 - \dfrac{m_2 g l_2}{2}\cos(q_1+q_2) \\[6pt] -\dfrac{m_2 g l_2}{2}\cos(q_1+q_2) \end{bmatrix}$$ These torques exactly cancel gravity at static equilibrium, which is the foundation of **gravity compensation** in industrial robots. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Velocity Mapping via Jacobian ```mermaid flowchart LR A["JOINT SPACE
q (n x 1)"] B["JOINT VELOCITY
q-dot (n x 1)"] C["JACOBIAN MATRIX
J(q) - 6 x n"] D["END-EFFECTOR
LINEAR VELOCITY v
3 x 1"] E["END-EFFECTOR
ANGULAR VELOCITY w
3 x 1"] F["SPATIAL VELOCITY
xi = [v; w] 6 x 1"] G["TASK / WORKSPACE
Cartesian motion"] A -.defines.-> C B -- multiplied by --> C C -- "J_v * q-dot" --> D C -- "J_w * q-dot" --> E D --> F E --> F F --> G style A fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style B fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style C fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 style D fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style E fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style F fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style G fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Potential Energy Computation Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD P1["JOINT VARIABLE q
(n x 1)"] P2["DH FORWARD KINEMATICS
0 to i transforms
0_T_i q"] P3["LOCAL COM POSITION
i_r_ci (constant 3 x 1)"] P4["BASE-FRAME COM
0_r_ci = 0_T_i x i_r_ci"] P5["MASS mi (scalar)"] P6["GRAVITY VECTOR g
3 x 1 = 0 0 9.81"] P7["LINK POTENTIAL ENERGY
Pi = mi gT 0_r_ci"] P8["SUMMATION BLOCK
i = 1 to n"] P9["TOTAL POTENTIAL ENERGY
P of q (scalar Joules)"] P10["PARTIAL DERIVATIVE
dP/dq"] P11["GRAVITY TORQUE
g of q (n x 1)"] P1 --> P2 P2 --> P4 P3 --> P4 P4 --> P7 P5 --> P7 P6 --> P7 P7 --> P8 P8 --> P9 P9 --> P10 P10 --> P11 style P1 fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style P2 fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style P3 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style P4 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style P5 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style P6 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style P7 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style P8 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style P9 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 style P10 fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style P11 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Jacobian Computation ```mermaid flowchart LR Q1["Joint Variable q_i"] Q2["Joint Type
Revolute or Prismatic"] Q3["Frame i minus 1
z-axis and origin"] Q4["End-effector
origin o_n"] Q5["Column Constructor
For revolute z x on minus o_i minus 1
For prismatic z and 0"] Q6["Column Vector J_i
6 x 1"] Q7["Concatenate All
J = J_1 J_2 ... J_n"] Q8["Full Jacobian
J of q 6 x n"] Q1 --> Q5 Q2 --> Q5 Q3 --> Q5 Q4 --> Q5 Q5 --> Q6 Q6 --> Q7 Q7 --> Q8 style Q1 fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style Q2 fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style Q3 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style Q4 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style Q5 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 style Q6 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style Q7 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style Q8 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 ``` # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define the Velocity Jacobian of a robotic manipulator. What is its physical significance?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Velocity Jacobian** $J(q)$ is a matrix function of joint positions that linearly maps the joint-space velocity vector $\dot{q}$ to the end-effector spatial velocity $\xi$ in the Cartesian task space. $$\xi = \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix} = J(q)\,\dot{q}$$ **Physical significance (2 marks for explanation):** 1. It represents the **instantaneous velocity transformation** between joint space and Cartesian space at a given configuration. 2. It enables **resolved-rate motion control** — given a desired end-effector velocity, joint velocities can be computed as $\dot{q} = J^{-1}\xi$ (or via pseudo-inverse $J^{+}$ when $J$ is non-square). 3. It reveals **singularities** — configurations where $J$ loses rank and the manipulator loses one or more degrees of freedom in task space. 4. It enables **static force/torque mapping** through its transpose: $\tau = J^{T} F$. --- ### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Write the expression for the total potential energy of an $n$-DOF robotic manipulator. Identify each term.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** $$P(q) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i\,\mathbf{g}^{T}\,{}^{0}\!r_{c_i}(q)$$ **Identification of terms (2 marks):** - **$m_i$** — Mass of link $i$ (in kg). - **$\mathbf{g}$** — Gravitational acceleration vector in the base frame (in m/s²), typically $[0,\ 0,\ -9.81]^T$. - **${}^{0}\!r_{c_i}(q)$** — Position vector of the center of mass of link $i$ expressed in the base frame $\{0\}$, which is an implicit function of joint variables $q$. - **$P_i$** — Potential energy contribution from link $i$. - The summation runs from $i = 1$ to $n$, where $n$ is the number of links/degrees of freedom of the manipulator. --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Question]** **(a)** For a 3R spatial manipulator, derive the columns of the velocity Jacobian corresponding to: (i) the first revolute joint, (ii) the second revolute joint, (iii) the third revolute joint. State the assumptions used in your derivation. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For the same manipulator, given the joint positions $q_1 = \pi/4$, $q_2 = \pi/6$, $q_3 = \pi/3$, and the end-effector position computed from forward kinematics as ${}^{0}\!o_3 = [0.6,\ 0.4,\ 0.3]^T$ m, determine the columns of the Jacobian if the origins of the intermediate frames are ${}^{0}\!o_0 = [0,\ 0,\ 0]^T$, ${}^{0}\!o_1 = [0.2,\ 0,\ 0.3]^T$, ${}^{0}\!o_2 = [0.4,\ 0.2,\ 0.3]^T$, and the joint axes (in base frame) are $\hat{z}_0 = [0,\ 0,\ 1]^T$, $\hat{z}_1 = [0,\ 1,\ 0]^T$, $\hat{z}_2 = [1,\ 0,\ 0]^T$. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation (7 marks):** **Assumptions:** 1. All joints are **revolute** with one degree of freedom each. 2. The DH convention is used to assign frames, with the $z$-axis of frame $\{i-1\}$ aligned with joint $i$. 3. All kinematic quantities are expressed in the **base frame** $\{0\}$. **General column formula for a revolute joint $i$:** $$J_i = \begin{bmatrix} \hat{z}_{i-1} \times \left({}^{0}\!o_n - {}^{0}\!o_{i-1}\right) \\ \hat{z}_{i-1} \end{bmatrix} \quad (6 \times 1)$$ **Derivation logic (3 marks):** - The end-effector linear velocity is $\omega_{i} \times r$ where $\omega_i = \dot{q}_i \hat{z}_{i-1}$ is the angular velocity of link $i$ and $r = o_n - o_{i-1}$ is the lever arm. - The end-effector angular velocity is $\omega_n = \dot{q}_i \hat{z}_{i-1}$ because all downstream links rigidly rotate with joint $i$. **Specific columns (4 marks):** $$\begin{aligned} J_1 &= \begin{bmatrix} \hat{z}_0 \times (o_3 - o_0) \\ \hat{z}_0 \end{bmatrix} \\[6pt] J_2 &= \begin{bmatrix} \hat{z}_1 \times (o_3 - o_1) \\ \hat{z}_1 \end{bmatrix} \\[6pt] J_3 &= \begin{bmatrix} \hat{z}_2 \times (o_3 - o_2) \\ \hat{z}_2 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned}$$ **Full Jacobian (6 × 3):** $$J(q) = \begin{bmatrix} \hat{z}_0 \times (o_3 - o_0) & \hat{z}_1 \times (o_3 - o_1) & \hat{z}_2 \times (o_3 - o_2) \\ \hat{z}_0 & \hat{z}_1 & \hat{z}_2 \end{bmatrix}$$ --- **(b) Numerical Evaluation (7 marks):** **Column 1 — Joint 1 revolute about $\hat{z}_0 = [0, 0, 1]^T$:** $$\begin{aligned} o_3 - o_0 &= \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 \\ 0.4 \\ 0.3 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 \\ 0.4 \\ 0.3 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned}$$ $$\begin{aligned} \hat{z}_0 \times (o_3 - o_0) &= \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \times \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 \\ 0.4 \\ 0.3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} (0)(0.3) - (1)(0.4) \\ (1)(0.6) - (0)(0.3) \\ (0)(0.4) - (0)(0.6) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.4 \\ 0.6 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned}$$ $$J_1 = \begin{bmatrix} -0.4 \\ 0.6 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Column 2 — Joint 2 revolute about $\hat{z}_1 = [0, 1, 0]^T$:** $$\begin{aligned} o_3 - o_1 &= \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 \\ 0.4 \\ 0.3 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 0.2 \\ 0 \\ 0.3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.4 \\ 0.4 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned}$$ $$\begin{aligned} \hat{z}_1 \times (o_3 - o_1) &= \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \times \begin{bmatrix} 0.4 \\ 0.4 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} (1)(0) - (0)(0.4) \\ (0)(0.4) - (0)(0) \\ (0)(0.4) - (1)(0.4) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ -0.4 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned}$$ $$J_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ -0.4 \\ 0 \\ 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Column 3 — Joint 3 revolute about $\hat{z}_2 = [1, 0, 0]^T$:** $$\begin{aligned} o_3 - o_2 &= \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 \\ 0.4 \\ 0.3 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 0.4 \\ 0.2 \\ 0.3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.2 \\ 0.2 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned}$$ $$\begin{aligned} \hat{z}_2 \times (o_3 - o_2) &= \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \times \begin{bmatrix} 0.2 \\ 0.2 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} (0)(0) - (0)(0.2) \\ (0)(0.2) - (1)(0) \\ (1)(0.2) - (0)(0.2) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 0.2 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned}$$ $$J_3 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 0.2 \\ 1 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Final Jacobian (6 × 3):** $$J = \begin{bmatrix} -0.4 & 0 & 0 \\ 0.6 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -0.4 & 0.2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$ --- ### Question B: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Question — Alternative Choice]** **(a)** Define **gravitational potential energy** of a robotic manipulator. Derive the general expression for the total potential energy of an $n$-DOF manipulator, clearly stating the physical meaning of each symbol used. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 2R planar manipulator has link lengths $l_1 = 1$ m, $l_2 = 0.5$ m, with link masses $m_1 = 2$ kg, $m_2 = 1$ kg. The center of mass of each link is located at the midpoint of the link. Gravity acts downward with $g = 9.81$ m/s². Compute the total potential energy of the manipulator when $q_1 = 60°$ and $q_2 = 45°$. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation (7 marks):** **Definition (2 marks):** The **gravitational potential energy** of a manipulator is the scalar energy stored in its links due to their positions in the Earth's gravitational field. It is the work done against gravity to assemble the manipulator configuration from a reference (zero-potential) state. **Derivation (5 marks):** For a point mass $m$ at position $\mathbf{r}$ in a uniform gravitational field $\mathbf{g}$: $$dP = -m\mathbf{g} \cdot d\mathbf{r} \quad \Rightarrow \quad P = m\mathbf{g}^{T}\mathbf{r} + C$$ Choosing the zero reference at the origin, $C = 0$. For a **rigid link** $i$ of mass $m_i$ with center of mass at ${}^{0}\!r_{c_i}$: $$P_i = m_i\,\mathbf{g}^{T}\,{}^{0}\!r_{c_i}$$ This follows from the **definition of center of mass**: $${}^{0}\!r_{c_i} = \frac{1}{m_i}\int_{V_i} \rho(\mathbf{r})\,\mathbf{r}\,dV$$ Summing over all $n$ links: $$P(q) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i\,\mathbf{g}^{T}\,{}^{0}\!r_{c_i}(q)$$ **Symbols:** - $P_i$ — potential energy of link $i$ (Joules) - $m_i$ — mass of link $i$ (kg) - $\mathbf{g}$ — gravity vector in base frame (m/s²) - ${}^{0}\!r_{c_i}(q)$ — position of COM of link $i$ in base frame (m), function of $q$ - $n$ — number of links/degrees of freedom --- **(b) Numerical Evaluation (7 marks):** Given: $l_1 = 1$ m, $l_2 = 0.5$ m, $m_1 = 2$ kg, $m_2 = 1$ kg, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $q_1 = 60°$, $q_2 = 45°$. **Step 1 — COM positions (1 mark):** $$\begin{aligned} {}^{0}\!r_{c_1} &= \begin{bmatrix} (l_1/2)\cos q_1 \\ (l_1/2)\sin q_1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5\cos 60° \\ 0.5\sin 60° \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.25 \\ 0.4330 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$ $$\begin{aligned} {}^{0}\!r_{c_2} &= \begin{bmatrix} l_1\cos q_1 + (l_2/2)\cos(q_1+q_2) \\ l_1\sin q_1 + (l_2/2)\sin(q_1+q_2) \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Compute $q_1 + q_2 = 105°$ (1 mark):** $$\begin{aligned} \cos 105° &= -0.2588 \\ \sin 105° &= 0.9659 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Evaluate COM of link 2 (1 mark):** $$\begin{aligned} {}^{0}\!r_{c_2} &= \begin{bmatrix} 1(0.5) + 0.25(-0.2588) \\ 1(0.8660) + 0.25(0.9659) \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 - 0.0647 \\ 0.8660 + 0.2415 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.4353 \\ 1.1075 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Compute $P_1$ (1 mark):** Using $\mathbf{g} = [0,\ -g,\ 0]^T$ (pointing downward): $$\begin{aligned} P_1 &= m_1\,\mathbf{g}^{T}\,{}^{0}\!r_{c_1} = 2\,[0,\ -9.81,\ 0]\begin{bmatrix} 0.25 \\ 0.4330 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = 2(-9.81)(0.4330) = -8.495 \text{ J} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Compute $P_2$ (1 mark):** $$\begin{aligned} P_2 &= m_2\,\mathbf{g}^{T}\,{}^{0}\!r_{c_2} = 1\,[0,\ -9.81,\ 0]\begin{bmatrix} 0.4353 \\ 1.1075 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = -9.81(1.1075) = -10.865 \text{ J} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 6 — Total Potential Energy (2 marks):** $$P = P_1 + P_2 = -8.495 + (-10.865) = -19.36 \text{ J}$$ The negative sign indicates that the system is at a lower gravitational potential than the zero reference (origin of base frame). The manipulator stores approximately **19.36 J** of potential energy at the given configuration. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting the cross-product sign convention**: The order $\hat{z} \times (o_n - o_{i-1})$ is **strict** — reversing it gives the wrong sign of linear velocity (loses 1–2 marks). > 2. **Mixing base-frame and tool-frame expressions**: All $z_{i-1}$ and $o_i$ must be expressed in the **base frame** $\{0\}$, not in the local frame $\{i-1\}$. KTU examiners specifically test this. > 3. **Treating prismatic joints as revolute**: Prismatic columns must be $[\hat{z}_{i-1};\ 0]$, **not** $[\hat{z}_{i-1} \times (o_n - o_{i-1});\ \hat{z}_{i-1}]$. Marks lost here are very common. > 4. **Sign of gravity in potential energy**: Using $\mathbf{g} = [0, 0, -9.81]^T$ vs. $[0, 0, 9.81]^T$ flips the sign of $P$. Be consistent with the chosen convention. > 5. **Forgetting to express COM in the base frame**: $P_i = m_i \mathbf{g}^{T} {}^{0}\!r_{c_i}$, not $m_i \mathbf{g}^{T} {}^{i}\!r_{c_i}$. The forward transformation ${}^{0}\!T_i$ must be applied first. > 6. **Not summing over all links**: Partial credit of 2–3 marks will be awarded if only one link's $P_i$ is computed. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Velocity Jacobian and Potential Energy** - ☐ **Velocity Jacobian $J(q)$** is a **6 × n** matrix (3 × n for planar) that maps $\dot{q} \mapsto \xi = [v;\ \omega]$. - ☐ The $i$-th column of $J$ corresponds to the **end-effector velocity contribution from unit $\dot{q}_i$**. - ☐ For a **revolute** joint $i$: $J_i = \begin{bmatrix} \hat{z}_{i-1} \times (o_n - o_{i-1}) \\ \hat{z}_{i-1} \end{bmatrix}$. - ☐ For a **prismatic** joint $i$: $J_i = \begin{bmatrix} \hat{z}_{i-1} \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$. - ☐ The **angular part** of $J_i$ is **always** the joint axis for revolute joints and **zero** for prismatic joints. - ☐ All quantities $\hat{z}_{i-1}$, $o_{i-1}$, and $o_n$ **must be expressed in the base frame** $\{0\}$. - ☐ The Jacobian has two methods of derivation: **Geometric (Direct)** using cross-products, and **Analytical (Differential)** using $\partial \Phi / \partial q$. Both give the same result. - ☐ **Singularities** occur when $J$ loses rank; detect via $\det(JJ^T) = 0$ (for square Jacobians) or rank deficiency. - ☐ The **static force relationship** is $\tau = J^{T}(q) F$ (force-torque duality). - ☐ The **resolved-rate** solution is $\dot{q} = J^{+}\xi_{desired}$ where $J^{+} = J^T(JJ^T)^{-1}$ is the Moore-Penrose pseudo-inverse. - ☐ **Potential energy** $P(q) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i\, \mathbf{g}^{T}\, {}^{0}\!r_{c_i}(q)$ is a **scalar** function of joint variables. - ☐ The center of mass position in the base frame is computed via forward kinematics: ${}^{0}\!r_{c_i} = {}^{0}\!T_i \begin{bmatrix} {}^{i}\!r_{c_i} \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$. - ☐ The **gravity torque vector** used in the Euler-Lagrange equation is $g(q) = \left[\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial q}\right]^{T}$. - ☐ **Sign convention for $\mathbf{g}$**: If $\mathbf{g}$ points downward, the chosen form may be $[0,\ 0,\ -9.81]^T$ (force convention) or $[0,\ 0,\ 9.81]^T$ (acceleration convention). Be consistent. - ☐ **Units**: $J$ is dimensionless times m/s (per rad/s for revolute), and $P$ is in **Joules**. - ☐ KTU 2024 frequently tests: **(i)** Jacobian column formulas, **(ii)** $P$ for 2R/3R manipulators, **(iii)** Gravity torque derivation.

topics.title: Generalised Force
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT302 - INTRODUCTION TO ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Generalised Force

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of robotic manipulators and analytical mechanics, a **Generalised Force** is defined as the force (or moment) conjugate to a generalised coordinate, evaluated through the principle of virtual work. Mathematically, if a robotic system is described by a set of $n$ independent **generalised coordinates** $q_1, q_2, \dots, q_n$ (which for serial manipulators are typically the joint displacements), then the corresponding **Generalised Forces** $Q_1, Q_2, \dots, Q_n$ (typically joint torques $\tau_i$ for revolute joints) are the quantities whose product with the virtual displacement $\delta q_i$ equals the virtual work done by the actual applied forces.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> The *Generalised Force* in robotics is the virtual work conjugate of the generalised coordinate. It is fundamentally linked to the end-effector force through the **Jacobian Transpose** relationship:
> $$\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T(\mathbf{q}) \cdot \mathbf{F}$$
> where $\boldsymbol{\tau} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times 1}$ is the joint torque vector, $\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})$ is the Jacobian matrix, and $\mathbf{F}$ is the Cartesian force/moment vector at the end-effector.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Imagine you are controlling a **robotic arm** by turning its joints. When you apply a torque at a joint, the gripper at the tip exerts a pushing/pulling force on the environment. 

* The **joint torques** ($\tau_1, \tau_2$) are the "Generalised Forces" — they live in the **joint space**.
* The **gripper's push/pull** ($\mathbf{F}_x, \mathbf{F}_y$) lives in the **Cartesian (task) space**.

Think of it like the gears of a bicycle: A small force on the pedal (joint space) generates a large force on the rear wheel (Cartesian space). The **Jacobian Transpose ($\mathbf{J}^T$)** acts as the "translator" or "gear ratio" between these two worlds. If the arm is stretched out fully, a tiny joint torque can produce a huge end-effector force, but with low speed — and vice versa.

## 1.3 Standardised Units and Nomenclature

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Notations used by KTU Board Examiners:**
> * **Generalised Coordinate ($q_i$)** — angular (radians) or linear (metres) displacement of joint $i$
> * **Generalised Force ($Q_i$ or $\tau_i$)** — torque ($N \cdot m$) for revolute joints; force ($N$) for prismatic joints
> * **Virtual Displacement ($\delta q_i$)** — infinitesimal hypothetical change in $q_i$
> * **Virtual Work ($\delta W$)** — energy units, **Joules (J)** or **N·m**
> * **Jacobian Matrix ($\mathbf{J}$)** — dimension $m \times n$ (task space rows $\times$ joint space columns)

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Principle of Virtual Work (The Bedrock Concept)

The **Principle of Virtual Work** states that for a system in dynamic equilibrium, the total virtual work done by all applied forces through any virtual displacement consistent with the constraints is **zero**.

For a serial manipulator with $n$ joints, the end-effector experiences an external Cartesian force-moment vector $\mathbf{F} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times 1}$ (where typically $m=6$ for 3D space). The joint actuators must apply a torque vector $\boldsymbol{\tau} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times 1}$ to maintain static equilibrium against this external load.

The total virtual work done is:
$$\delta W = \boldsymbol{\tau}^T \delta \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{F}^T \delta \mathbf{x} = 0$$

where:
* $\delta \mathbf{q} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times 1}$ is the virtual joint displacement
* $\delta \mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times 1}$ is the virtual Cartesian displacement of the end-effector

## 2.2 Deriving the Relationship

The **differential kinematics** equation states:
$$\delta \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) \delta \mathbf{q}$$

Substituting this into the virtual work equation:
$$\delta W = \boldsymbol{\tau}^T \delta \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{F}^T \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) \delta \mathbf{q} = 0$$

Since this must hold for **all arbitrary** $\delta \mathbf{q}$:
$$\boldsymbol{\tau}^T - \mathbf{F}^T \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) = \mathbf{0}^T$$

Taking the transpose gives the **Master Equation of Generalised Force in Robotics**:
$$\boxed{\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T(\mathbf{q}) \cdot \mathbf{F}}$$

## 2.3 The "Why" and "How" — Logical Breakdown

* **Why does the transpose appear?** Because virtual work equates two energy flows. A row vector in one space becomes a column vector in the other when we isolate the force term — naturally producing a transpose.
* **How is this useful for control?** If we know the desired Cartesian force $\mathbf{F}$ (e.g., the robot must push with 10N to the right), we can directly compute the required joint torques $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ using $\mathbf{J}^T$. This is the foundation of **force control** and **impedance control**.
* **Static Force Transformation**: At any frozen configuration $\mathbf{q}$, the matrix $\mathbf{J}^T(\mathbf{q})$ acts as a linear map from task-space forces to joint-space torques.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Equation** | **Dimensions** | **Physical Meaning** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Virtual Work Equivalence | $\delta W = \boldsymbol{\tau}^T \delta \mathbf{q} = \mathbf{F}^T \delta \mathbf{x}$ | Joules (J) | Energy equivalence |
| Differential Kinematics | $\delta \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) \delta \mathbf{q}$ | $m \times 1 = (m \times n)(n \times 1)$ | Velocity/displacement mapping |
| Generalised Force (Master Eq.) | $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T(\mathbf{q}) \mathbf{F}$ | $n \times 1 = (n \times m)(m \times 1)$ | Joint torque from Cartesian force |
| Static Equilibrium | $\boldsymbol{\tau}_{\text{applied}} + \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F}_{\text{ext}} = \mathbf{0}$ | $n \times 1$ | Net generalised force is zero |
| Power Equivalence | $\boldsymbol{\tau}^T \dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{F}^T \mathbf{v}$ | Watts (W) | Power conservation across spaces |
| Power Form of Gen. Force | $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F}$ | $n \times 1$ | Derived from $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{J}\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ |
| Generalised Inertia Force | $\boldsymbol{\tau}_{\text{inertia}} = \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}}$ | $n \times 1$ | Lagrangian formulation |
| Lagrangian L | $L = K - V$ | J | Kinetic minus Potential energy |
| Euler-Lagrange | $\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = Q_i$ | $N \cdot m$ or $N$ | Definition of Generalised Force $Q_i$ |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Wrench Sensing**: Industrial robots like the *KUKA LBR iiwa* or *Franka Emika Panda* measure external wrenches at the wrist and use $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F}$ to estimate the contact forces for compliant assembly tasks.
* **Human-Robot Interaction (HRI)**: Cobots use this principle to detect collisions and react safely.
* **Gait Analysis in Legged Robots**: Mapping ground reaction forces to joint torques for stable walking.
* **Biomechanics**: Computing joint reaction forces in the human knee/hip during walking using external ground forces.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Generalised Force from the Euler-Lagrange Equation

The **Lagrangian** $L$ of a manipulator is defined as the difference between total kinetic energy $K$ and total potential energy $V$:
$$L(\mathbf{q}, \dot{\mathbf{q}}) = K(\mathbf{q}, \dot{\mathbf{q}}) - V(\mathbf{q})$$

The **Euler-Lagrange equation of motion** for the $i$-th joint is:
$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = Q_i \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, \dots, n$$

**Step-by-step expansion for the $i$-th coordinate:**

**Step 1**: The derivative of $L$ with respect to $\dot{q}_i$ gives the **generalised momentum conjugate** to $q_i$:
$$p_i = \frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}$$

**Step 2**: The total time derivative of the momentum is:
$$\frac{d p_i}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right)$$

**Step 3**: The rate of change of momentum minus the positional gradient of $L$ yields the **Generalised Force**:
$$Q_i = \frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i}$$

This is the *intrinsic, coordinate-free definition* of the Generalised Force $Q_i$ that KTU expects in Part A theory questions.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: The Master Equation $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F}$ (Full Exhaustive Derivation)

**Starting Premise**: Consider a serial manipulator with $n$ revolute joints in static equilibrium. The end-effector exerts a Cartesian wrench $\mathbf{F} \in \mathbb{R}^{6 \times 1}$ on the environment.

**Step 1 — State the Principle of Virtual Work for the system**:
The total virtual work performed by all active forces (joint torques and end-effector forces) through any kinematically admissible virtual displacement must vanish:
$$\delta W = \boldsymbol{\tau}^T \delta \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{F}^T \delta \mathbf{x} = 0$$

*Explanation*: The joint torques $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ do positive work $\boldsymbol{\tau}^T \delta \mathbf{q}$, while the external reaction force $\mathbf{F}$ at the end-effector does negative work $-\mathbf{F}^T \delta \mathbf{x}$ (force opposing the infinitesimal motion).

**Step 2 — Apply the kinematic constraint via the Jacobian**:
The end-effector virtual displacement is related to the joint virtual displacement by the Jacobian:
$$\delta \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) \delta \mathbf{q}$$

*Explanation*: This is the *first-order approximation* of the forward kinematics $\mathbf{x} = f(\mathbf{q})$; differentiating gives $\delta \mathbf{x} = \frac{\partial f}{\partial \mathbf{q}} \delta \mathbf{q} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) \delta \mathbf{q}$.

**Step 3 — Substitute the kinematic constraint into the virtual work**:
$$\delta W = \boldsymbol{\tau}^T \delta \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{F}^T \left[\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) \delta \mathbf{q}\right] = 0$$

**Step 4 — Factor out the common term $\delta \mathbf{q}^T$**:
$$\left[\boldsymbol{\tau}^T - \mathbf{F}^T \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\right] \delta \mathbf{q} = 0$$

**Step 5 — Apply the arbitrariness principle**:
Since $\delta \mathbf{q}$ is an *arbitrary* virtual displacement (the joints are independent), the bracketed coefficient must be the zero row vector:
$$\boldsymbol{\tau}^T - \mathbf{F}^T \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) = \mathbf{0}^T$$

**Step 6 — Transpose both sides**:
$$\boldsymbol{\tau} - \mathbf{J}^T(\mathbf{q}) \mathbf{F} = \mathbf{0}$$

**Step 7 — Rearrange to obtain the final result**:
$$\boxed{\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T(\mathbf{q}) \mathbf{F}}$$

*Conclusion*: The joint-space generalised force vector is obtained by mapping the Cartesian force vector through the transpose of the geometric Jacobian.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Power Equivalence Form

Starting from the velocity-level kinematics:
$$\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) \dot{\mathbf{q}}$$

The power delivered by the Cartesian force must equal the power delivered by the joint torques (energy conservation):
$$P = \mathbf{F}^T \dot{\mathbf{x}} = \boldsymbol{\tau}^T \dot{\mathbf{q}}$$

Substituting $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) \dot{\mathbf{q}}$:
$$\mathbf{F}^T \left[\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) \dot{\mathbf{q}}\right] = \boldsymbol{\tau}^T \dot{\mathbf{q}}$$

$$\left[\mathbf{F}^T \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) - \boldsymbol{\tau}^T\right] \dot{\mathbf{q}} = 0$$

Since $\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ is arbitrary:
$$\boldsymbol{\tau}^T = \mathbf{F}^T \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) \implies \boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T(\mathbf{q}) \mathbf{F}$$

This confirms the result from the **velocity/power domain**, which is often the cleaner approach expected in KTU 14-mark problems.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Generalised Force Variants

| **Context** | **Generalised Force Form** | **Remarks** |
|---|---|---|
| Conservative field (gravity) | $Q_i = -\frac{\partial V}{\partial q_i}$ | Negative gradient of potential |
| Applied non-conservative | $Q_i = \boldsymbol{\tau}_i^{\text{applied}}$ | External motor torque |
| Total dynamic (Euler-Lagrange) | $Q_i = \frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i}$ | Full dynamics formulation |
| From Cartesian wrench | $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T(\mathbf{q}) \mathbf{F}$ | Static/quasi-static mapping |
| Coriolis + Gravity decomposition | $Q_i = \sum_j M_{ij}\ddot{q}_j + \sum_{j,k} C_{ijk}\dot{q}_j\dot{q}_k + G_i$ | Full Newton-Euler terms |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Generalised Force Transformation Pipeline

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how a Cartesian wrench $\mathbf{F}$ is transformed into joint torques $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ through the Jacobian Transpose pathway, which is the central architecture of any force-controlled robotic system.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Cartesian Wrench F m by 1"]:::input --> B["Configuration q n by 1"]
    B --> C["Forward Kinematics f of q"]
    C --> D["Pose x m by 1"]
    A --> E["Multiply J Transpose times F"]
    D --> F["Compute Jacobian J of q 6 by n"]
    F --> E
    E --> G["Joint Torque Tau n by 1"]:::output
    G --> H["Actuator Command to Motors"]
    H --> I["Robot Joint Motion"]
    I --> J["New Configuration q new"]
    J --> B

    classDef input fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#005580,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Energy-Flow Diagram

This diagram captures the *virtual work equivalence* — the heart of the generalised force concept.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph jointSpace["JOINT SPACE DOMAIN"]
        Q["Generalised Force Tau n by 1"]:::gs --> W1["Virtual Work Tau transpose delta q"]
    end
    subgraph taskSpace["TASK SPACE CARTESIAN DOMAIN"]
        F["Cartesian Wrench F m by 1"]:::gs --> W2["Virtual Work F transpose delta x"]
    end
    Q -.->|Jacobian Transpose J transpose| F
    W1 ===|"Equal by Principle of Virtual Work"| W2
    deltaQ["delta q"]:::var --> W1
    deltaX["delta x"]:::var --> W2
    deltaQ -.->|delta x equals J delta q| deltaX

    classDef gs fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef var fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Function-Flow Architecture — Generalised Force in the Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Fext["External Wrench Fext on End Effector"]:::ext --> SumNode(("+"))
    Fdes["Desired Wrench Fdes from Task Planner"]:::cmd --> SumNode
    SumNode --> Ferr["Force Error delta F"]
    Ferr --> Kf["Force Controller Gain Matrix Kf"]
    Kf --> Jcalc["Compute J transpose from current q"]
    Jcalc --> TauOut["Joint Torque Command Tau"]:::out
    TauOut --> Motors["Joint Motors"]
    Motors --> Manip["Physical Manipulator"]
    Manip --> Sensors["Joint Encoders and FTSensors"]
    Sensors --> FK["Forward Kinematics Update"]
    FK --> Jcalc
    Sensors --> Fext

    classDef ext fill:#ffeeba,stroke:#856404,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef cmd fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram**: The Cartesian wrench $\mathbf{F}$ enters the system and is mapped via the Jacobian Transpose $\mathbf{J}^T(\mathbf{q})$ — which itself depends on the current joint configuration $\mathbf{q}$ — into the joint torque vector $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ that physically drives the manipulator.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define Generalised Force. State the principle of virtual work.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** A Generalised Force $Q_i$ is the quantity conjugate to a generalised coordinate $q_i$, such that the virtual work done by the system during a virtual displacement $\delta q_i$ is $\delta W = Q_i \, \delta q_i$. For a robotic manipulator with $n$ joints, the generalised forces are the joint torques $\tau_1, \tau_2, \dots, \tau_n$.

**Principle of Virtual Work (1 mark):** For a system in static equilibrium, the total virtual work done by all active forces through any virtual displacement consistent with the constraints is zero: $\delta W_{\text{total}} = 0$.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**State and explain the relationship between joint torques and end-effector forces using the Jacobian.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The joint torque vector $\boldsymbol{\tau} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times 1}$ is related to the end-effector Cartesian force-moment vector $\mathbf{F} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times 1}$ by the **Jacobian Transpose** relation:
$$\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T(\mathbf{q}) \cdot \mathbf{F}$$
where $\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})$ is the geometric Jacobian evaluated at the current joint configuration $\mathbf{q}$. This equation is derived from the **principle of virtual work** which equates the work done in joint space to that done in Cartesian space: $\boldsymbol{\tau}^T \delta \mathbf{q} = \mathbf{F}^T \delta \mathbf{x}$, combined with the differential kinematic constraint $\delta \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) \delta \mathbf{q}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** Starting from the principle of virtual work, derive the relationship between the joint torque vector $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ and the end-effector Cartesian force vector $\mathbf{F}$ for a serial manipulator. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a 2-DOF planar manipulator with link lengths $L_1$ and $L_2$ at a configuration where the Jacobian is given by:
$$\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1 s_1 - L_2 s_{12} & -L_2 s_{12} \\ L_1 c_1 + L_2 c_{12} & L_2 c_{12} \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$
If the end-effector exerts a Cartesian force-moment wrench $\mathbf{F} = [10 \text{ N}, \; 0 \text{ N}, \; 5 \text{ N·m}]^T$, compute the required joint torques $\tau_1$ and $\tau_2$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

##### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Assumptions stated (1 mark):**
1. The manipulator is in **static (or quasi-static) equilibrium**.
2. The end-effector is in **rigid contact** with the environment, exerting a pure Cartesian wrench $\mathbf{F}$.
3. Frictionless joints (or friction is lumped into $\boldsymbol{\tau}$).

**Step 1 (1 mark):** The total virtual work of all active forces is:
$$\delta W = \boldsymbol{\tau}^T \delta \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{F}^T \delta \mathbf{x} = 0$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** The differential kinematic relation is $\delta \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) \delta \mathbf{q}$.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Substituting:
$$\delta W = \boldsymbol{\tau}^T \delta \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{F}^T \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) \delta \mathbf{q} = 0$$
$$\left[\boldsymbol{\tau}^T - \mathbf{F}^T \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\right] \delta \mathbf{q} = 0$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Since $\delta \mathbf{q}$ is arbitrary:
$$\boldsymbol{\tau}^T = \mathbf{F}^T \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Transposing:
$$\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T(\mathbf{q}) \mathbf{F}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Final statement with physical interpretation: The joint torques (generalised forces) are obtained by mapping the Cartesian wrench through the transpose of the geometric Jacobian. The matrix $\mathbf{J}^T$ acts as a configuration-dependent force transformer.

**Energy/Engineering interpretation (1 mark):** The relation preserves power: $P = \boldsymbol{\tau}^T \dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{F}^T \mathbf{J}\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{F}^T \mathbf{v}$, confirming consistency of energy flow between joint and Cartesian spaces.

##### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute the Jacobian Transpose:
$$\mathbf{J}^T(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1 s_1 - L_2 s_{12} & L_1 c_1 + L_2 c_{12} & 0 \\ -L_2 s_{12} & L_2 c_{12} & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Let us assume the numerical configuration is such that $s_1 = \sin 30° = 0.5$, $c_1 = \cos 30° = 0.866$, $s_{12} = \sin 75° = 0.966$, $c_{12} = \cos 75° = 0.259$, and $L_1 = L_2 = 1$ m (typical textbook values).

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Substituting the values into the Jacobian:
$$\mathbf{J} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.5 - 0.966 & -0.966 \\ 0.866 + 0.259 & 0.259 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1.466 & -0.966 \\ 1.125 & 0.259 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** The Jacobian Transpose becomes:
$$\mathbf{J}^T = \begin{bmatrix} -1.466 & 1.125 & 0 \\ -0.966 & 0.259 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** The required joint torques are:
$$\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F} = \begin{bmatrix} -1.466 & 1.125 & 0 \\ -0.966 & 0.259 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 10 \\ 0 \\ 5 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Performing the matrix multiplication:
$$\tau_1 = (-1.466)(10) + (1.125)(0) + (0)(5) = -14.66 \text{ N·m}$$
$$\tau_2 = (-0.966)(10) + (0.259)(0) + (1)(5) = -9.66 + 5 = -4.66 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Final Answer:
$$\boxed{\tau_1 = -14.66 \text{ N·m}, \quad \tau_2 = -4.66 \text{ N·m}}$$

The negative signs indicate that the actuators must apply torques in the *opposite* direction to the applied Cartesian wrench's tendency to rotate the joints.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** With the help of the Euler-Lagrange equation, explain how the concept of generalised force arises in robot dynamics. Define the Lagrangian and derive the equation of motion for a simple 1-DOF pendulum of mass $m$ and length $L$ driven by an external torque $\tau$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 2-link RR planar manipulator has the following inertia matrix at a particular configuration:
$$\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{bmatrix} M_{11} & M_{12} \\ M_{12} & M_{22} \end{bmatrix}, \quad \text{where } M_{11} = 5, M_{12} = 2, M_{22} = 3$$
If the joint accelerations are $\ddot{\mathbf{q}} = [2, 1]^T$ rad/s², compute the inertia-induced generalised forces at the joints. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

##### Part (a) — Euler-Lagrange & Pendulum (7 marks)

**Definition of Lagrangian (1 mark):** $L(\mathbf{q}, \dot{\mathbf{q}}) = K - V$, where $K$ is the total kinetic energy and $V$ is the total potential energy of the system.

**Generalised Force from Euler-Lagrange (1 mark):** The Euler-Lagrange equation for the $i$-th generalised coordinate is:
$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = Q_i$$
where $Q_i$ is the **non-conservative generalised force** (e.g., the motor torque $\tau_i$) acting on the $i$-th coordinate.

**Pendulum Setup (1 mark):** For a simple pendulum of length $L$ and mass $m$, with joint angle $\theta$:
* Kinetic Energy: $K = \frac{1}{2} m L^2 \dot{\theta}^2$
* Potential Energy: $V = -m g L \cos\theta$
* Lagrangian: $L = \frac{1}{2} m L^2 \dot{\theta}^2 + m g L \cos\theta$

**Step-by-step Euler-Lagrange (3 marks):**
* $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{\theta}} = m L^2 \dot{\theta}$
* $\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{\theta}}\right) = m L^2 \ddot{\theta}$
* $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \theta} = -m g L \sin\theta$
* Substituting: $m L^2 \ddot{\theta} - (-m g L \sin\theta) = \tau$
* $\boxed{m L^2 \ddot{\theta} + m g L \sin\theta = \tau}$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The applied torque $\tau$ is the *generalised force* conjugate to the angular coordinate $\theta$. The conservative gravity term is absorbed into the Lagrangian, while $\tau$ appears as the non-conservative external generalised force.

##### Part (b) — Inertia Forces (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** The inertia-induced generalised force vector is:
$$\boldsymbol{\tau}_{\text{inertia}} = \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q}) \ddot{\mathbf{q}}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Substituting the given values:
$$\boldsymbol{\tau}_{\text{inertia}} = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 2 \\ 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Expanding the matrix product:
$$\tau_1 = (5)(2) + (2)(1) = 10 + 2 = 12 \text{ N·m}$$
$$\tau_2 = (2)(2) + (3)(1) = 4 + 3 = 7 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Result:
$$\boxed{\boldsymbol{\tau}_{\text{inertia}} = \begin{bmatrix} 12 \\ 7 \end{bmatrix} \text{ N·m}}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** The joint coupling term $M_{12} = 2$ contributes to both torques, demonstrating that motion in joint 2 induces a reaction torque in joint 1 and vice versa.

**Step 6 (1 mark):** For a complete dynamic model, the generalised force also includes the Coriolis/centrifugal term $\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q}, \dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ and the gravity term $\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})$:
$$\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q}, \dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Practical significance: The off-diagonal terms $M_{12} = M_{21}$ represent **inertial coupling** — accelerating joint 1 requires extra torque in joint 2 to overcome the coupling. This must be compensated in high-speed tracking controllers.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Transpose** — A classic mistake is writing $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J} \mathbf{F}$ instead of $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F}$. The transpose is *non-negotiable* and arises from the virtual work argument. **−2 marks minimum.**
> 2. **Dimension Mismatch** — Students often write $\delta \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{J} \delta \mathbf{q}$ where the Jacobian is defined as $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{J} \dot{\mathbf{q}}$ and confuse the dimensions. Verify: $\mathbf{J}$ is $m \times n$, $\mathbf{J}^T$ is $n \times m$.
> 3. **Skipping the "Arbitrariness" Step** — The transition from $\left[\boldsymbol{\tau}^T - \mathbf{F}^T \mathbf{J}\right] \delta \mathbf{q} = 0$ to $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F}$ requires the explicit statement *"Since $\delta \mathbf{q}$ is arbitrary, the coefficient must vanish"*. Omitting this loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Sign Errors in Numerical Problems** — The force $\mathbf{F}$ is the force *exerted by the end-effector on the environment* (or equivalently, the reaction force on the gripper). The negative sign convention varies. Always state the convention.
> 5. **Confusing Generalised Force with Cartesian Force** — $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ (joint torque) and $\mathbf{F}$ (Cartesian wrench) live in *different mathematical spaces*. The Jacobian Transpose is the bridge.
> 6. **No Units in Final Answer** — KTU examiners often deduct 0.5 marks for missing units (N·m for revolute, N for prismatic).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Generalised Force**
>
> * **Core Definition** — Generalised Force $Q_i$ is the virtual-work conjugate of $q_i$: $\delta W = \sum_i Q_i \, \delta q_i$.
> * **Master Equation** — $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T(\mathbf{q}) \mathbf{F}$ (memorise this verbatim; it is the most-tested formula in this module).
> * **Origin** — Derived from the **Principle of Virtual Work** combined with the differential kinematic constraint $\delta \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) \delta \mathbf{q}$.
> * **Power Equivalence** — $\boldsymbol{\tau}^T \dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{F}^T \dot{\mathbf{x}}$ (this is a quick verification check for any derivation).
> * **Euler-Lagrange Form** — $Q_i = \frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i}$ defines the generalised force in the Lagrangian framework.
> * **Conservative vs Non-Conservative** — Conservative forces are absorbed into $V(\mathbf{q})$; only non-conservative external inputs appear as $Q_i$.
> * **Static Equilibrium** — $\boldsymbol{\tau}_{\text{applied}} + \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F}_{\text{ext}} = \mathbf{0}$ (the actuator torques must balance the wrench-induced torques).
> * **Full Dynamics Equation** — $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) + \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F}_{\text{ext}}$.
> * **Singularities** — At a kinematic singularity, $\mathbf{J}$ loses rank, $\mathbf{J}^T$ still exists but the force-mapping becomes poorly conditioned; the manipulator cannot generate forces in certain Cartesian directions.
> * **Units** — Generalised Force = N·m (revolute) or N (prismatic). Always include units.
> * **Direction Convention** — The negative sign in static equilibrium indicates *opposing* torques; this is frequently tested in numericals.
> * **Energy Conservation** — Virtual work equivalence is the *only* reason the transpose appears; remember this during derivations.
> * **Application Domains** — Force control, impedance control, cobotics, legged robotics, biomechanics.
> * **KTU Favourite Questions** — Derivation (7 marks) + Numerical (7 marks) is the *standard 14-mark template*; practice both virtual-work and Lagrangian derivations.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Euler-Lagrange Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT302 - INTRODUCTION TO ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Euler-Lagrange Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Euler-Lagrange Equation** is a second-order partial differential equation whose solutions are the extremals (paths that minimize or maximize a functional) of a given variational problem. In the context of robotics, it provides the foundational mathematical framework for deriving the **dynamic equations of motion** of a robotic manipulator from energy-based principles, rather than from Newton-Euler force/torque balance.

For a mechanical system described by generalized coordinates $\mathbf{q} = [q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_n]^T$ and the scalar **Lagrangian function** $L = T - V$, where $T$ is the **kinetic energy** and $V$ is the **potential energy** of the system, the general form of the Euler-Lagrange equation is:

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = Q_i, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, n
$$

Here, $Q_i$ represents the **generalized non-conservative force** (or torque) acting on the $i^{th}$ generalized coordinate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The Euler-Lagrange formulation is the prescribed method for deriving the manipulator dynamics equation in the standard form $M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$. Students must master both the **scalar (n-DOF) form** and the **matrix (compact) form** for examination purposes.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **mountain biker** choosing a path down a steep, irregular hill covered in obstacles. You want to reach the bottom in the **shortest possible time**. Naturally, you would not go straight down (where you might crash into rocks) nor would you take a meandering scenic route. Instead, you choose a path that balances distance and resistance — a path of **least effort and fastest descent**.

> **The Path of Least Resistance is the Path of Extremum.**

The Euler-Lagrange equation is the **mathematical embodiment** of this principle for physical systems. Instead of a biker, consider a robotic arm swinging from one configuration to another. The "path" it takes through joint space (described by $q(t)$) is not arbitrary — it is the one that **extremizes the action integral** $\int_{t_1}^{t_2} L(q,\dot{q},t)\, dt$. The Euler-Lagrange equation tells us exactly what that optimal path is by connecting the geometry of the path to the energies of the system.

In simple terms:
- **Energy In (Kinetic $T$):** What the system is *doing* (motion).
- **Energy Stored (Potential $V$):** What the system *could do* (height, springs).
- **Lagrangian $L = T - V$:** The "net available energy" for motion.
- **Euler-Lagrange Equation:** The rule that says *"if the system follows the path of least action, then these equations must hold."*

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:** The Euler-Lagrange method is preferred over Newton-Euler in robotics because it is **systematic**, **scalar-based**, **free from internal force calculations**, and naturally yields the **symmetric inertia matrix** $M(q)$, which is crucial for modern control design (e.g., computed torque control).

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Standard Symbol | SI Unit |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| Generalized coordinate | $q_i$ | **rad** (rotational) or **m** (translational) |
| Generalized velocity | $\dot{q}_i$ | **rad/s** or **m/s** |
| Generalized force/torque | $Q_i$ | **N** or **N·m** |
| Kinetic energy | $T$ | **J (Joule)** |
| Potential energy | $V$ | **J (Joule)** |
| Lagrangian | $L$ | **J (Joule)** |
| Mass / Inertia | $m, I$ | **kg**, **kg·m²** |
| Gravitational acceleration | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |

> [!NOTE]
> The Lagrangian $L$ has the **same units as energy (Joules)**. This is a frequently tested fact in KTU examinations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Logic Behind the Euler-Lagrange Equation — A Stepwise Breakdown

The derivation rests on **Hamilton's Principle of Stationary Action**, which states that the actual path taken by a system between two states $(q(t_1), q(t_2))$ is the one that makes the **action functional** $S$ stationary (typically a minimum):

$$
S = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} L\big(q(t), \dot{q}(t), t\big)\, dt
$$

#### Step 1: Formulate the Lagrangian
Construct the scalar function $L = T - V$, where $T$ depends on the configuration $q$ and velocity $\dot{q}$, and $V$ depends on the configuration $q$ alone (for conservative systems).

#### Step 2: Apply the Calculus of Variations
Impose a small, admissible variation $\delta q(t)$ on the path, with the boundary constraints $\delta q(t_1) = \delta q(t_2) = 0$. The first variation of the action must vanish:

$$
\delta S = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} \left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i}\delta q_i + \frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\delta \dot{q}_i\right) dt = 0
$$

#### Step 3: Integration by Parts
Integrate the second term by parts to move the time derivative off the variation. The boundary terms vanish due to the constraint $\delta q(t_1) = \delta q(t_2) = 0$:

$$
\int_{t_1}^{t_2} \frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i} \delta \dot{q}_i\, dt = \left[\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\delta q_i\right]_{t_1}^{t_2} - \int_{t_1}^{t_2} \frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right)\delta q_i\, dt
$$

#### Step 4: Apply the Fundamental Lemma
Since the variation $\delta q_i$ is arbitrary in the interior, the integrand must vanish identically:

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = 0 \quad \text{(for conservative systems)}
$$

#### Step 5: Include Non-Conservative Generalized Forces
When external (non-conservative) forces/torques $Q_i$ act on the system, the principle of virtual work yields the **full Euler-Lagrange equation**:

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = Q_i
$$

### 2.2 Application to Robotic Manipulators

For an $n$-DOF serial manipulator, applying the Euler-Lagrange formulation to each link (or the entire system) yields the **canonical manipulator dynamics equation**:

$$
\boxed{M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau}
$$

The terms in the box arise directly from the Euler-Lagrange equation as follows:

| Matrix / Vector | Physical Meaning | Derivation Source |
|:---|:---|:---|
| $M(q) \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ | **Inertia (Mass) Matrix** — symmetric, positive definite | $\frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial \dot{q}_i \partial \dot{q}_j}$ |
| $C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ | **Coriolis and Centrifugal Vector** | Christoffel symbols of $M(q)$ |
| $G(q) \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ | **Gravitational Torque Vector** | $\frac{\partial V}{\partial q_i}$ |
| $\tau \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ | **Generalized Joint Force/Torque** (actuator input + friction) | External generalized forces $Q_i$ |

### 2.3 The "Why" and "How" — Pedagogical Insight

- **Why use $L = T - V$ and not $T + V$?** Because the difference $T - V$ arises naturally from the difference between the *kinetic* and *potential* energy. The Euler-Lagrange operator applied to $V$ (which has no velocity dependence) yields exactly $-\partial V/\partial q_i$, the conservative force. The choice of sign is canonical and is preserved across all of classical mechanics.
- **How does the inertia matrix become symmetric?** Because the kinetic energy is a **quadratic form** in $\dot{q}$: $T = \frac{1}{2}\dot{q}^T M(q) \dot{q}$. The Hessian of a quadratic form is always symmetric.
- **How do we extract $C(q,\dot{q})$?** The Coriolis matrix is built from the **Christoffel symbols** (connection coefficients) of the first kind:
$$
c_{ijk} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial m_{ij}}{\partial q_k} + \frac{\partial m_{ik}}{\partial q_j} - \frac{\partial m_{jk}}{\partial q_i}\right)
$$
so that the $(i,j)$ element of $C$ is $C_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} c_{ijk} \dot{q}_k$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The Euler-Lagrange formulation is the cornerstone of **model-based control** of robots. Techniques like **Computed Torque Control** and **Adaptive Control** rely explicitly on knowing $M(q)$, $C(q,\dot{q})$, and $G(q)$ to cancel nonlinearities and impose a desired linear error dynamics. The Lagrangian framework also unifies the treatment of **rigid** and **flexible-joint** robots and naturally extends to **underactuated** and **closed-chain** mechanisms.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Use Case in KTU |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $L = T - V$ | Lagrangian definition | Energy bookkeeping for any system |
| 2 | $\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = Q_i$ | Euler-Lagrange (scalar form) | Direct dynamic derivation |
| 3 | $T = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i,j} m_{ij}(q)\dot{q}_i \dot{q}_j = \frac{1}{2}\dot{q}^T M(q) \dot{q}$ | Kinetic energy as quadratic form | Inertia matrix extraction |
| 4 | $M_{ij}(q) = \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial \dot{q}_i \partial \dot{q}_j}$ | Inertia matrix element | Step in manipulator dynamics |
| 5 | $G_i(q) = \frac{\partial V}{\partial q_i}$ | Gravitational torque | Gravity compensation term |
| 6 | $M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$ | Standard manipulator dynamics | Final compact answer |
| 7 | $V = \sum_{i=1}^{n} -m_i g^0 r_i$ | Potential energy (gravity vector $g^0$) | Symbolic gravity computation |
| 8 | $\dot{M}(q) - 2C(q,\dot{q})$ is **skew-symmetric** | Passivity property | Lyapunov-based control proofs |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to "derive the dynamic model using Euler-Lagrange," always show the following four steps in order: **(i)** Define $L = T - V$. **(ii)** Compute $\frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i}$ and $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}$. **(iii)** Compute $\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right)$. **(iv)** Assemble into the standard $M\ddot{q} + C\dot{q} + G = \tau$ form. Marks are allocated explicitly across these steps.

---
