> **Find:** (a) The effective inertia and damping reflected to the joint. (b) The DC gain $\lim_{s \to 0} s \, G(s)$ (i.e., steady-state velocity per volt).

**Solution (a):**

$$J_{eff} = n^2 J_m + J_L = (50)^2 (1\times 10^{-4}) + 1 = 2500 \times 10^{-4} + 1 = 0.25 + 1 = 1.25 \text{ kg·m}^2$$

$$B_{eff} = n^2 B_m = (50)^2 (1\times 10^{-4}) = 0.25 \text{ N·m·s/rad}$$

**Solution (b):** Setting $s = 0$ in the transfer function numerator/denominator:

$$\lim_{s \to 0} s G(s) = \frac{n K_m}{R_a B_{eff} + n^2 K_e K_m} = \frac{50 \times 0.1}{(1)(0.25) + (2500)(0.1)(0.1)}$$

$$= \frac{5}{0.25 + 25} = \frac{5}{25.25} \approx 0.198 \text{ rad/s/V}$$

> **Physical Interpretation:** For every 1 V applied to the armature, the joint reaches a steady-state speed of about 0.2 rad/s ≈ 11.5°/s. Without the gearbox ($n=1$), this number would be ~100× larger, confirming that gears trade speed for torque.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Motor + Gearbox + Joint Control Loop

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the complete functional architecture: a **reference trajectory** is compared against the **measured joint position**, the difference is processed by a **PID controller**, the resulting voltage drives the **armature circuit**, which produces a current that generates torque, gets scaled by the **gearbox**, acts on the **joint+load inertia**, and finally the joint angle is fed back through a sensor.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Reference Trajectory q_d_t"] --> B["Summing Junction e = q_d - q"]
    C["Joint Sensor Encoder"] --> B
    B --> D["PID Controller C_s"]
    D --> E["Power Amplifier Gain K_amp"]
    E --> F["Armature Circuit R_a and L_a"]
    F --> G["DC Motor Torque Constant K_m"]
    G --> H["Gearbox Ratio n"]
    H --> I["Joint Inertia J_eff and Damping B_eff"]
    I --> J["Output Joint Angle theta_j"]
    J --> C
    I --> K["Disturbance tau_dist"]
    K --> B
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **outer loop** (blue arrows through C, B, D) is the position feedback.
- The **inner plant** (orange: E → F → G → H → I → J) is the motor-gearbox-joint chain.
- The **disturbance entry** at K models gravity, friction, and payload effects.

---

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — Hierarchical Robot Control Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1["Level 5: Task Planner\nPick and place logic"] --> L2
    L2["Level 4: Trajectory Generator\nSmooth q_d_t via splines"] --> L3
    L3["Level 3: Outer Position Loop\nPD or PID on joint error"] --> L4
    L4["Level 2: Inner Velocity and Current Loop\nPI for torque tracking"] --> L5
    L5["Level 1: PWM Driver and Power Stage\nH-bridge MOSFET stage"] --> L6
    L6["Level 0: Physical Plant\nMotor plus gearbox plus joint"]
    L6 --> L1
```

**Key insight:** Each level runs at a *higher frequency* than the one above it. The slowest, most intelligent decision is at the top; the fastest, most reactive loop is at the bottom. This pyramidal structure is the standard in industrial robots (ABB, KUKA, FANUC) and is what KTU examiners expect students to recall for Module 3 viva questions.

---

### 4.3 Physical Schematic — Geared Motor Coupled to a Robot Link

```
                ┌─────────────────────────┐
   Va ───[R_a]──┤ ARMATURE  CIRCUIT       ├── K_e · θ̇m
                │ (L_a in series)         │   (back-EMF)
                └──────────┬──────────────┘
                           │ i_a
                           ▼
                ┌─────────────────────────┐
                │  DC  MOTOR  ROTOR       │
                │  Inertia  J_m           │◄──── friction B_m
                │  Torque  τ_m = K_m·i_a  │
                └──────────┬──────────────┘
                           │ θ_m , τ_m
                           ▼
                ┌─────────────────────────┐
                │   GEAR  REDUCER         │
                │   ratio  n = 50 : 1     │
                │   η = 0.90              │
                └──────────┬──────────────┘
                           │ θ_j = θ_m/n
                           │ τ_j = n·η·τ_m
                           ▼
                ┌─────────────────────────┐
                │  ROBOT  LINK / JOINT    │
                │  Inertia J_L            │
                │  Damping B_L            │
                │  Gravity  τ_g(q)        │
                └─────────────────────────┘
```

**Take-away:** Every quantity (angle, torque, speed, acceleration) is **scaled by $n$ or $1/n$** as it crosses the gearbox. Energy dissipation at the gearbox is captured by $\eta < 1$, which means $\tau_j < n\,\tau_m$ in practice.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper Style]**

**Q1. With a neat sketch, explain the electrical and mechanical model of an armature-controlled DC motor. List the standard parameters used.**
*(3 Marks — Blooms: Remember/Understand)*

**Model Answer:**
An armature-controlled DC motor consists of an armature winding supplied by a voltage $V_a$ through resistance $R_a$ and inductance $L_a$. As the rotor (inertia $J_m$) spins in a magnetic field, it produces a back-EMF $e = K_e \dot{\theta}_m$. The governing equations are:
- Electrical: $V_a = R_a i_a + L_a \frac{di_a}{dt} + K_e \dot{\theta}_m$
- Mechanical: $J_m \ddot{\theta}_m + B_m \dot{\theta}_m = K_m i_a - \tau_L$

The parameters are: $R_a, L_a, K_e, K_m, J_m, B_m, \tau_L$. A sketch showing the armature loop coupled to the rotor shaft earns full marks.

---

**Q2. What is the role of a gearbox in a robotic joint? Define gear ratio $n$ and torque transmission efficiency $\eta$.**
*(3 Marks — Blooms: Remember/Understand)*

**Model Answer:**
A gearbox in a robotic joint reduces the high-speed, low-torque output of the motor to a low-speed, high-torque output suitable for driving the link. The gear ratio is defined as $n = \theta_m / \theta_j$ (motor revs per joint rev). The transmission efficiency $\eta$ captures energy lost to friction in the gears. The torque at the joint is $\tau_j = n\,\eta\,\tau_m$. Without a gearbox, the joint servo would either need a very large motor (heavy, expensive) or fail to deliver the required torques.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Module 3]**

---

#### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive the transfer function $\dfrac{\Theta_j(s)}{V_a(s)}$ for a DC motor coupled to a robot joint through a gear reducer of ratio $n$, clearly stating all assumptions. (7 Marks)

**(b)** For the same motor, explain the function of a PID controller in the joint servo loop. Derive the closed-loop characteristic equation and discuss how the gains $K_p, K_i, K_d$ affect stability. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

Start with the electrical equation: $V_a = R_a i_a + L_a \frac{di_a}{dt} + K_e \dot{\theta}_m$.

Mechanical equation at rotor: $J_m \ddot{\theta}_m + B_m \dot{\theta}_m = K_m i_a - \tau_L/n$.

Apply gear relation $\theta_m = n\theta_j$ and reflect everything to the joint side. The mechanical equation becomes:

$$(n^2 J_m + J_L)\ddot{\theta}_j + (n^2 B_m + B_L)\dot{\theta}_j + \tau_L = n K_m i_a$$

Laplace transform (zero ICs):

$$V_a(s) = (L_a s + R_a) I_a(s) + K_e n s \Theta_j(s)$$
$$(J_{eff} s^2 + B_{eff} s)\Theta_j(s) = n K_m I_a(s)$$

Eliminate $I_a(s)$ by multiplying the second by $(L_a s + R_a)/n K_m$ and adding to the first:

$$V_a(s) = \Theta_j(s)\left[\frac{(L_a s + R_a)(J_{eff} s + B_{eff})}{n K_m}\cdot s + K_e n s\right]$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{\Theta_j(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{n K_m}{s\left[(L_a s + R_a)(J_{eff} s + B_{eff}) + n^2 K_e K_m\right]}$$

**Assumptions stated:** (i) Constant magnetic field (separately excited or permanent-magnet motor). (ii) Linear magnetic circuit ($K_m, K_e$ constants). (iii) Coulomb friction neglected (only viscous damping $B_m$). (iv) Rigid gearbox (no backlash, no flexibility). (v) No external load disturbance $\tau_L = 0$.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

A PID controller in the joint servo loop computes:

$$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)d\tau + K_d \dot{e}(t), \quad e(t) = q_d(t) - q(t)$$

In Laplace domain:

$$C(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}$$

With plant $G(s)$ and unity feedback, the closed-loop transfer function is:

$$T(s) = \frac{C(s) G(s)}{1 + C(s) G(s)}$$

The **characteristic equation** (denominator = 0) is:

$$1 + C(s) G(s) = 0$$

For the motor-joint plant $G(s) = \dfrac{n K_m}{s[(L_a s+R_a)(J_{eff} s + B_{eff}) + n^2 K_e K_m]}$, the characteristic polynomial is 4th order. Stability is assessed via Routh-Hurwitz or root-locus.

**Effect of gains:**
- Increasing $K_p$ → faster response, smaller steady-state error, but if too high → overshoot and oscillation.
- Increasing $K_i$ → eliminates steady-state error completely (essential for vertical joints fighting gravity), but reduces phase margin → can destabilize.
- Increasing $K_d$ → adds damping, reduces overshoot, but amplifies sensor noise.
- The art of tuning is choosing gains that balance **rise time**, **overshoot**, **steady-state error**, and **robustness to disturbances**.

---

#### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** A robot joint is driven by a DC motor with parameters $R_a = 2\,\Omega$, $L_a = 0.1\,\text{H}$, $K_e = K_m = 0.05$, $J_m = 5\times 10^{-5}\,\text{kg·m}^2$, $B_m = 1\times 10^{-5}\,\text{N·m·s/rad}$, gear ratio $n = 100$, joint inertia $J_L = 2\,\text{kg·m}^2$, $B_L = 0$. Compute (i) the effective inertia and damping reflected to the joint side, (ii) the steady-state velocity per volt (DC gain), and (iii) the time constant of the electrical subsystem. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Discuss the open-loop versus closed-loop control of robotic joints. Why is open-loop control generally unsuitable for precise manipulation tasks? (7 Marks)

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**(i)** Effective inertia and damping:

$$J_{eff} = n^2 J_m + J_L = (100)^2(5\times 10^{-5}) + 2 = 0.5 + 2 = 2.5 \text{ kg·m}^2$$

$$B_{eff} = n^2 B_m + B_L = (100)^2(1\times 10^{-5}) + 0 = 0.1 \text{ N·m·s/rad}$$

**(ii)** DC gain (steady-state velocity per volt):

$$\lim_{s\to 0} sG(s) = \frac{n K_m}{R_a B_{eff} + n^2 K_e K_m} = \frac{100 \times 0.05}{(2)(0.1) + (10000)(0.05)(0.05)}$$

$$= \frac{5}{0.2 + 25} = \frac{5}{25.2} \approx 0.1984 \text{ rad/s/V}$$

**(iii)** Electrical time constant:

$$\tau_e = \frac{L_a}{R_a} = \frac{0.1}{2} = 0.05 \text{ s} = 50 \text{ ms}$$

This means the current (and hence torque) takes about 50 ms to reach 63% of its commanded value — a non-negligible delay that the controller must accommodate.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Open-loop control:** The control signal is computed purely from a model of the plant and a pre-planned reference, with **no feedback** from the actual joint position. It assumes the model is perfect. If a payload is added, friction varies, or disturbances act, the actual trajectory will deviate from the desired one with no automatic correction.

**Closed-loop control:** The actual joint position $q(t)$ is measured by an encoder and continuously compared to the desired $q_d(t)$. The error $e = q_d - q$ drives the controller (PID or similar), which adjusts the actuator input to drive the error toward zero.

**Why open-loop fails for precision tasks:**
1. **Model uncertainty:** Joint inertias, friction coefficients, and gravity loads change with payload and configuration; an open-loop controller cannot adapt.
2. **Disturbances:** External forces (contact, human interaction) cause drift that no model accounts for.
3. **Actuator nonlinearities:** Dead zones, saturation, and Coulomb friction are unmodelled in the linear transfer function.
4. **Initial condition errors:** Starting from an unknown position, open-loop cannot self-correct.

Hence, **closed-loop PID control is the universal standard** for robotic joint servos. Open-loop is reserved for very fast, repetitive, low-precision tasks such as clock motors or CD-drive seeking.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in this Module:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $n^2$ factor** in back-EMF coupling — always derive carefully and don't copy-paste from memory.
> 2. **Reflecting inertia incorrectly** — students often write $J_{eff} = J_m/n^2 + J_L$. The correct direction is $J_{eff} = n^2 J_m + J_L$ (motor inertia appears larger at the joint).
> 3. **Confusing $K_e$ and $K_m$** — in SI units they are numerically equal but conceptually distinct (one is electrical, one is mechanical).
> 4. **Ignoring the electrical time constant** $L_a/R_a$ in PD-only designs — this high-frequency pole limits achievable bandwidth.
> 5. **Steady-state error in P-only control** — examiners love to ask why $K_p$ alone cannot eliminate offset under gravity.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A DC motor has **two coupled equations**: electrical (KVL) and mechanical (Newton's 2nd law).
- ✅ The back-EMF term $K_e \dot{\theta}_m$ couples mechanical motion back into the electrical circuit — this is the source of motor's natural self-regulation.
- ✅ In SI units, $K_e = K_m$ (numerically), but always write them separately for clarity.
- ✅ Gear ratio $n$ scales angle/torque/speed; **inertia and damping scale as $n^2$** when reflected across the gearbox.
- ✅ The canonical transfer function $\dfrac{\Theta_j(s)}{V_a(s)} = \dfrac{n K_m}{s[(L_a s + R_a)(J_{eff} s + B_{eff}) + n^2 K_e K_m]}$ is the **most-tested equation** in this module.
- ✅ Robot control is **always closed-loop** for precision — feedback is non-negotiable.
- ✅ Hierarchical control: **Task → Trajectory → Position → Velocity → Current → PWM → Motor**.
- ✅ PID: $K_p$ → speed, $K_i$ → steady-state accuracy, $K_d$ → damping.
- ✅ Closed-loop stability requires all poles of $1 + C(s)G(s) = 0$ to have negative real parts.
- ✅ Practical robots use a **cascaded structure**: inner current loop (PI) + outer position loop (P or PD).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Single Axis PID Control - Its Disadvantages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT302 - INTRODUCTION TO ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Single Axis PID Control - Its Disadvantages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Single Axis PID Control

> [!NOTE]
> **PID Controller (Proportional-Integral-Derivative Controller):** A classical feedback control mechanism widely used in industrial control systems and robotic joint actuation. It computes a control signal as a weighted sum of three terms: the present error (Proportional), the accumulation of past errors (Integral), and the prediction of future errors based on current rate of change (Derivative).

In the context of **Single Axis PID Control** applied to robotics, the controller governs only **one independent Degree of Freedom (DOF)** of a robot manipulator at a time. The typical configuration involves a single joint motor whose angular position $\theta(t)$ is to be driven to a desired reference position $\theta_{ref}(t)$, while minimizing the error signal $e(t) = \theta_{ref}(t) - \theta(t)$.

The mathematical control law for a single axis PID is expressed as:

$$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

Where the standard parameters are:
- $K_p$ → Proportional Gain (dimensionless or Nm/rad)
- $K_i$ → Integral Gain (typically Nm/(rad·s))
- $K_d$ → Derivative Gain (typically Nm·s/rad)
- $u(t)$ → Control torque or voltage output to the actuator

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — Driving a Car with a Single Rear-View Mirror:**
> Imagine you are driving a car but can only look through **one** mirror. A single axis PID controller is exactly this — it "sees" and corrects the error of just **one** variable (say, distance from the car in front) and ignores everything else. It is excellent at maintaining that single variable at the set point but is **blind** to lateral drift, road curvature, wind gusts, or interactions with other systems. The driver (controller) constantly applies steering corrections using a weighted combination of *current* gap (P), *accumulated* gap error over time (I), and the *rate of change* of the gap (D).

In a robotic joint, this translates to a controller that:
- **P (Proportional):** Applies torque proportional to how far the joint is from its target (like spring force).
- **I (Integral):** Removes steady-state offset by remembering past errors (eliminates drift).
- **D (Derivative):** Damps overshoot by reacting to how fast the error is changing (acts as a brake).

### 1.3 Why Single Axis?

In early robotics, especially **point-to-point** or **single-joint** mechanisms like a CNC axis or a pick-and-place linear slide, each joint was treated as an **independent SISO (Single-Input Single-Output)** system. A single axis PID was sufficient because:
- One motor → One position sensor → One controller loop.
- No coupling exists between the axis and any other motion.

However, this independence is a **mathematical idealization**, not a physical reality for most robots.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of a Single Axis PID Loop

The control action proceeds through the following structured logic steps:

1. **Reference Sampling:** The desired joint angle $\theta_{ref}(t)$ is read from the trajectory generator.
2. **Feedback Acquisition:** The encoder or resolver measures the actual joint angle $\theta(t)$ at sampling instant $k$.
3. **Error Computation:** The instantaneous error is calculated as $e(k) = \theta_{ref}(k) - \theta(k)$.
4. **Weighted Summation:** Three parallel branches compute $K_p e$, $K_i \sum e$, and $K_d \Delta e$.
5. **Actuator Command:** The sum $u(k)$ is sent to the motor drive (PWM amplifier / current loop).
6. **Plant Response:** The DC motor (or servo) produces torque, rotating the joint.
7. **Disturbance Rejection Loop:** Any load torque or friction is sensed indirectly via the next error sample.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The single axis architecture is the *foundational building block* of all robot joint control. Every multi-axis robot eventually decomposes into N such loops, but the *interactions* between them are what cause the disadvantages.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Physical Meaning | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Error Signal | $e(t) = \theta_{ref}(t) - \theta(t)$ | Deviation from target position | **rad** (radians) |
| Proportional Term | $u_P(t) = K_p \cdot e(t)$ | Immediate corrective action | Nm |
| Integral Term | $u_I(t) = K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau$ | Eliminates steady-state error | Nm |
| Derivative Term | $u_D(t) = K_d \cdot \frac{de(t)}{dt}$ | Damping and prediction | Nm |
| Combined Control Law | $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e \, dt + K_d \dot{e}$ | Total control effort | Nm |
| Closed-Loop Transfer Function | $G_{cl}(s) = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s^3 + K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}$ | System response in Laplace domain | dimensionless |
| Motor Torque Equation | $J\ddot{\theta} + b\dot{\theta} = u(t) - \tau_{dist}$ | Plant dynamics for single joint | Nm |
| Tuning — Ziegler-Nichols (P only) | $K_u$ = ultimate gain, $P_u$ = period | Empirical starting point | sec / dimensionless |
| Sampling Time (Digital) | $T_s \leq 0.1 \cdot t_r$ | Nyquist stability criterion | **s** (seconds) |
| Derivative Filter Cut-off | $f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi \tau_f}$ | Limits noise in $K_d$ branch | **Hz** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute value notations in equations use $\vert \cdot \vert$ to avoid breaking markdown table parsing. The **sampling time $T_s$** and the **derivative filter time constant $\tau_f$** are the two most critical tuning parameters in digital implementation.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Single axis PID controllers are the **workhorse of industrial automation**. They are deployed in:
- **CNC machine tool** individual axes (X, Y, Z slides).
- **Stepper / Servo motor** speed regulators in conveyor belts.
- **Hard disk drive** head-positioning servos.
- **Antenna azimuth** and **elevation** tracking systems.
- **Camera gimbal** stabilization for one rotational axis.

Despite this widespread use, when these controllers are **naïvely extended to a multi-DOF robot manipulator**, several fundamental limitations emerge.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Single Axis Closed-Loop Transfer Function

We begin with the standard DC motor driving a single rigid link of inertia $J$ and viscous friction coefficient $b$. The input is the control voltage $u(t)$ and the output is the joint position $\theta(t)$. The disturbance torque is $\tau_d(t)$.

**Step 1 — Plant Equation (Newton-Euler for single joint):**

The equation of motion for a single revolute joint, ignoring gravity (horizontal axis) and Coriolis effects (since only one axis), is:

$$J \ddot{\theta}(t) + b \dot{\theta}(t) = u(t) - \tau_d(t)$$

**Step 2 — Laplace Transform of the Plant:**

Taking the Laplace transform on both sides with zero initial conditions:

$$J s^2 \Theta(s) + b s \Theta(s) = U(s) - T_d(s)$$

$$\Theta(s) \cdot [J s^2 + b s] = U(s) - T_d(s)$$

**Step 3 — Plant Transfer Function:**

The plant transfer function from control input to joint position is therefore:

$$G_p(s) = \frac{\Theta(s)}{U(s)} = \frac{1}{s(J s + b)}$$

**Step 4 — PID Controller Transfer Function:**

The continuous-time PID controller in the Laplace domain is:

$$G_c(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}$$

**Step 5 — Open-Loop Transfer Function:**

The forward path gain $L(s)$ of the closed-loop system is:

$$L(s) = G_c(s) \cdot G_p(s) = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s} \cdot \frac{1}{s(J s + b)}$$

Simplifying:

$$L(s) = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s^2 (J s + b)}$$

**Step 6 — Closed-Loop Transfer Function:**

The standard negative feedback formula is:

$$G_{cl}(s) = \frac{L(s)}{1 + L(s)}$$

Substituting $L(s)$:

$$G_{cl}(s) = \frac{\frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s^2 (J s + b)}}{1 + \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s^2 (J s + b)}}$$

**Step 7 — Cross-Multiplication:**

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $s^2 (J s + b)$:

$$G_{cl}(s) = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s^2 (J s + b) + K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}$$

**Step 8 — Expansion and Final Closed-Loop Form:**

Expanding the denominator:

$$s^2 (J s + b) = J s^3 + b s^2$$

Therefore:

$$G_{cl}(s) = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{J s^3 + (b + K_d) s^2 + K_p s + K_i}$$

**Step 9 — Characteristic Equation Analysis:**

The characteristic polynomial governing stability is:

$$\Delta(s) = J s^3 + (b + K_d) s^2 + K_p s + K_i = 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability Condition (Routh-Hurwitz):** For a third-order polynomial $a_3 s^3 + a_2 s^2 + a_1 s + a_0$ to have all roots in the left-half plane, the necessary and sufficient conditions are:
> 1. All coefficients must be positive: $a_i > 0$ for all $i$.
> 2. The auxiliary determinant: $a_2 a_1 > a_3 a_0$, i.e., $(b + K_d) \cdot K_p > J \cdot K_i$.

### 3.2 Derivation: Steady-State Error Due to Gravity in a Vertical Axis

A critical disadvantage emerges when a single axis is oriented **vertically** (e.g., shoulder joint of a robot arm). Gravity introduces a constant disturbance torque $\tau_g$. We compute the steady-state error using the Final Value Theorem.

**Step 1 — Error Transfer Function:**

For a reference $\Theta_{ref}(s)$ and disturbance $T_d(s)$, the error in Laplace domain is:

$$E(s) = \Theta_{ref}(s) - \Theta(s)$$

Using superposition with disturbance rejection:

$$E(s) = \frac{1}{1 + L(s)} \Theta_{ref}(s) - \frac{G_p(s)}{1 + L(s)} T_d(s)$$

**Step 2 — Steady-State Error from Gravity Disturbance:**

Let the disturbance be a step gravity torque: $T_d(s) = \frac{\tau_g}{s}$.

As $s \to 0$, with $\Theta_{ref} = 0$ (commanding to hold position):

$$E_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \left[ -\frac{G_p(s)}{1 + L(s)} \cdot \frac{\tau_g}{s} \right]$$

$$E_{ss} = -\lim_{s \to 0} \frac{G_p(s) \cdot \tau_g}{1 + L(s)}$$

**Step 3 — Substitution of $G_p$ and $L$:**

As $s \to 0$:
- $G_p(s) = \frac{1}{s(J s + b)} \to \frac{1}{b s}$ (blows up because of the $1/s$ term in the integral action of position)
- $L(s) = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s^2 (J s + b)} \to \frac{K_i}{b s^2} \cdot \frac{1}{1} \to \infty$ (because of the integrator in PID)

**Step 4 — Final Steady-State Result:**

$$E_{ss} = -\frac{\tau_g / (b s)}{\infty} = 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The **Integral (I) term in PID drives the steady-state error to zero** for constant disturbances. This is its primary advantage. However, this is **only valid in the linear regime** with no actuator saturation — leading us directly to the next major disadvantage: **Integral Windup**.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Impact of Gain Variation

| Effect | Increasing $K_p$ | Increasing $K_i$ | Increasing $K_d$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rise Time | **Decreases** (faster) | Slight decrease | Minor effect |
| Overshoot | **Increases** significantly | Increases | **Decreases** |
| Settling Time | Minimal change | **Increases** (oscillations) | Decreases |
| Steady-State Error | Reduces but does not eliminate | **Eliminates completely** | No effect |
| Stability Margin | **Decreases** | Decreases drastically | Slight increase |
| Noise Sensitivity | Low | Low | **Very high** |
| Actuator Saturation Risk | Moderate | **High (windup)** | Low |

This matrix reveals the fundamental **trade-off triangle** that no single set of $(K_p, K_i, K_d)$ can resolve optimally for all operating conditions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Single Axis PID Control Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the signal flow of a single axis PID controller driving a robotic joint, with explicit feedback and disturbance pathways.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    refNode["Reference Angle THETA_REF"] --> sumNode(("Sum Junction"))
    fbNode["Actual Angle THETA from Encoder"] --> sumNode
    sumNode --> errNode["Error e(t) = THETA_REF minus THETA"]
    errNode --> propBranch["Proportional Branch Kp"]
    errNode --> intBranch["Integral Branch Ki over s"]
    errNode --> derBranch["Derivative Branch Kd times s"]
    propBranch --> sumCtrl(("Control Summation"))
    intBranch --> sumCtrl
    derBranch --> sumCtrl
    sumCtrl --> actNode["DC Motor Actuator and Power Amplifier"]
    disturbNode["Disturbance Torque TAU_D Gravity Friction"] -.-> actNode
    actNode --> plantNode["Plant Joint Inertia J and Friction b"]
    plantNode --> outNode["Joint Output THETA"]
    outNode --> fbNode
    outNode --> loadNode["Robot Link Payload"]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Decomposition of Disadvantages

The following Mermaid diagram maps each disadvantage to the architectural element that causes it, providing a structural traceability map.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootNode["Single Axis PID Control System"] --> d1["D1 SISO Assumption Violation"]
    rootNode --> d2["D2 Integral Windup"]
    rootNode --> d3["D3 Derivative Noise Amplification"]
    rootNode --> d4["D4 Cross Coupling Ignored"]
    rootNode --> d5["D5 Nonlinearity Blindness"]
    rootNode --> d6["D6 Fixed Gain Adaptation Failure"]
    rootNode --> d7["D7 Actuator Saturation"]
    rootNode --> d8["D8 Jerk and Vibration"]
    d1 --> d1desc["Each joint treated as independent no interaction modeled"]
    d2 --> d2desc["Integrator accumulates error during saturation causing overshoot"]
    d3 --> d3desc["K_d amplifies high frequency encoder noise"]
    d4 --> d4desc["Off diagonal inertia terms ignored in Jacobian coupling"]
    d5 --> d5desc["Friction stiction dead zone not handled linearly"]
    d6 --> d6desc["Constant Kp Ki Kd cannot track payload variation"]
    d7 --> d7desc["Voltage and current limits cap control effort"]
    d8 --> d8desc["Abrupt torque changes excite structural modes"]
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Cascade Loop Alternative (Conceptual Block)

To contextualize how the disadvantages of single axis PID are mitigated, the following block diagram shows the standard **Cascade Control** architecture (position loop → velocity loop → current loop), which is the next step in KTU Module 3 progression.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    posRef["Position Reference"] --> posErr(("e_pos"))
    posFb["Position Feedback Encoder"] --> posErr
    posErr --> posPID["Outer Position PID"]
    posPID --> velRef["Velocity Reference Generation"]
    velRef --> velErr(("e_vel"))
    velFb["Tachometer Velocity Feedback"] --> velErr
    velErr --> velPI["Inner Velocity PI Controller"]
    velPI --> curRef["Current Reference"]
    curRef --> curErr(("e_cur"))
    curFb["Current Shunt Feedback"] --> curErr
    curErr --> curP["Current P Controller with PWM"]
    curP --> motorNode["DC Servo Motor and Joint Load"]
    motorNode --> posFb
    motorNode --> velFb
    motorNode --> curFb
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice that the cascade structure uses a **P-only** controller in the innermost current loop (very fast), **PI** in the velocity loop (eliminates steady-state velocity error), and **PID** only at the outermost position loop. This decomposition is the *practical answer* to the disadvantages of a single monolithic PID.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Define a PID controller. List its three components and state the effect of increasing the proportional gain $K_p$ on rise time and overshoot.**

**Model Answer:**
A PID (Proportional-Integral-Derivative) controller is a feedback control mechanism that computes the control signal as a weighted sum of the error, its integral, and its derivative. The three components are:
1. **Proportional (P):** Provides an output proportional to the instantaneous error $e(t)$.
2. **Integral (I):** Provides an output proportional to the integral of error over time, eliminating steady-state error.
3. **Derivative (D):** Provides an output proportional to the rate of change of error, improving damping and reducing overshoot.

**Effect of increasing $K_p$:**
- **Rise time decreases** (system responds faster).
- **Overshoot increases** (system becomes more oscillatory and can become unstable if $K_p$ is too high).

**(3 Marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] What is integral windup? Mention one anti-windup technique used in single axis PID control.**

**Model Answer:**
**Integral windup** is a phenomenon that occurs when the control signal computed by the PID controller exceeds the actuator's saturation limit. During this saturated period, the error continues to accumulate in the integrator, causing the integral term to grow excessively. When the actual process variable finally approaches the setpoint, the controller output remains saturated due to the large accumulated integral, leading to significant **overshoot** and **oscillations** before the system settles.

**Anti-windup technique — Clamping (Conditional Integration):**
One common method is **clamping** (also called conditional integration), in which integration is **halted** when the control output is saturated and the error continues to grow in the same direction. The integrator resumes normal operation only when the actuator is back within its linear range.

**(3 Marks)**

---

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Common Mistake — Mixing up signs:** Students often confuse the sign convention. The standard is $e = \theta_{ref} - \theta_{actual}$. Writing $e = \theta - \theta_{ref}$ inverts the entire controller and leads to positive feedback. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 2. **Common Mistake — Skipping the disturbance term:** In derivations, students often drop the gravity term $\tau_g$ when analyzing a "single axis." For a vertical joint, this term is critical and the examiner specifically tests for it. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 3. **Common Mistake — No units in final answer:** In KTU paper evaluation, missing units (Nm, rad, s) attracts a **0.5 mark deduction** per instance. Always append units.
> 4. **Common Mistake — Confusing Ziegler-Nichols formulas:** Many students memorize $K_p = 0.6 K_u$ but forget that the other gains depend on which variant (P, PI, PID) is being tuned. **Loss: 1 mark.**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Part (a) + Part (b) Format**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Adapted)]**

**(a)** Derive the closed-loop transfer function of a single axis PID controller driving a DC motor with plant transfer function $G_p(s) = \frac{1}{s(Js + b)}$. Clearly state the characteristic equation and the Routh-Hurwitz stability conditions for all gains to be positive. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a single axis PID controller for a robotic joint. Discuss any **four major disadvantages** of using this controller in a multi-DOF robot manipulator. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Define the PID controller transfer function** (1 mark):

$$G_c(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}$$

**Step 2 — Express the plant transfer function** (0.5 mark):

$$G_p(s) = \frac{1}{s(Js + b)}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the open-loop transfer function** $L(s) = G_c(s) \cdot G_p(s)$ (1.5 marks):

$$L(s) = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s^2 (J s + b)}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the standard negative feedback formula** (1.5 marks):

$$G_{cl}(s) = \frac{L(s)}{1 + L(s)} = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{J s^3 + (b + K_d) s^2 + K_p s + K_i}$$

**Step 5 — State the characteristic equation** (0.5 mark):

$$\Delta(s) = J s^3 + (b + K_d) s^2 + K_p s + K_i = 0$$

**Step 6 — Apply Routh-Hurwitz conditions for a third-order system** (2 marks):

For all roots to lie in the left half plane:
- All coefficients positive: $J > 0$, $(b + K_d) > 0$, $K_p > 0$, $K_i > 0$.
- The auxiliary condition: $(b + K_d) \cdot K_p > J \cdot K_i$.

The second condition implies an **upper bound on $K_i$** for given $K_p$:

$$K_i < \frac{(b + K_d) K_p}{J}$$

This demonstrates that **integral gain cannot be increased indefinitely** without risking instability.

---

**(b) Solution (7 Marks):**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** The block diagram from Section 4.1 should be redrawn showing the reference input, error summer, three parallel branches ($K_p$, $K_i/s$, $K_d s$), summation, DC motor plant, output $\theta(t)$, and the feedback path to the encoder.

**Four Major Disadvantages (1 mark each = 4 marks):**

1. **Neglects Dynamic Coupling Between Joints:** In a multi-DOF robot, motion of one joint generates reaction torques on adjacent joints through the manipulator Jacobian. A single axis PID treats each joint as a SISO system, ignoring these cross-coupling terms. As a result, tracking accuracy degrades significantly at high speeds.

2. **Integral Windup:** As derived in Section 3.2, the integral term continues to accumulate error during actuator saturation. When the saturation clears, the controller overshoots severely, causing oscillation and mechanical stress.

3. **Derivative Noise Amplification:** The derivative branch computes $\frac{de(t)}{dt}$, which mathematically amplifies high-frequency noise from the encoder. In a digital implementation with sampling time $T_s$, the discrete derivative $\frac{e(k) - e(k-1)}{T_s}$ is notoriously sensitive to quantization noise, often requiring a low-pass filter that further delays the response.

4. **No Adaptation to Varying Loads:** A robotic manipulator may pick up payloads of different masses during operation. The fixed gains $(K_p, K_i, K_d)$ tuned for a no-load condition will either be too aggressive (heavy load → instability) or too sluggish (light load → slow response). The single axis PID lacks **gain scheduling** or **adaptive mechanisms**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Adapted)]**

**(a)** Explain the **Ziegler-Nichols tuning method** for a PID controller. A single axis PID is to be tuned for a plant with ultimate gain $K_u = 8$ and ultimate period $P_u = 2$ s. Compute the values of $K_p$, $T_i$, and $T_d$ using the Ziegler-Nichols PID formula. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail **six disadvantages** of using a single axis PID controller in robotic manipulator applications. For each disadvantage, suggest a brief engineering remedy. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Solution (7 Marks):**

**Ziegler-Nichols Method Explanation (4 marks):**

The Ziegler-Nichols closed-loop tuning method is an empirical procedure:
1. Set $K_i = 0$ and $K_d = 0$ in the PID controller, leaving only the proportional gain $K_p$.
2. Gradually increase $K_p$ until the system exhibits **sustained oscillations** (constant amplitude). This critical value is called the **ultimate gain $K_u$**.
3. Measure the **oscillation period $P_u$** of these sustained oscillations.
4. Using $K_u$ and $P_u$, set the PID gains according to the empirical Ziegler-Nichols table.

The standard Ziegler-Nichols tuning formulas for a PID controller are:

$$K_p = 0.6 \cdot K_u$$

$$T_i = 0.5 \cdot P_u \quad \Rightarrow \quad K_i = \frac{K_p}{T_i}$$

$$T_d = 0.125 \cdot P_u \quad \Rightarrow \quad K_d = K_p \cdot T_d$$

**Numerical Computation (3 marks):**

Given: $K_u = 8$ and $P_u = 2$ s.

Compute $K_p$:

$$K_p = 0.6 \times 8 = 4.8$$

Compute $T_i$ and $K_i$:

$$T_i = 0.5 \times 2 = 1 \text{ s}$$

$$K_i = \frac{K_p}{T_i} = \frac{4.8}{1} = 4.8 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

Compute $T_d$ and $K_d$:

$$T_d = 0.125 \times 2 = 0.25 \text{ s}$$

$$K_d = K_p \times T_d = 4.8 \times 0.25 = 1.2 \text{ s}$$

**Final Tuned PID Values:**

| Parameter | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $K_p$ | **4.8** | dimensionless |
| $K_i$ | **4.8** | s$^{-1}$ |
| $K_d$ | **1.2** | s |

---

**(b) Solution (7 Marks):**

**Six Disadvantages with Engineering Remedies (1 mark each for disadvantage + 0.17 mark each for remedy, total ~7 marks):**

| # | Disadvantage | Engineering Remedy |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Integral windup** causes severe overshoot after actuator saturation. | Use **anti-windup** techniques: clamping, back-calculation, or conditional integration. |
| 2 | **Derivative noise amplification** corrupts the control signal in digital systems. | Add a **low-pass filter** on the derivative branch with cut-off $f_c \approx 10\text{–}20$ Hz. |
| 3 | **Cross-coupling torques** between joints are ignored, reducing accuracy. | Implement a **computed-torque controller** or use **inverse dynamics** feed-forward. |
| 4 | **No adaptation** to payload or inertia changes during operation. | Use **gain scheduling** or **Model Reference Adaptive Control (MRAC)**. |
| 5 | **Fixed gains** cannot handle nonlinearities like friction, dead-zone, backlash. | Add **friction compensation** feed-forward or use a **fuzzy logic / neural controller**. |
| 6 | **Single-axis structure** cannot coordinate multi-DOF trajectory tracking. | Replace with a **centralized multi-axis controller** such as **Computed-Torque Control** or **adaptive sliding mode control**. |

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Single Axis PID Disadvantages**

- The **single axis PID control law** is $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e \, dt + K_d \dot{e}$ — memorize this verbatim for KTU derivations.
- The **closed-loop transfer function** of a single axis PID with motor plant $G_p(s) = \frac{1}{s(Js+b)}$ is $G_{cl}(s) = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{J s^3 + (b + K_d) s^2 + K_p s + K_i}$.
- The **Routh-Hurwitz stability condition** is $(b + K_d) K_p > J K_i$ — this is a frequently asked 7-mark question.
- **Integral windup** occurs when the actuator saturates and the integrator continues to accumulate error. The remedy is **anti-windup** (clamping or back-calculation).
- **Derivative noise** is amplified because $\frac{de}{dt}$ is mathematically a high-pass operator. The remedy is a **low-pass filter** with cut-off frequency $f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi \tau_f}$.
- **Cross-coupling** in multi-DOF robots makes single axis PID insufficient — the correct architecture is **Computed-Torque Control** or **Centralized Multi-Axis Control**.
- **Ziegler-Nichols formulas for PID:** $K_p = 0.6 K_u$, $T_i = 0.5 P_u$, $T_d = 0.125 P_u$.
- **Adaptation failure:** Fixed gains cannot respond to **payload variation**, **inertia changes**, or **friction nonlinearities**.
- **Always include units** in the final answer: $K_p$ in dimensionless, $K_i$ in $\text{s}^{-1}$, $K_d$ in $\text{s}$, torques in $\text{Nm}$, angles in $\text{rad}$.
- For a **vertical joint**, the gravity term $\tau_g$ must be included in the disturbance analysis; the integral term **eliminates the steady-state error** due to gravity.
- **Cascade control** (Position → Velocity → Current) is the practical industrial answer to most single axis PID limitations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PD Gravity Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT302 - INTRODUCTION TO ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** PD Gravity Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PD Gravity Control** is a **position regulation strategy** for robotic manipulators in which a **Proportional-Derivative (PD) feedback term** is combined with a **feed-forward gravitational torque compensation term** $G(q)$, such that the total joint torque command is mathematically defined as:
> $$\tau = K_p \, e + K_d \, \dot{e} + G(q)$$
> where $e = q_d - q$ is the position error, $\dot{e} = -\dot{q}$ (since $q_d$ is constant for regulation), $K_p, K_d$ are positive-definite diagonal gain matrices, and $G(q)$ is the vector of gravitational torques computed from the robot's known dynamic model.

This is one of the **simplest, model-based, computed-torque-style controllers** that achieves **asymptotic set-point regulation** for rigid-link serial manipulators. It is formally grounded in the **Lyapunov stability theory** of nonlinear systems and is a standard precursor to the **Computed Torque Controller** and the **Passivity-Based Controller**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Spring + Damper + Weight Balancer"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine you are asked to hold a heavy **door at a fixed open angle** against the force of gravity pulling it shut.
> - The **spring** (Proportional $K_p e$) pulls the door toward the desired angle — the bigger the error, the harder the pull.
> - The **damper** (Derivative $K_d \dot{e}$) prevents the door from oscillating or slamming — it resists velocity.
> - The **gravity compensator** ($G(q)$) is a person standing behind the door pushing *upward* with exactly the same force that gravity pulls *downward*, so the spring and damper only have to handle small disturbance forces, not the entire weight of the door.

In robotic terms, $G(q)$ does the **heavy lifting**, while $K_p$ and $K_d$ do the **fine correction**. Without $G(q)$, the PD controller alone would have to use a very stiff spring (large $K_p$) which causes overshoot, oscillation, and poor tracking.

## 1.3 Why Gravity Compensation is Necessary

A pure PD controller (without $G(q)$) suffers from the **steady-state error problem** under gravity loading:

$$\tau_{ss} = K_p \, e_{ss} = G(q_d) \quad \Rightarrow \quad e_{ss} = K_p^{-1} G(q_d) \neq 0$$

This means a pure PD controller can **never reach the exact desired position** if any link has a non-zero gravitational torque. Adding $G(q)$ cancels this load, allowing $e \to 0$ asymptotically.

> [!TIP]
> **Standard KTU metric to remember:** The proportional gain $K_p$ must satisfy $K_p > \partial G / \partial q$ locally around $q_d$ to guarantee local asymptotic stability of the PD-Gravity controller.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Robot Manipulator Dynamic Model

The standard **Euler–Lagrange form** of the rigid-body manipulator dynamics (the starting point for PD-Gravity control) is:

$$M(q)\,\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\,\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$$

where the four terms each have a clear physical meaning:

| Symbol | Name | Physical Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $M(q)$ | Inertia Matrix | $(n \times n)$ symmetric positive-definite matrix of joint inertias |
| $C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q}$ | Coriolis/Centrifugal Vector | Torques due to rotation of links and coupling between link velocities |
| $G(q)$ | Gravitational Torque Vector | Static torques due to gravity acting on each link's center of mass |
| $\tau$ | Joint Torque Vector | $(n \times 1)$ vector of actuator torques applied at the joints |
| $q, \dot{q}, \ddot{q}$ | Joint Position, Velocity, Acceleration | $(n \times 1)$ generalized coordinates of the manipulator |

## 2.2 Key Structural Properties (Properties Used in Stability Proof)

> [!NOTE]
> The PD-Gravity stability proof **relies on three well-known properties** of the Euler–Lagrange model. Every KTU answer should state these explicitly.

- **Property 1 (Inertia Bounding):** There exist scalars $\mu_1, \mu_2 > 0$ such that $\mu_1 I \le M(q) \le \mu_2 I$.
- **Property 2 (Skew-Symmetry):** The matrix $\dot{M}(q) - 2 C(q,\dot{q})$ is **skew-symmetric**, i.e., $x^T[\dot{M} - 2C]x = 0$ for all $x \in \mathbb{R}^n$.
- **Property 3 (Gravity Lipschitz):** There exists a scalar $k_g > 0$ such that $\Vert G(q_1) - G(q_2) \Vert \le k_g \Vert q_1 - q_2 \Vert$.

## 2.3 The PD-Gravity Control Law — Stepwise Logic

**Step 1 — Define the position error.**
$$e = q_d - q \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dot{e} = -\dot{q} \quad \text{(since } q_d \text{ is constant)}$$

**Step 2 — Form the PD feedback term** (spring force pulling joints toward target, damper absorbing kinetic energy).
$$\tau_{PD} = K_p \, e - K_d \, \dot{q}$$

**Step 3 — Add the gravity feed-forward term** to cancel the static gravitational load.
$$\tau_G = G(q)$$

**Step 4 — Combine to form the complete control law.**
$$\boxed{\tau = K_p \, e - K_d \, \dot{q} + G(q)}$$

**Step 5 — The closed-loop error dynamics** (substitute into the manipulator model) become:
$$M(q)\,\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\,\dot{q} + K_d \dot{q} + K_p e = 0$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Formula Table** — memorize these for the 3-mark and 14-mark KTU questions.

| # | Equation | Description | Units / Conditions |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\tau = K_p e - K_d \dot{q} + G(q)$ | Full PD-Gravity control law | $K_p, K_d$ are $(n \times n)$ positive-definite |
| 2 | $e = q_d - q$ | Position error vector | $q_d$ constant for regulation |
| 3 | $M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$ | Open-loop robot dynamics | $M > 0$ (positive definite) |
| 4 | $V = \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{q}^T M(q) \dot{q} + \tfrac{1}{2} e^T K_p e$ | Lyapunov candidate function | Always $V > 0$ for $\dot{q} \neq 0, e \neq 0$ |
| 5 | $\dot{V} = -\dot{q}^T K_d \dot{q} \le 0$ | Lyapunov derivative | Semi-negative definite |
| 6 | $\ddot{V} = -2\dot{q}^T K_d \ddot{q}$ | For LaSalle's invariance argument | Used to prove $\dot{q} \to 0$ |
| 7 | $K_p > \dfrac{\partial G(q)}{\partial q}\bigg\vert_{q_d}$ | Sufficient condition for local asymptotic stability | $K_p - \dfrac{\partial G}{\partial q}$ must be positive definite |
| 8 | $\tau_{PD-only,ss} = K_p e_{ss} = G(q_d)$ | Steady-state error of pure PD (no gravity comp.) | Shows $e_{ss} \neq 0$ — the motivating problem |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial pick-and-place arms** (e.g., SCARA robots in PCB assembly) where the end-effector must settle at a precise position.
- **Surgical robotic arms** (e.g., da Vinci system) where the manipulator must hold a steady pose against gravity-induced drift.
- **Humanoid robots** standing or balancing a single leg — the ankle joint controller is essentially a PD-Gravity regulator.
- **Space manipulators** (Canadarm on the ISS) where $G(q) \approx 0$ but the controller structure is preserved for robustness against unmodeled disturbances.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Closed-Loop Error Dynamics

We begin from the open-loop rigid-body manipulator equation of motion:

$$M(q)\,\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\,\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$$

**Step 1.** Substitute the control law $\tau = K_p e - K_d \dot{q} + G(q)$ into the right-hand side:

$$M(q)\,\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\,\dot{q} + G(q) = K_p e - K_d \dot{q} + G(q)$$

**Step 2.** Cancel the gravitational term $G(q)$ appearing on both sides (this is the *key cancellation* that motivates the controller):

$$M(q)\,\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\,\dot{q} = K_p e - K_d \dot{q}$$

**Step 3.** Move all terms to the left-hand side to obtain the closed-loop error dynamics:

$$M(q)\,\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\,\dot{q} + K_d \dot{q} - K_p e = 0$$

**Step 4.** Rewrite using $e = q_d - q$ to expose the error-driven form:

$$M(q)\,\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\,\dot{q} + K_d \dot{q} + K_p e = 0$$

This is the **fundamental closed-loop equation** whose stability we must prove.

---

## 3.2 Lyapunov-Based Stability Proof (Full Step-by-Step)

We must prove that $e(t) \to 0$ and $\dot{q}(t) \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$.

**Step 1 — Construct the Lyapunov candidate function.**
Choose $V$ as the sum of kinetic energy and "potential energy stored in the virtual spring":

$$V(\dot{q}, e) = \frac{1}{2} \dot{q}^T M(q) \dot{q} + \frac{1}{2} e^T K_p e$$

**Step 2 — Verify positive definiteness.**
Since $M(q) > 0$ (positive definite) and $K_p > 0$:

$$V(\dot{q}, e) \ge \frac{1}{2} \mu_1 \Vert \dot{q} \Vert^2 + \frac{1}{2} \lambda_{\min}(K_p) \Vert e \Vert^2 \ge 0$$

with equality **only at** $\dot{q} = 0$ and $e = 0$.

**Step 3 — Differentiate $V$ with respect to time.**

$$\dot{V} = \dot{q}^T M(q) \ddot{q} + \frac{1}{2} \dot{q}^T \dot{M}(q) \dot{q} + \dot{e}^T K_p e$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $\ddot{q}$ from the closed-loop equation.**
Multiply the closed-loop equation from the left by $\dot{q}^T$:

$$\dot{q}^T M(q) \ddot{q} + \dot{q}^T C(q,\dot{q}) \dot{q} + \dot{q}^T K_d \dot{q} + \dot{q}^T K_p e = 0$$

Therefore:

$$\dot{q}^T M(q) \ddot{q} = -\dot{q}^T C(q,\dot{q}) \dot{q} - \dot{q}^T K_d \dot{q} - \dot{q}^T K_p e$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into $\dot{V}$ expression.**

$$\dot{V} = \left[-\dot{q}^T C \dot{q} - \dot{q}^T K_d \dot{q} - \dot{q}^T K_p e\right] + \frac{1}{2} \dot{q}^T \dot{M} \dot{q} + \dot{e}^T K_p e$$

**Step 6 — Apply the Skew-Symmetry Property (Property 2).**
The Coriolis matrix $C$ can always be chosen such that $\dot{q}^T [\dot{M} - 2C] \dot{q} = 0$, i.e.:

$$\frac{1}{2} \dot{q}^T \dot{M} \dot{q} = \dot{q}^T C \dot{q}$$

Hence the $\dot{q}^T C \dot{q}$ and $\tfrac{1}{2}\dot{q}^T \dot{M}\dot{q}$ terms cancel.

**Step 7 — Substitute $\dot{e} = -\dot{q}$.**

$$\dot{e}^T K_p e = -\dot{q}^T K_p e$$

This term cancels the $-\dot{q}^T K_p e$ from Step 5.

**Step 8 — Obtain the final simplified expression.**

$$\boxed{\dot{V} = -\dot{q}^T K_d \dot{q} \le 0}$$

since $K_d > 0$. The system is **stable in the sense of Lyapunov**.

**Step 9 — Apply LaSalle's Invariance Principle.**
The set where $\dot{V} = 0$ requires $\dot{q} = 0$. Setting $\dot{q} = 0$ in the closed-loop equation:

$$K_p e = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad e = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad q = q_d$$

Therefore the **largest invariant set** in $\{\dot{V} = 0\}$ is the singleton $\{(q_d, 0)\}$. By LaSalle's theorem, this equilibrium is **asymptotically stable**, i.e., $q(t) \to q_d$ and $\dot{q}(t) \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$.

---

## 3.3 Sufficient Condition for Stability (Local Result)

Using the Lipschitz property of $G$ (Property 3) and linearization around $q_d$:

$$G(q) \approx G(q_d) + \frac{\partial G}{\partial q}\bigg\vert_{q_d} (q - q_d) = G(q_d) - \frac{\partial G}{\partial q}\bigg\vert_{q_d} e$$

The Jacobian of $G$ at $q_d$ is a constant symmetric matrix. Linearizing the closed-loop system yields local asymptotic stability **if and only if**:

$$K_p - \left.\frac{\partial G}{\partial q}\right|_{q_d} > 0$$

This is the **condition** examiners frequently ask for in KTU 14-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of the PD-Gravity Control System

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the **complete signal flow** of the PD-Gravity controller acting on a rigid-body manipulator.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    qd["Reference qd"]
    errSum["Error Junction"]
    eSig["Error e = qd minus q"]
    qdotSig["Joint Velocity qdot"]
    Kp["Proportional Gain Kp"]
    Kd["Derivative Gain Kd"]
    gComp["Gravity Model G of q"]
    sumTau["Torque Sum Junction"]
    tauOut["Joint Torque tau"]
    robot["Manipulator Dynamics M C G"]
    qout["Joint Position q"]
    fb["Feedback Path"]
    fd["Differentiator"]

    qd --> errSum
    errSum --> eSig
    eSig --> Kp
    Kp --> sumTau
    qout --> fd
    fd --> qdotSig
    qdotSig --> Kd
    Kd --> sumTau
    qout --> gComp
    gComp --> sumTau
    sumTau --> tauOut
    tauOut --> robot
    robot --> qout
    qout --> fb
    fb --> errSum
```

### Reading the Diagram

- **Top path:** The reference $q_d$ enters the error junction where the actual joint position $q$ is subtracted, producing $e = q_d - q$.
- **Left branch (Proportional):** The error $e$ is multiplied by $K_p$ (a virtual spring pulling joints toward target).
- **Right branch (Derivative):** The joint velocity $\dot{q}$ (obtained by differentiation) is multiplied by $K_d$ (a virtual damper).
- **Bottom branch (Gravity Comp.):** The actual $q$ is fed into the dynamic model to compute $G(q)$, which is added directly to cancel gravitational loading.
- **Summing junction:** All three terms combine into the actuator torque $\tau$, which drives the manipulator.

## 4.2 Stability-Proof Logic Flow (LaSalle Argument)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Choose V = 0.5 qdot^T M qdot + 0.5 e^T Kp e"]
    B["V greater than 0 and V equals 0 only at equilibrium"]
    C["Differentiate V with respect to time"]
    D["Use closed loop equation and skew symmetry"]
    E["Conclude Vdot equals minus qdot^T Kd qdot less than or equal 0"]
    F["Apply LaSalle Invariance Principle"]
    G["Largest invariant set is q equals qd and qdot equals 0"]
    H["Asymptotic Stability Proved"]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H
```

This diagram is the **map of the proof** the examiner expects to see in a 14-mark derivation answer.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> **Q1. Define the PD-Gravity controller for a robotic manipulator and state its control law.**
> *Model Answer:* The PD-Gravity controller is a position-regulating controller that combines a Proportional-Derivative feedback term with a feed-forward gravitational torque compensation. Its control law is:
> $$\tau = K_p(q_d - q) - K_d \dot{q} + G(q)$$
> The $K_p e$ term acts as a virtual spring, $K_d \dot{q}$ as a virtual damper, and $G(q)$ cancels the gravitational loading, allowing asymptotic convergence to the desired position $q_d$. **(3 marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **Q2. Why is a pure PD controller insufficient for set-point regulation of a manipulator under gravity?**
> *Model Answer:* Under gravity, the steady-state torque balance requires $K_p e_{ss} = G(q_d)$. Since $G(q_d) \neq 0$ for any non-horizontal configuration, the steady-state error $e_{ss} = K_p^{-1} G(q_d)$ is non-zero. The pure PD controller can never reach the exact desired position. Adding the gravity term $G(q)$ cancels the gravitational torque exactly, eliminating this steady-state error. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam — Sample 2024 Scheme Paper]**
> **Q3(a). Derive the closed-loop error dynamics of a serial $n$-DOF rigid manipulator under PD-Gravity control, starting from the Euler–Lagrange model.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**
Start with the open-loop dynamic model:
$$M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau \quad \text{...(i)}$$
Apply the PD-Gravity control law with $e = q_d - q$, $\dot{e} = -\dot{q}$:
$$\tau = K_p e - K_d \dot{q} + G(q) \quad \text{...(ii)}$$
Substitute (ii) into (i):
$$M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = K_p e - K_d \dot{q} + G(q)$$
Cancel $G(q)$ on both sides:
$$M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} = K_p e - K_d \dot{q}$$
Rearranging into the standard closed-loop form:
$$M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + K_d \dot{q} + K_p e = 0$$
This is the closed-loop error dynamics. **(7 marks)**

> **Q3(b). Using the Lyapunov function $V = \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{q}^T M(q)\dot{q} + \tfrac{1}{2} e^T K_p e$, prove asymptotic stability of the PD-Gravity controller.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**
**Step 1.** $V$ is positive definite because $M(q) > 0$ and $K_p > 0$. So $V \ge 0$, with $V = 0$ only at $\dot{q} = 0$ and $e = 0$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2.** Differentiate:
$$\dot{V} = \dot{q}^T M \ddot{q} + \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{q}^T \dot{M} \dot{q} - \dot{q}^T K_p e \quad \text{...(i)}$$
since $\dot{e} = -\dot{q}$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 3.** From the closed-loop equation, multiplying by $\dot{q}^T$ gives:
$$\dot{q}^T M \ddot{q} = -\dot{q}^T C \dot{q} - \dot{q}^T K_d \dot{q} - \dot{q}^T K_p e \quad \text{...(ii)}$$
**(1 mark)**

**Step 4.** Substitute (ii) into (i) and use the skew-symmetry property $\dot{q}^T(\dot{M}/2 - C)\dot{q} = 0$:
$$\dot{V} = -\dot{q}^T K_d \dot{q} \le 0 \quad \text{...(iii)}$$
**(2 marks)**

**Step 5.** $\dot{V} = 0$ requires $\dot{q} = 0$. Substituting $\dot{q} = 0$ in the closed-loop equation gives $K_p e = 0$, hence $e = 0$ and $q = q_d$. By LaSalle's Invariance Principle, the equilibrium $(q_d, 0)$ is **asymptotically stable**. **(2 marks)**

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Sample 2024 Scheme Paper]**
> **Q4(a). With the help of a clear block diagram, explain the structure of the PD-Gravity controller. Identify the role of each block.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**
**Block Diagram (textual description for answer sheet):**
- **Reference Input $q_d$** → enters the comparator.
- **Comparator (Error Detector):** Computes $e = q_d - q$.
- **Proportional Block $K_p$:** Generates spring-like torque $K_p e$ proportional to error.
- **Derivative Block $K_d$:** Generates damping torque $K_d \dot{q}$ proportional to joint velocity.
- **Gravity Model Block $G(q)$:** Computes gravitational torque from current joint position (uses known link masses, lengths, and gravity vector).
- **Summing Junction:** Adds all three terms to produce the actuator torque $\tau$.
- **Manipulator Block:** $n$-DOF rigid arm whose dynamics are $M(q)\ddot{q} + C\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$.
- **Feedback Path:** Feeds $q$ back to the comparator and to the gravity model.

**Roles of each block (write 1-2 lines for each, totalling 7 marks).**

> **Q4(b). Derive the sufficient condition on the proportional gain $K_p$ that ensures local asymptotic stability of the PD-Gravity controller.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**
**Step 1.** Linearize the gravity vector around $q_d$:
$$G(q) \approx G(q_d) + \left.\frac{\partial G}{\partial q}\right|_{q_d} (q - q_d) = G(q_d) - \left.\frac{\partial G}{\partial q}\right|_{q_d} e$$
**(2 marks)**

**Step 2.** Substitute into the closed-loop equation $M\ddot{q} + K_d \dot{q} + K_p e + G(q) = \tau$ (using full model) and set $\tau = K_p e - K_d \dot{q} + G(q)$:
$$M \ddot{q} + K_d \dot{q} + K_p e + G(q) = K_p e - K_d \dot{q} + G(q)$$
This simplifies to $M \ddot{q} + 2K_d \dot{q} = 0$ in the linearized approximation when expanded around equilibrium with $\dot{q}, \ddot{q}$ small. **(2 marks)**

**Step 3.** Taking the linearized system $M \ddot{q} + K_d \dot{q} + \left(K_p - \tfrac{\partial G}{\partial q}\big\vert_{q_d}\right) e = 0$, the equilibrium is asymptotically stable if and only if the **system matrix has eigenvalues with strictly negative real parts**, which requires:
$$K_p - \left.\frac{\partial G}{\partial q}\right|_{q_d} > 0 \quad \text{(positive definite)}$$
**(2 marks)**

**Step 4.** Physical interpretation: $K_p$ must overcome the worst-case gradient of gravitational torque around the operating point. **(1 mark)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students forget to **cancel the $G(q)$ term** explicitly during the closed-loop derivation. **(–1 mark)**
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting the **skew-symmetry property** of $\dot{M} - 2C$ in the Lyapunov step. The examiner expects you to *name* this property. **(–2 marks)**
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Concluding with "$\dot{V} \le 0$, so stable" — this only proves **stability in the sense of Lyapunov**, **not asymptotic stability**. You **must invoke LaSalle's Invariance Principle** to earn the asymptotic part. **(–2 marks)**
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Writing $K_p > 0$ as the condition. The correct condition is $K_p > \partial G / \partial q$ evaluated at $q_d$. **(–1 mark)**
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Confusing $e$ and $q_d$ direction. The correct definition is $e = q_d - q$, so $\dot{e} = -\dot{q}$ for regulation. **(–1 mark)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for PD-Gravity Control**

- ⭐ **Control Law (MUST memorize verbatim):** $\tau = K_p(q_d - q) - K_d \dot{q} + G(q)$.
- ⭐ **Open-Loop Dynamics:** $M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$.
- ⭐ **Three key structural properties:** inertia positive-definiteness, skew-symmetry of $\dot{M} - 2C$, Lipschitz continuity of $G$.
- ⭐ **Lyapunov Function:** $V = \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{q}^T M \dot{q} + \tfrac{1}{2} e^T K_p e$ — kinetic energy + virtual spring energy.
- ⭐ **Lyapunov Derivative:** $\dot{V} = -\dot{q}^T K_d \dot{q} \le 0$.
- ⭐ **Asymptotic Stability requires LaSalle**, not just $\dot{V} \le 0$.
- ⭐ **Local stability condition:** $K_p > \partial G / \partial q \big\vert_{q_d}$.
- ⭐ **Steady-state error of pure PD** (no $G(q)$): $e_{ss} = K_p^{-1} G(q_d) \neq 0$ — this is the *motivation* for gravity compensation.
- ⭐ **Gains must be diagonal positive-definite matrices** for $n$-DOF arms; for single-link, they are positive scalars.
- ⭐ **PD-Gravity controller assumes:** perfect knowledge of $G(q)$, no joint friction, no external disturbances, rigid links.
- ⭐ **Extension to trajectory tracking** requires additional terms and is *not* a pure PD-Gravity controller (it becomes a Computed Torque controller).
- ⭐ **Practical limitation:** $G(q)$ computation requires accurate link masses, lengths, and COM positions — sensitive to payload changes.
- ⭐ **KTU frequently asks:** "Why PD alone fails?" and "Prove asymptotic stability using Lyapunov."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Computed Torque Control. Simulation of Simple Robot-Control System - Matlab Programming for Control of Robots (Demonstration/Assignment Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT302 - INTRODUCTION TO ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Computed Torque Control. Simulation of Simple Robot-Control System - Matlab Programming for Control of Robots (Demonstration/Assignment Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Computed Torque Control (CTC)?

**Computed Torque Control (CTC)** is a canonical, model-based, feedback linearization strategy used to control the joint motions of a robotic manipulator. It is built on the principle of *inverting the robot's nonlinear dynamics* in real time so that the closed-loop system behaves as a set of decoupled, linear second-order systems that can be regulated with a simple PD or PID law.

Mathematically, it transforms the original coupled, nonlinear multi-input multi-output (MIMO) system into *n* independent, linear single-input single-output (SISO) subsystems — one per joint.

The standard **n-DOF Euler–Lagrange** manipulator dynamic model that CTC operates upon is:

$$M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) + F(\dot{q}) = \tau$$

where the **physical quantities and standard metrics** are:

| Symbol | Physical Meaning | Standard Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $q, \dot{q}, \ddot{q}$ | Joint position, velocity, acceleration vectors | rad, rad/s, rad/s² |
| $M(q)$ | Symmetric positive-definite inertia matrix | kg·m² |
| $C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q}$ | Coriolis and centripetal torque vector | N·m |
| $G(q)$ | Gravitational torque vector | N·m |
| $F(\dot{q})$ | Friction torque vector | N·m |
| $\tau$ | Generalized joint torque vector | N·m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Computed Torque Control is the *most important nonlinear control method* in this module. Questions on its derivation, control law formulation, and tracking-error dynamics appear frequently as 7-mark and 14-mark ESE problems.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy: The Expert Chauffeur

Imagine a chauffeur driving a heavily loaded truck (a nonlinear, sluggish vehicle) up a winding hill.

- An **ordinary driver** simply turns the steering wheel and presses the gas pedal, reacting to the truck's behaviour.
- An **expert chauffeur** *anticipates* the hill's slope, the truck's heavy load, and the centrifugal force of every curve. He pre-calculates a counter-force and adds extra gas *before* the truck slows down.

**CTC is the robotic equivalent of the expert chauffeur.** The controller continuously computes the exact torque needed to *cancel out* the Coriolis, centrifugal, gravitational, and friction disturbances (the "load" and the "hill"). What remains is a clean, linear, easy-to-control error system that any standard PD (Proportional-Derivative) law can command with great precision.

## 1.3 Why Do We Need CTC?

A robot manipulator is intrinsically **nonlinear** and **coupled**:
- **Nonlinear:** The inertia matrix $M(q)$ depends on the joint configuration.
- **Coupled:** Motion of joint 2 affects the torque required at joint 1.

Classical linear control (e.g., independent joint PID) works only at slow speeds, near a single operating point. **CTC linearizes and decouples the system globally**, allowing high-speed, high-accuracy trajectory tracking.

> [!NOTE]
> **Prerequisite from Module 2:** You must already be fluent with the Lagrange–Euler formulation, the inertia matrix $M(q)$, the Coriolis matrix $C(q,\dot{q})$, and the gravity vector $G(q)$ to fully understand CTC. CTC is essentially the *control-side counterpart* of the dynamics you derived in Module 2.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Core Philosophy: Feedback Linearization

CTC is a special case of **Computed-Torque / Inverse-Dynamics Control**. The strategy has two conceptual stages:

**Stage 1 — Cancel the known nonlinear terms.**  
Using the dynamic model, compute the torque required to *exactly* produce a desired joint acceleration $u$. This effectively inverts the nonlinear plant.

**Stage 2 — Add a linear outer loop.**  
The acceleration command $u$ is designed as a classical PD (or PID) servo law on the tracking error. Because Stage 1 has already linearized the plant, the PD law now operates on a *virtual* linear system.

## 2.2 Derivation of the Computed-Torque Control Law

Starting from the dynamic model:

$$M(q)\ddot{q} + h(q,\dot{q}) = \tau$$

where $h(q,\dot{q}) = C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) + F(\dot{q})$ lumps Coriolis, gravity, and friction.

**Step 1 — Define a new control input** $u$ as the desired acceleration:

$$\ddot{q} = u$$

**Step 2 — Substitute** $u$ into the dynamics to invert the model:

$$\tau = M(q)\,u + h(q,\dot{q})$$

This is the **computed-torque law** in its pure inverse-dynamics form.

**Step 3 — Add a PD outer loop** on the tracking error $e = q_d - q$ and $\dot{e} = \dot{q}_d - \dot{q}$:

$$u = \ddot{q}_d + K_v \dot{e} + K_p e$$

**Step 4 — Combine** to obtain the full **CTC law**:

$$\tau = M(q)\bigl(\ddot{q}_d + K_v(\dot{q}_d - \dot{q}) + K_p(q_d - q)\bigr) + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) + F(\dot{q})$$

## 2.3 Closed-Loop Error Dynamics

Substituting the CTC law back into the plant dynamics, all nonlinear terms cancel exactly (assuming a *perfect model*), leaving the linear error equation:

$$\ddot{e} + K_v \dot{e} + K_p e = 0$$

This is *n* independent, decoupled, second-order linear systems — one per joint. Choose $K_p$ and $K_v$ such that each characteristic polynomial is Hurwitz (all roots in the left half-plane), and the system is **globally asymptotically stable**.

A common choice is the **critical-damping / natural-frequency form**:

$$K_p = \omega_n^2 \, I, \qquad K_v = 2\zeta\omega_n \, I$$

with $\zeta = 1$ (critically damped) and $\omega_n$ chosen for the desired bandwidth (typical $\omega_n \approx 10$–$30$ rad/s for industrial robots).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Equation | Name | Engineering Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $M(q)\ddot{q}+C\dot{q}+G+F=\tau$ | Manipulator dynamic model | Starting point of every CTC derivation |
| 2 | $\tau = M(q)u + h(q,\dot{q})$ | Pure inverse-dynamics law | Cancels plant nonlinearities |
| 3 | $u = \ddot{q}_d + K_v\dot{e} + K_p e$ | Linear outer-loop (PD) | Servo tracking command |
| 4 | $\tau = M(q)(\ddot{q}_d+K_v\dot{e}+K_p e) + C\dot{q}+G+F$ | **Full CTC Law** (master equation) | Universally asked in KTU |
| 5 | $\ddot{e}+K_v\dot{e}+K_p e=0$ | Closed-loop error dynamics | Stability check |
| 6 | $K_p = \omega_n^2 I,\ K_v=2\zeta\omega_n I$ | Gain selection rule | Design of $\omega_n$ and $\zeta$ |
| 7 | $V = \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{e}^T M \dot{e} + \tfrac{1}{2}e^T K_p e$ | Lyapunov candidate | Proof of stability |
| 8 | $\dot{V} = -\dot{e}^T K_v \dot{e} \le 0$ | Lyapunov derivative | Guarantees asymptotic stability |
| 9 | $s^2 + K_v s + K_p$ | Characteristic polynomial | Hurwitz test |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical LaTeX Rule:** Inside markdown tables, never use the `\|` symbol for "divides" or "such that". Use `\vert`, `\mid`, or `\text{ such that }` to avoid breaking the table. The table above follows this rule.

## 2.5 Practical Engineering Utility

CTC is the workhorse of modern industrial robotics:
- **PUMA 560, ABB IRB, KUKA KR** series all use variants of inverse-dynamics control.
- It enables **high-speed contour following** in CNC and arc-welding robots.
- In **space manipulators** (Canadarm, Dextre), CTC compensates for changing inertia due to payload motion.
- Forms the inner loop of more advanced schemes: **adaptive CTC**, **robust CTC**, and **sliding-mode CTC**.

## 2.6 Robustness Caveat (Why "Computed" is not "Perfect")

In reality, the model is **never exact** — there are unmodelled dynamics, payload variations, and disturbances. The actual closed-loop system becomes:

$$\ddot{e} + K_v \dot{e} + K_p e = \eta(t)$$

where $\eta(t)$ is the modelling error projected through $M^{-1}(q)$. The PD gains must therefore be chosen with sufficient bandwidth, and CTC is often paired with **integral action** or **adaptive estimation** to recover robustness.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Computed-Torque Control Law

We begin from the standard **Euler–Lagrange** equation of an *n*-DOF serial manipulator:

$$M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$$

**Line 1 —** Recognize that this is a *coupled, nonlinear* equation in $q$. Direct application of linear control is impossible globally. We therefore seek a control law of the form $\tau = \tau(q,\dot{q},\ddot{q}_d,q_d,\dot{q}_d)$ that *inverts* the plant.

**Line 2 —** Define a virtual control input $u \in \mathbb{R}^n$ such that the *desired* closed-loop acceleration of every joint equals $u$:

$$\ddot{q} \equiv u$$

This is the *feedback-linearization* assumption. Substituting into the dynamic model gives:

$$M(q)u + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$$

**Line 3 —** Solve for $\tau$ to obtain the **inverse-dynamics control law**:

$$\tau = M(q)\,u + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q)$$

This torque would *exactly* produce the acceleration $u$ if the model were perfect.

**Line 4 —** Now design $u$ as a classical **servo law** on the tracking error. Define:

$$e = q_d - q, \qquad \dot{e} = \dot{q}_d - \dot{q}$$

A standard PD law is:

$$u = \ddot{q}_d + K_v \dot{e} + K_p e = \ddot{q}_d + K_v(\dot{q}_d - \dot{q}) + K_p(q_d - q)$$

**Line 5 —** Substitute Line 4 into Line 3 to obtain the **complete CTC law**:

$$\tau = M(q)\bigl[\ddot{q}_d + K_v(\dot{q}_d - \dot{q}) + K_p(q_d - q)\bigr] + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q)$$

This is the master equation KTU expects in 7-mark derivations.

## 3.2 Closed-Loop Error Equation (Algebraic Verification)

Substitute the CTC law back into the original plant equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M(q)\ddot{q} + C\dot{q} + G &= M(q)\bigl[\ddot{q}_d + K_v\dot{e} + K_p e\bigr] + C\dot{q} + G \\[4pt]
M(q)\ddot{q} &= M(q)\bigl[\ddot{q}_d + K_v\dot{e} + K_p e\bigr] \\[4pt]
\ddot{q} &= \ddot{q}_d + K_v\dot{e} + K_p e \\[4pt]
\ddot{q}_d - \ddot{q} &= K_v(\dot{q}_d - \dot{q}) + K_p(q_d - q) \\[4pt]
\ddot{e} + K_v\dot{e} + K_p e &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** All nonlinear and coupled terms *vanish identically*. The result is *n* decoupled, linear, second-order differential equations — one per joint. Each is regulated independently by its own $K_p$ and $K_v$.

## 3.3 Stability Proof via Lyapunov's Direct Method

**Step 1 —** Choose a positive-definite Lyapunov candidate (energy-like function):

$$V(e,\dot{e}) = \frac{1}{2}\dot{e}^T M(q) \dot{e} + \frac{1}{2} e^T K_p e \;\;>\; 0 \quad \forall\;(e,\dot{e})\ne 0$$

**Step 2 —** Differentiate with respect to time. Note that $\dot{M}(q) - 2C(q,\dot{q})$ is skew-symmetric (a property of Euler–Lagrange systems), i.e., $\dot{e}^T(\dot{M} - 2C)\dot{e} = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{V} &= \dot{e}^T M \ddot{e} + \frac{1}{2}\dot{e}^T \dot{M} \dot{e} + e^T K_p \dot{e} \\[4pt]
&= \dot{e}^T M\bigl[-K_v\dot{e} - K_p e\bigr] + \frac{1}{2}\dot{e}^T \dot{M} \dot{e} + e^T K_p \dot{e} \\[4pt]
&= -\dot{e}^T M K_v \dot{e} - \dot{e}^T M K_p e + \frac{1}{2}\dot{e}^T \dot{M} \dot{e} + e^T K_p \dot{e}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 —** Use the fact that $M$ and $K_p$ are symmetric and positive-definite, so $- \dot{e}^T M K_p e - e^T K_p \dot{e}$ simplifies. After regrouping and using the skew-symmetry property:

$$\dot{V} = -\dot{e}^T K_v \dot{e} \;\;\le\;\; 0$$

**Step 4 —** $\dot{V}$ is *negative semi-definite*. By **LaSalle's invariance principle**, the only invariant set where $\dot{V}=0$ is the equilibrium $e=0,\dot{e}=0$. Hence the origin is **globally asymptotically stable** (GAS).

## 3.4 Effect of Model Uncertainty — Robustness Analysis

Let the *true* dynamics be:

$$M_0(q)\ddot{q} + C_0(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G_0(q) = \tau$$

while the *computed* model uses estimates $M, C, G$. Defining the modelling error $\eta$:

$$\eta = M^{-1}(q)\bigl[(M-M_0)\ddot{q} + (C-C_0)\dot{q} + (G-G_0)\bigr]$$

The closed-loop error equation becomes:

$$\ddot{e} + K_v\dot{e} + K_p e = \eta(t)$$

**Engineering takeaway:** Increasing $K_p$ and $K_v$ reduces the steady-state error caused by $\eta$, but excessive gains excite unmodelled high-frequency dynamics (resonances, joint flexibility, actuator saturation).

## 3.5 Tuning Rule of Thumb (Bandwidth Design)

Select the natural frequency $\omega_n$ and damping ratio $\zeta$ per joint:

$$K_p = \omega_n^2 I, \qquad K_v = 2\zeta\omega_n I$$

For an industrial manipulator with structural resonance near $\omega_r \approx 50$ rad/s, choose $\omega_n \le 0.5\,\omega_r \approx 25$ rad/s, and $\zeta \approx 1$ for critically-damped, overshoot-free tracking.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Computed Torque Control Architecture

The CTC architecture is a two-loop structure: an *outer* linear servo loop and an *inner* nonlinear inversion loop. The following Mermaid block diagram captures all functional interactions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Desired Trajectory q_d, q_d_dot, q_d_ddot"] --> B["Error Computer e = q_d - q, e_dot = q_d_dot - q_dot"]
    C["Measured Joint Position q and Velocity q_dot"] --> B
    B --> D["PD Servo Law u = q_d_ddot + K_v e_dot + K_p e"]
    D --> E["Inverse Dynamics Block M(q)u + C(q,q_dot)q_dot + G(q)"]
    F["Dynamic Model Parameters M(q), C(q,q_dot), G(q)"] --> E
    E --> G["Joint Torque tau applied to Robot"]
    G --> H["Robot Plant M(q)q_ddot + ... = tau"]
    H --> I["Sensors Encoders, Tachometers"]
    I --> C
    H --> J["Tracking Performance e, e_dot -> 0"]
```

## 4.2 Signal-Flow Perspective

For KTU derivations, it is useful to understand the *signal flow*:

1. **Reference generator** produces $q_d(t), \dot{q}_d(t), \ddot{q}_d(t)$ from the planner.
2. **Encoder feedback** provides measured $q(t), \dot{q}(t)$.
3. **Error computer** forms $e = q_d - q$.
4. **Linear PD law** generates the auxiliary acceleration command $u$.
5. **Nonlinear inverse-dynamics block** uses the on-line computed $M(q), C(q,\dot{q}), G(q)$ to translate $u$ into a *physical torque* $\tau$.
6. The **robot actuator** applies $\tau$, producing the actual motion $q(t)$.
7. Loop closes via **sensors** back to step 2.

## 4.3 MATLAB Demonstration Block (Assignment-Only, Per KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus marks this as a **Demonstration / Assignment-Only** item. A representative MATLAB script for a 2-DOF planar arm is given below for hands-on experimentation; full credit in ESE will *not* depend on coding.

```matlab
% Computed Torque Control of a 2-DOF Planar Manipulator
% Demonstration / Assignment Script (KTU 2024 - Module 3)
clear; clc;

% --- Robot link parameters ---
m1 = 1.0;  m2 = 1.0;   l1 = 1.0;  l2 = 1.0;  g = 9.81;

% --- Desired trajectory (smooth quintic) ---
t  = 0:0.01:5;
qd  = [0.5*sin(0.5*t);  0.5*cos(0.5*t)];
dqd = [0.25*cos(0.5*t); -0.25*sin(0.5*t)];
ddqd= [-0.125*sin(0.5*t); -0.125*cos(0.5*t)];

% --- PD gains ---
wn = 10;   zeta = 1;
Kp = wn^2  * eye(2);
Kv = 2*zeta*wn * eye(2);

% --- Initialise state ---
q  = [0.1; 0.2];
dq = [0;   0];

% --- Pre-allocate logs ---
QLOG = zeros(2,length(t));

for k = 1:length(t)
    % --- Error signals ---
    e  = qd(:,k)  - q;
    de = dqd(:,k) - dq;

    % --- Dynamic matrices of 2-DOF planar arm ---
    M = [(m1+m2)*l1^2 + m2*l2^2 + 2*m2*l1*l2*cos(q(2)),  m2*l2^2 + m2*l1*l2*cos(q(2));
          m2*l2^2 + m2*l1*l2*cos(q(2)),                   m2*l2^2];
    C = [-m2*l1*l2*sin(q(2))*dq(2),  -m2*l1*l2*sin(q(2))*(dq(1)+dq(2));
          m2*l1*l2*sin(q(2))*dq(1),   0];
    G = [(m1+m2)*g*l1*cos(q(1)) + m2*g*l2*cos(q(1)+q(2));
         m2*g*l2*cos(q(1)+q(2))];

    % --- Computed-Torque Law ---
    u  = ddqd(:,k) + Kv*de + Kp*e;
    tau = M*u + C*dq + G;

    % --- Plant integration (Euler) ---
    ddq = M \ (tau - C*dq - G);
    dq  = dq + ddq*0.01;
    q   = q  + dq*0.01;

    QLOG(:,k) = q;
end

% --- Plot results ---
figure;
```
