```matlab
subplot(2,1,1); plot(t,QLOG(1,:),'b',t,qd(1,:),'r--'); 
legend('q_1','q_{d1}'); xlabel('t (s)'); ylabel('rad');
subplot(2,1,2); plot(t,QLOG(2,:),'b',t,qd(2,:),'r--'); 
legend('q_2','q_{d2}'); xlabel('t (s)'); ylabel('rad');
```

**Expected behaviour:** The solid lines ($q_1, q_2$) should converge to and overlap the dashed lines ($q_{d1}, q_{d2}$) within ~1 second, with no steady-state error — the hallmark of correctly tuned CTC.

## 4.4 Equivalent Block Diagram (Feedback-Linearization View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Reference q_d"] --> SUM1(("+"))
    SUM1 --> C["Linear Compensator K_p + K_v s"]
    C --> INV["Nonlinear Inverse M^-1 cancels Plant"]
    INV --> P["Robot Plant nonlinear, coupled"]
    P --> Q["Actual q"]
    Q --> SUM1
    Q --> OUT["Tracking error e -> 0"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Computed Torque Control. Why is it also called inverse-dynamics control? [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**Model Answer (≈ 90 words):**

Computed Torque Control is a model-based, feedback-linearization strategy in which the joint torque is computed in real time as the sum of (i) a **feedforward term** that cancels the manipulator's nonlinear dynamics, and (ii) a **linear PD feedback** term that stabilizes the resulting error dynamics. It is called *inverse-dynamics control* because the control law explicitly inverts the Euler–Lagrange dynamic model $M(q)\ddot{q}+C\dot{q}+G=\tau$ to translate a desired acceleration into a torque command.

---

### Q2. State the closed-loop error dynamics equation of a robot under Computed Torque Control. What condition on $K_p$ and $K_v$ guarantees stability? [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**Model Answer (≈ 80 words):**

For a perfectly modelled *n*-DOF manipulator under CTC, the closed-loop tracking-error dynamics reduces to:

$$\ddot{e} + K_v \dot{e} + K_p e = 0$$

This is a set of *n* decoupled linear second-order systems. Stability is guaranteed if the gain matrices $K_p$ and $K_v$ are **symmetric positive-definite**, ensuring that all eigenvalues of the closed-loop characteristic polynomial $s^2 + K_v s + K_p$ lie strictly in the open left half of the complex plane (Hurwitz condition).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — Full 14-Mark Problem

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Starting from the Euler–Lagrange dynamic model of an *n*-DOF serial manipulator, derive the **Computed Torque Control law**. Clearly state every assumption. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Show that under the derived CTC law, the closed-loop tracking error satisfies a **decoupled linear second-order differential equation**. Hence design $K_p$ and $K_v$ for a critically-damped response with natural frequency $\omega_n = 15$ rad/s. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Assumption 1:** The dynamic model $M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$ is *known exactly*.

**Assumption 2:** Joint position $q$ and velocity $\dot{q}$ are measurable (encoders, tachometers).

**Assumption 3:** The control input $\tau$ can be applied *instantaneously* (no actuator dynamics).

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Plant:}\quad M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau \\[4pt]
&\text{Let the desired acceleration be:}\quad \ddot{q} = u \\[4pt]
&\text{Inverse dynamics gives:}\quad \tau = M(q)u + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) \\[4pt]
&\text{Choose the auxiliary input as a PD law:}\quad u = \ddot{q}_d + K_v\dot{e} + K_p e \\[4pt]
&\text{where}\quad e = q_d - q,\;\; \dot{e} = \dot{q}_d - \dot{q} \\[6pt]
&\boxed{\;\tau = M(q)\bigl[\ddot{q}_d + K_v(\dot{q}_d - \dot{q}) + K_p(q_d - q)\bigr] + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q)\;}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Computed-Torque Control law**. *(7 marks distributed: 1 for assumptions, 3 for inversion step, 3 for substitution and final law.)*

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Step 1 — Substitute the CTC law back into the plant:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M(q)\ddot{q} + C\dot{q} + G &= M(q)[\ddot{q}_d + K_v\dot{e} + K_p e] + C\dot{q} + G \\[4pt]
M(q)\ddot{q} &= M(q)[\ddot{q}_d + K_v\dot{e} + K_p e] \\[4pt]
\ddot{q} &= \ddot{q}_d + K_v\dot{e} + K_p e \\[4pt]
\ddot{q}_d - \ddot{q} &= K_v(\dot{q}_d - \dot{q}) + K_p(q_d - q) \\[4pt]
\ddot{e} + K_v\dot{e} + K_p e &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a *decoupled* linear second-order ODE in $e(t)$ — the nonlinear coupling has been **exactly cancelled**. Each joint is now controlled independently. *(4 marks)*

**Step 2 — Gain design for critical damping:**

Given the standard second-order form $s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = 0$, we identify:

$$K_p = \omega_n^2 I, \qquad K_v = 2\zeta\omega_n I$$

For critical damping, $\zeta = 1$; with $\omega_n = 15$ rad/s:

$$K_p = (15)^2 I = 225\,I \;\text{rad/s}^2, \qquad K_v = 2(1)(15)\,I = 30\,I \;\text{rad/s}$$

Explicitly for a 2-DOF arm:

$$K_p = \begin{bmatrix}225 & 0\\ 0 & 225\end{bmatrix},\quad K_v = \begin{bmatrix}30 & 0\\ 0 & 30\end{bmatrix}$$

The closed-loop poles are at $s = -\omega_n = -15$ rad/s (repeated, real, negative) → critically damped, exponential convergence. *(3 marks)*

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — Alternative 14-Mark Problem

**[KTU University Exam – Sample, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With the aid of a neat **block diagram**, explain the functional architecture of a Computed-Torque controlled robotic manipulator. Identify the two loops and the role of the inverse-dynamics block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using **Lyapunov's direct method**, prove that the equilibrium $e=0, \dot{e}=0$ of the CTC closed-loop system is **globally asymptotically stable**. Clearly state the choice of Lyapunov function and the property of $M(q)$ used. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

A **block diagram** of the CTC architecture is given in Section 4.1. The system has **two cascaded loops**:

- **Outer (Linear Servo) Loop:** Compares desired $q_d$ with measured $q$, forms the error $e$, and processes it through a PD compensator $K_p + K_v s$ to produce the *acceleration command* $u = \ddot{q}_d + K_v\dot{e} + K_p e$.

- **Inner (Nonlinear Inverse) Loop:** Takes the acceleration command $u$ and, using the on-line computed $M(q), C(q,\dot{q}), G(q)$, converts it into a *joint torque* $\tau$ that would produce exactly $u$ at the current state.

**Role of the inverse-dynamics block:** It *cancels* the plant's nonlinearity and coupling term-by-term in real time. After this cancellation, the plant seen by the outer loop is a *unity-gain double integrator* $\ddot{q} = u$, which is trivially stabilized by the PD law. *(7 marks: 3 for block diagram, 2 for loop identification, 2 for inverse-dynamics role.)*

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Step 1 — Choose a positive-definite Lyapunov candidate:**

$$V(e,\dot{e}) = \frac{1}{2}\dot{e}^T M(q)\dot{e} + \frac{1}{2} e^T K_p e$$

Since $M(q)$ is **symmetric positive-definite** and $K_p$ is positive-definite (by design), $V > 0$ for all $(e,\dot{e}) \ne 0$ and $V(0,0) = 0$. *(2 marks)*

**Step 2 — Differentiate along trajectories**, using $\ddot{e} = -K_v\dot{e} - K_p e$ from the closed-loop error equation and noting the **skew-symmetry property** $\dot{M}(q) = C(q,\dot{q}) + C^T(q,\dot{q})$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{V} &= \dot{e}^T M \ddot{e} + \frac{1}{2}\dot{e}^T \dot{M}\dot{e} + e^T K_p \dot{e} \\[4pt]
&= \dot{e}^T M(-K_v\dot{e} - K_p e) + \frac{1}{2}\dot{e}^T \dot{M}\dot{e} + e^T K_p \dot{e} \\[4pt]
&= -\dot{e}^T M K_v \dot{e} - \dot{e}^T M K_p e + e^T K_p \dot{e} + \frac{1}{2}\dot{e}^T \dot{M}\dot{e}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify** using the symmetry of $M$ and $K_p$, and the skew-symmetry of $\dot{M} - 2C$:

$$\dot{V} = -\dot{e}^T K_v \dot{e}$$

Since $K_v$ is positive-definite, $\dot{V} \le 0$ with equality only when $\dot{e} = 0$. *(3 marks)*

**Step 4 — Invoke LaSalle's invariance principle:** the largest invariant set in $\{\dot{V} = 0\}$ is the singleton $\{(0,0)\}$. Hence the equilibrium is **globally asymptotically stable**. *(2 marks)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in CTC Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the gravity term $G(q)$** in the final law. The most frequent 1-mark loss.
> 2. **Confusing $\ddot{q}$ with $u$.** Students often write $M(q)\ddot{q}$ on the right-hand side of the control law by mistake.
> 3. **Omitting assumptions.** At least 1 mark is reserved for stating *model knowledge*, *sensor availability*, and *negligible actuator dynamics*.
> 4. **Not showing the back-substitution** that proves the error equation is $\ddot{e} + K_v\dot{e} + K_p e = 0$. The cancellation step is mandatory.
> 5. **In Lyapunov proofs,** forgetting the **skew-symmetry of $\dot{M}-2C$** is a 1-mark loss every time.
> 6. **MATLAB questions** in ESE are *not* expected; the syllabus marks them as "Demonstration/Assignment only". Don't waste time memorizing code.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Master Equation (memorize verbatim):**  
  $\tau = M(q)[\ddot{q}_d + K_v(\dot{q}_d - \dot{q}) + K_p(q_d - q)] + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) + F(\dot{q})$
- **Closed-loop error equation (after perfect cancellation):** $\ddot{e} + K_v\dot{e} + K_p e = 0$
- **Gain design for critical damping:** $K_p = \omega_n^2 I$, $K_v = 2\zeta\omega_n I$, choose $\zeta = 1$ and $\omega_n \le 0.5\,\omega_{\text{resonance}}$.
- **Stability requirement:** $K_p > 0$ and $K_v > 0$ (symmetric positive-definite).
- **Lyapunov function:** $V = \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{e}^T M \dot{e} + \tfrac{1}{2}e^T K_p e$; derivative $\dot{V} = -\dot{e}^T K_v \dot{e} \le 0$.
- **Key property used:** $M(q)$ is symmetric positive-definite; $\dot{M} - 2C$ is skew-symmetric.
- **CTC ≠ Adaptive CTC:** Plain CTC assumes a *known* model; adaptive variants estimate $M, C, G$ on-line.
- **Engineering bandwidth limit:** Don't push $\omega_n$ above structural resonance; actuator saturation and unmodelled flexibility will destabilize the system.
- **MATLAB scripts** (e.g., the 2-DOF arm demo in §4.3) are for *lab demonstration and assignment evaluation only*; they are **not** part of the ESE theory paper.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Locomotion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT302 - INTRODUCTION TO ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Locomotion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Locomotion in Robotics — Foundational Concepts

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Locomotion** is the mechanism by which an active agent (robot) propels itself through its environment, generating the controlled, coordinated motion of its appendages (wheels, legs, tracks, fins, or other effectors) in order to translate its body from one location to another.

In robotics, locomotion is distinguished from **manipulation** (which moves objects *relative* to the robot). Locomotion is concerned with moving the **robot's own center of mass** through space. The discipline involves three inter-locked sub-problems:

1. **Locomotion mechanism** — the physical actuator arrangement (wheels, legs, tracks, etc.)
2. **Stability & balance** — ensuring the robot does not topple (static vs dynamic stability)
3. **Gait / motion pattern** — the time-sequence of actuation that produces forward translation

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview & Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are watching an **ant carrying a leaf** across a sandy beach. Despite being blown by wind and walking over uneven dunes, the ant reaches its destination. Its success comes from one principle: **continuous, stable ground contact**. A robot "walks" the same way — it must keep its Center of Gravity (CoG) safely supported over a polygon of contact points.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy**
> Think of a person walking on a tightrope (low stability — must constantly adjust, like a biped robot) vs a person walking on a flat floor with both feet planted (very stable — like a quadruped or hexapod). The *number of contact legs* and the *foot spacing* define the **support polygon**, which is the geometric key to all robotic locomotion.

## 1.3 Why Locomotion is Harder than Wheeled Motion

A car on a highway is stable in **every** posture as long as it has 4 tires on the ground. A walking robot is **never** stable in a single static posture — it must *constantly* plan foot placement, balance, and joint torques. This is why the KTU 2024 syllabus spends an entire module on locomotion.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Gravitational acceleration:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- **Standard wheel radius** in mobile robotics: $r = 0.05 \text{ m}$ to $0.30 \text{ m}$
- **Standard step length** of a humanoid robot: $s = 0.20 \text{ m}$ to $0.35 \text{ m}$
- **Stability margin** is dimensionless (measured in metres along the ground plane).
- **Degrees of freedom (DOF)** in a single leg: typically **3 active DOF** (hip yaw, hip pitch, knee pitch) for planar walking, plus 3 passive DOF at the foot/ankle.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Major Categories of Robotic Locomotion

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Categorization (must be memorized)**

| Category | Medium | Energy Efficiency | Stability | Terrain Adaptability |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Wheeled** | Hard/flat | **High** | **High** (static) | **Low** |
| **Legged** | Rough/uneven | Low | Medium (gait-dependent) | **High** |
| **Tracked** | Soft/loose | Medium | **High** | Medium |
| **Aerial / Aquatic / Snake** | Specialised | Varies | Varies | Varies |

## 2.2 Number of Legs — Stability Analysis

The single most important metric in legged locomotion is the **support polygon**. For a robot standing on $n$ feet:

| # of Legs ($n$) | Static Stability? | Stable Gait | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (Hopping) | **No** | Dynamic only | One-legged robots must *always* be in motion. Example: Boston Dynamics "Hopper". |
| 2 (Bipedal) | Borderline | Dynamic | CoG projection must fall **inside** single foot; humans shift weight. |
| 3 (Tripod) | **Yes** (always) | Static | Like a camera tripod — any 3 points define a stable plane. |
| 4 (Quadruped) | **Yes** (during static phases) | Static or dynamic | Cheapest stable legged platform (e.g., Sony AIBO). |
| 6 (Hexapod) | **Yes**, even while walking | Static | Three legs form a tripod gait (alternating). |
| 8+ (Octopod, etc.) | Yes | Static | Most stable; used in research and inspection. |

## 2.3 Key Parameters of a Walking Step

When a legged robot takes one step, four parameters define the geometry:

- **Step length ($s$)** — the horizontal distance the body moves forward during one step.
- **Stride length ($S$)** — the distance moved during one complete locomotion cycle (two steps for symmetric gaits): $S = 2s$.
- **Step height ($h$)** — the vertical clearance the foot lifts off the ground.
- **Step period ($T$)** — time required to complete one stride.

## 2.4 Wheeled Locomotion — Wheel Geometry

A standard wheel can be described by three parameters:

- **Radius** $r$ (m)
- **Encoder resolution** $N$ (ticks/revolution)
- **Distance per tick** $d = \dfrac{2 \pi r}{N}$ (m/tick)

For a **differential drive** robot with two drive wheels separated by wheel-base $L$, the velocity relations are:

$$v_{\text{left}} = \omega \left(R - \frac{L}{2}\right)$$

$$v_{\text{right}} = \omega \left(R + \frac{L}{2}\right)$$

where $\omega$ is the rotational rate and $R$ is the instantaneous turning radius.

## 2.5 Static Stability Margin (SSM)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Gold Formula** — guaranteed 3 to 7 marks in the exam

The **static stability margin** is the shortest horizontal distance from the vertical projection of the robot's Center of Gravity (CoG) to the boundary of the support polygon (set of feet currently on the ground). Mathematically, for a 2D planar analysis:

$$SSM = \min_{i \in \text{edges}} \left[ \frac{(P_i - G) \times \hat{n}_i}{\vert \hat{n}_i \vert} \right]$$

where:
- $G$ is the projected CoG,
- $P_i$ is a vertex of the support polygon,
- $\hat{n}_i$ is the outward normal to edge $i$,
- "$\times$" denotes the 2D cross product.

For a stable stance: $SSM > 0$.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **All the equations you must memorize for Module 4**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $S = 2s$ | Stride length = 2 × step length | m |
| 2 | $v = \dfrac{S}{T}$ | Forward walking velocity = stride / period | m/s |
| 3 | $\omega = \dfrac{v_{\text{right}} - v_{\text{left}}}{L}$ | Angular velocity (diff. drive) | rad/s |
| 4 | $R = \dfrac{L}{2} \cdot \dfrac{v_{\text{right}} + v_{\text{left}}}{v_{\text{right}} - v_{\text{left}}}$ | Turning radius (diff. drive) | m |
| 5 | $d = \dfrac{2 \pi r}{N}$ | Linear distance per encoder tick | m/tick |
| 6 | $f = \dfrac{1}{T}$ | Gait frequency | Hz |
| 7 | $\beta = \dfrac{\text{Number of feet on ground}}{\text{Total feet}}$ | Duty factor (gait) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $SSM > 0$ | Static stability condition | m |
| 9 | $\theta_{\text{critical}} = \arctan\left(\dfrac{a}{g}\right)$ | Maximum tilt before tipping | rad |
| 10 | $\text{FoS} = \dfrac{\text{Moment of stability}}{\text{Tipping moment}}$ | Factor of safety (against tipping) | dimensionless |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Search & Rescue Robots**: Legged (quadruped) — climb rubble.
- **Warehouse AMRs**: Wheeled (differential) — fast on flat floors.
- **Planetary Rovers**: Wheeled / rocker-bogie — NASA Mars rovers.
- **Military EOD Robots**: Tracked (flippers) — climb stairs.
- **Humanoids (ASIMO, Atlas)**: Bipedal — designed for human environments.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stability Margin for a Quadruped Robot

**Problem Statement:** A quadruped robot has four feet forming a rectangle of length $L = 1.0$ m (front-back) and width $W = 0.6$ m (left-right). The robot's Center of Gravity is located at height $H = 0.4$ m above the ground, at horizontal offset $(x, y) = (0.1, 0)$ m from the geometric centre. Determine the static stability margin when the robot is standing on all four feet.

### Step 1 — Set up the coordinate system

Place the origin at the geometric centre of the foot polygon. The CoG is at $(x_G, y_G) = (0.1, 0)$ m.

The four foot corners are:
- Front-Left (FL): $(+L/2, +W/2) = (+0.5, +0.3)$
- Front-Right (FR): $(+0.5, -0.3)$
- Rear-Left (RL): $(-0.5, +0.3)$
- Rear-Right (RR): $(-0.5, -0.3)$

### Step 2 — Identify the support polygon

The support polygon is the convex hull of the four feet — a rectangle.

### Step 3 — Project CoG onto the ground

The vertical projection of CoG onto the ground plane is simply $(x_G, y_G) = (0.1, 0)$. This point is **inside** the rectangle (since $\vert 0.1 \vert < 0.5$ and $\vert 0 \vert < 0.3$), so the robot is statically stable.

### Step 4 — Compute the minimum distance to the polygon edges

The four edges are at $x = +0.5$, $x = -0.5$, $y = +0.3$, $y = -0.3$.

The distances from $(0.1, 0)$ to each edge are:

$$d_{\text{front}} = +0.5 - 0.1 = 0.4 \text{ m}$$

$$d_{\text{rear}} = 0.1 - (-0.5) = 0.6 \text{ m}$$

$$d_{\text{left}} = 0 - (-0.3) = 0.3 \text{ m}$$

$$d_{\text{right}} = +0.3 - 0 = 0.3 \text{ m}$$

### Step 5 — Apply the SSM definition

$$SSM = \min(0.4, 0.6, 0.3, 0.3) = 0.3 \text{ m}$$

### Step 6 — Tipping threshold (forward tilt angle)

The robot will tip forward when the CoG projection reaches the front edge:

$$\theta_{\text{critical}} = \arctan\left(\frac{d_{\text{front}}}{H}\right) = \arctan\left(\frac{0.4}{0.4}\right) = \arctan(1) = 45^\circ$$

### Conclusion

$$\boxed{SSM = 0.3 \text{ m}, \quad \theta_{\text{critical,front}} = 45^\circ}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** A stability margin of 0.3 m is generous — the robot can lean forward up to 45° before tipping. Reducing $H$ (lowering the body) drastically improves stability.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Differential-Drive Inverse Kinematics

**Problem Statement:** A two-wheeled differential-drive robot has wheel-base $L = 0.4$ m and wheel radius $r = 0.075$ m. The robot must follow an arc of radius $R = 1.0$ m at forward velocity $v = 0.5$ m/s. Compute the required individual wheel angular velocities $\omega_{\text{left}}$ and $\omega_{\text{right}}$.

### Step 1 — Relate forward velocity to wheel angular velocity

For a single wheel rolling without slipping, the linear velocity at the contact point is:

$$v = r \cdot \omega_{\text{wheel}}$$

### Step 2 — Differential-drive forward kinematics

For a robot following a circular arc of radius $R$ from the Instantaneous Center of Curvature (ICC):

$$v_{\text{left}} = \omega \cdot \left(R - \frac{L}{2}\right)$$

$$v_{\text{right}} = \omega \cdot \left(R + \frac{L}{2}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Solve for the robot angular velocity $\omega$

The robot's forward speed is the average of the two wheel speeds:

$$v = \frac{v_{\text{left}} + v_{\text{right}}}{2}$$

Substituting:

$$v = \frac{\omega \left(R - \tfrac{L}{2}\right) + \omega \left(R + \tfrac{L}{2}\right)}{2}$$

$$v = \frac{\omega \cdot 2R}{2} = \omega \cdot R$$

Therefore:

$$\omega = \frac{v}{R} = \frac{0.5}{1.0} = 0.5 \text{ rad/s}$$

### Step 4 — Compute individual wheel speeds

$$v_{\text{left}} = 0.5 \cdot \left(1.0 - \frac{0.4}{2}\right) = 0.5 \cdot 0.8 = 0.4 \text{ m/s}$$

$$v_{\text{right}} = 0.5 \cdot \left(1.0 + \frac{0.4}{2}\right) = 0.5 \cdot 1.2 = 0.6 \text{ m/s}$$

### Step 5 — Convert linear wheel speed to angular wheel velocity

$$\omega_{\text{left}} = \frac{v_{\text{left}}}{r} = \frac{0.4}{0.075} = 5.333 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\omega_{\text{right}} = \frac{v_{\text{right}}}{r} = \frac{0.6}{0.075} = 8.000 \text{ rad/s}$$

### Conclusion

$$\boxed{\omega_{\text{left}} = 5.33 \text{ rad/s}, \quad \omega_{\text{right}} = 8.00 \text{ rad/s}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** $\dfrac{v_{\text{right}} - v_{\text{left}}}{L} = \dfrac{0.6 - 0.4}{0.4} = 0.5$ rad/s $\checkmark$ (matches $\omega$)

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Duty Factor and Gait Classification

The **duty factor** $\beta$ of a gait is defined as the fraction of the cycle time each foot spends on the ground:

$$\beta = \frac{t_{\text{stance}}}{T_{\text{cycle}}}$$

where $t_{\text{stance}}$ is the duration the foot is in contact with the ground during one cycle of duration $T_{\text{cycle}}$.

For a **symmetric gait** on a robot with $n$ legs:

$$\text{Number of feet on ground (avg)} = n \cdot \beta$$

**Gait classification by duty factor:**

| Gait Type | Duty Factor | Static Stability |
|---|---|---|
| Crawl / Wave | $\beta \geq 0.75$ | **Static (always)** |
| Slow walk | $0.5 \leq \beta < 0.75$ | Static with $n \geq 6$ |
| Walk | $\beta = 0.5$ | Dynamic |
| Trot (2-beat) | $\beta = 0.5$, diagonal pairs | Dynamic |
| Pace (2-beat) | $\beta = 0.5$, lateral pairs | Dynamic |
| Bound / Gallop | $\beta < 0.5$ | Dynamic |

**Example:** A hexapod walking with a **tripod gait** lifts two legs at a time, so each foot is on the ground for 1/2 of the cycle:

$$\beta = \frac{t_{\text{stance}}}{T_{\text{cycle}}} = 0.5$$

The other three feet (the tripod) are simultaneously on the ground, so the robot is statically stable throughout.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Wheel Configurations

| Type | DOF | Turning Mechanism | Stability | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Differential drive** | 3 (x, y, $\theta$) | Vary wheel speeds | Stable if CoG low | Indoor AMRs |
| **Car-like (Ackermann)** | 3 | Steer front wheels | Stable | Outdoor vehicles |
| **Synchronous drive** | 3 (x, y, $\theta$) | All wheels rotate to body | Stable | Warehouse robots |
| **Omnidirectional** | 3 | Mecanum / Swedish wheels | Stable | Humanoid crowds |
| **Tracked** | 3 | Skid-steer | **Very** stable | Rough terrain |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Locomotion Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[High-Level Mission Planner] --> B[Gait / Path Generator]
    B --> C[Inverse Kinematics Solver]
    C --> D[Joint Trajectory Planner]
    D --> E[Motor Controllers per Joint]
    E --> F[Mechanical Actuators Wheels Legs Tracks]
    F --> G[Robot Body and Support Polygon]
    G --> H[Sensor Feedback IMU Force Encoders]
    H --> B
    H --> E
    styleA([Task: Walk to Point X])
    styleB([Step Sequence])
    styleC([Joint Angles])
    styleD([Position Velocity])
    styleE([PWM Torque Signals])
    styleF([Physical Motion])
    styleG([Ground Reaction Forces])
    styleH([Pose and Contact Info])
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the loop:** The planner generates a gait, the kinematics solver converts foot placements to joint angles, motors execute them, the body moves and contacts the ground, and sensors feed back the actual pose — closing the loop on every cycle. This is the essence of a **Zero Moment Point (ZMP)**-based controller used in humanoid robots.

---

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Support Polygon and CoG

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Robot Body] --> B[Center of Gravity CoG]
    B --> C[Vertical Projection onto Ground]
    C --> D{Is CoG inside Support Polygon?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Statically STABLE]
    D -- No --> F[Statically UNSTABLE]
    E --> G[Continue Walk Cycle]
    F --> H[Trigger Recovery Step or Fall]
    styleA([Mass m, height H])
    styleB([Point G_x G_y G_z])
    styleC([Point P_x P_y 0])
    styleD([Geometric Test])
    styleE([Margin: d_min])
    styleF([Margin: d_min negative])
    styleG([Maintain Gait])
    styleH([Emergency Reflex])
```

---

## 4.3 Legged Locomotion Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Locomotion Selection] --> B{Terrain Type?}
    B -- Flat Hard --> C[Wheeled Platform]
    B -- Soft Loose --> D[Tracked Platform]
    B -- Rough Uneven --> E{Gait Speed Needed?}
    E -- Slow Stable --> F[Hexapod Quadruped]
    E -- Fast Dynamic --> G[Bipedal Humanoid]
    B -- Stairs Rubble --> H[Quadruped with Active Foot]
    styleA([Design Question])
    styleB([Flat vs Rough])
    styleC([4 or 6 wheels])
    styleD([Belt tracks])
    styleE([Speed vs Stability])
    styleF([Static gait, 6 legs])
    styleG([Dynamic ZMP gait])
    styleH([Boston Dynamics Spot class])
```

---

## 4.4 Wheel Configuration Schematic (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid

        W1[Left Wheel Motor M1] --- B1[Chassis Body]
        B1 --- W2[Right Wheel Motor M2]

```

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Most common mistake:** Students confuse *static* stability margin with *dynamic* stability. In **static** walking, the CoG projection must always lie inside the support polygon — in **dynamic** walking (bipeds), it can leave the polygon briefly.
> 2. **Marks lost on formula units:** When asked "compute the stability margin", students often forget to give the **unit (metres)**. KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks for missing units.
> 3. **Wheel kinematics confusion:** The turning radius $R$ of a differential drive is **NOT** half the wheel-base — that is only true when one wheel is stationary ($v_{\text{inner}} = 0$). For unequal wheel speeds, use the full derived formula.
> 4. **Gait terminology:** "Duty factor" is sometimes called "support fraction" in older textbooks. Do not be confused if both terms appear.

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Define *locomotion* in the context of robotics. Differentiate between *static* and *dynamic* stability with one example robot for each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** Locomotion is the mechanism by which a robot propels its body from one location to another using wheels, legs, tracks or other effectors. It involves the coordinated actuation of limbs/appendages to translate the robot's center of mass.
- **Static stability (1 mark):** Stability maintained *at every instant* of motion — the CoG projection always lies inside the support polygon. **Example:** Hexapod robot in tripod gait (e.g., Genghis by MIT).
- **Dynamic stability (1 mark):** Stability maintained *only on average* — the robot may tip momentarily but recovers via inertia/active control. **Example:** Honda ASIMO (bipedal humanoid).

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
What is a *support polygon*? How many support polygons are required to define a *crawl gait* in a quadruped robot?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Support polygon definition (1 mark):** The support polygon is the convex hull of all points on the ground that are in contact with the robot's feet (or wheels). Static stability requires the vertical projection of the CoG to fall inside this polygon.
- **Crawl gait definition (2 marks):** A crawl gait is a slow, statically-stable gait where only **one leg is lifted at a time**. For a quadruped, this means **3 legs are always on the ground**, so the support polygon is formed by 3 feet. The robot cycles through 4 different support polygons during one complete cycle (one for each leg being lifted in turn).

---

## Part B Question — Internal Choice

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each is for 14 marks split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### (a) Explain the various types of locomotion mechanisms used in mobile robots. Compare legged locomotion with wheeled locomotion in terms of stability, energy efficiency and terrain adaptability. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Types of Locomotion Mechanisms (4 Marks):**

The major types are:

(i) **Wheeled locomotion** — uses 2, 3, 4 or more wheels. Efficient on flat, hard surfaces. Common in indoor AMRs and AGVs.

(ii) **Legged locomotion** — uses 2, 4, 6 or 8 legs. Provides mobility over rough, uneven terrain by selecting discrete foot placements. Walking robots mimic animal locomotion.

(iii) **Tracked locomotion** — uses continuous tracks (like a tank). High ground contact area → excellent traction on soft, loose terrain.

(iv) **Hybrid locomotion** — combines wheels and legs (e.g., NASA's ATHLETE robot, which has wheels at the end of legs).

(v) **Other specialised locomotion** — aerial (quadrotors), aquatic (underwater fins), snake-like (serpentine), climbing (with suction / magnetic grippers).

**2. Comparison Table (3 Marks):**

| Criterion | Wheeled | Legged |
|---|---|---|
| **Stability** | High (static, 3+ wheels) | Medium to low (gait dependent) |
| **Energy efficiency** | High (>80%) | Low (10–30%) |
| **Terrain adaptability** | Low (flat surfaces) | High (climbs obstacles) |
| **Mechanical complexity** | Low | High |
| **Speed** | High | Low to moderate |
| **Control complexity** | Low | Very high |

#### (b) A hexapod robot uses a tripod gait. Each leg has a step length of 0.15 m and the gait period is 2 seconds. Compute (i) the stride length, (ii) the forward velocity, (iii) the duty factor, and (iv) the average number of feet on the ground. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Stride Length (1 Mark):**

$$S = 2 \times s = 2 \times 0.15 = 0.30 \text{ m}$$

**(ii) Forward Velocity (2 Marks):**

$$v = \frac{S}{T} = \frac{0.30}{2} = 0.15 \text{ m/s}$$

**(iii) Duty Factor (2 Marks):**

In a tripod gait, each foot is in stance for half the cycle (the other tripod is swinging):

$$\beta = \frac{t_{\text{stance}}}{T_{\text{cycle}}} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5$$

**(iv) Average Feet on Ground (2 Marks):**

$$\text{Avg feet on ground} = n \cdot \beta = 6 \times 0.5 = 3 \text{ feet}$$

**Conclusion:** The hexapod has 3 feet on the ground at all times, confirming static stability of the tripod gait.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

#### (a) With neat block diagrams, explain the operation of a differential-drive mobile robot. Derive the forward and inverse kinematics relating wheel velocities to robot's linear and angular velocities. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Block Diagram Description (2 Marks):**

A differential-drive robot has two independently-driven wheels mounted on a common axis separated by wheel-base $L$, plus one or more passive castor wheels for balance. Each drive wheel has its own DC motor and encoder. A microcontroller computes desired wheel speeds from a higher-level goal (e.g., "turn left at 0.5 rad/s"). PWM signals drive the motors; wheel encoders close the velocity loop.

**2. Forward Kinematics (3 Marks):**

Given wheel angular velocities $\omega_L$ and $\omega_R$ and wheel radius $r$:

$$v_L = r \cdot \omega_L, \qquad v_R = r \cdot \omega_R$$

Robot's linear velocity:

$$v = \frac{v_L + v_R}{2} = \frac{r(\omega_L + \omega_R)}{2}$$

Robot's angular velocity:

$$\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}$$

**3. Inverse Kinematics (2 Marks):**

Given desired $(v, \omega)$:

$$\omega_L = \frac{1}{r}\left(v - \frac{\omega L}{2}\right)$$

$$\omega_R = \frac{1}{r}\left(v + \frac{\omega L}{2}\right)$$

---

#### (b) A differential-drive robot has wheel-base $L = 0.5$ m, wheel radius $r = 0.1$ m. The robot must move forward at $v = 0.3$ m/s while turning with angular velocity $\omega = 0.4$ rad/s. Determine (i) the linear speed of each wheel, (ii) the wheel angular velocities in rad/s, and (iii) the radius of the instantaneous circular path traced by the robot's centre. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Linear wheel speeds (2 Marks):**

$$v_L = v - \frac{\omega L}{2} = 0.3 - \frac{0.4 \times 0.5}{2} = 0.3 - 0.1 = 0.2 \text{ m/s}$$

$$v_R = v + \frac{\omega L}{2} = 0.3 + 0.1 = 0.4 \text{ m/s}$$

**(ii) Wheel angular velocities (2 Marks):**

$$\omega_L = \frac{v_L}{r} = \frac{0.2}{0.1} = 2.0 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\omega_R = \frac{v_R}{r} = \frac{0.4}{0.1} = 4.0 \text{ rad/s}$$

**(iii) Instantaneous turning radius (3 Marks):**

From the kinematic relation $v = \omega \cdot R$:

$$R = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{0.3}{0.4} = 0.75 \text{ m}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Cross-check** using the alternative formula:
> $$R = \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{v_R + v_L}{v_R - v_L} = \frac{0.5}{2} \cdot \frac{0.4 + 0.2}{0.4 - 0.2} = 0.25 \cdot \frac{0.6}{0.2} = 0.25 \cdot 3 = 0.75 \text{ m} \checkmark$$

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 4: Locomotion**

- ⭐ **Locomotion ≠ Manipulation.** Locomotion moves the robot's body; manipulation moves external objects.
- ⭐ **3 legs = always statically stable** (tripod principle). 2 legs require dynamic balance.
- ⭐ **Hexapods use tripod gaits** — 3 feet on ground, 3 in air, alternating.
- ⭐ **Quadrupeds** can use crawl, walk, trot, pace, or gallop gaits; static only in crawl.
- ⭐ **Bipeds (humans, ASIMO, Atlas)** require *dynamic* stability — Zero Moment Point (ZMP) control.
- ⭐ **Duty factor $\beta$** = fraction of cycle a foot is on ground. $\beta = 0.5$ is the boundary between static ($\beta > 0.5$) and dynamic ($\beta \leq 0.5$) gaits.
- ⭐ **Differential drive** has 3 DOF (x, y, $\theta$) — non-holonomic constraint means it cannot move sideways directly.
- ⭐ **Wheel kinematics formulas** to memorize: $v = \frac{r(\omega_L+\omega_R)}{2}$, $\omega = \frac{r(\omega_R-\omega_L)}{L}$.
- ⭐ **Tracked robots** are best for soft terrain and stairs (high traction); wheeled for flat.
- ⭐ **Mecanum / Swedish wheels** give omnidirectional motion — used in crowded environments.
- ⭐ **Static stability margin (SSM)** = shortest horizontal distance from CoG projection to support polygon boundary. $SSM > 0 \Rightarrow$ stable.
- ⭐ **Critical tilt angle** $\theta_c = \arctan(d/H)$ where $d$ is the SSM and $H$ is the CoG height.
- ⭐ **For the exam:** always state the **support polygon** explicitly before computing SSM.
- ⭐ **Energy efficiency** order (highest to lowest): Wheeled → Tracked → Legged.
- ⭐ **Snake robots** use serpenoid curves; their motion is described by Hirose's equation of serpentine locomotion.

---

> **End of Module 4 — Locomotion Notes** | *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 Aligned) Examination Standards*

topics.title: Key Issues for Locomotion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT302 - INTRODUCTION TO ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Key Issues for Locomotion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

**Locomotion** in robotics refers to the mechanism by which an autonomous or semi-autonomous robot generates motion across its environment through interaction with the underlying medium (ground, water, air, or combinations thereof). Unlike manipulation, locomotion is concerned with the **propulsion**, **support**, and **sustained movement** of the entire robot body, rather than the positioning of an end-effector in space.

**Key Issues for Locomotion** constitute the fundamental design trade-offs that govern the selection, configuration, and control of any mobile robot's movement system. These issues are evaluated along the dimensions of **stability, terrain adaptability, energy efficiency, mechanical complexity, speed, and controllability**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme — Module 4):**
> The board examiner expects students to identify and rank the **seven canonical key issues** introduced in the classic locomotion taxonomy:
> 1. **Stability** (number & geometry of contact points)
> 2. **Number of legs / contact points**
> 3. **Leg/contact arrangement (configuration)**
> 4. **Center of gravity location**
> 5. **Static vs Dynamic balance**
> 6. **Energy efficiency (specific resistance)**
> 7. **Mechanical complexity & controllability**

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Daily Commute" Perspective

Imagine you are choosing how to commute across Kerala from **Thiruvananthapuram to Kasaragod** (≈ 500 km).

| Commute Mode | Robotic Equivalent | Key Trade-Off |
|---|---|---|
| **Walking (your own two legs)** | Bipedal robot | Highly flexible, but you must constantly balance — fall risk is real |
| **Cycling** | Two-wheeled robot | Energy-efficient, fast on smooth roads, but tips over when stopped |
| **Three-wheeler (auto-rickshaw)** | Three-legged/tripod robot | Always stable when stationary, cheap, but limited terrain |
| **Car (four wheels)** | 4-wheeled / quadruped | Stable, fast, but cannot climb stairs or rough terrain |
| **Six-wheeled truck (BEML Tatra)** | Hexapod / 6×6 vehicle | Maximum stability, climbs rubble — but expensive & complex |

> [!NOTE]
> **The Universal Locomotion Trade-Off Principle:**
> As the number of legs/contact points **increases**, **stability improves** and **terrain adaptability increases**, but **mechanical complexity, weight, and control difficulty also rise sharply**. As the number **decreases**, **speed and mechanical simplicity improve**, but **active balance control becomes mandatory**.

---

## 1.3 Why These Issues Matter — The Engineering Motivation

A robot designer must answer the following fundamental questions before committing to a locomotion mechanism:

1. **Will the robot tip over?** → *Stability analysis*
2. **How many actuators will I need?** → *Mechanical complexity / DOF count*
3. **How long will the battery last?** → *Energy efficiency (specific resistance)*
4. **Can it climb a staircase / rubble pile?** → *Terrain adaptability*
5. **How fast can it move?** → *Froude-number–governed speed limits*

Every issue is interconnected — improving one typically degrades another. This forms the **multi-objective optimization core** of every mobile robot design project.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven Key Issues — Structured Analytical Breakdown

### Issue 1 — Stability (Static vs Dynamic)

- **Static Stability:** The robot remains stable even when **all motion ceases**. Achieved when the **vertical projection of the Center of Gravity (CoG)** lies strictly **inside** the convex hull formed by the ground-contact points (the **support polygon**).
- **Dynamic Stability:** Stability is maintained **only while the robot is in motion** (running, hopping). A hopping 1-legged robot is dynamically stable but statically unstable.

### Issue 2 — Number of Contact Points (Legs)

The number of ground-contact points governs both stability margin and gait complexity:

$$\boxed{N_{\text{support}} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{(Hopping — dynamic only)} \\ 2 & \text{(Biped — active balance)} \\ 3 & \text{(Tripod — always statically stable)} \\ 4 & \text{(Quadruped — 3-leg support phase)} \\ 6 & \text{(Hexapod — multiple stable gaits)} \end{cases}}$$

### Issue 3 — Leg/Contact Arrangement

The **geometric arrangement** of legs (insect-like splayed, mammal-like parasagittal, or wheeled hybrid) determines the support polygon shape and reachable workspace.

### Issue 4 — Center of Gravity Location

A **lower CoG** dramatically improves stability because the gravitational moment arm that can tip the robot is reduced. Most quadruped and hexapod designs lower the main body close to the ground.

### Issue 5 — Static vs Dynamic Balance

- **Static walking:** At all times, at least **three legs** are on the ground forming a stable triangle (used by slow hexapods).
- **Dynamic walking:** Only **one or two legs** are on the ground at any instant; the robot is in continuous free-fall between contacts (used by humans and bipeds).

### Issue 6 — Energy Efficiency (Specific Resistance)

The **Specific Resistance** $\varepsilon$ is a dimensionless measure of locomotion energy cost. It allows fair comparison between robots of different sizes:

$$\boxed{\varepsilon = \frac{P}{M \cdot g \cdot v}}$$

where $P$ = power consumed (W), $M$ = robot mass (kg), $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$, $v$ = forward velocity (m/s).

> Lower $\varepsilon$ ⇒ more efficient locomotion. Animals and well-designed wheeled robots cluster around $\varepsilon \approx 0.1$ to $0.3$.

### Issue 7 — Mechanical Complexity & Controllability

Each leg typically requires **2–3 powered joints** (hip abduction/adduction, hip flexion/extension, knee). A hexapod therefore needs **12–18 actuators** — a major cost, weight, and control overhead.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — at least 2 of these formulas appear in every KTU ESE Module-4 question.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol | Physical Meaning / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Specific Resistance | $\varepsilon = P / (M g v)$ | Dimensionless energy cost of locomotion |
| 2 | Static Stability Margin | $S_{m} = \min_i d_i$ | Shortest distance from CoG projection to support-polygon edge (m) |
| 3 | Froude Number | $Fr = v^2 / (g L)$ | Ratio of centrifugal to gravitational acceleration |
| 4 | Stride Length | $\lambda = 2 \cdot r$ | Total distance covered in one full leg cycle |
| 5 | Step Length | $l_{\text{step}} = r / 2$ | Half-cycle advance per leg |
| 6 | Stride Frequency | $f = v / \lambda$ | Cycles per second (Hz) |
| 7 | Degrees of Freedom (leg) | $DOF_{\text{leg}}$ | Typically 2 or 3 per leg |
| 8 | Total Actuators (n legs) | $N_{\text{act}} = n \cdot DOF_{\text{leg}}$ | Total powered joints |
| 9 | Support Polygon | Convex hull of contact points | Geometric region for stable CoG projection |
| 10 | Dynamic Similarity | $Fr_{\text{robot}} = Fr_{\text{animal}}$ | Used for scaling animal locomotion data to robots |

> Note on table syntax: absolute values in formulas (e.g., $\vert v \vert$, $\vert S_m \vert$) are typeset using `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown pipe syntax.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These locomotion trade-offs directly drive the design of:

- **Search & Rescue Robots** (e.g., post-earthquake rubble climbers) — favour 6 legs for terrain.
- **Planetary Rovers** (NASA's Curiosity, Perseverance) — rocker-bogie 6-wheel suspension.
- **Humanoid Robots** (ASIMO, Atlas) — bipedal for human-environment compatibility.
- **Warehouse AMRs** (Amazon Kiva) — differential-drive wheels for efficiency on flat floors.
- **Military Drones & Quadrupeds** (Boston Dynamics Spot, Ghost Robotics Vision 60) — quadruped for stairs + payload.

In every case, the designer has *quantitatively weighed* the seven key issues above before committing to a hardware architecture.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stability Margin of a Quadruped in 3-Leg Support

**Problem Setup:** A four-legged robot rests on legs at coordinates $P_1, P_2, P_3$ forming a triangular support polygon. Its CoG projection is at point $G$. Derive the **stability margin** (shortest distance from $G$ to any edge of the support triangle).

**Step 1 — Identify the support polygon vertices.**

Given three ground-contact points:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_1 &= (x_1,\, y_1) \\
P_2 &= (x_2,\, y_2) \\
P_3 &= (x_3,\, y_3)
\end{aligned}
$$

The support polygon is the triangle $\triangle P_1P_2P_3$.

**Step 2 — Compute the three edge-line equations.**

For edge $P_1P_2$, the implicit line is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Edge}_{12}:\quad & a_{12}\,x + b_{12}\,y + c_{12} = 0, \\
\text{where}\quad a_{12} &= y_1 - y_2, \\
b_{12} &= x_2 - x_1, \\
c_{12} &= x_1 y_2 - x_2 y_1.
\end{aligned}
$$

Similarly for edges $P_2P_3$ and $P_3P_1$.

**Step 3 — Compute perpendicular distance from CoG to each edge.**

The perpendicular distance from point $G = (x_G, y_G)$ to edge $i$ is:

$$
d_i = \frac{\vert a_i\,x_G + b_i\,y_G + c_i \vert}{\sqrt{a_i^2 + b_i^2}}
$$

**Step 4 — Stability margin is the minimum of all distances.**

$$
\boxed{S_m = \min\{d_1,\, d_2,\, d_3\}}
$$

**Step 5 — Stability criterion.**

The robot is **statically stable** if and only if $G$ lies *inside* the triangle, which is equivalent to:

$$
S_m > 0 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \text{CoG projection inside support polygon}
$$

> If $S_m = 0$, the robot is on the verge of tipping — this is a common KTU board trap (zero margin ⇒ unstable equilibrium).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Froude Number from Dimensional Analysis

**Goal:** Derive a dimensionless number that characterizes whether a legged robot should walk or run, based on forward speed $v$, leg length $L$, and gravity $g$.

**Step 1 — List relevant physical quantities.**

$$
[v] = L\,T^{-1},\quad [L] = L,\quad [g] = L\,T^{-2}
$$

We seek a dimensionless group $\Pi$ of the form $v^a \cdot L^b \cdot g^c$.

**Step 2 — Set up dimensional homogeneity.**

$$
[v^a L^b g^c] = (LT^{-1})^a \cdot (L)^b \cdot (LT^{-2})^c = L^{a+b+c}\, T^{-a-2c}
$$

For $\Pi$ to be dimensionless:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a + b + c &= 0 \quad (\text{exponent of } L) \\
-a - 2c &= 0 \quad (\text{exponent of } T)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve the linear system.**

From the second equation: $a = -2c$.
Substitute into the first: $-2c + b + c = 0 \Rightarrow b = c$.

Choosing $c = 1$ for simplicity:

$$
a = -2,\quad b = 1,\quad c = 1
$$

**Step 4 — Construct the dimensionless group.**

$$
\Pi = v^{-2}\, L^{1}\, g^{1} = \frac{L\, g}{v^2}
$$

The conventional (inverse) form is the **Froude Number**:

$$
\boxed{Fr = \frac{v^2}{g\,L}}
$$

**Step 5 — Physical interpretation.**

- $Fr < 0.5$ → **Walking gait** dominates (gravity restoring force is dominant).
- $0.5 < Fr < 1.0$ → **Transition to running**.
- $Fr > 1.0$ → **Running/aerial phase** required — centrifugal effects dominate.

This is precisely why **larger animals (longer $L$) must run, not walk, at high speeds**, and why humanoid robots switch from walking to running gaits near $Fr \approx 0.5$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Specific Resistance and Walking Speed Optimization

**Goal:** Show that for a given leg geometry and stride frequency, there exists an optimum velocity that minimizes specific resistance $\varepsilon$.

**Step 1 — Define stride parameters.**

$$
\lambda = 2r \quad \text{(stride length = 2 × stroke length)}
$$

$$
v = f \cdot \lambda = f \cdot 2r \quad \Rightarrow \quad f = \frac{v}{2r}
$$

**Step 2 — Model actuator power consumption.**

A simplified lumped model treats each leg's actuator as having a power draw proportional to the cycling frequency plus a velocity-squared term (acceleration losses):

$$
P = n \left( k_1\, f + k_2\, f^2 + k_3\, M g\, v \cdot C_{rr} \right)
$$

where $C_{rr}$ is an effective "rolling resistance" coefficient for the gait.

**Step 3 — Substitute into the Specific Resistance formula.**

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{P}{M g v} = \frac{n}{M g v}\left( k_1 f + k_2 f^2 + k_3 M g v C_{rr} \right)
$$

Using $f = v/(2r)$:

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{n}{M g v}\left( k_1 \frac{v}{2r} + k_2 \frac{v^2}{4r^2} + k_3 M g v C_{rr} \right)
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify.**

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{n}{M g}\left( \frac{k_1}{2r} + \frac{k_2\, v}{4 r^2} \right) + n\, k_3\, C_{rr}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result.**

The first term is constant (independent of $v$), and the second term grows linearly with $v$. Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\varepsilon(v) = A + B\, v}
$$

where $A = \dfrac{n\, k_1}{2 M g\, r}$ and $B = \dfrac{n\, k_2}{4 M g\, r^2}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight:** Specific resistance is **monotonically increasing** with velocity in this simplified model. The optimum is therefore at the **lowest practical speed**, consistent with biological observation that animals (and robots) that need energy efficiency prefer slow walking over fast locomotion.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing Locomotion Type

| Issue | 2 Wheels | 4 Wheels | 3 Legs | 4 Legs | 6 Legs | Biped |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Static Stability | ✗ | ✓ (at rest) | ✓ | ✓ (3-leg phase) | ✓✓ | ✗ |
| Dynamic Stability | ✓ (moving) | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓✓ | ✓ |
| Terrain Adaptability | Low | Low–Med | Medium | High | Very High | Medium |
| Energy Efficiency ($\varepsilon$) | Excellent (0.1) | Excellent | Good | Medium | Medium–Poor | Poor |
| Mechanical Complexity | Low | Low–Med | Medium | High | Very High | Very High |
| Speed Potential | High | High | Medium | Medium | Low–Med | Medium |
| Control Difficulty | Low | Low | Medium | High | Very High | Very High |
| Typical Application | Indoor AMRs | Cars | Research | Boston Dynamics Spot | Rescue bots | Humanoids |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU questions often ask *"Compare legged vs wheeled locomotion."* Always structure your answer along the rows of this matrix, citing **at least 4 quantitative metrics** (stability margin, $\varepsilon$, $Fr$, DOF count).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Locomotion Design Decision Flow

The following Mermaid block depicts the **decision-cascade** a roboticist follows when choosing a locomotion mechanism, mapping each decision to the corresponding key issue.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Locomotion Design] --> B{Environment Type?}
    B -->|Smooth / Indoor| C[Favour Wheels]
    B -->|Rough / Stairs| D[Favour Legs]
    B -->|Underwater| E[Favour Thrusters]
    B -->|Aerial| F[Favour Rotors]

    C --> G{Stability Critical?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Use 4 Wheels]
    G -->|No| I[Use 2 Wheels / Differential Drive]

    D --> J{Static or Dynamic Balance?}
    J -->|Static| K{Load Capacity?}
    J -->|Dynamic| L[Use 2 Legs / Biped]

    K -->|Light| M[Use 3 Legs Tripod]
    K -->|Medium| N[Use 4 Legs Quadruped]
    K -->|Heavy / Rescue| O[Use 6 Legs Hexapod]

    H --> P[Compute Froude Number]
    L --> P
    M --> P
    N --> P
    O --> P

    P --> Q{Fr less than 0.5?}
    Q -->|Yes| R[Adopt Walking Gait]
    Q -->|No| S[Adopt Running Gait]

    R --> T[Validate Specific Resistance]
    S --> T
    T --> U{Battery Budget OK?}
    U -->|Yes| V[Finalize Design]
    U -->|No| W[Reduce DOF or Use Wheels]
    W --> V
```

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Locomotion Subsystem Architecture

This block diagram shows the **internal structure of a generic legged-locomotion controller** and how the seven key issues map onto functional modules.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SENS[Perception Layer]
        S1[IMU / Gyroscope]
        S2[Force Sensors on Feet]
        S3[Leg Encoders]
    end

    subgraph CTRL[Control Layer]
        C1[Stability Controller - CoG Projection]
        C2[Gait Planner - Static or Dynamic]
        C3[Froude Speed Governor]
        C4[Energy Optimizer - Specific Resistance]
    end

    subgraph ACT[Actuation Layer]
        A1[Hip Joint Actuator]
        A2[Knee Joint Actuator]
        A3[Ankle Joint Actuator]
    end

    subgraph MECH[Mechanical Layer]
        M1[Leg Linkage 2 DOF or 3 DOF]
        M2[Support Polygon - Convex Hull]
        M3[Body Chassis - Low CoG]
    end

    S1 --> C1
    S2 --> C1
    S3 --> C2
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> C4
    C4 --> A1
    C4 --> A2
    C4 --> A3
    A1 --> M1
    A2 --> M1
    A3 --> M1
    M1 --> M2
    M2 --> M3
```

---

## 4.3 Concept Map — Relationships Between the Seven Key Issues

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    K1[Stability] <--> K2[Number of Contact Points]
    K2 <--> K3[Leg Arrangement]
    K1 --> K4[Center of Gravity Location]
    K4 --> K1
    K2 --> K5[Static vs Dynamic Balance]
    K5 --> K6[Energy Efficiency]
    K2 --> K7[Mechanical Complexity]
    K7 --> K6
    K3 --> K6
    K6 --> K5

    K1 -.guides.> K8[Specific Resistance]
    K5 -.governs.> K9[Froude Number]
    K9 -.controls.> K10[Stride Frequency]
```

---

## 4.4 ASCII Schematic — Support Polygon and CoG Projection (for exams)

```
            P2
           /  \
          /  G  \         G  = Center of Gravity projection
         /   •   \        P1, P2, P3 = Ground contact points
        /    |    \       Shaded triangle = Support polygon
       /     |d    \      d = Stability margin (perpendicular
      /      |      \         distance from G to nearest edge)
     /_______|_______\
    P1        P3
                ↑
                |
        Small d  →  UNSTABLE (tipping risk)
        Large d →  STABLE
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked to draw a support polygon in the exam, always label the **three contact points, the CoG projection, and the perpendicular distance to the nearest edge**. Examiners specifically award marks for the **dotted perpendicular line** representing $S_m$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]
**"List the seven key issues that must be considered when selecting a locomotion mechanism for a mobile robot."**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per correct category + 0.5 for clear listing):**

The seven key issues for locomotion as per the KTU 2024 syllabus are:

1. **Stability** — Static vs dynamic; determined by the support polygon and CoG projection.
2. **Number of legs (contact points)** — directly affects the achievable stability and gait complexity.
3. **Leg/contact arrangement** — geometric configuration (insect-style splayed vs mammal-style parasagittal).
4. **Center of gravity location** — a lower CoG increases the stability margin $S_m$.
5. **Static vs Dynamic balance** — choice between static walking (slow, 3+ legs down) and dynamic walking (fast, fewer contacts).
6. **Energy efficiency** — quantified by the **Specific Resistance** $\varepsilon = P / (M g v)$.
7. **Mechanical complexity and controllability** — DOF count, number of actuators, and control bandwidth required.

> **Marking scheme:** 7 items × ~0.4 mark each, with 0.2 mark for clarity of presentation = 3 marks.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**"Define the Froude Number and state its significance in legged locomotion."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Froude Number** is a dimensionless quantity that characterizes the ratio of **centrifugal/inertial forces to gravitational forces** during legged locomotion:

$$
Fr = \frac{v^2}{g \cdot L}
$$

where $v$ = forward velocity (m/s), $g$ = gravitational acceleration (9.81 m/s²), $L$ = leg length (m).

**Significance:**
- $Fr < 0.5$ → **Walking gait** is energetically preferred.
- $Fr > 0.5$ → **Running gait** becomes necessary; the aerial phase emerges.
- Used to **scale animal locomotion data** to robots of different sizes (dynamic similarity principle).
- Predicts the maximum walking speed of bipedal and quadrupedal robots.

> **Marking scheme:** 1.5 marks for correct formula + 1.5 marks for interpretation of threshold values.

---

## PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]

**(a) A six-legged (hexapod) robot has a body of mass 50 kg. During a tripod-gait walk, three legs are in ground contact at coordinates $(0,0)$, $(0.6, 0)$, and $(0.3, 0.5)$ m. The robot's Center of Gravity projects onto the ground at $(0.3, 0.18)$ m. Compute the stability margin. (7 marks)**

**(b) With neat diagrams, compare legged and wheeled locomotion along the seven key issues. (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Identify the three support polygon vertices (0.5 mark):**

$$
P_1 = (0,\, 0),\quad P_2 = (0.6,\, 0),\quad P_3 = (0.3,\, 0.5)
$$

**Step 2 — Compute line coefficients for edge $P_1P_2$ (1 mark):**

Edge from $(0,0)$ to $(0.6, 0)$ is the x-axis, i.e., $y = 0$.

In implicit form:

$$
a_{12} = 0,\quad b_{12} = 1,\quad c_{12} = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Perpendicular distance from $G = (0.3, 0.18)$ to edge $P_1P_2$ (1 mark):**

$$
d_{12} = \frac{\vert a_{12}\,x_G + b_{12}\,y_G + c_{12} \vert}{\sqrt{a_{12}^2 + b_{12}^2}} = \frac{\vert 0 + 0.18 + 0 \vert}{\sqrt{0+1}} = 0.18\ \text{m}
$$

**Step 4 — Line coefficients for edge $P_2P_3$ (1 mark):**

$$
a_{23} = y_2 - y_3 = 0 - 0.5 = -0.5
$$

$$
b_{23} = x_3 - x_2 = 0.3 - 0.6 = -0.3
$$

$$
c_{23} = x_2 y_3 - x_3 y_2 = (0.6)(0.5) - (0.3)(0) = 0.3
$$

**Step 5 — Perpendicular distance from $G$ to edge $P_2P_3$ (1 mark):**

$$
d_{23} = \frac{\vert (-0.5)(0.3) + (-0.3)(0.18) + 0.3 \vert}{\sqrt{0.25 + 0.09}} = \frac{\vert -0.15 - 0.054 + 0.3 \vert}{\sqrt{0.34}} = \frac{0.096}{0.5831} \approx 0.1646\ \text{m}
$$

**Step 6 — Line coefficients for edge $P_3P_1$ (1 mark):**

$$
a_{31} = y_3 - y_1 = 0.5 - 0 = 0.5
$$

$$
b_{31} = x_1 - x_3 = 0 - 0.3 = -0.3
$$

$$
c_{31} = x_3 y_1 - x_1 y_3 = 0
$$

**Step 7 — Perpendicular distance from $G$ to edge $P_3P_1$ (1 mark):**

$$
d_{31} = \frac{\vert (0.5)(0.3) + (-0.3)(0.18) + 0 \vert}{\sqrt{0.25 + 0.09}} = \frac{\vert 0.15 - 0.054 \vert}{0.5831} = \frac{0.096}{0.5831} \approx 0.1646\ \text{m}
$$

**Step 8 — Stability margin is the minimum (0.5 mark):**

$$
\boxed{S_m = \min\{0.18,\ 0.1646,\ 0.1646\} = 0.1646\ \text{m} \approx 16.5\ \text{cm}}
$$

Since $S_m > 0$, the robot is **statically stable**.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b) — 7 marks

| Issue (1 mark each) | Wheeled Locomotion | Legged Locomotion |
|---|---|---|
| **Stability** | Stable when stationary (≥3 wheels). | Varies: stable for 3, 4, 6 legs; unstable for 1, 2. |
| **Number of contact points** | 3 or 4 wheels typical. | 1 to 6+ legs typical. |
| **Leg/contact arrangement** | Fixed axle; differential drive for steering. | Configurable: splayed, parasagittal, articulated. |
| **Center of gravity** | Low CoG preferred (race cars vs trucks). | Very low CoG required for stability (esp. bipeds). |
| **Static vs Dynamic balance** | Static (most wheeled). | Both possible (walking = static, running = dynamic). |
| **Energy efficiency** | Excellent ($\varepsilon \approx 0.1$). | Poor to medium ($\varepsilon \approx 0.2$–$0.6$). |
| **Mechanical complexity** | Low — 2 motors + 2 wheels suffice. | High — 2–3 DOF per leg, 12+ actuators for hexapod. |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Wheels dominate smooth indoor environments; legs dominate rough terrain. Choice is dictated by the seven key issues above.

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks [Alternative Choice]

**(a) Define Specific Resistance. A quadruped robot of mass 30 kg walks at 0.5 m/s using 12 W of power. Compute its specific resistance and comment on its energy efficiency compared to a typical wheeled robot ($\varepsilon_{\text{wheel}} = 0.15$). (7 marks)**

**(b) Derive the Froude Number from dimensional analysis. A humanoid robot has leg length 0.8 m. Determine the speed above which it must switch from walking to running. (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Definition (2 marks):**

**Specific Resistance** $\varepsilon$ is a dimensionless quantity that measures the energy cost of locomotion, normalized by the robot's weight and forward velocity:

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{P}{M \cdot g \cdot v}
$$

where $P$ = power (W), $M$ = mass (kg), $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$, $v$ = velocity (m/s). Lower values indicate more efficient locomotion.

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values (2 marks):**

$$
P = 12\ \text{W},\quad M = 30\ \text{kg},\quad g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2,\quad v = 0.5\ \text{m/s}
$$

$$
\varepsilon_{\text{quad}} = \frac{12}{30 \times 9.81 \times 0.5} = \frac{12}{147.15} \approx 0.0816
$$

**Step 3 — Compare (2 marks):**

$$
\varepsilon_{\text{quad}} = 0.0816 < \varepsilon_{\text{wheel}} = 0.15
$$

**Step 4 — Comment (1 mark):**

The quadruped is **more energy-efficient** than the typical wheeled robot in this specific scenario. However, this is unusual — generally legged robots have $\varepsilon \geq 0.2$. The low value here suggests either (i) the power measurement was taken at very low speed, or (ii) the robot uses an exceptionally efficient gait such as a passive dynamic walk. Real quadrupeds (e.g., Boston Dynamics Spot) typically exhibit $\varepsilon \approx 0.2$–$0.5$.

> **Marking scheme:** 2 + 2 + 2 + 1 = 7 marks.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — State the variables (1 mark):**

Relevant physical quantities: forward velocity $v$, leg length $L$, gravitational acceleration $g$.

**Step 2 — Dimensional setup (1.5 marks):**

We seek dimensionless $\Pi = v^a L^b g^c$:

$$
[v^a L^b g^c] = (LT^{-1})^a L^b (LT^{-2})^c = L^{a+b+c}\, T^{-a-2c}
$$

Setting both exponents to zero:

$$
a + b + c = 0,\quad -a - 2c = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Solve (1.5 marks):**

From the second equation: $a = -2c$. Substituting:

$$
-2c + b + c = 0 \Rightarrow b = c
$$

Choose $c = 1$: then $a = -2$, $b = 1$.

$$
\Pi = v^{-2}\, L^1\, g^1 = \frac{L\, g}{v^2}
$$

The Froude Number is conventionally expressed as:

$$
\boxed{Fr = \frac{v^2}{g\,L}}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the walking-running threshold (2 marks):**

The transition from walking to running occurs at $Fr \approx 0.5$.

$$
Fr = 0.5 = \frac{v_{\text{crit}}^2}{g\,L}
$$

$$
v_{\text{crit}} = \sqrt{0.5 \cdot g \cdot L} = \sqrt{0.5 \times 9.81 \times 0.8}
$$

$$
v_{\text{crit}} = \sqrt{3.924} \approx 1.98\ \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 5 — Conclude (1 mark):**

The humanoid robot must switch from a walking gait to a running gait when its forward speed exceeds **≈ 1.98 m/s** (≈ 7.1 km/h). This is consistent with the natural human walking-to-running transition speed (~7–8 km/h).

> **Marking scheme:** 1 + 1.5 + 1.5 + 2 + 1 = 7 marks.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to check $S_m > 0$** — Many students compute only the minimum distance and stop. They lose 0.5–1 mark for not explicitly stating whether the CoG projection lies *inside* the support polygon.
>
> 2. **Mixing up Static vs Dynamic stability** — A hopping 1-legged robot is NOT statically stable. Writing "1 leg = most stable" will fetch **zero marks**.
>
> 3. **Specific Resistance unit errors** — Forgetting $g$ in the denominator leads to a dimensionally wrong answer. Examiners specifically deduct for missing units.
>
> 4. **Froude Number direction error** — The standard form is $Fr = v^2/(gL)$, NOT $Fr = gL/v^2$. A common slip costs 1 mark.
>
> 5. **Listing "7 key issues" without technical depth** — Each issue must be tied to a *measurable quantity* (e.g., $S_m$, $\varepsilon$, $Fr$). A bare bullet list fetches at most 50% marks.
>
> 6. **Drawing the support polygon without the perpendicular distance marker** — The dotted perpendicular from CoG to the nearest edge is the *stability margin*. Without it, you lose 0.5 mark in the diagram portion.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 4 — Key Issues for Locomotion)**

- ☐ **Stability** is governed by the **support polygon** (convex hull of ground contacts) and the **CoG projection**. Static stability requires $G$ inside the polygon → $S_m > 0$.
- ☐ **More legs = better stability** but **higher mechanical complexity and control cost**. Hexapod tripod gait = 3 legs down always.
- ☐ **Specific Resistance** $\varepsilon = P / (M g v)$ is dimensionless; **lower is better**. Animals cluster at $\varepsilon \approx 0.2$–$0.3$.
- ☐ **Froude Number** $Fr = v^2 / (g L)$ is the master metric for gait selection: **walk if $Fr < 0.5$, run if $Fr > 0.5$**.
- ☐ **Stride length** $\lambda = 2r$; **step length** = $r/2$; **stride frequency** $f = v/\lambda$.
- ☐ **Center of Gravity**: lowering CoG increases the **tipping moment arm resistance** → larger stability margin.
- ☐ **Static vs Dynamic balance**: Static = motion can stop anytime; Dynamic = motion *must* continue (hopping, running).
- ☐ **Mechanical complexity** scales as $N_{\text{actuators}} = N_{\text{legs}} \times DOF_{\text{per leg}}$ (typically 2–3 DOF/leg).
- ☐ **Dimensional analysis** is the rigorous way to derive locomotion metrics — the **Froude Number derivation from $\Pi$-theorem is a frequently asked question**.
- ☐ **Comparative table (Wheels vs Legs)** is the most-likely 7-mark question — memorize the 7-row matrix.
- ☐ Always draw the **support polygon with perpendicular stability margin** in any stability-related answer diagram.
- ☐ KTU examiner rewards **equations over adjectives** — use $S_m$, $\varepsilon$, $Fr$, $\lambda$ explicitly in every answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Legged Mobile Robots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT302 - INTRODUCTION TO ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Legged Mobile Robots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Legged Mobile Robots** are a class of locomotion systems that use articulated, multi-segmented limbs (legs) for surface traversal, in contrast to wheeled or tracked platforms. Each leg typically comprises a kinematic chain of **rigid links connected by revolute (or prismatic) joints**, terminated by a foot (contact point) that interacts discretely with the ground.

The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically classifies legged robots by **number of legs** and **gait characteristics**, emphasizing the trade-off between **stability, speed, and mechanical complexity**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Hexapod as an Ant:**
> Imagine an ant crossing a rocky, uneven surface. Unlike a car (wheeled) or a tank (tracked), the ant does not need a continuous prepared path. It lifts and places its 6 legs in carefully timed sequences so that at least **3 legs are always firmly on the ground**, forming a stable tripod. A legged robot mimics this biological strategy: instead of rolling, it **picks up, swings, and places** discrete feet at calculated positions, adapting one step at a time to terrain irregularities.

**Why legs instead of wheels?**

| Locomotion Type | Terrain Adaptability | Mechanical Complexity | Energy Efficiency (Flat Ground) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Wheeled | Low | Low | **High** |
| Tracked | Medium | Medium | Medium |
| Legged | **High** | **High** | Lower |

A standard wheel requires a **continuously prepared path**, but a leg can step **over** obstacles, climb stairs, and select isolated ground contact points — making legged platforms indispensable for **search & rescue, planetary exploration (e.g., NASA's ATHLETE), and forestry robotics**.

## 1.3 Classification by Number of Legs

The KTU 2024 syllabus categorizes legged robots primarily by the count of supporting limbs:

- **One-Legged (Monoped):** Hopping robots. Statically unstable; requires **dynamic stability** throughout the motion.
- **Two-Legged (Biped):** Humanoid form. Highly complex, generally **dynamically stable** when walking, statically stable when standing.
- **Three-Legged (Triped):** Always statically stable (a triangle is the minimal closed support polygon).
- **Four-Legged (Quadruped):** Mammalian form. Supports both static and dynamic gaits.
- **Six-Legged (Hexapod):** Insect form. The most **inherently stable** configuration; offers many gait options.
- **Eight or More:** Rare, used in specialized research.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Foundational Concepts — The Three Pillars of Legged Locomotion

Every legged robot is governed by three interrelated concepts that the KTU examiner expects you to master:

### Pillar 1: The Support Polygon
The **support polygon** (also called the *support pattern* or *footprint polygon*) is the **convex hull of all ground contact points** formed by the feet currently touching the ground.

> [!NOTE]
> For **N** legs touching the ground, the support polygon is the smallest convex polygon enclosing those **N** foot positions. If the robot has only 2 feet on the ground, the support polygon is a **line segment** — the robot is essentially balanced on a "tightrope" and is statically unstable unless the CG projection falls exactly on that line.

### Pillar 2: Center of Gravity (CG) / Center of Mass (CoM)
The **CG** is the unique point at which the **entire weight of the robot can be considered to act**. Mathematically, for a system of point masses $m_i$ located at $\vec{r}_i$:

$$\vec{r}_{CG} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \vec{r}_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i}$$

When projected vertically onto the ground plane, this gives a single point — the **CG Projection**. All stability analysis reduces to a **2D geometry problem** in the ground plane.

### Pillar 3: Stability — Static vs. Dynamic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability Criteria (KTU Board Favorite):**
> - **Static Stability:** The vertical projection of the CG onto the ground lies **strictly within** the support polygon. The robot is stable even when **frozen** mid-stride.
> - **Dynamic Stability:** The CG projection **may leave** the support polygon, but the robot is stable because of **inertia, momentum, and controlled limb motion** (e.g., human walking, hopping robots).
> - A system is said to possess **N-leg static stability** if the robot can be statically stable with **N legs** supporting it.

## 2.2 Stability Margin — The Quantitative Measure

The **stability margin (SM)** is defined as the **shortest distance** from the CG projection to any edge of the support polygon. It quantifies *how* stable the configuration is — not just *whether* it is stable.

$$\boxed{SM = \min_{i} d(\vec{r}_{CG,\text{proj}}, \text{edge}_i)}$$

where $d(\vec{r}_{CG,\text{proj}}, \text{edge}_i)$ is the perpendicular distance from the CG projection to the $i$-th edge of the support polygon. The robot is statically stable **if and only if** $SM > 0$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — MEMORIZE THIS FOR KTU EXAMS**

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units | Key Condition |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | CG Position | $\vec{r}_{CG} = \frac{\sum m_i \vec{r}_i}{\sum m_i}$ | m | 2D for planar, 3D for spatial |
| 2 | Static Stability Criterion | $\vec{r}_{CG,\text{proj}} \in \text{Interior}(\text{Support Polygon})$ | — | Strictly inside (SM > 0) |
| 3 | Stability Margin | $SM = \min d(\text{CG}_{\text{proj}}, \text{edge}_i)$ | m | Larger = more stable |
| 4 | # Gaits (per $N$ legs) | $G = (2N - 1)!$ | — | For $N$ symmetric legs |
| 5 | Minimum Legs for Static Stability on 3-D Terrain | $N_{min} = 3$ | — | Triangle is minimal closed polygon |
| 6 | Walk Cycle Time | $T = t_{\text{transfer}} + t_{\text{propulsion}}$ | s | $t_{\text{transfer}}$ = swing time, $t_{\text{propulsion}}$ = support time |
| 7 | Duty Factor (DF) | $\beta = \frac{t_{\text{propulsion}}}{T}$ | dimensionless | $0 < \beta < 1$ |
| 8 | Wave Gait Leg Phase | $\phi_i = \frac{i-1}{N-1}$ (rad fraction) | rad | $i = 1, 2, \ldots, N-1$ |
| 9 | Tripod Gait (Hexapod) | 3 legs lift simultaneously | — | Alternating tripods |
| 10 | Energy per Unit Distance | $\epsilon = \frac{E}{d}$ | J/m | Lower = more efficient |

## 2.4 Leg Configurations and Joints

A typical **mammalian leg** is modeled as a 3-DOF chain: **hip abduction/adduction**, **hip flexion/extension**, and **knee flexion/extension**, giving a total of $3N$ joints for $N$ legs. A typical **insect leg** is similar but with the body plane as the reference.

The **two main leg geometries** studied in KTU:

1. **Mammalian Configuration (Elbow / Knee Forward):** Better for forward speed; the leg swings in a parasagittal plane.
2. **Insect Configuration (Crawling, sprawled posture):** Legs project laterally; lower CG, more lateral stability, but less efficient for fast forward motion.

## 2.5 Why This Matters in Engineering

Legged robots are the **only practical solution** for unstructured, broken, or vertical terrain. Industrial use cases include:
- **Defense / EOD** (e.g., Boston Dynamics' Spot, ANYbotics' ANYmal).
- **Planetary Rovers** (NASA's ATHLETE, ETH Zurich's SpaceBok).
- **Disaster Response** (DARPA Robotics Challenge humanoids).
- **Medical & Rehabilitation** (exoskeletons, prosthetic legs).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Number of Distinct Gaits for N Symmetric Legs

**Statement:** For a robot with $N$ symmetric, evenly-spaced legs, the total number of distinct gaits is $(2N - 1)!$.

### Step 1 — Define a Gait by its Phase Vector
A gait is fully specified by the **phase** of each leg — the fraction of the walk cycle at which that leg lifts off the ground. For $N$ legs, this gives a phase vector of length $N$:

$$\vec{\phi} = (\phi_1, \phi_2, \ldots, \phi_N), \quad 0 \leq \phi_i < 1$$

### Step 2 — Normalize the Phase Vector
Because the cycle has rotational symmetry, a constant offset added to all phases produces the same gait. We can therefore **fix $\phi_1 = 0$** without loss of generality, leaving $N - 1$ free phase variables.

### Step 3 — Count Valid Phase Orderings
Each of the remaining $N - 1$ legs has a unique phase $\phi_i \in (0, 1)$. Furthermore, by the **mirror symmetry** of legged locomotion (a gait is unchanged if all phases are reversed, $\phi_i \to 1 - \phi_i$), we identify forward and reverse gaits. The total count becomes:

$$G = \frac{(N - 1)!}{2} \times 2 \times 2 = (2N - 1)!$$

### Step 4 — Verification with Known Cases
- $N = 2$ (Biped): $G = 3! = 6$ — but in practice bipeds only have **2 gaits** (walk, run) due to additional balance constraints, showing the formula gives an **upper bound** for biologically realistic systems.
- $N = 4$ (Quadruped): $G = 7! = 5040$ — out of which only a few (crawl, pace, trot, bound, gallop) are biologically meaningful.
- $N = 6$ (Hexapod): $G = 11! = 39,916,800$ — overwhelmingly many, but only **wave gait** and **tripod gait** dominate in insects.

## 3.2 Derivation: Stability Margin for a Quadruped in Crawl Gait

**Problem Setup:** A 4-legged robot of mass $M$ has its body CG at coordinates $(x_{cg}, y_{cg})$ in the body-fixed frame. The 4 feet form a rectangle of width $2a$ (lateral) and length $2b$ (longitudinal) around the CG. Find the stability margin in the longitudinal direction.

### Step 1 — Define Foot Positions
The four foot positions in the ground plane are:

$$\begin{aligned}
F_1 &= (+a, +b), \quad F_2 = (-a, +b) \\
F_3 &= (-a, -b), \quad F_4 = (+a, -b)
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Form the Support Polygon
With all 4 feet on the ground, the support polygon is a **rectangle** with vertices at the four foot positions. The edges run parallel to the X and Y axes.

### Step 3 — Compute Distances to Longitudinal Edges
The longitudinal edges of the support polygon lie at $y = +b$ and $y = -b$. The perpendicular distance from the CG projection $(x_{cg}, y_{cg})$ to these edges is:

$$d_y = \min(b - y_{cg},\; y_{cg} - (-b)) = b - \vert y_{cg} \vert$$

This is valid only when $\vert y_{cg} \vert \leq b$ (i.e., CG lies inside the polygon).

### Step 4 — Compute Distances to Lateral Edges
Similarly, the lateral edges lie at $x = +a$ and $x = -a$:

$$d_x = a - \vert x_{cg} \vert$$

### Step 5 — Take the Minimum
By definition, the stability margin is the **shortest distance to any edge**:

$$\boxed{SM = \min(a - \vert x_{cg} \vert,\; b - \vert y_{cg} \vert)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** If $a < b$ (a long, narrow body like a dog), the **lateral** direction limits the stability margin. This is why quadrupeds have **wider gaits** when they walk slowly on uneven ground.

## 3.3 Derivation: Walking Speed from Duty Factor

The **duty factor** $\beta$ is the fraction of the walk cycle during which a leg is in contact with the ground:

$$\beta = \frac{t_{\text{propulsion}}}{T}$$

The forward velocity of a leg during its propulsion phase is $v_p$. The **average speed** of the robot is:

$$v = \beta \cdot v_p$$

**Proof:** Over one full cycle of duration $T$, the leg is in propulsion for time $\beta T$ and covers a distance $v_p \cdot \beta T$. The lift/transfer (swing) phase produces no forward motion. Hence:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Distance per cycle} &= v_p \cdot t_{\text{propulsion}} = v_p \cdot \beta T \\
\text{Average speed} &= \frac{\text{Distance per cycle}}{T} = \beta \cdot v_p
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Insight:** A leg with $\beta = 1$ is **always on the ground** (crawling); $\beta < 0.5$ means the leg spends more time in the air than on the ground (running). For static walking, $\beta \geq 0.5$ is generally required so that the other legs can maintain a stable support polygon.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023 Model]** Differentiate between static stability and dynamic stability in legged robots.

**Model Answer:**

**Static Stability:** A legged robot is said to be statically stable if the vertical projection of its center of gravity (CG) onto the ground plane lies **strictly inside** the convex hull (support polygon) formed by the ground contact feet. The robot remains stable **even if all motion is suddenly frozen** at any instant of its gait. This property requires at least **3 legs** on the ground simultaneously (a triangle is the minimal closed support polygon).

**Dynamic Stability:** The CG projection **may fall outside** the support polygon during locomotion, but the robot does not topple because it possesses sufficient **inertia, momentum, and active control** to keep the body balanced. Examples include **human walking**, **biped running**, and **one-legged hopping robots** (e.g., the Raibert hopper). Dynamic stability allows faster gaits but requires **real-time sensors (IMU, force sensors) and fast feedback control**.

| Aspect | Static Stability | Dynamic Stability |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| CG projection | Inside support polygon | May leave polygon |
| Minimum legs on ground | 3 | 1 (hopper) or 2 (biped) |
| Speed | Slower | Faster |
| Control complexity | Low | High |
| Energy per distance | Higher (more feet moving) | Lower |

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model]** What is a "support polygon" and how is it used in stability analysis of legged robots?

**Model Answer:**
