The **support polygon** (also called the *support pattern*) is defined as the **smallest convex polygon whose vertices are the ground-contact points of all feet currently touching the ground**. For a robot with $N$ feet on the ground at positions $\{(x_1, y_1), (x_2, y_2), \ldots, (x_N, y_N)\}$, the support polygon is the convex hull of these points.

**Use in Stability Analysis:**
1. The vertical projection of the robot's CG onto the ground must lie **inside this polygon** for the robot to be statically stable.
2. The **distance from the CG projection to the nearest edge** of the polygon gives the **stability margin** $SM = \min_i d(\text{CG}_{\text{proj}}, \text{edge}_i)$.
3. If the robot has only 2 feet on the ground, the support polygon collapses to a **line segment** — the robot is statically stable only if its CG projection lies exactly on this line (zero margin → practically unstable).
4. If the robot has 3 feet on the ground forming a triangle, it is always statically stable as long as the CG projection is inside the triangle.
5. For 4 or more feet, the polygon becomes a **quadrilateral, pentagon, or hexagon**, and richer stability analysis is possible.

This concept is fundamental for **gait design** — the gait must be chosen so that at every instant, the support polygon contains the CG projection.

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Regulation:** Part B questions carry **14 marks each** and offer an **internal choice** between two alternative questions. You must answer **only one** of the two options.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Explain with neat diagrams the various configurations of legged robots based on the **number of legs** (1-leg, 2-leg, 3-leg, 4-leg, 6-leg). Discuss the stability characteristics of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **4-legged robot** has its body modeled as a uniform rectangle of mass $M = 20 \text{ kg}$ with dimensions $0.8 \text{ m}$ (length) $\times$ $0.5 \text{ m}$ (width). All four feet are placed at the four corners of a rectangle of $1.0 \text{ m}$ (length) $\times$ $0.7 \text{ m}$ (width), centered on the body's geometric center. Calculate the **stability margin** in both the longitudinal and lateral directions. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Q.A(a)

**One-Legged (Monoped) Robot:**
- A single leg supports the entire robot.
- The support polygon is a **single point**, so the robot is **always statically unstable**.
- It must rely on **dynamic stability** — hopping motion (e.g., Raibert's hopper at CMU, 1980s).
- Mechanical simplicity but very high control complexity.

**Two-Legged (Biped) Robot:**
- Mimics human locomotion.
- Statically stable only when both feet are on the ground and CG projection is between them.
- Walking is **dynamically stable** — the robot would fall over if frozen mid-stride.
- Used in humanoid robots (e.g., ASIMO, Atlas, NAO).
- 2 legs $\Rightarrow$ minimal contact, highest efficiency on flat ground.

**Three-Legged (Triped) Robot:**
- A triangle is the **smallest closed polygon** in 2D.
- With 3 feet on the ground, the robot is **always statically stable** (any CG projection inside the triangle is possible).
- Mechanically more complex than biped due to asymmetric dynamics; rarely used in practice.

**Four-Legged (Quadruped) Robot:**
- Mammalian configuration.
- Can be **statically or dynamically stable** depending on the gait.
- **Gaits:** Crawl (always 3 feet down), Pace, Trot, Bound, Gallop.
- Examples: Boston Dynamics' Spot, ANYbotics' ANYmal, MIT Mini-Cheetah.

**Six-Legged (Hexapod) Robot:**
- Insect configuration.
- **Inherently the most stable** — multiple gaits possible while always maintaining $\geq 3$ feet on the ground.
- **Gaits:** Wave gait, Tripod gait.
- Used in planetary exploration, military reconnaissance, search & rescue.

**Summary Table:**

| # Legs | Stability | Typical Application |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Dynamic only | Research hoppers |
| 2 | Static (stand) / Dynamic (walk) | Humanoids |
| 3 | Static (3 legs on ground) | Rare |
| 4 | Static or Dynamic | Service robots, military |
| 6 | Static (multiple gaits) | Exploration, military |

---

#### Solution to Q.A(b)

**Given:**
- Body: $0.8 \text{ m} \times 0.5 \text{ m}$, mass $M = 20 \text{ kg}$, uniform (CG at body geometric center).
- Foot rectangle: $1.0 \text{ m} \times 0.7 \text{ m}$, centered on the body.

**Step 1 — Identify CG Position**
The uniform rectangular body has its CG at its geometric center. By the symmetric placement of the feet, the CG projection in the ground frame is at the **center of the foot rectangle**, i.e., $(x_{cg}, y_{cg}) = (0, 0)$.

**Step 2 — Identify Support Polygon Edges**
The four feet are at $(\pm 0.5, \pm 0.35)$. The support polygon is a rectangle with:
- Longitudinal edges at $y = +0.35$ and $y = -0.35$.
- Lateral edges at $x = +0.5$ and $x = -0.5$.

**Step 3 — Compute Longitudinal Stability Margin**
The perpendicular distance from $(0, 0)$ to the longitudinal edge $y = \pm 0.35$:

$$d_{\text{long}} = 0.35 - \vert 0 \vert = 0.35 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Lateral Stability Margin**
The perpendicular distance from $(0, 0)$ to the lateral edge $x = \pm 0.5$:

$$d_{\text{lat}} = 0.5 - \vert 0 \vert = 0.5 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Determine Overall Stability Margin**
By definition, the stability margin is the **shortest distance** to any edge:

$$SM = \min(d_{\text{long}}, d_{\text{lat}}) = \min(0.35, 0.5) = \boxed{0.35 \text{ m}}$$

The **longitudinal direction** is the limiting one. To improve stability, the robot should either widen the stance in the longitudinal direction or shift the CG laterally.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2022 Model]**

**(a)** With a clear figure, explain the **wave gait** and **tripod gait** for a hexapod robot. Compare their stability and speed characteristics. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hexapod robot walks with a **tripod gait** such that each tripod is in propulsion for $0.4 \text{ s}$ and in transfer (swing) for $0.4 \text{ s}$ per cycle. If the propulsion speed of each foot relative to the body is $0.25 \text{ m/s}$, calculate: (i) the **duty factor**, (ii) the **walk cycle time**, (iii) the **average forward speed** of the robot. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Q.B(a)

**Wave Gait (Hexapod):**
- The legs are lifted and placed **one at a time**, in a wave pattern from the rear to the front.
- For 6 legs numbered 1 (rear-right) to 6 (front-left), the lift sequence is: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6.
- At every instant, **5 legs are on the ground** and only 1 is swinging.
- **Highly stable** (5-leg support polygon), but **slow** because only one leg moves at a time.
- Used in **careful terrain traversal** (e.g., NASA Mars rover prototypes).

**Tripod Gait (Hexapod):**
- The 6 legs are divided into **two alternating tripods**:
  - **Tripod A:** Right-Front (RF), Left-Middle (LM), Right-Rear (RR)
  - **Tripod B:** Left-Front (LF), Right-Middle (RM), Left-Rear (LR)
- The two tripods **lift and place simultaneously**, 180° out of phase.
- At every instant, **3 legs are on the ground** (one full tripod).
- **Faster** than wave gait because 3 legs move together, but the support polygon is smaller (triangle).
- Used in **fast hexapods** (e.g., cockroach-inspired robots).

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Wave Gait | Tripod Gait |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Legs in air | 1 | 3 |
| Legs on ground | 5 | 3 |
| Support polygon | Pentagon (large) | Triangle (smaller) |
| Speed | Slow | **Fast** |
| Stability | **High** | Moderate |
| Energy efficiency | Moderate | High |
| Best for | Rough terrain | Flat / open ground |

---

#### Solution to Q.B(b)

**Given:**
- Propulsion time per tripod: $t_p = 0.4 \text{ s}$
- Transfer (swing) time per tripod: $t_t = 0.4 \text{ s}$
- Propulsion foot speed: $v_p = 0.25 \text{ m/s}$

**Step 1 — Calculate the Walk Cycle Time**
The walk cycle is the time for one tripod to complete a full sequence (propulsion + transfer):

$$T = t_p + t_t = 0.4 + 0.4 = \boxed{0.8 \text{ s}}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the Duty Factor**
The duty factor $\beta$ is the fraction of the cycle during which a leg is in propulsion (i.e., on the ground producing forward motion):

$$\beta = \frac{t_p}{T} = \frac{0.4}{0.8} = \boxed{0.5}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the Average Forward Speed**
Using the relation derived in Section 3.3:

$$v = \beta \cdot v_p = 0.5 \times 0.25 = \boxed{0.125 \text{ m/s}}$$

Equivalently, distance per cycle $= v_p \cdot t_p = 0.25 \times 0.4 = 0.1 \text{ m}$, and $v = 0.1 / 0.8 = 0.125 \text{ m/s}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Many students incorrectly compute the duty factor as $t_t / T$ (using the **transfer** time instead of the **propulsion** time). The duty factor is **always** the fraction of time the leg is **on the ground supporting propulsion**, NOT the fraction in the air. A tripod gait with equal propulsion and transfer times gives $\beta = 0.5$ — this is the **boundary** between walking and running. A gait with $\beta < 0.5$ is considered a **run**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Legged vs Wheeled:** Legs offer superior **terrain adaptability** at the cost of **mechanical and control complexity** and **energy efficiency**.
- **Support Polygon:** Smallest convex polygon enclosing all ground-contact feet. **Static stability = CG projection strictly inside this polygon.**
- **Stability Margin:** Shortest distance from CG projection to any edge of the support polygon. **SM = 0 → critical, SM < 0 → unstable.**
- **Minimum legs for static stability:** **3 legs** (a triangle is the smallest closed polygon). 1 leg and 2 legs require **dynamic stability**.
- **One-leg (Monoped):** Always dynamically stable. Example: Raibert hopper.
- **Two-leg (Biped):** Static when standing, dynamic when walking. Example: ASIMO, Atlas.
- **Three-leg (Triped):** Always statically stable with 3 feet on ground. Rare in practice.
- **Four-leg (Quadruped):** Mammalian. Gaits: **Crawl, Pace, Trot, Bound, Gallop**.
- **Six-leg (Hexapod):** Insect. Gaits: **Wave** (stable, slow) and **Tripod** (fast).
- **Number of Gaits:** Upper bound = $(2N - 1)!$ for $N$ symmetric legs. Only a few are biologically/mechanically meaningful.
- **Duty Factor $\beta = t_{\text{propulsion}} / T$:** $\beta = 0.5$ is the walking-running boundary. $\beta \geq 0.5$ is required for statically stable walking.
- **Average speed:** $v = \beta \cdot v_p$.
- **Leg Configuration:** **Mammalian** (parasagittal, knee forward) vs **Insect** (sprawled, leg projects laterally). Insect config gives lower CG and more lateral stability.
- **Common KTU Mistake:** Confusing the **duty factor** with the **transfer fraction**; remember it is always the **ground-contact fraction**.
- **Key Constants to Memorize:** Minimum static-stability legs = 3; number-of-gaits upper bound formula = $(2N-1)!$.
- **Energy Insight:** Static walking is **less energy-efficient** than dynamic walking on flat ground — counterintuitively, humans use less energy running than walking at certain speeds because dynamic gaits leverage gravity and leg spring mechanics.
- **Industrial Relevance:** Boston Dynamics (Spot, Atlas), ANYbotics (ANYmal), NASA (ATHLETE rover), MIT (Mini-Cheetah) — these are the **exam-favorite real-world examples** to cite in KTU answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wheeled Mobile Robots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT302 - INTRODUCTION TO ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Wheeled Mobile Robots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Wheeled Mobile Robot (WMR)

A **Wheeled Mobile Robot (WMR)** is a class of ground-based autonomous or semi-autonomous robotic system whose locomotion is achieved exclusively through the rolling motion of wheels in direct contact with the operating surface. As per the KTU 2024 syllabus framework (PCBRT302 – Module 4), WMRs are studied as the most commercially significant subclass of mobile robots, dominating industrial automation, planetary exploration, autonomous vehicles, AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles), and domestic service robotics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Scope):** *"Wheeled Mobile Robots – Legged and wheeled, characteristics of wheeled mobile robots, holonomic and non-holonomic constraints, classification of wheel types – standard wheel, castor wheel, Swedish wheel, spherical wheel, wheel configurations, kinematics models, degree of mobility, degree of steerability, maneuverability."*

Mathematically, a WMR is represented as a **rigid body** with the following state parameters in a 2D plane:

- $(x, y)$ → Position of the robot's reference point in the global (inertial) frame.
- $\theta$ → Orientation angle of the robot's longitudinal axis with respect to the global X-axis.

The configuration space $\mathcal{C}$ of a planar mobile robot is therefore **3-dimensional** ($\mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$):

$$\mathcal{C} = \{(x, y, \theta) \mid x, y \in \mathbb{R}, \theta \in [0, 2\pi)\}$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🧠

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy: The Supermarket Shopping Cart**
> 
> Imagine pushing a **shopping cart** in a supermarket:
> - The **two rear wheels** are **fixed and aligned** with the cart's body. You cannot rotate them independently — they roll only in the direction the cart points. These mimic a **Standard Fixed Wheel**.
> - The **front wheels** swivel freely, allowing the cart to turn. These mimic a **Castor Wheel** or **Standard Steering Wheel**.
> - The cart itself **cannot move sideways** — if you try to slide it left while it faces forward, the wheels jam. This is the **non-holonomic constraint** that makes car-like and cart-like motion counter-intuitive (you need a 3-point turn to re-orient!).
> - Now imagine a **Mecanum-wheeled wheelchair** in a hospital — it can slide sideways without rotating. That is a **holonomic** system.

This analogy helps students visualize why **kinematic constraints** matter: the wheel geometry directly decides *what motions are possible* and *what motions are forbidden*.

## 1.3 Why Wheeled Mobile Robots? — Engineering Relevance

| Domain | WMR Application |
|---|---|
| **Industrial Automation** | AGVs in warehouses (Amazon Kiva robots), assembly lines |
| **Planetary Exploration** | NASA's Mars Rovers (Sojourner, Spirit, Curiosity, Perseverance) — Rocker-Bogie suspension |
| **Defense** | PackBot, TALON bomb disposal robots |
| **Domestic Service** | Roomba (iRobot), robotic lawn mowers |
| **Healthcare** | Da Vinci surgical carts, hospital meal delivery robots |
| **Automotive** | The modern autonomous car (a special WMR case) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics used in KTU Board Examinations:**
> - **DOF (Degrees of Freedom)** of a robot in 3D space = **6** (3 translation + 3 rotation).
> - **DOF of a planar (2D) rigid body** = **3** (x, y, θ).
> - **Degree of Mobility** ($\delta_m$) and **Degree of Steerability** ($\delta_s$) are KTU-high-yield terminologies.

## 1.4 Fundamental Categories of WMR Locomotion

| Category | Description | KTU-Relevant Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Synchronous Drive (Synchro Drive)** | All wheels are driven and steered simultaneously by a single motor | CYE personal robot, Nomad XR4000 |
| **Differential Drive** | Two independently driven wheels on a common axis | Pioneer P3-DX, Roomba, Mars Sojourner |
| **Car-like (Ackermann)** | Two fixed rear wheels + two steered front wheels | Autonomous cars (Google Waymo) |
| **Tricycle Drive** | One steered + driven wheel in front, two fixed wheels at rear | Domestic service robots |
| **Omni-directional** | Uses Swedish/Mecanum wheels to achieve sideways motion | KUKA youBot, Research platforms |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Wheel Types — The Five Canonical Wheels

The KTU 2024 syllabus mandates a thorough understanding of the **five fundamental wheel types**. Each wheel imposes specific kinematic constraints on the robot.

### 2.1.1 Standard Fixed Wheel (Standard Wheel)
- **Geometry:** A conventional car-type wheel that is **fixed** in orientation relative to the robot chassis.
- **Axes:** Two perpendicular axes → **rolling axis** (contact) and **steering axis** (perpendicular to rolling, lying in the wheel plane).
- **Constraint:** The wheel can **only roll forward/backward** along its rolling axis. It cannot translate sideways.
- **Example:** Rear wheels of a car, both wheels of a differential drive robot.

### 2.1.2 Standard Steering Wheel (Steered Standard Wheel)
- **Geometry:** A standard wheel mounted on a **rotatable steering joint** about a vertical axis.
- **Capability:** The wheel's rolling direction can be **actively rotated** relative to the chassis.
- **Example:** Front wheels of an Ackermann-steered car.

### 2.1.3 Castor Wheel
- **Geometry:** A standard wheel mounted on an **offset steering joint** — the steering axis does **not** pass through the wheel's contact point.
- **Key Feature:** The wheel **passively self-aligns** with the direction of motion (like the wheel on a shopping cart).
- **Constraint:** Since the steering axis is offset, the wheel imposes **only one constraint** (the perpendicular rolling constraint), making it very useful in designs where steerability is needed without active steering motors.

### 2.1.4 Swedish Wheel (Mecanum / Universal Wheel)
- **Geometry:** A central hub with **passive rollers** arranged at ±45° around the circumference.
- **Magic Property:** The wheel can roll **forward AND sideways simultaneously** — it has **zero net steering axis constraint** when passively oriented.
- **Example:** Mecanum-wheeled platforms, KUKA youBot.

### 2.1.5 Spherical Wheel (Ball Wheel)
- **Geometry:** A true ball bearing used as a wheel.
- **Capability:** Omni-directional rolling in any direction.
- **Difficulty:** Hard to implement mechanically — requires complex actuation (often magnetic or omnidirectional rollers in contact with the ball).

## 2.2 The Three Fundamental Kinematic Constraints

For every wheel in contact with the ground, **two constraints** are applied to the robot's motion. The KTU board frequently asks students to derive and identify these.

### Constraint 1: Rolling Constraint (No Slipping Perpendicular to Wheel Plane)
The component of the wheel's velocity at the contact point, **perpendicular to the wheel's plane**, must be zero. This is the "no side-slip" condition.

$$[\sin(\alpha + \beta), \quad -\cos(\alpha + \beta), \quad \ell \cos\beta] \, \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} - r \dot{\varphi} \cos\beta = 0$$

Where:
- $\alpha$ = steering angle of the wheel
- $\beta$ = fixed angle between the wheel's rolling axis and the wheel plane
- $\ell$ = distance from robot center to wheel
- $r$ = wheel radius
- $\dot{\varphi}$ = wheel's rotational velocity

### Constraint 2: Sliding Constraint (No Sliding Along the Steering Axis)
The component of velocity at the contact point **along the steering axis** (vertical axis) must be zero. This prevents the wheel from sliding sideways if it is a fixed/steered wheel. **Castor and Swedish wheels relax this constraint.**

$$[\cos(\alpha + \beta), \quad \sin(\alpha + \beta), \quad \ell \sin\beta] \, \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = 0$$

## 2.3 Holonomic vs Non-Holonomic Constraints

This is the **single most important conceptual distinction** in Module 4 of PCBRT302.

> [!NOTE]
> **Holonomic Constraint:** A constraint that is *integrable* — it restricts the **accessible configuration space** itself. If a robot has $n$ DOF and $k$ holonomic constraints, its effective DOF = $n - k$.
> 
> **Non-Holonomic Constraint:** A constraint that is *not integrable* — it restricts the *velocity* space but **NOT** the configuration space. The robot can still reach any $(x, y, \theta)$ eventually, but cannot do so via a straight-line path.

| Property | Holonomic | Non-Holonomic |
|---|---|---|
| Reduces DOF permanently? | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (only instantaneously) |
| Velocity constraint integrable? | ✅ Yes (closed-form) | ❌ No (involves $\dot{q}$) |
| Example | Robotic arm joint limit, parallel manipulator closed loop | Car's "cannot move sideways" rule |
| Configurability | Reachable configs form a submanifold | Reachable configs = full manifold |

**Mathematical Form:** A constraint is **holonomic** if it can be written as $f(q) = 0$. It is **non-holonomic** if it appears only in differential form $g(q, \dot{q}) = 0$ and cannot be integrated.

## 2.4 Degree of Mobility, Steerability, and Maneuverability

This is a **KTU-favorite 7 or 14 mark question**.

### Degree of Mobility ($\delta_m$)
The number of **independently controllable degrees of freedom** in the robot's configuration $(x, y, \theta)$. A differential drive robot has $\delta_m = 2$ (translate in $x$ and rotate in $\theta$).

### Degree of Steerability ($\delta_s$)
The number of **independently controllable steering axes**. A car has $\delta_s = 1$ (only the front wheels steer).

### Degree of Maneuverability ($\delta_M$)
$$\delta_M = \delta_m + \delta_s$$

### Complete Grading of Wheeled Mobile Robots

| Configuration | $\delta_m$ | $\delta_s$ | $\delta_M$ | Holonomic? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Differential Drive (2 fixed wheels) | 2 | 0 | 2 | No |
| Tricycle (1 steer + 2 fixed) | 2 | 1 | 3 | No |
| Car (Ackermann, 2 steer + 2 fixed) | 2 | 1 | 3 | No |
| Synchro Drive (3 steer + drive) | 2 | 1 | 3 | No |
| Omni (3 Swedish + 1 castor) | 3 | 0 | 3 | **Yes** ✅ |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet 📋

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Configuration space | $\mathcal{C} \subset \mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$ | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 2 | Robot pose | $\xi = [x, y, \theta]^T$ | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 3 | Maneuverability | $\delta_M = \delta_m + \delta_s$ | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 4 | DOF (planar) | $3 = 3 \times 1 - N_{fixed} - N_{steered} + \ldots$ (Gruebler's count) | ⭐⭐ |
| 5 | Rolling constraint | $\dot{x} \sin\gamma - \dot{y} \cos\gamma - \ell \dot{\theta} \cos(\gamma-\alpha) = r\dot{\varphi}$ | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 6 | Sliding constraint | $\dot{x} \cos\gamma + \dot{y} \sin\gamma + \ell \dot{\theta} \sin(\gamma-\alpha) = 0$ | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 7 | Differential drive forward kinematics | $\dot{x} = (r/2)(\dot{\varphi}_R + \dot{\varphi}_L)\cos\theta$ | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 8 | Differential drive angular velocity | $\dot{\theta} = (r/L)(\dot{\varphi}_R - \dot{\varphi}_L)$ | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 9 | Holonomic vs Non-holonomic | Integrable in $q$ vs. only in $(q,\dot{q})$ | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 10 | Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR) | All wheel axes must intersect at one point | ⭐⭐ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These formulas are the foundation of **autonomous vehicle path planning** (Tesla FSD), **robotic surgery kinematics**, **Mars rover dead-reckoning**, and **warehouse AGV fleet management**. The "non-holonomic parking problem" is literally the parallel parking feature in modern cars.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Kinematic Model of a Differential Drive Robot ⭐ KTU HOT QUESTION

A **differential drive robot** has two independently driven coaxial wheels of radius $r$, separated by an axle of length $L$. Let $\dot{\varphi}_R$ and $\dot{\varphi}_L$ be the angular velocities of the right and left wheels respectively.

### Step 1 — Define the Robot's Posture
Let the robot's reference point be the midpoint of the wheel axle. The pose in the world frame is:
$$\xi = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ \theta \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 2 — Express Linear Velocity of Each Wheel Contact Point
The **right wheel contact point** moves with velocity $v_R = r\dot{\varphi}_R$ in the forward direction.
The **left wheel contact point** moves with velocity $v_L = r\dot{\varphi}_L$ in the forward direction.

### Step 3 — Compute the Robot's Forward Velocity $v$
Assuming no wheel slip, the center of the robot moves with the **average** of the two wheel velocities:
$$v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{r(\dot{\varphi}_R + \dot{\varphi}_L)}{2}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Robot's Angular Velocity $\omega$
The robot rotates about a point along the axle. Using the standard relation for an Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR), the angular velocity equals the difference in wheel velocities divided by the axle length:
$$\omega = \dot{\theta} = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{r(\dot{\varphi}_R - \dot{\varphi}_L)}{L}$$

### Step 5 — Compose the Forward Kinematics in the World Frame
Since the robot's body axis is oriented at angle $\theta$ with respect to the world X-axis, the velocity in the world frame is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{x} &= v \cos\theta = \frac{r}{2}(\dot{\varphi}_R + \dot{\varphi}_L)\cos\theta \\
\dot{y} &= v \sin\theta = \frac{r}{2}(\dot{\varphi}_R + \dot{\varphi}_L)\sin\theta \\
\dot{\theta} &= \frac{r}{L}(\dot{\varphi}_R - \dot{\varphi}_L)
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 6 — Invert for Inverse Kinematics
Given a desired $v$ and $\omega$, the required wheel speeds are:
$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{\varphi}_R &= \frac{2v + L\omega}{2r} \\
\dot{\varphi}_L &= \frac{2v - L\omega}{2r}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this important?** This is the **forward kinematics** of the most common research WMR (Pioneer, Roomba, TurtleBot). KTU board problems almost always ask students to derive this and compute trajectories for a given $(v, \omega)$ or wheel speed profile.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Wheel Constraint Equations for a Generic Wheel

Consider a single wheel attached to a robot at position $(x, y, \theta)$. The wheel has its center at distance $\ell$ from the robot's reference point, at a fixed mounting angle $\alpha$ (the angle of the wheel plane relative to the robot chassis).

### Step 1 — Position of the Wheel Contact Point in the World Frame
$$\begin{aligned}
x_c &= x + \ell \cos(\theta + \beta) \\
y_c &= y + \ell \sin(\theta + \beta)
\end{aligned}$$

where $\beta$ is the fixed angle between the wheel's rolling axis and the wheel's mounting axle (often $\beta = 0$ for a standard wheel, $\beta = \pi/2$ for a Swedish wheel).

### Step 2 — Differentiate Position
$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{x}_c &= \dot{x} - \ell \dot{\theta} \sin(\theta + \beta) \\
\dot{y}_c &= \dot{y} + \ell \dot{\theta} \cos(\theta + \beta)
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Rolling Constraint (No Slip Perpendicular to Wheel Plane)
The wheel's rolling axis makes an angle $(\theta + \alpha + \beta)$ with the world X-axis. The velocity component along this axis is $r\dot{\varphi}$. The perpendicular component must be zero:
$$\dot{x}_c \sin(\theta + \alpha + \beta) - \dot{y}_c \cos(\theta + \alpha + \beta) + r\dot{\varphi}\cos\alpha = 0$$

### Step 4 — Simplify Using Trigonometric Identities
Expanding and grouping terms in $\dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{\theta}$ yields the canonical form:
$$[\sin(\alpha+\beta),\ -\cos(\alpha+\beta),\ \ell\cos\beta] \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = r\dot{\varphi}\cos\alpha$$

This is the **rolling constraint** as it appears in KTU reference materials.

### Step 5 — Apply the Sliding Constraint (No Slip Along Steering Axis)
For fixed and steered standard wheels, the velocity component along the steering axis (perpendicular to rolling axis, in the wheel plane) must be zero:
$$[\cos(\alpha+\beta),\ \sin(\alpha+\beta),\ \ell\sin\beta] \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For Swedish and Castor Wheels:** The sliding constraint is **dropped** (set to zero coefficient automatically) because their steering axis passes through or is specially aligned with the contact point. This is why Mecanum-wheeled robots can move sideways!

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Gruebler's Formula for Planar Mechanism DOF

For a planar WMR, the **degrees of freedom (DOF)** can be computed using a modified Gruebler count:

$$\text{DOF} = 3(N - 1) - 2N_{fixed} - N_{steered}$$

Where:
- $N$ = total number of links (including the ground)
- $N_{fixed}$ = number of fixed standard wheels (each provides 2 constraints)
- $N_{steered}$ = number of steered standard wheels (each provides 2 constraints)
- $N_{castor}$ = castor wheels contribute only 1 constraint each

### Worked Example: Differential Drive Robot
- Number of links: $N = 3$ (chassis, left wheel, right wheel, plus ground = 4 total; subtract 1 for ground = 3 moving links)
- Number of fixed wheels: $N_{fixed} = 2$
- Number of steered wheels: $N_{steered} = 0$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{DOF} &= 3(3 - 1) - 2(2) - 0 \\
&= 3(2) - 4 \\
&= 6 - 4 \\
&= 2
\end{aligned}$$

✅ The differential drive robot has **2 DOF** — confirming the KTU textbook statement.

### Worked Example: Tricycle Drive
- 1 steering wheel in front (1 fixed + 1 steered, i.e., 2 fixed constraints + 1 steering constraint)
- 2 fixed rear wheels

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{DOF} &= 3(4 - 1) - 2(2 + 1) - 0 \\
&= 3(3) - 2(3) \\
&= 9 - 6 = 3
\end{aligned}$$

✅ Tricycle has **3 DOF** in the kinematic model (x, y, θ), but only 2 are controllable ($\delta_m = 2$, with 1 steering DOF $\delta_s = 1$).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Revision Gold ⭐)

| Property | Differential Drive | Car (Ackermann) | Omni-directional (Mecanum) | Synchro Drive |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **# Actuators** | 2 (drive) | 3 (2 steer + 1 drive) | 4 (independent drive) | 2 (drive + steer) |
| **$\delta_m$** | 2 | 2 | 3 | 2 |
| **$\delta_s$** | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| **$\delta_M$** | 2 | 3 | 3 | 3 |
| **Holonomic?** | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ |
| **Can Rotate in place?** | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ |
| **Move Sideways?** | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ |
| **Mechanical Complexity** | Low | Medium | High | Medium |
| **Commercial Use** | AGVs, Roombas | Self-driving cars | Research, factory robots | Cleaning robots |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU question setters love a 7-mark question asking: *"Compare the maneuverability and holonomic properties of differential drive, car-like, and omni-directional robots."* This matrix is your perfect answer template.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: WMR Architecture & Kinematic Pipeline

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the complete **functional architecture** of a Wheeled Mobile Robot's control and kinematic pipeline, from sensing to actuation. This is the type of system-level diagram KTU examiners reward in 14-mark answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Global Goal: Target Pose x_t, y_t, theta_t] --> B[Path Planner Module]
    B --> C[Trajectory Generator: v_des, omega_des]
    C --> D[Inverse Kinematics Solver]
    D --> E[Wheel Velocity Commands: phi_dot_L, phi_dot_R]
    E --> F[Motor Drivers and Power Electronics]
    F --> G[Wheel Actuators: Left and Right Motors]
    G --> H[Physical Wheel Rolling and ICR Formation]
    H --> I[Robot Body Motion: x_dot, y_dot, theta_dot]
    I --> J[Encoder Feedback: phi_L, phi_R]
    I --> K[IMU and Odometry Sensor Fusion]
    J --> L[Pose Estimator: x_est, y_est, theta_est]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Controller: Error e = pose_target - pose_estimated]
    M --> C
    I --> N[Non-holonomic Constraint Enforcer]
    N --> O[Feasible Motion in SE 2 Space]
    H --> P[Kinematic Constraint Block]
    P --> Q[Rolling Constraint Equation]
    P --> R[Sliding Constraint Equation]
```

### Reading the Diagram

- **Top Loop (Open Loop / Command Path):** Goal → Path Plan → Desired $(v, \omega)$ → Inverse Kinematics → Wheel Commands → Motors → Wheels → Motion.
- **Inner Loop (Closed Loop / Feedback):** Encoders + IMU → Pose Estimator → Error computation → Corrected $(v, \omega)$ commands.
- **Side Block (Constraints):** The physical motion is always filtered through the **rolling** and **sliding constraint** equations, which are the mathematical embodiment of the no-slip assumption.

## 4.2 Mermaid State Diagram: WMR Kinematic Constraint States

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Static: Robot Idle
    Static --> Rolling: Motor Active
    Rolling --> Turning: Differential Speed Applied
    Turning --> Spinning: In-Place Rotation
    Spinning --> Rolling: Net Forward Velocity Restored
    Rolling --> Slipping: Constraint Violation
    Slipping --> Rolling: Slip Detected, Speed Reduced
    Slipping --> SafetyStop: Persistent Slip
    SafetyStop --> Static: Manual Reset
    Rolling --> [*]: Power Off
    Static --> [*]: Power Off
```

### State Explanations

- **Rolling:** Both wheels at the same speed → straight-line translation.
- **Turning:** Wheels at different speeds → curved path with non-zero ICR.
- **Spinning:** Wheels at equal and opposite speeds → pure in-place rotation ($\dot{\theta} \neq 0$, $v = 0$).
- **Slipping:** Constraint violation (e.g., wheel slip on wet surface) → safety subsystem triggered.

## 4.3 Schematic: Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR) Geometry

The **ICR** is the geometric center about which a non-holonomic WMR is rotating at any instant. Every wheel's rolling axis must pass through this single point — this is the KTU standard theorem.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Robot Chassis Body] -->|Perpendicular Offset r| B[Right Wheel]
    A -->|Perpendicular Offset r| C[Left Wheel]
    B -->|Rolling Axis Line| D[Instantaneous Center of Rotation ICR]
    C -->|Rolling Axis Line| D
    D -.->|Radius R, Angular Velocity omega| A
    E[ICR is on the perpendicular bisector of the wheel axle] -.-> D
    F[For straight motion, ICR is at infinity] -.-> D
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The ICR theorem is the geometric basis for **Ackermann steering geometry** in real cars. The steering angles of the inner and outer front wheels are *different* so that their rolling axes intersect at exactly the same ICR on the rear-axle extended line. This avoids tire scrub.

## 4.4 Comparison: Holonomic vs Non-Holonomic Motion Patterns

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Holonomic: Omni Wheel
        H1[Move Forward] --> H2[Slide Sideways]
        H2 --> H3[Rotate In Place]
        H3 --> H4[Diagonal Motion]
        H4 --> H1
    end
    subgraph NonHolonomic: Differential Drive
        N1[Move Forward] --> N2[Curve Right or Left]
        N2 --> N3[Rotate In Place via Zero Radius Turn]
        N3 --> N4[Reverse Straight]
        N4 --> N1
    end
```

The diagram visually emphasizes that an **omni-directional (holonomic) robot** can transition between motions along any direction, whereas a **differential drive (non-holonomic) robot** must follow a constrained motion manifold.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **Q1.** Define a *Wheeled Mobile Robot*. With neat sketches, classify the **five standard wheel types** used in WMR design and state one distinguishing feature of each.

#### Model Answer:

A **Wheeled Mobile Robot (WMR)** is a ground-based mobile robot that uses wheels as its primary locomotion mechanism to traverse a surface. The five standard wheel types are:

| # | Wheel Type | Distinguishing Feature |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Standard Fixed Wheel** | Orientation is fixed relative to chassis; 2 constraints applied |
| 2 | **Standard Steering Wheel** | Rotatable about a vertical steering axis; 2 constraints applied |
| 3 | **Castor Wheel** | Steering axis offset from contact point; only 1 constraint applied (self-aligning) |
| 4 | **Swedish Wheel** | Has passive rollers around circumference; 1 constraint applied; allows sideways rolling |
| 5 | **Spherical Wheel** | True ball; 0 constraints; theoretically omni-directional |

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Examiners award 1 mark for the definition and 0.4 marks for each wheel's distinguishing feature. Sketches are **highly recommended** for full marks.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

> **Q2.** Differentiate between **holonomic** and **non-holonomic** constraints. Give one example of each from wheeled mobile robotics.

#### Model Answer:

| Aspect | Holonomic Constraint | Non-Holonomic Constraint |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Constraint expressible as $f(q) = 0$ — reduces reachable configuration space | Constraint expressible only in differential form $g(q,\dot{q}) = 0$ — does not reduce configuration space |
| **Integrable** | Yes | No |
| **Example (WMR)** | A Mecanum-wheeled omni-directional robot — can translate sideways instantaneously | A car (Ackermann steering) — cannot translate sideways; must maneuver |
| **Path vs Configuration** | Limits where the robot can be | Limits how the robot can move at each instant |

**Conclusion:** Holonomic constraints are *geometric* and limit reachable poses; non-holonomic constraints are *kinematic* and limit instantaneous velocities.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** ⚠️
> - Students frequently **lose 2–3 marks** by confusing **degree of mobility ($\delta_m$)** with **maneuverability ($\delta_M$)**. Memorize: $\delta_M = \delta_m + \delta_s$.
> - In kinematic derivations, **mixing robot-frame and world-frame velocities** is the most common error. Always state the frame explicitly.
> - For differential drive, the inverse kinematics formula $\dot{\varphi}_L = (2v - L\omega)/2r$ has a **minus sign** — students often write `+` instead of `−` for the left wheel. Lose 1 mark.
> - Do not forget the **radian-degree trap** in $\theta$ — state whether $\theta$ is in radians (in $\sin/\cos$ arguments) consistently.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 4]`

> **Q.A (a)** Derive the **forward kinematic equations** of a differential drive wheeled mobile robot, clearly defining all variables. Use these equations to compute the position and orientation of the robot after 5 seconds, given:
> - Wheel radius $r = 0.05$ m,
> - Axle length $L = 0.3$ m,
> - Initial pose: $(x_0, y_0, \theta_0) = (0, 0, 0)$,
> - Right wheel speed $\dot{\varphi}_R = 10$ rad/s, Left wheel speed $\dot{\varphi}_L = 8$ rad/s (constant).
>
> **(7 Marks)**
>
> **Q.A (b)** With reference to the **Gruebler's formula**, compute the **degrees of freedom (DOF)** and the **degree of maneuverability ($\delta_M$)** for the following robots: (i) Differential drive, (ii) Ackermann-steered car, (iii) Omni-directional robot with 4 Mecanum wheels. State clearly whether each is **holonomic** or **non-holonomic**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

**Step 1: Define variables.**
- $r$ = wheel radius, $L$ = axle length, $\dot{\varphi}_R$, $\dot{\varphi}_L$ = wheel angular velocities.
- $v$ = robot forward velocity, $\omega = \dot{\theta}$ = robot angular velocity.

**Step 2: Forward Kinematic Equations (from Section 3.1):**

$$\begin{aligned}
v &= \frac{r(\dot{\varphi}_R + \dot{\varphi}_L)}{2} \\
\omega &= \frac{r(\dot{\varphi}_R - \dot{\varphi}_L)}{L} \\
\dot{x} &= v \cos\theta \\
\dot{y} &= v \sin\theta \\
\dot{\theta} &= \omega
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Substitute numerical values.**

$$\begin{aligned}
v &= \frac{0.05 \times (10 + 8)}{2} = \frac{0.05 \times 18}{2} = 0.45 \text{ m/s} \\
\omega &= \frac{0.05 \times (10 - 8)}{0.3} = \frac{0.05 \times 2}{0.3} = \frac{0.1}{0.3} = 0.333 \text{ rad/s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Integrate over $t = 5$ s (constant $v$ and $\omega$):**

Since $\theta_0 = 0$, the robot moves in a circle of radius $R = v/\omega$:

$$R = \frac{0.45}{0.333} = 1.35 \text{ m}$$

The angular displacement is:
$$\theta(t) = \omega t = 0.333 \times 5 = 1.667 \text{ rad} \approx 95.5°$$

Using the exact closed-form solution for constant $(v, \omega)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
x(t) &= x_0 + \frac{v}{\omega}[\sin(\theta_0 + \omega t) - \sin\theta_0] \\
y(t) &= y_0 - \frac{v}{\omega}[\cos(\theta_0 + \omega t) - \cos\theta_0] \\
\theta(t) &= \theta_0 + \omega t
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Final numerical answer.**

$$\begin{aligned}
x(5) &= 0 + \frac{0.45}{0.333}[\sin(1.667) - 0] = 1.35 \times 0.995 = 1.343 \text{ m} \\
y(5) &= 0 - 1.35[\cos(1.667) - 1] = -1.35 \times (-0.099) = 0.134 \text{ m} \\
\theta(5) &= 1.667 \text{ rad} \approx 95.5°
\end{aligned}$$

✅ **Final Pose:** $(x, y, \theta) = (1.343 \text{ m}, 0.134 \text{ m}, 1.667 \text{ rad})$.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Using Gruebler's Formula (planar version):**

$$\text{DOF} = 3(N - 1) - 2N_{fixed} - N_{steered}$$

**(i) Differential Drive:** 2 fixed wheels, $N = 3$ (chassis + 2 wheels + ground):
- DOF = $3(2) - 2(2) - 0 = 2$
- $\delta_m = 2$, $\delta_s = 0$, $\delta_M = 2$
- **Non-holonomic** (cannot move sideways instantaneously)

**(ii) Ackermann Car:** 2 fixed rear + 2 steered front wheels, $N = 5$:
- DOF = $3(4) - 2(4) - 0 = 4$, but the steering constraint is **redundant** (2 front wheels share 1 steering DOF), so the **kinematic DOF = 3** (x, y, θ), with $\delta_m = 2$, $\delta_s = 1$, $\delta_M = 3$.
- **Non-holonomic** (cannot move sideways or rotate in place)

**(iii) Omni-directional (4 Mecanum wheels):** 4 Swedish wheels, $N = 5$:
- Each Swedish wheel contributes 1 constraint (rolling only) → 4 total constraints.
- DOF = $3(4) - 1(4) = 8$ in the loose Gruebler count, but the **operational DOF for the chassis = 3** (x, y, θ).
- $\delta_m = 3$, $\delta_s = 0$, $\delta_M = 3$.
- **Holonomic** ✅ (can move sideways)

**Summary Table:**

| Robot | DOF | $\delta_m$ | $\delta_s$ | $\delta_M$ | Holonomic? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Differential Drive | 2 | 2 | 0 | 2 | No |
| Ackermann Car | 3 | 2 | 1 | 3 | No |
| Omni-directional (Mecanum) | 3 | 3 | 0 | 3 | **Yes** |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024 - Module 4]`

> **Q.B (a)** Explain the **rolling constraint** and **sliding constraint** equations for a generic wheel of a mobile robot, clearly stating the geometric meaning of each. Why does the **Swedish wheel** relax the sliding constraint? **(7 Marks)**
>
> **Q.B (b)** What is the **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)**? For an Ackermann-steered car with wheelbase $d$ and front-wheel steering angle $\delta_f$, derive the **turning radius** $R$ of the vehicle. Given $d = 2.5$ m and $\delta_f = 30°$, compute the turning radius. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

**Rolling Constraint:** This constraint ensures that the wheel **rolls without slipping** in the direction perpendicular to its wheel plane. Mathematically:

$$[\sin(\alpha+\beta),\ -\cos(\alpha+\beta),\ \ell\cos\beta] \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = r\dot{\varphi}\cos\alpha$$

**Geometric meaning:** The component of the contact-point velocity *perpendicular* to the wheel's rolling axis must equal $r\dot{\varphi}$ (the rolling velocity of the wheel).

**Sliding Constraint:** This constraint ensures that the wheel does not **slide along its steering axis** (the vertical axis about which the wheel can pivot):

$$[\cos(\alpha+\beta),\ \sin(\alpha+\beta),\ \ell\sin\beta] \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = 0$$

**Geometric meaning:** The component of the contact-point velocity *along the steering axis* must be zero. This eliminates sideways skidding of fixed and actively steered wheels.

**Why Swedish wheel relaxes the sliding constraint:**
A **Swedish (Mecanum) wheel** has passive rollers arranged at ±45° around its circumference. Because the rollers can spin freely, the wheel can accommodate lateral motion passively — the wheel itself *acts* as if it has no preferred lateral direction. Setting $\beta = \pi/2$ in the sliding constraint makes its coefficient $\ell\sin\beta = \ell \neq 0$ vanish in the limit, effectively dropping the lateral constraint. This is what gives the Swedish wheel its omni-directional capability.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Definition of ICR:** The **Instantaneous Center of Rotation** is the point in the plane about which the entire robot body is rotating at the current instant. For any non-slipping wheel, the **rolling axis must pass through the ICR**.

**Derivation of Turning Radius for Ackermann Car:**

Consider a car with:
- Wheelbase $d$ (distance between front and rear axles),
- Front-wheel steering angle $\delta_f$ (angle of the front wheel's rolling axis with respect to the car's longitudinal axis).

**Step 1:** The rear wheels are fixed; their rolling axis is along the car's longitudinal axis. Therefore, the ICR lies on the **extended line of the rear axle** (perpendicular to the rolling axis at the rear contact point).

**Step 2:** The front wheels are steered at angle $\delta_f$; their rolling axis is at $\delta_f$ to the longitudinal axis. The ICR must also lie on this front rolling axis.

**Step 3:** Geometrically, the ICR is at the intersection of:
- The perpendicular to the rear rolling axis (a lateral line through the rear axle).
- The front rolling axis itself.

**Step 4:** From the right triangle formed by the wheelbase $d$ and the ICR:

$$\tan(\delta_f) = \frac{d}{R}$$

Solving for $R$:

$$R = \frac{d}{\tan(\delta_f)}$$

**Step 5: Numerical computation for $d = 2.5$ m, $\delta_f = 30°$:**

$$R = \frac{2.5}{\tan(30°)} = \frac{2.5}{0.5774} = 4.33 \text{ m}$$

✅ **The car's turning radius is 4.33 m.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world relevance:** A typical sedan has a turning radius of ~5–6 m. The formula above is exactly what automotive engineers use to design steering geometry, and it is the basis of the **Ackermann steering condition** that ensures all four wheels track concentric circles.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🔖

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4**

- ✅ A **WMR** has a 3-DOF configuration space $(x, y, \theta)$ in the plane, denoted $SE(2)$ or $\mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$.
- ✅ **Five canonical wheel types:** Standard Fixed, Standard Steering, Castor, Swedish, Spherical — each characterized by a unique combination of rolling/sliding constraints.
- ✅ **Rolling constraint** = no slip perpendicular to wheel plane. **Sliding constraint** = no slip along steering axis.
- ✅ **Holonomic** = integrable geometric constraint; **Non-holonomic** = velocity-only constraint. The car is the canonical non-holonomic example.
- ✅ **Degree of Mobility ($\delta_m$)** = # controllable DOFs of the chassis. **Degree of Steerability ($\delta_s$)** = # controllable steering axes. **Maneuverability** = $\delta_m + \delta_s$.
- ✅ **Differential drive** has $\delta_M = 2$ (only translation + rotation, no independent steering). **Car** has $\delta_M = 3$. **Omni-directional (Mecanum)** has $\delta_M = 3$ and is **holonomic**.
- ✅ The **differential drive forward kinematic equations** are: $v = \frac{r(\dot{\varphi}_R + \dot{\varphi}_L)}{2}$, $\omega = \frac{r(\dot{\varphi}_R - \dot{\varphi}_L)}{L}$.
- ✅ The **ICR** is the geometric point about which the robot is rotating at any instant; for a non-slipping WMR, all wheel rolling axes must pass through the ICR.
- ✅ **Ackermann turning radius** formula: $R = d / \tan(\delta_f)$.
- ✅ The **Swedish wheel** is the only wheel type that *passively* allows lateral rolling, making it the foundation of all holonomic WMRs.
- ✅ The **Castor wheel** reduces the sliding constraint to a single one because its steering axis is *offset* from the contact point — this is why shopping-cart wheels self-align.
- ✅ Always **state the reference frame** (robot body frame vs. world frame) when writing kinematic equations. KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for ambiguity.
- ✅ **Sign convention trap:** In differential drive inverse kinematics, $\dot{\varphi}_L$ uses a **minus** sign: $\dot{\varphi}_L = (2v - L\omega)/2r$.
- ✅ A WMR's **reachable configuration space** is *larger* than its **instantaneously accessible velocity space** — this is the *defining* characteristic of a non-holonomic system.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Aerial Mobile Robots. Mobile Robot Kinematics (Differential Drive Robot)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT302 - INTRODUCTION TO ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Aerial Mobile Robots. Mobile Robot Kinematics (Differential Drive Robot)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Aerial Mobile Robots (UAVs)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
An **Aerial Mobile Robot** is an untethered, autonomous or semi-autonomous flying vehicle capable of navigating a three-dimensional environment using onboard sensors, actuators, and control systems. The most common architecture is the **rotary-wing quadrotor (quadcopter)**, which uses four independently controlled rotors to generate thrust and torques along multiple axes. Aerial mobile robots fall under the broader classification of **Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs)** or **Remotely Piloted Aircraft Systems (RPAS)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Aerial Mobile Robots**
> Aerial robots are characterized by **6 Degrees of Freedom (6-DOF)** in free space: 3 translational (surge, sway, heave) and 3 rotational (roll, pitch, yaw). A standard quadrotor is **underactuated** — it has only **4 control inputs** (rotor speeds) for 6 DOF, meaning not all axes can be controlled independently.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of an aerial mobile robot as a "flying calculator." Just as a desktop computer can only process what its processor allows, a quadrotor can only move in ways its **4 rotors permit**. If you imagine standing in a pool with four jet thrusters attached to your back, arms, and legs — you can rise, tilt, and turn, but you cannot instantaneously move sideways like a crab. The rotors tilt the entire body, and the body tilts to produce sideways motion. This **body-tilting-to-move** is the essence of an underactuated system.

**Key Performance Metrics (Industry-Standard Values):**
- **Hover Thrust:** ≈ 2 × (mass × gravity) for a quadrotor (i.e., each rotor supplies ~50% of weight).
- **Degrees of Freedom (DOF):** **6** for fully free 3D motion.
- **Standard Quadrotor Configuration:** **'X'** or **'+'** layout (with 4 rotors at the corners/ends of a cross).
- **Operating Frequency Range:** 1 kHz–8 kHz for rotor speed control loops.

### 1.2 Mobile Robot Kinematics

**Formal Definition:**
**Mobile Robot Kinematics** is the study of the geometry of motion of a mobile robot without considering the forces or torques that cause the motion. It describes the **mathematical relationship between the robot's actuator commands (wheel speeds)** and the resulting **motion of the robot in the world frame (pose)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Sub-Problems of Kinematics**
> 1. **Forward Kinematics:** Given wheel velocities (or body velocities), compute the robot's pose $(x, y, \theta)$ over time. *(Sensor → Pose)*
> 2. **Inverse Kinematics:** Given a desired body velocity $(v, \omega)$, compute the required wheel velocities $(v_L, v_R)$. *(Goal → Actuator)*

### 1.3 Differential Drive Robot (DDR)

**Formal Definition:**
A **Differential Drive Robot (DDR)** is a non-holonomic wheeled mobile robot driven by **two independently actuated wheels** mounted on a common axle. By varying the relative speeds of the left and right wheels, the robot can move forward, backward, rotate in place (zero-radius turn), or follow a curved path. It is the **most widely studied** mobile platform in academic robotics.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine driving a **wheelchair with two large drive wheels** and a small caster wheel in the front. Both hands on the wheels, you can:
- Push both wheels equally → go straight.
- Push the right wheel faster than the left → turn left.
- Push the wheels in opposite directions → spin in place like a top.

The differential drive robot works on the **exact same principle**, just with motors instead of hands.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The differential drive robot has **3-DOF in the world frame** (pose = $x, y, \theta$) but only **2 control inputs** (left and right wheel velocities). Hence, it is also **underactuated** and **non-holonomic** — it cannot move instantaneously sideways.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architecture of a Quadrotor (Aerial Mobile Robot)

A quadrotor has **four rotors** arranged symmetrically. The rotors are grouped into two pairs:
- **Pair A (CW — ClockWise spinning):** Rotor 1 & Rotor 3
- **Pair B (CCW — Counter-ClockWise spinning):** Rotor 2 & Rotor 4

This counter-rotation **cancels net body yaw torque** in hover. By differentially changing rotor speeds, the robot generates controlled motion:

| Control Input | Mechanism | Resulting Motion |
|---|---|---|
| Increase all 4 rotors equally | Net thrust ↑ | **Heave (vertical climb)** |
| Increase Rotor 1, decrease Rotor 3 (front/back pair) | Pitch torque | **Pitch → Forward/Backward surge** |
| Increase Rotor 2, decrease Rotor 4 (left/right pair) | Roll torque | **Roll → Lateral sway** |
| Increase CW pair, decrease CCW pair (or vice versa) | Yaw torque | **Yaw rotation** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is a quadrotor underactuated?** The 4 rotors give 4 inputs (thrust + 3 torques), but the body has 6 DOF. The 3 translational DOF and yaw are *coupled* through body tilt — you cannot translate in any horizontal direction *without* first rotating the body.

### 2.2 Differential Drive Robot — Geometric Model

**Assumptions:**
- The robot moves on a **flat, horizontal 2D plane** (a planar world).
- The two drive wheels have the **same radius $r$** and are mounted on a common axle separated by a **wheelbase $b$** (distance between wheel contact points).
- A passive **caster wheel** (or ball) supports the robot at the front/rear but does not contribute to motion.
- Wheels roll **without slipping**.

**Configuration / Pose Vector:**
$$
\xi = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ \theta \end{bmatrix}
$$

Where:
- $(x, y)$ = position of the **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)** reference point (typically midpoint between the wheels) in the world frame.
- $\theta$ = orientation of the robot's local x-axis relative to the world x-axis.

**Wheel Velocity Relationships:**
Let $v_L$ and $v_R$ be the linear velocities of the left and right wheel contact points (in m/s), and $\omega_L$, $\omega_R$ the angular velocities of the wheels (in rad/s). Then:

$$
v_L = r \, \omega_L, \quad v_R = r \, \omega_R
$$

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Linear velocity of robot (body) | $v = \dfrac{v_R + v_L}{2}$ | m/s | Average of wheel speeds |
| 2 | Angular velocity of robot (body) | $\omega = \dfrac{v_R - v_L}{b}$ | rad/s | Curvature rate |
| 3 | Instantaneous radius of curvature | $R = \dfrac{v}{\omega} = \dfrac{b}{2} \cdot \dfrac{v_R + v_L}{v_R - v_L}$ | m | Inf for straight motion |
| 4 | Forward kinematics (world frame) | $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta,\ \dot{y} = v\sin\theta,\ \dot{\theta} = \omega$ | m/s, rad/s | Pose update |
| 5 | Inverse kinematics | $v_L = v - \dfrac{b}{2}\omega, \quad v_R = v + \dfrac{b}{2}\omega$ | m/s | Required wheel speeds |
| 6 | ICR location (geometric) | Lies on axle extension, at distance $R$ from midpoint | m | On the side of slower wheel |
| 7 | Angular velocity of a wheel | $\omega_{\text{wheel}} = \dfrac{v_{\text{wheel}}}{r}$ | rad/s | $r$ = wheel radius |
| 8 | Quadrotor DOF | **6** (3 translational + 3 rotational) | — | $x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi$ |
| 9 | Quadrotor Control Inputs | **4** (4 rotor speeds) | — | Underactuated |
| 10 | Aerial robot thrust equation | $T = \sum_{i=1}^{4} k_f \, \omega_i^2$ | N | $k_f$ = thrust coefficient |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention Rule:** In KTU textbooks, $\omega > 0$ corresponds to **counter-clockwise (CCW)** rotation (standard math convention). If the right wheel is faster than the left, the robot turns **left** (CCW) — which means $\theta$ **increases**.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Differential drive robots** are the workhorses of indoor service robots (e.g., **TurtleBot**, **iRobot Create**, **Husarion ROSbot**) because of their simple mechanical design and ability to turn in place.
- **Aerial mobile robots** (quadrotors) are revolutionizing logistics (Amazon Prime Air), agriculture (DJI Agras), inspection (power lines, wind turbines), and defense (surveillance).
- The kinematic models derived here form the **foundation for control, path planning, and SLAM** algorithms in the **Robot Operating System (ROS)**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Forward Kinematics of Differential Drive Robot

**Goal:** Given wheel velocities $(v_L, v_R)$, derive the rate of change of the robot's pose $(\dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{\theta})$.

**Step 1 — Identify the Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR).**
For a rigid body moving on a plane, at any instant, the motion can be described as a pure rotation about a point — the ICR. For a differential drive robot:
- Both wheels rotate about the ICR.
- The ICR lies on the line passing through both wheel contact points (i.e., on the robot's main axle).
- The ICR is on the side of the **slower wheel**.

Let the distance from the ICR to the left wheel be $R_L$ and to the right wheel be $R_R$. The wheelbase is $b = R_L + R_R$.

The angular velocity of the entire robot body (around the ICR) is the same for both wheels:

$$
\omega = \frac{v_L}{R_L} = \frac{v_R}{R_R}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $R_L$ and $R_R$.**
Let $R$ be the distance from the ICR to the **center of the axle** (the reference point on the robot). Then:
- $R_L = R - b/2$ (if the ICR is to the **left** of the right wheel — typical case when $v_L < v_R$).
- $R_R = R + b/2$.

Substituting:
$$
\omega = \frac{v_L}{R - b/2} = \frac{v_R}{R + b/2}
$$

**Step 3 — Cross-multiply and solve for $\omega$.**

$$
v_L \, (R + b/2) = v_R \, (R - b/2)
$$

$$
v_L R + v_L \cdot \frac{b}{2} = v_R R - v_R \cdot \frac{b}{2}
$$

Rearranging to collect $R$ terms:
$$
v_L R - v_R R = -v_L \cdot \frac{b}{2} - v_R \cdot \frac{b}{2}
$$

$$
R (v_L - v_R) = -\frac{b}{2}(v_L + v_R)
$$

$$
R = \frac{b}{2} \cdot \frac{v_L + v_R}{v_R - v_L}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the angular velocity $\omega$.**
Using the relation $\omega = v_L / (R - b/2)$:

$$
\omega = \frac{v_L}{\dfrac{b}{2} \cdot \dfrac{v_L + v_R}{v_R - v_L} - \dfrac{b}{2}}
$$

$$
\omega = \frac{v_L}{\dfrac{b}{2}\left(\dfrac{v_L + v_R}{v_R - v_L} - 1\right)} = \frac{v_L}{\dfrac{b}{2}\left(\dfrac{v_L + v_R - v_R + v_L}{v_R - v_L}\right)}
$$

$$
\omega = \frac{v_L}{\dfrac{b}{2} \cdot \dfrac{2v_L}{v_R - v_L}} = \frac{v_L (v_R - v_L)}{b \, v_L} = \frac{v_R - v_L}{b}
$$

> ✓ **Final Result for angular velocity:**

$$
\boxed{\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{b}}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the linear velocity $v$ of the robot's center.**
The linear velocity is the velocity of the center point, which is at distance $R$ from the ICR:

$$
v = \omega \cdot R = \left(\frac{v_R - v_L}{b}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{b}{2} \cdot \frac{v_L + v_R}{v_R - v_L}\right)
$$

$$
v = \frac{v_R - v_L}{b} \cdot \frac{b}{2} \cdot \frac{v_L + v_R}{v_R - v_L}
$$

$$
\boxed{v = \frac{v_L + v_R}{2}}
$$

**Step 6 — Project body velocity to world frame.**
The robot's linear velocity $v$ is along the **body's local x-axis**. In the world frame, this axis makes angle $\theta$ with the world x-axis. Therefore, the world-frame velocity components are:

$$
\dot{x} = v \cos\theta
$$

$$
\dot{y} = v \sin\theta
$$

$$
\dot{\theta} = \omega
$$

**Final Forward Kinematics Model:**

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\cos\theta}{2} & \dfrac{\cos\theta}{2} \\[6pt] \dfrac{\sin\theta}{2} & \dfrac{\sin\theta}{2} \\[6pt] -\dfrac{1}{b} & \dfrac{1}{b} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v_L \\ v_R \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The matrix $\begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ is the **Jacobian** of the differential drive kinematic model. It maps body velocities to world-frame velocities.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Inverse Kinematics of Differential Drive Robot

**Goal:** Given desired body velocity $(v, \omega)$, find the required wheel velocities $(v_L, v_R)$.

We have two equations from Section 2.3:
- $v = (v_L + v_R)/2$ ... (i)
- $\omega = (v_R - v_L)/b$ ... (ii)

**Step 1 — Solve the system as a linear system.**
From (i): $v_L + v_R = 2v$.
From (ii): $-v_L + v_R = b\omega$.

**Step 2 — Add and subtract.**
Adding: $2v_R = 2v + b\omega \Rightarrow v_R = v + \dfrac{b}{2}\omega$.
Subtracting (i) − (ii): $2v_L = 2v - b\omega \Rightarrow v_L = v - \dfrac{b}{2}\omega$.

**Final Inverse Kinematics Model:**

$$
\boxed{v_L = v - \frac{b}{2}\omega, \quad v_R = v + \frac{b}{2}\omega}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Special Case Verification:**
> - $v_L = v_R$ (both wheels same) → $\omega = 0$ → **straight line motion.** ✓
> - $v_L = -v_R$ (opposite directions) → $v = 0$, $\omega = 2v_R/b$ → **pure rotation in place (zero-radius turn).** ✓

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Wheel Commands vs. Robot Motion

| Left Wheel $v_L$ | Right Wheel $v_R$ | Linear $v$ | Angular $\omega$ | Resulting Motion |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $v_L = v_R > 0$ | Same | $v > 0$ | $0$ | Straight line (forward) |
| $v_L = v_R < 0$ | Same | $v < 0$ | $0$ | Straight line (backward) |
| $v_L = 0, v_R > 0$ | Positive | $v_R/2$ | $v_R/b$ | **Rotate left** about left wheel |
| $v_L > 0, v_R = 0$ | Zero | $v_L/2$ | $-v_L/b$ | **Rotate right** about right wheel |
| $v_L = -v_R$ | Opposite | $0$ | $2v_R/b$ | Spin in place (CW or CCW) |
| $v_L \neq v_R$ (same sign) | Different | Nonzero | Nonzero | **Curved arc** (radius $R$) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Differential Drive Robot — Top-Down Geometric Schematic (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TB
    nodeA["World Frame X-Y Plane"]
    nodeB["Robot Body Reference Point"]
    nodeC["Left Wheel Contact Point"]
    nodeD["Right Wheel Contact Point"]
    nodeE["ICR Instantaneous Center of Rotation"]
    nodeF["Wheelbase b"]
    nodeG["Robot Heading Angle Theta"]
    nodeH["Body Local X Axis"]
    nodeI["Body Local Y Axis"]
    nodeJ["Caster Wheel"]

    nodeA --- nodeB
    nodeB --- nodeC
    nodeB --- nodeD
    nodeC --- nodeD
    nodeC --- nodeE
    nodeD --- nodeE
    nodeB --- nodeF
    nodeB --- nodeG
    nodeB --- nodeH
    nodeB --- nodeI
    nodeB --- nodeJ

    style nodeA fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style nodeB fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600
    style nodeC fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#009900
    style nodeD fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#009900
    style nodeE fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000
    style nodeJ fill:#f0f0f0,stroke:#666666
```

**Description of the schematic:**
- The **World Frame** $X-Y$ is fixed in the environment.
- The **Robot Body Reference Point** is at the center of the axle, between the two drive wheels.
- **Left and Right Wheels** are separated by the **wheelbase $b$**.
- The **ICR** lies on the axle line, at distance $R$ from the body center.
- **Body Local X-axis** points forward (along the heading), making angle $\theta$ with the World X-axis.
- The **Caster Wheel** is a passive support that does not affect kinematics.

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Differential Drive Control Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    stepA["Desired Pose Xd Yd Thetad"]
    stepB["Trajectory Planner"]
    stepC["Body Velocity Cmd v omega"]
    stepD["Inverse Kinematics Solver"]
    stepE["Wheel Velocity Cmd vL vR"]
    stepF["Motor Controller PWM"]
    stepG["Left Motor"]
    stepH["Right Motor"]
    stepI["Encoder Feedback"]
    stepJ["Forward Kinematics Odometry"]
    stepK["Estimated Pose X Y Theta"]
    stepL["Pose Error Feedback"]

    stepA --> stepB
    stepB --> stepC
    stepC --> stepD
    stepD --> stepE
    stepE --> stepF
    stepF --> stepG
    stepF --> stepH
    stepG --> stepI
    stepH --> stepI
    stepI --> stepJ
    stepJ --> stepK
    stepK --> stepL
    stepL --> stepB

    style stepA fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style stepD fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600
    style stepJ fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#009900
    style stepL fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000
```

**Explanation of the control flow:**
1. A **desired pose** is given (goal location and orientation).
2. The **Trajectory Planner** generates a smooth path and outputs the desired body velocity $(v, \omega)$ at each instant.
3. The **Inverse Kinematics Solver** converts $(v, \omega)$ to wheel speeds $(v_L, v_R)$ using the formulas in Section 2.3.
4. The **Motor Controller** generates **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation)** signals to drive the left and right motors.
5. **Encoders** on the wheels measure actual wheel rotation and feed back to the **Forward Kinematics Odometry** module.
6. The odometry module estimates the current pose $(x, y, \theta)$ by integrating the kinematic equations.
7. The **Pose Error** (difference between desired and actual) is fed back to the planner for **closed-loop control**.

---

### 4.3 Quadrotor (Aerial Mobile Robot) — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    nodeA["Pilot Input or Mission Planner"]
    nodeB["Trajectory Generator"]
    nodeC["Position Controller X Y Z"]
    nodeD["Attitude Controller Roll Pitch Yaw"]
    nodeE["Motor Mixing Algorithm"]
    nodeF["Rotor 1 Front Right CW"]
    nodeG["Rotor 2 Rear Right CCW"]
    nodeH["Rotor 3 Rear Left CW"]
    nodeI["Rotor 4 Front Left CCW"]
    nodeJ["IMU Inertial Measurement Unit"]
    nodeK["State Estimator"]
    nodeL["Feedback to Controllers"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeE --> nodeG
    nodeE --> nodeH
    nodeE --> nodeI
    nodeF --> nodeJ
    nodeG --> nodeJ
    nodeH --> nodeJ
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeL
    nodeL --> nodeC

    style nodeA fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style nodeE fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600
    style nodeJ fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#009900
    style nodeK fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000
```

**Key insight:** A quadrotor uses a **cascaded control architecture** — an outer loop controls position (which generates desired roll/pitch/yaw), and an inner loop controls attitude (which generates motor commands). The **IMU** (gyroscope + accelerometer) provides feedback for the inner loop, while a **state estimator** (often using Extended Kalman Filter) fuses IMU, GPS, and visual data for the outer loop.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**
**Define a Differential Drive Robot. Explain why it is classified as a non-holonomic system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Differential Drive Robot (DDR)** is a wheeled mobile robot that uses two independently driven wheels mounted on a common axle to achieve motion. By varying the relative speeds of the two wheels, the robot can move forward, backward, or rotate.

It is classified as **non-holonomic** because the robot's **controllable degrees of freedom (2: linear and angular velocities)** are **less than** its total **configuration space degrees of freedom (3: $x, y, \theta$)**. A non-holonomic constraint is one that is **not integrable** — the robot cannot move instantaneously in all directions of its configuration space. Specifically, a DDR cannot translate sideways without first rotating. Mathematically, the constraint is:

$$
-\dot{x} \sin\theta + \dot{y} \cos\theta = 0
$$

This constraint is non-integrable, confirming non-holonomic behavior. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**
**What is meant by an Aerial Mobile Robot? Why is a quadrotor considered underactuated?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An **Aerial Mobile Robot** is an unmanned flying vehicle capable of autonomous or semi-autonomous navigation in 3D space, equipped with onboard sensors, actuators, and a control system. Examples include quadrotors, helicopters, and fixed-wing UAVs.

A **quadrotor** is considered **underactuated** because it has **6 Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** in free space — 3 translational $(x, y, z)$ and 3 rotational $(\phi, \theta, \psi)$ — but only **4 control inputs** (the speeds of the 4 rotors). Therefore, the system has more DOF than actuators, making it impossible to command all 6 DOF independently. Horizontal motion is achieved only by tilting the body (coupling translation with rotation). **(3 marks)**

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying **7 marks**. Internal choice is provided.

---

#### **QUESTION A — `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`**

**(a)** Derive the forward kinematics equations of a differential drive robot. Clearly define all variables and state the assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A differential drive robot has wheel radius $r = 0.05$ m and wheelbase $b = 0.3$ m. The left and right wheel angular velocities are $\omega_L = 10$ rad/s and $\omega_R = 12$ rad/s respectively. Compute the linear velocity $v$, angular velocity $\omega$, and the radius of curvature $R$ of the robot's path. Also, find the ICR location relative to the robot's center. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Forward Kinematics Derivation (7 marks):**
*Refer to Section 3.1 of these notes for the complete derivation. The student must present:*
1. Pose vector definition: $\xi = [x, y, \theta]^T$ — **1 mark**
2. Identification of ICR and its location — **1 mark**
3. Derivation of $\omega = (v_R - v_L)/b$ — **2 marks**
4. Derivation of $v = (v_L + v_R)/2$ — **2 marks**
5. Final forward kinematics equations with Jacobian — **1 mark**

**Key equations to present:**
$$
\dot{x} = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_L + \omega_R)\cos\theta, \quad \dot{y} = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_L + \omega_R)\sin\theta, \quad \dot{\theta} = \frac{r}{b}(\omega_R - \omega_L)
$$

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $r = 0.05$ m
- $b = 0.3$ m
- $\omega_L = 10$ rad/s
- $\omega_R = 12$ rad/s

**Step 1 — Compute wheel linear velocities.**
$$
v_L = r \cdot \omega_L = 0.05 \times 10 = 0.5 \text{ m/s}
$$
$$
v_R = r \cdot \omega_R = 0.05 \times 12 = 0.6 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute body linear velocity $v$.**
$$
v = \frac{v_L + v_R}{2} = \frac{0.5 + 0.6}{2} = \frac{1.1}{2} = 0.55 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute body angular velocity $\omega$.**
$$
\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{b} = \frac{0.6 - 0.5}{0.3} = \frac{0.1}{0.3} = 0.333 \text{ rad/s} \quad (\approx 0.333 \text{ rad/s})
$$

Since $\omega > 0$, the robot turns **counter-clockwise (left)**.

**Step 4 — Compute radius of curvature $R$.**
$$
R = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{0.55}{0.333} = 1.65 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 5 — Locate the ICR.**
The ICR lies on the robot's axle at distance $R = 1.65$ m from the body center, on the **side of the slower wheel (left wheel)**, since the robot is turning left.

> ✓ **Final Answer:** $v = 0.55$ m/s, $\omega = 0.333$ rad/s (CCW), $R = 1.65$ m, ICR is 1.65 m to the left of the robot's center.

---

#### **QUESTION B — `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]` (Internal Choice Alternative)**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of a quadrotor aerial mobile robot with a neat block diagram. Discuss its degrees of freedom and explain why it is underactuated. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A differential drive robot with wheelbase $b = 0.4$ m needs to follow a circular path of radius $R = 2$ m at a linear speed of $v = 1$ m/s. Using the inverse kinematics equations, determine the required left and right wheel velocities. Also find the wheel angular velocities if wheel radius $r = 0.08$ m. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Quadrotor Architecture (7 marks):**

*Key points to be presented:*
1. Definition of aerial mobile robot and quadrotor — **1 mark**
2. 6-DOF description ($x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi$) — **1 mark**
3. 4 control inputs (rotor speeds), 4-rotor X or + configuration — **1 mark**
4. Explanation of how rotor differential speeds generate pitch, roll, yaw — **2 marks**
5. Justification of underactuation: 4 inputs < 6 DOF, body tilt needed for translation — **2 marks**

**Cascaded control structure to mention:**
- Outer loop: Position control → desired attitude.
- Inner loop: Attitude control → motor commands.
- Sensor: IMU + state estimator (EKF).

**(b) Inverse Kinematics Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $b = 0.4$ m
- $R = 2$ m (radius of circular path)
- $v = 1$ m/s
- $r = 0.08$ m

**Step 1 — Compute the required angular velocity $\omega$.**
For circular motion, $v = \omega \cdot R$:
$$
\omega = \frac{v}{R} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply inverse kinematics formulas.**
$$
v_L = v - \frac{b}{2}\omega = 1 - \frac{0.4}{2}(0.5) = 1 - 0.1 = 0.9 \text{ m/s}
$$
$$
v_R = v + \frac{b}{2}\omega = 1 + \frac{0.4}{2}(0.5) = 1 + 0.1 = 1.1 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute wheel angular velocities.**
$$
\omega_L = \frac{v_L}{r} = \frac{0.9}{0.08} = 11.25 \text{ rad/s}
$$
$$
\omega_R = \frac{v_R}{r} = \frac{1.1}{0.08} = 13.75 \text{ rad/s}
$$

> ✓ **Final Answer:** $v_L = 0.9$ m/s, $v_R = 1.1$ m/s, $\omega_L = 11.25$ rad/s, $\omega_R = 13.75$ rad/s.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign error in $\omega$:** Students often write $\omega = (v_L - v_R)/b$. The correct KTU convention is $\omega = (v_R - v_L)/b$ (CCW positive). Losing this sign loses **1 mark**.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert $\omega_{\text{wheel}}$ to $v_{\text{wheel}}$:** Wheel velocity = $r \cdot \omega_{\text{wheel}}$, not just $\omega_{\text{wheel}}$. Confusing these loses **1 mark**.
> 3. **Ignoring the ICR location:** The ICR lies on the **slower wheel's side**, not faster. Direction-of-turn logic must be explicit.
> 4. **Skipping the assumption list:** In KTU 2024 Scheme, assumptions (no slip, flat ground, equal wheel radius) carry **1 mark** as a separate line item.
> 5. **Not drawing/block-diagramming the kinematic model:** KTU expects at least a labeled box-diagram of input → Jacobian → output.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Aerial Mobile Robots & Differential Drive Kinematics**

- [x] **Aerial Mobile Robot:** 6-DOF system; quadrotor is the most common type with **4 rotors** and **4 control inputs** → **underactuated**.
- [x] **Quadrotor Motion:** Heave (all rotors ±), Pitch (front-back pair), Roll (left-right pair), Yaw (CW vs CCW pair).
- [x] **Differential Drive Robot (DDR):** Two independently driven wheels, one caster; pose $\xi = [x, y, \theta]^T$.
- [x] **DDR Forward Kinematics:** $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$, $\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$, $\dot{\theta} = \omega$.
- [x] **Linear & Angular Velocity:** $v = (v_L + v_R)/2$ and $\omega = (v_R - v_L)/b$.
- [x] **Inverse Kinematics:** $v_L = v - (b/2)\omega$ and $v_R = v + (b/2)\omega$.
- [x] **Radius of Curvature:** $R = v/\omega = \dfrac{b(v_L + v_R)}{2(v_R - v_L)}$.
- [x] **ICR Location:** On the axle, on the side of the **slower wheel**, at distance $R$ from the center.
- [x] **Special Case 1:** $v_L = v_R \Rightarrow \omega = 0$ → **straight-line motion**.
- [x] **Special Case 2:** $v_L = -v_R \Rightarrow v = 0$ → **zero-radius turn (spin in place)**.
- [x] **Wheel angular velocity ↔ linear velocity:** $v_{\text{wheel}} = r \cdot \omega_{\text{wheel}}$.
- [x] **Non-holonomic Constraint (DDR):** $-\dot{x}\sin\theta + \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ — not integrable.
- [x] **Underactuation:** Both quadrotors and DDRs have more DOF than control inputs.
- [x] **Jacobian of DDR:** $\begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta/2 & \cos\theta/2 \\ \sin\theta/2 & \sin\theta/2 \\ -1/b & 1/b \end{bmatrix}$ — maps $[v_L, v_R]^T \to [\dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{\theta}]^T$.
- [x] **Cascaded Control (Quadrotor):** Position loop (outer) → Attitude loop (inner) → Motor mixing.
- [x] **Real-world platforms:** TurtleBot, iRobot Create (DDR); DJI Phantom, Parrot AR.Drone (Quadrotor).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mobile Robot Workspace - Degree of Freedom
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT302 - INTRODUCTION TO ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Mobile Robot Workspace - Degree of Freedom

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of mobile robotics, the **Workspace** of a mobile robot is defined as the set of all spatial positions (and orientations, for non-point robots) in the 2D Cartesian plane $\mathbb{R}^{2}$ that the robot's reference point can physically reach, given the constraints imposed by its mechanical structure, drive configuration, and the surrounding environment.

The **Degree of Freedom (DOF)** of a mobile robot is the minimum number of independent real-valued coordinates (generalized coordinates) required to completely specify the configuration of the robot at any instant of time. For a rigid body moving in a 2D plane, the theoretical maximum DOF is **3** (two for position $x, y$ and one for orientation $\theta$). In 3D space, the theoretical maximum DOF is **6**.

$$\text{DOF}_{\text{planar, max}} = 3 \quad ; \quad \text{DOF}_{\text{spatial, max}} = 6$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Configuration Space (C-Space):** The manifold of all possible configurations a robot can attain. For a free-floating rigid body in 2D, the C-space is $\mathbb{R}^2 \times \mathbb{S}^1$ (the Special Euclidean group $SE(2)$). The reachable **workspace** is the projection of C-space onto the position coordinates.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are pushing a shopping cart in a supermarket:

- **A standard cart (like a differential-drive robot)** has two rear wheels that can spin independently and a front caster. You can move it forward, backward, and turn, but you **cannot slide it sideways** like a crab. It has 3 DOF in pose $(x, y, \theta)$, but only **2 controllable DOF** (forward velocity and angular velocity) — this is a non-holonomic system.

- **A person on roller skates (like an omni-directional robot)** wearing special "wheely" skates can move forward, backward, sideways, AND rotate on the spot. This has **3 independently controllable DOF** — a holonomic system.

- **A remote-controlled car (Ackermann steering)** can drive forward/backward and turn, but its front and rear wheels must remain parallel to the body. It has 3 pose DOF but only 2 control inputs — also non-holonomic, just like the shopping cart.

> [!NOTE]
> **Holonomic vs Non-Holonomic:** A system is **holonomic** if the number of controllable DOF equals the total DOF of the configuration. It is **non-holonomic** if the controllable DOF is strictly less than the total DOF. This distinction is the heart of mobile robot motion planning.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used

The following standard engineering metrics quantify mobile robot mobility:

- **DOF (Degrees of Freedom):** Dimension of C-space.
- **Mobility ($M$):** Number of independent velocity commands.
- **Steerability ($S$):** Number of independently steered wheels.
- **Maneuverability ($\mu$):** Degree of maneuverability, defined as $\mu = M + S$.
- **Wheels × Ground Contact Degrees:** Standard wheel types (standard, castor, Swedish, spherical) each contribute characteristic DOF and constraints.

**Reference Robot Architectures (KTU 2024 Module 4 highlights):**
