$$M_{\text{wrist}} = 20.01 \, \text{N·m} \quad \text{vs.} \quad \tau_{\max} = 80 \, \text{N·m}$$

Since $20.01 < 80$, **the wrist torque limits are NOT exceeded**.

**Step 5 — Percentage of limit used.**

$$\% \text{ used} = \frac{20.01}{80} \times 100 = 25.02\%$$

The wrist is operating at only **25% of its rated torque capacity**, leaving a 75% safety margin.

**Conclusion:** The wrist torque limits are safe with a comfortable margin. The object can be handled without wrist damage.

---

> **[!WARNING]**
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units:** Many students lose 1–2 marks by writing $g = 9.81$ without converting mm to m before computing torques. **Always convert to SI units first.**
> 2. **Mixing up axes:** $\tau_x$ is caused by force in the Z-direction OR moment arm in the Y-direction, NOT by the X-offset. The cross-product nature of torques confuses many students.
> 3. **Ignoring acceleration loads:** In a real problem, you must compute the **dynamic load** $W_{\text{dyn}} = m(g+a)$ when the robot is accelerating, not just $mg$.
> 4. **Forgetting the safety factor:** In real industrial applications, a 1.5× to 2.0× safety factor is applied to the rated payload. Operating at 100% of rated capacity is considered unsafe.
> 5. **Confusing rated vs maximum payload:** The rated payload is the working limit; the maximum payload is the structural limit (which if exceeded, may cause permanent damage).

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> **[!TIP]**
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Capacity and Speed:**

- **Payload capacity (W)** is the maximum mass a robot can handle, **including the gripper and workpiece**.
- Capacity **decreases as reach increases** due to the lever-arm effect ($\tau = W \cdot L \cdot g$).
- Always apply a **safety factor of 1.5× to 2.0×** to the actual payload when selecting a robot.
- **Wrist torque limit:** $M_{\text{wrist}} = \sqrt{\tau_x^2 + \tau_y^2 + \tau_z^2} \leq \tau_{\max,\text{rated}}$.
- **Dynamic load** during acceleration: $W_{\text{dyn}} = m(g+a)$, which is **greater than** the static load.
- **TCP speed** is computed using the Jacobian: $\mathbf{v}_{\text{TCP}} = J(q) \cdot \dot{\mathbf{q}}$.
- **Speed-torque coupling:** $P = \tau \cdot \omega$ (fixed motor power) — increasing payload reduces achievable speed.
- **Trapezoidal profile times:** $T_a = T_d = v_{\max}/a_{\max}$; $T_c = d/v_{\max} - v_{\max}/a_{\max}$.
- **Triangular profile** occurs when $d \leq v_{\max}^2/a_{\max}$; total time = $2\sqrt{d/a_{\max}}$.
- **S-curve profile** has lower jerk but slightly longer cycle time.
- **Cycle time** $T_{\text{cycle}} = T_{\text{accel}} + T_{\text{const}} + T_{\text{decel}} + T_{\text{pause}}$.
- **Production rate** $R = 3600/T_{\text{cycle}}$ in **parts per hour**.
- **Stopping distance** for safety: $d_{\text{stop}} = v^2/(2a_{\text{emergency}})$.
- The **load-displacement curve** is the primary graphical tool for verifying robot-application compatibility.
- **Joint speeds** are typically 100–200 °/s for major axes and 200–600 °/s for wrist axes.
- **Singularities** in the Jacobian cause loss of DOF and infinite joint speeds — paths must avoid them.
- **Units reminder:** Payload in **kg or N**, torque in **N·m**, speed in **mm/s or °/s**, acceleration in **m/s² or rad/s²**, cycle time in **seconds**, production rate in **parts/hour**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stroke and Reach
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT302 - INTRODUCTION TO ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Stroke and Reach

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reach** is defined as the **maximum radial distance** from the **base coordinate origin (waist axis)** of the robot manipulator to the **end-effector (tool center point / TCP)** when the arm is fully extended along an arbitrary straight-line direction.
>
> **Stroke** is defined as the **total linear range of translation** available along a specific axis (typically a prismatic / linear joint) between its **two mechanical end-stops**, or the **angular range** swept by a revolute joint between its **software/hardware limits**.

* **Reach (R)** — measured in **mm** or **m**. For a serial manipulator of *n* links, the theoretical maximum reach is the sum of all link lengths $L_i$.
* **Stroke (S)** — measured in **mm**, **rad**, or **deg**. It is *per-axis* (not per-robot).
* **Working Reach (Effective Reach)** — the subset of total reach in which the robot can carry **rated payload** at **rated speed** with **rated repeatability**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Arm

Imagine you are standing at a kitchen counter and trying to grab a coffee mug:

* Your **Reach** is the distance from your **shoulder** to your **fingertips** when you fully stretch your arm straight out. No matter how the cup is placed on the counter, if it lies within this radial envelope, your fingers *can* touch it (with correct posture).
* Your **Stroke** is the ability of, say, your **elbow** to bend. The elbow cannot rotate infinitely — it has hard stops (≈ 0° fully extended to ≈ 145° fully bent). This angular sweep is the elbow's **rotational stroke**.

In a robot, **Reach defines the size of the workspace bubble**, while **Stroke defines how much each individual joint can actually move within that bubble**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PCBRT302 – Module 4):**
> Stroke and Reach are classified as *manipulator geometric specifications*. They directly drive **work envelope sizing**, **cell layout**, and **payload-vs-arm-deflection trade-offs** in industrial robotics design.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics (Manufacturer Datasheets)

| Specification | Symbol | Typical Industrial Value |
|---|---|---|
| Maximum Reach | $R_{max}$ | **0.5 m – 3.0 m** |
| Stroke (Prismatic) | $S_p$ | **100 mm – 2000 mm** |
| Stroke (Revolute) | $S_\theta$ | **±90° to ±360°** |
| Effective Reach | $R_{eff}$ | **≈ 90% of $R_{max}$** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Conceptual Decomposition — Step by Step

**STEP 1 — Identify the manipulator's kinematic chain.**
Locate the *base frame* and the *tool center point (TCP)*. The vector between these two is the *radial reach vector* $\vec{R}$.

**STEP 2 — Determine if each joint contributes translation or rotation.**
* **Revolute joint (R):** Adds an angular degree of freedom but does **not** directly extend reach — it reorients the next link.
* **Prismatic joint (P):** Adds a *linear* contribution to reach, equal to its stroke $d_i$.

**STEP 3 — Compute Maximum Reach.**
For a serial manipulator with link lengths $L_1, L_2, \dots, L_n$ and prismatic joint displacements $d_i$:

* The **theoretical maximum reach** is the sum of all rigid link lengths plus the maximum extension of all prismatic joints.
* The **theoretical minimum reach** (folded) is the magnitude of the *vector difference* of all link vectors.

**STEP 4 — Differentiate Reachable vs Dextrous Workspace.**
* **Reachable Workspace:** All points in 3D space that the TCP *can reach with at least one orientation*.
* **Dextrous Workspace:** All points where the TCP *can reach with all possible orientations* — always a subset of the reachable workspace.

**STEP 5 — Account for Joint Limits.**
Real robots have **software limits**, **mechanical hard stops**, and **singularity avoidance zones**. The *operational stroke* is always less than the *mechanical stroke*.

> [!TIP]
> **The "Why" Behind the Formulas:** Reach and Stroke are *geometric limits* — they are *kinematically independent* of payload, speed, or controller. However, *effective* reach shrinks under load because of **joint deflection** and **link compliance**. A 3 kg payload can reduce a 1 m reach by 5–10 mm.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Max Reach (fully extended) | $R_{max} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} L_i + \sum_{j} d_{j,max}$ | m | Sum of rigid links + max prismatic ext. |
| 2 | Min Reach (folded) | $R_{min} = \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert$ (for 2-link planar) | m | Use $\vert \cdot \vert$ for absolute value |
| 3 | Linear Stroke | $S_p = d_{max} - d_{min}$ | m | End-stop to end-stop |
| 4 | Angular Stroke | $S_\theta = \theta_{max} - \theta_{min}$ | rad or deg | Joint sweep range |
| 5 | Reachable Workspace Area (2-link planar) | $A = \pi (R_{max}^2 - R_{min}^2)$ | m² | Annular region |
| 6 | Effective Reach | $R_{eff} = R_{max} - \delta_{defl}$ | m | $\delta_{defl}$ = deflection under load |
| 7 | Workspace Volume (spherical approx.) | $V = \frac{4}{3}\pi R_{max}^3$ | m³ | Overestimates; use for ball joints only |
| 8 | Working Envelope Ratio | $\eta = R_{eff} / R_{max}$ | dimensionless | Typically 0.85 – 0.95 |

> [!NOTE]
> All table entries use $\vert \cdot \vert$ written as the word "absolute value" or rendered via $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ in math mode to avoid breaking markdown pipe syntax.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Cell Layout Design:** Reach defines the size of the **robotic work envelope** — critical for placing conveyors, fixtures, and safety fencing.
* **Painting / Welding Robots:** Need *large reach* but *low payload* — long-reach arms like the **ABB IRB 5500** (reach ≈ 3.2 m).
* **Assembly Robots:** Need *high dextrous workspace* but *small reach* — SCARA arms like **Epson G6** (reach ≈ 0.45 m).
* **Cartesian / Gantry Robots:** Reach is *not* a single value — it is the **bounding box** $(S_x \times S_y \times S_z)$ of three prismatic strokes.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Reach of a 2-Link Planar (RR) Manipulator

**Given:**
* Link 1 of length $L_1$ rotating about the base by angle $\theta_1$.
* Link 2 of length $L_2$ rotating relative to Link 1 by angle $\theta_2$.

**Goal:** Derive the maximum reach $R_{max}$ and minimum reach $R_{min}$.

**Step 1 — Write the Position Vector of TCP.**

Using the **forward kinematics** for a 2-link planar RR arm (in the $x$–$y$ plane):

$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\
y &= L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

These two equations give the Cartesian coordinates of the TCP for any joint configuration.

**Step 2 — Compute the Squared Distance from Base.**

The reach is the radial distance from the origin to the TCP:

$$
r^2 = x^2 + y^2
$$

Substituting the expressions for $x$ and $y$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
r^2 &= \bigl(L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)\bigr)^2 \\
    &\quad + \bigl(L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)\bigr)^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Expand the Squared Terms.**

Expanding the two squared terms:

$$
\begin{aligned}
r^2 &= L_1^2 \cos^2\theta_1 + 2L_1 L_2 \cos\theta_1 \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) + L_2^2 \cos^2(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\
    &\quad + L_1^2 \sin^2\theta_1 + 2L_1 L_2 \sin\theta_1 \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) + L_2^2 \sin^2(\theta_1+\theta_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Pythagorean Identity $\sin^2 + \cos^2 = 1$.**

Grouping the $L_1^2$ and $L_2^2$ terms:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_1^2(\cos^2\theta_1 + \sin^2\theta_1) &= L_1^2 \cdot 1 = L_1^2 \\
L_2^2(\cos^2(\theta_1+\theta_2) + \sin^2(\theta_1+\theta_2)) &= L_2^2 \cdot 1 = L_2^2
\end{aligned}
$$

So the equation simplifies to:

$$
r^2 = L_1^2 + L_2^2 + 2L_1 L_2\bigl[\cos\theta_1 \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) + \sin\theta_1 \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)\bigr]
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Cosine Difference Identity.**

Recall the trigonometric identity:

$$
\cos(A - B) = \cos A \cos B + \sin A \sin B
$$

With $A = \theta_1 + \theta_2$ and $B = \theta_1$, the bracketed term becomes $\cos\bigl((\theta_1+\theta_2) - \theta_1\bigr) = \cos\theta_2$:

$$
r^2 = L_1^2 + L_2^2 + 2L_1 L_2 \cos\theta_2
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the Law of Cosines.**

This is precisely the **Law of Cosines** for a triangle with sides $L_1$, $L_2$, and included angle $\theta_2$ opposite the side $r$. Therefore:

$$
r(\theta_2) = \sqrt{L_1^2 + L_2^2 + 2L_1 L_2 \cos\theta_2}
$$

**Step 7 — Find Maximum Reach.**

$r$ is maximized when $\cos\theta_2 = +1$, i.e., $\theta_2 = 0$ (arm fully extended straight):

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{max} &= \sqrt{L_1^2 + L_2^2 + 2L_1 L_2 \cdot (1)} \\
        &= \sqrt{(L_1 + L_2)^2} \\
        &= L_1 + L_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Find Minimum Reach.**

$r$ is minimized when $\cos\theta_2 = -1$, i.e., $\theta_2 = \pi$ (arm fully folded back):

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{min} &= \sqrt{L_1^2 + L_2^2 + 2L_1 L_2 \cdot (-1)} \\
        &= \sqrt{(L_1 - L_2)^2} \\
        &= \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Summary (2-Link Planar RR Manipulator):**
> * $R_{max} = L_1 + L_2$ — when arm is straight.
> * $R_{min} = \lvert L_1 - L_2 \rvert$ — when arm is fully folded.
> * Workspace is an **annulus** of inner radius $R_{min}$ and outer radius $R_{max}$.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Workspace Area of a 2-Link Planar Manipulator

The reachable workspace is the area of the annulus:

$$
A_{workspace} = \pi \bigl(R_{max}^2 - R_{min}^2\bigr)
$$

Substituting the derived values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{workspace} &= \pi \bigl[(L_1 + L_2)^2 - (L_1 - L_2)^2\bigr] \\
              &= \pi \bigl[(L_1^2 + 2L_1 L_2 + L_2^2) - (L_1^2 - 2L_1 L_2 + L_2^2)\bigr] \\
              &= \pi \cdot 4 L_1 L_2 \\
              &= 4\pi L_1 L_2
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** The 2-link planar workspace area depends only on the **product** $L_1 \cdot L_2$, not on individual lengths. So an arm with $L_1 = 0.3$ m, $L_2 = 0.7$ m has the **same workspace area** as one with $L_1 = L_2 = 0.46$ m (geometric mean). However, the **reach envelope shape differs**.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Prismatic Joint Linear Stroke

**Given:** A linear (prismatic) axis with end-stop positions $p_{min}$ and $p_{max}$ in the joint coordinate frame.

**Definition of Linear Stroke:**

$$
S_p = p_{max} - p_{min}
$$

**Example Calculation:**
For a Cartesian gantry robot with $x$-axis limits $[0, 1500]$ mm and $y$-axis limits $[-500, +500]$ mm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_x &= 1500 - 0 = 1500 \text{ mm} \\
S_y &= 500 - (-500) = 1000 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

The total planar work envelope area is:

$$
A_{xy} = S_x \times S_y = 1500 \times 1000 = 1{,}500{,}000 \text{ mm}^2 = 1.5 \text{ m}^2
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Workspace Envelope — 2-Link Planar RR Manipulator

The following block diagram illustrates the *anatomy of reach* in a 2-link planar RR manipulator and the geometric construction of its annulus workspace:

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Base Origin O] --> B[Link 1 of Length L1]
    B --> C[Joint 2 Elbow Pivot]
    C --> D[Link 2 of Length L2]
    D --> E[Tool Center Point TCP]
    F[Inner Boundary Rmin] --> G[Annulus Region Workspace]
    H[Outer Boundary Rmax] --> G
    G --> I[Reachable Workspace]
    J[Inner Disc D1] --> K[Unreachable Zone 1]
    L[Beyond Rmax] --> M[Unreachable Zone 2]
    A -.-> F
    A -.-> H
    E --> N[Radial Vector r theta2]
    N --> O[Angle theta1 Orientation]
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**
* **Node A** is the base origin.
* **Nodes B, C, D** form the kinematic chain $L_1 \to \text{elbow} \to L_2$.
* **Node E** is the TCP — the point whose radial distance $r$ from A defines the instantaneous reach.
* **Nodes F, H** are the minimum and maximum reach boundaries ($R_{min}$, $R_{max}$).
* **Node G** is the annular region swept by the TCP — the **reachable workspace**.
* **Nodes K, M** are unreachable zones (inside the inner disc and outside the outer disc).

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — Reach & Stroke Specification Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1[Step 1 Identify Kinematic Chain Type RR PR RRP etc] --> S2[Step 2 List All Link Lengths Li]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 List All Prismatic Strokes Sp]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Compute Rmax Sum of Li and Max Sp]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Compute Rmin Geometric Fold]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Determine Workspace Volume or Area]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 Apply Deflection Correction Reff]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8 Generate Datasheet Specification Sheet]
    D1[Manufacturer Datasheet] --> S2
    D2[Payload and Rigidity Data] --> S7
    S8 --> OUT[Final Cell Layout and Fence Design]
```

**Interpretation of the Flow:**
* The left column captures the *inputs* (kinematic structure, link data, payload).
* The central pipeline (**S1 → S8**) is the *algorithmic process* of computing reach and stroke specifications.
* The right column shows *upstream inputs* (manufacturer datasheet, deflection data) feeding into specific pipeline steps.
* The terminal output (**OUT**) is the engineered deliverable — a fully specified cell layout ready for installation.

### 4.3 Joint-Type vs Reach/Stroke Behavior

| Joint Type | Contributes to Reach? | Stroke Specification |
|---|---|---|
| Revolute (R) | Indirectly (via link length $L$) | Angular: $\pm \theta_{max}$ in **deg** or **rad** |
| Prismatic (P) | Directly (adds stroke $S_p$) | Linear: $S_p$ in **mm** or **m** |
| Fixed / Helical | No | N/A |
| Spherical (ball) | Indirectly (3 DOF orientation) | Angular on all 3 axes |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
> **Q1.** Define *Reach* and *Stroke* of a robotic manipulator. State one industrial application where maximum reach is the critical design parameter.
>
> **Model Answer:**
> * **Reach** is the maximum radial distance from the robot's base coordinate origin to the end-effector (TCP) when the manipulator is fully extended.
> * **Stroke** is the total range of motion (linear or angular) of a single joint, bounded by its mechanical end-stops or software limits.
> * **Critical-application example:** *Automotive painting robots* (e.g., painting a car body) require maximum reach to cover large panels without relocating the robot, hence reach dominates over payload in design. (3 marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Q2.** Differentiate between **Reachable Workspace** and **Dextrous Workspace**.
>
> **Model Answer:**
> * The **Reachable Workspace** is the set of all points in 3D space that the TCP can reach with **at least one orientation**.
> * The **Dextrous Workspace** is the subset of reachable workspace where the TCP can be positioned with **all possible orientations** (full 360° wrist rotation).
> * The dextrous workspace is always a **proper subset** of the reachable workspace; for a 2-link planar arm, the dextrous workspace is typically an isolated point at $(L_1, 0)$ when $\theta_2 = 0$. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

> **🟦 QUESTION A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern]**
>
> **(a)** For a 2-link planar RR manipulator with link lengths $L_1 = 0.5$ m and $L_2 = 0.4$ m, derive the expressions for the **maximum reach** and **minimum reach**. Compute the numerical values. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** If a prismatic joint with stroke $S_p = 0.3$ m is added as the third joint (forming an RRP manipulator), how does the maximum reach change? Sketch the new workspace envelope qualitatively. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — 7 Marks Solution:**

*Step 1:* From the derivation in Section 3.1, for a 2-link RR planar manipulator:

$$
R_{max} = L_1 + L_2
$$

*Step 2:* Substitute the given values:

$$
R_{max} = 0.5 + 0.4 = 0.9 \text{ m}
$$

*Step 3:* Minimum reach occurs when the arm is folded ($\theta_2 = 180°$):

$$
R_{min} = \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert = \vert 0.5 - 0.4 \vert = 0.1 \text{ m}
$$

*Step 4:* The workspace is an annulus with:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{workspace} &= \pi (R_{max}^2 - R_{min}^2) \\
              &= \pi (0.9^2 - 0.1^2) \\
              &= \pi (0.81 - 0.01) \\
              &= 0.80\pi \approx 2.513 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer (a):** $R_{max} = 0.9$ m, $R_{min} = 0.1$ m, $A_{workspace} \approx 2.513$ m². (7 marks)

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks Solution:**

*Step 1:* With a prismatic third joint (RRP), the third link $L_3$ extends linearly by stroke $S_p = 0.3$ m.

*Step 2:* The new maximum reach is now the sum of both rigid links **plus** the maximum prismatic extension:

$$
R_{max}^{new} = L_1 + L_2 + S_p = 0.5 + 0.4 + 0.3 = 1.2 \text{ m}
$$

*Step 3:* The new minimum reach (TCP fully retracted, arm folded) is:

$$
R_{min}^{new} = \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert = 0.1 \text{ m}
$$

(The prismatic joint adds zero reach when retracted; only $L_2$ remains in the radial direction when folded.)

*Step 4:* The **new workspace is a thick annular region** with variable outer radius $R_{outer}$ ranging from:

$$
R_{outer,min} = L_1 + L_2 = 0.9 \text{ m} \quad (\text{when } S_p = 0)
$$

$$
R_{outer,max} = L_1 + L_2 + S_p = 1.2 \text{ m} \quad (\text{when } S_p = 0.3)
$$

*Step 5:* Qualitative Sketch — the workspace is a **ring-shaped region** whose *outer boundary* is itself variable depending on prismatic extension.

**Answer (b):** $R_{max}^{new} = 1.2$ m, $R_{min}^{new} = 0.1$ m, workspace becomes a variable-thickness annulus. (7 marks)

---

> **🟩 QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]**
>
> **(a)** Define **Effective Reach** and explain how it differs from the *theoretical maximum reach*. Provide a numerical example. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A Cartesian gantry robot has three prismatic axes with the following limits: $x \in [0, 2.0]$ m, $y \in [-1.0, 1.0]$ m, $z \in [0, 1.5]$ m. Compute:
> * (i) The stroke along each axis. (3 marks)
> * (ii) The total reachable workspace volume. (2 marks)
> * (iii) The maximum radial reach of the TCP from the base origin. (2 marks)

### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — 7 Marks Solution:**

*Step 1:* **Definition of Effective Reach:**
The *effective reach* ($R_{eff}$) is the subset of the theoretical reach within which the manipulator can operate at its *rated payload*, *rated speed*, and *rated repeatability*, *without* violating any mechanical, thermal, or stiffness constraints.

*Step 2:* **Key Difference from $R_{max}$:**
* $R_{max}$ is a purely *geometric* parameter — the sum of link lengths and maximum prismatic extensions.
* $R_{eff}$ is an *engineering* parameter — reduced by link deflection, joint compliance, payload-induced sag, and thermal expansion.

*Step 3:* **Numerical Example:**

A 6-DOF articulated robot has:
* Theoretical maximum reach $R_{max} = 1.5$ m.
* At rated payload of 10 kg, the TCP deflects by $\delta = 0.05$ m.

Then:

$$
R_{eff} = R_{max} - \delta_{defl} = 1.5 - 0.05 = 1.45 \text{ m}
$$

*Step 4:* The **Working Envelope Ratio** is:

$$
\eta = \frac{R_{eff}}{R_{max}} = \frac{1.45}{1.50} \approx 0.967
$$

This ~96.7% ratio is typical for rigid industrial robots.

**Answer (a):** $R_{eff} = 1.45$ m, $\eta = 0.967$. (7 marks)

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks Solution:**

**Sub-part (i) — Stroke per axis (3 marks):**

Using $S = p_{max} - p_{min}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_x &= 2.0 - 0 = 2.0 \text{ m} \\
S_y &= 1.0 - (-1.0) = 2.0 \text{ m} \\
S_z &= 1.5 - 0 = 1.5 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sub-part (ii) — Workspace Volume (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{workspace} &= S_x \times S_y \times S_z \\
               &= 2.0 \times 2.0 \times 1.5 \\
               &= 6.0 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sub-part (iii) — Maximum Radial Reach (2 marks):**

The TCP position at maximum extension is $(x, y, z) = (2.0, \pm 1.0, 1.5)$ m. The radial reach is the Euclidean distance from base origin $(0,0,0)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{max} &= \sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2} \\
        &= \sqrt{2.0^2 + 1.0^2 + 1.5^2} \\
        &= \sqrt{4.00 + 1.00 + 2.25} \\
        &= \sqrt{7.25} \\
        &\approx 2.693 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer (b):** $S_x = 2.0$ m, $S_y = 2.0$ m, $S_z = 1.5$ m, $V = 6.0$ m³, $R_{max} \approx 2.69$ m. (7 marks)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (Read Carefully!):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting Absolute Value in $R_{min}$:** Students often write $R_{min} = L_2 - L_1$ when $L_2 < L_1$, which gives a *negative* number — physically meaningless. Always wrap in $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ or explicitly state "magnitude of the difference."
> 2. **Confusing Reach with Workspace Diameter:** A manipulator with $R_{max} = 1$ m does **NOT** have a workspace diameter of 2 m — because the *minimum* reach is rarely zero. The diameter is $2 \cdot R_{max}$, but the *filled annular width* is $R_{max} - R_{min}$.
> 3. **Mixing up Stroke with Reach:** Stroke is *per-axis*; Reach is *end-to-end*. Students commonly write "$S_x = 2.0$ m reach" — this loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Skipping Units in Final Answer:** KTU examiners *will* deduct **½ mark** for missing SI units (m, rad, mm, deg).
> 5. **Forgetting to Square and Square-Root:** In the reach formula derivation, students often write $r = L_1^2 + L_2^2 + 2L_1 L_2 \cos\theta_2$ without taking the square root — losing 1 mark.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

* ✅ **Reach ($R$):** Maximum radial distance from base to TCP — *end-to-end geometric* metric.
* ✅ **Stroke ($S$):** Per-axis range of motion — *linear* $S_p$ or *angular* $S_\theta$.
* ✅ **2-Link RR Planar:** $R_{max} = L_1 + L_2$, $R_{min} = \lvert L_1 - L_2 \rvert$, workspace = annulus.
* ✅ **Law of Cosines Connection:** The reach expression $r = \sqrt{L_1^2 + L_2^2 + 2L_1 L_2 \cos\theta_2}$ is *exactly* the Law of Cosines for a triangle.
* ✅ **Effective Reach:** $R_{eff} = R_{max} - \delta_{defl}$ — accounts for payload sag and link compliance.
* ✅ **Reachable vs Dextrous Workspace:** Reachable ≥ Dextrous; the dextrous set is a *subset*.
* ✅ **Cartesian (Gantry) Reach:** Bounding-box volume $V = S_x \cdot S_y \cdot S_z$, *not* spherical.
* ✅ **Industrial Rule of Thumb:** $\eta = R_{eff} / R_{max} \approx 0.85 - 0.95$ for modern robots.
* ✅ **Units:** Always report reach in **m**, stroke in **mm** (linear) or **rad** (angular) for full KTU marks.
* ✅ **Visualization Skill:** Be ready to sketch the annular workspace and label $R_{max}$, $R_{min}$, $L_1$, $L_2$, and the TCP — examiners award up to 2 marks for a clean, correctly-labeled diagram.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Repeatability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT302 - INTRODUCTION TO ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Repeatability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU / ISO 9283 Terminology)

**Repeatability** of a manipulator is a measure of the ability of the robot's end-effector to return to a previously taught or commanded pose. More precisely, as per **ISO 9283** (the standard governing industrial robot performance criteria, adopted as the KTU-recommended reference), repeatability is defined as:

> *The closeness of agreement between the positions/orientations achieved by the robot's end-effector for repeated executions of a programmed motion to the same commanded pose, under the same conditions of load, speed, path, and ambient environment.*

Mathematically, it is the dispersion of these reached points, expressed in **millimetres (mm)** as a statistical radius around the mean position — typically **three standard deviations ($3\sigma$)** of the cluster of measured positions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Repeatability is *not* the same as **accuracy**. Accuracy is the deviation from the *commanded* (target) position, while repeatability is the deviation from the *mean* of the actually achieved positions. A robot can be highly repeatable but only moderately accurate.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are blindfolded and asked to repeatedly touch the tip of your nose with your right index finger. You will probably miss the actual tip by some distance each time (your **accuracy** is poor), but your finger will land in a small, tight cluster around one spot (your **repeatability** is excellent).

A second person might be a sharpshooter who can usually hit the bullseye (high accuracy), but if asked to fire 30 shots at the *same* point, the bullets scatter widely (low repeatability).

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Implication:** In most modern manufacturing cells (welding, pick-and-place, PCB assembly), the *commanded* pose is taught by physically jogging the robot to a target and recording that location. Therefore, what matters is that the robot returns to the **taught** pose — i.e., **repeatability dominates accuracy** in real production.

## 1.3 Standard Metric Highlighted

The accepted numerical value reported in every robot datasheet (FANUC, ABB, KUKA, Yaskawa) is:

$$R_{3\sigma} = \pm 3 \sigma \quad [\text{mm}]$$

where $\sigma$ is the standard deviation of the Euclidean position errors at the tool centre point (TCP) for $N$ repeated runs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $3\sigma$?** In a normal (Gaussian) distribution, $3\sigma$ covers **99.73%** of all outcomes — i.e., the robot will land within this band 9973 times out of 10,000 cycles. This is the industry-accepted confidence interval.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Repeatability Test — Step-by-Step Logic

The ISO 9283 protocol for measuring pose repeatability is performed as follows:

1. **Teach a Pose:** Command the robot to move to a specific pose $P_0$ and record the actual TCP position $\mathbf{p}_i$ for run $i$.
2. **Repeat the Cycle:** Execute the same programmed motion $N$ times (typically $N = 30$ or $N = 50$). On every cycle, the robot must return to the *same* taught pose under identical conditions.
3. **Measure Reached Points:** For each run $i$, record the Cartesian coordinates of the TCP as $\mathbf{p}_i = [x_i, y_i, z_i]^T$.
4. **Compute the Cluster Centre:** Calculate the mean position $\bar{\mathbf{p}}$.
5. **Compute Dispersion:** For each run, find the Euclidean distance $d_i = \vert \mathbf{p}_i - \bar{\mathbf{p}} \vert$.
6. **Report the Statistic:** The repeatability is reported as $\pm 3\sigma$, where $\sigma$ is the standard deviation of the $d_i$ values (or, equivalently, the radius of the smallest sphere that encloses 99.73% of the points).

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Step 1 (Teach):** Industrial robots do not know absolute world coordinates with high precision. They learn positions by "recording" joint angles, so the *taught* pose is the reference.
- **Step 2 (Repeat):** Random errors (backlash, thermal drift, encoder quantization) accumulate differently each cycle; we need statistics, not a single shot.
- **Step 4 (Mean):** The true "intended" return point is the centroid of the cluster, since no single trial is the "correct" one.
- **Step 5 (Euclidean Distance):** The TCP is a 3-D point, so 1-D statistics on a single axis are misleading; the radial spread is what matters physically.
- **Step 6 ($3\sigma$):** Single-point distances can be noisy; standard deviation normalizes over the sample.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Mean position vector | $\bar{\mathbf{p}} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\mathbf{p}_i$ | mm | Centroid of cluster |
| 2 | Position error vector | $\mathbf{e}_i = \mathbf{p}_i - \bar{\mathbf{p}}$ | mm | Per-cycle deviation |
| 3 | Scalar radial error | $d_i = \sqrt{e_{ix}^2 + e_{iy}^2 + e_{iz}^2}$ | mm | Always $\geq 0$ |
| 4 | Standard deviation | $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(d_i - \bar{d})^2}$ | mm | Bessel-corrected |
| 5 | Repeatability index | $R = \pm 3\sigma$ | mm | Reported value |
| 6 | Component-wise repeatability | $R_x = \pm 3\sigma_x, \; R_y = \pm 3\sigma_z$ | mm | Axis-resolved |
| 7 | Bidirectional error (play) | $\Delta = \vert p_{\rightarrow} - p_{\leftarrow}\vert$ | mm | Tests backlash only |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Repeatability is the single most quoted specification in industrial-robot procurement. SCARA and delta robots achieve $R = \pm 0.02$ mm, while large painting robots may have $R = \pm 1$ to $\pm 3$ mm. The choice depends on the **tolerance band** of the manufactured part.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Welding Cells:** Weld seam tolerance of $\pm 0.3$ mm requires $R \leq 0.2$ mm.
- **PCB Assembly:** 0402 chip components need $R \leq 0.05$ mm.
- **Palletizing:** $R \leq 5$ mm is acceptable.
- **Surgical Robotics:** Sub-millimetre ($R \leq 0.1$ mm) repeatability is mandatory.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: From Raw Measurements to Repeatability Index

**Problem Setup:** A 6-axis robot is commanded to return to a taught pose 30 times. The Cartesian coordinates of the TCP for each run $i$ are recorded as $(x_i, y_i, z_i)$ in millimetres. Compute the repeatability index $R = \pm 3\sigma$.

---

### Step 1 — Record the Position Set

The $N=30$ measurements form a dataset:

$$\mathcal{P} = \{ (x_i, y_i, z_i) \mid i = 1, 2, \ldots, 30 \}$$

---

### Step 2 — Compute the Centroid (Mean Position)

The mean position vector is the arithmetic average of each Cartesian coordinate. We take the average **independently** for each axis:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{x} &= \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i = \frac{1}{30}\sum_{i=1}^{30} x_i \\[6pt]
\bar{y} &= \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i \\[6pt]
\bar{z} &= \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} z_i
\end{aligned}
$$

So the centroid is:

$$\bar{\mathbf{p}} = [\bar{x},\, \bar{y},\, \bar{z}]^T$$

**Why axis-wise averaging?** It yields the unique point that minimizes the sum of squared Euclidean distances to all members of the cluster — i.e., the geometric centre of the point cloud.

---

### Step 3 — Compute Per-Cycle Error Vectors

For each run $i$, the deviation of the actual reached point from the centroid is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
e_{ix} &= x_i - \bar{x} \\
e_{iy} &= y_i - \bar{y} \\
e_{iz} &= z_i - \bar{z}
\end{aligned}
$$

These are the components of the error vector $\mathbf{e}_i = \mathbf{p}_i - \bar{\mathbf{p}}$.

---

### Step 4 — Convert 3-D Errors to Scalar Distances

The scalar radial distance of run $i$ from the centroid is the Euclidean norm of the error vector:

$$d_i = \sqrt{(x_i - \bar{x})^2 + (y_i - \bar{y})^2 + (z_i - \bar{z})^2}$$

This is the **distance from the centroid**, not from the commanded pose. Note: $d_i \geq 0$ for all $i$.

---

### Step 5 — Compute the Mean Radial Error

$$\bar{d} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} d_i$$

This is the average spread of the cluster; it is **not** the repeatability, but it is a useful diagnostic.

---

### Step 6 — Compute the Standard Deviation $\sigma$

The standard deviation measures the dispersion of the radial distances around their own mean:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(d_i - \bar{d})^2} \\[6pt]
       &= \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1}\left[\sum_{i=1}^{N} d_i^2 - N\bar{d}^2\right]}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why $N-1$ (Bessel's correction)?** We are estimating the true population variance from a *sample*. Using $N$ in the denominator biases the estimate downward; $N-1$ provides an unbiased estimator.

---

### Step 7 — Report the Repeatability Index

Finally, the reported repeatability of the robot at this pose is:

$$R = \pm 3\sigma \quad [\text{mm}]$$

**Physical Interpretation:** If you command the robot to the same pose many times, 99.73% of the reached points will lie inside a sphere of radius $3\sigma$ centred at $\bar{\mathbf{p}}$.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Sources of Repeatability Error

| Source | Mechanism | Effect on $\sigma$ | Mitigation |
|--------|-----------|-------------------|-----------|
| **Joint backlash** | Gears have play; direction reversal causes dead zone | Largest contributor in geared joints | Use harmonic drives, pre-loaded gears |
| **Encoder quantization** | Resolver/encoder has finite resolution ($\pm 1$ LSB) | Discrete jumps in reported angle | Higher-resolution encoders |
| **Mechanical compliance** | Links, gears, cables flex under load | Position drifts as payload changes | Stiffer structures, pre-tensioned cables |
| **Thermal expansion** | Motors heat up, lengthen links | Slow drift during continuous operation | Thermal compensation algorithms |
| **Friction (static)** | Stiction prevents micro-motion | Holding position noisy | Closed-loop control, friction compensation |
| **Controller resolution** | DAC/command quantization | Set-point granularity limits | Higher bit-depth controllers |
| **Cable/hose routing** | Catena movement varies with pose | Pose-dependent errors | Careful path planning, cable management |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $3\sigma$ and not $\sigma$ alone?** Reporting $\sigma$ alone only covers 68% of cases. For a production line running 100,000 cycles/day, a $1\sigma$ spec would mean 32,000 defects — economically unacceptable. $3\sigma$ is the industrial sweet spot balancing precision and yield.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Given:** For a SCARA robot commanded to the same point 5 times, the TCP readings are:

| Run $i$ | $x_i$ (mm) | $y_i$ (mm) | $z_i$ (mm) |
|--------|------------|------------|------------|
| 1 | 500.10 | 300.20 | 100.05 |
| 2 | 500.15 | 300.18 | 100.10 |
| 3 | 500.08 | 300.22 | 100.02 |
| 4 | 500.12 | 300.19 | 100.08 |
| 5 | 500.09 | 300.21 | 100.04 |

**Step 1 — Centroid:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{x} &= \frac{500.10 + 500.15 + 500.08 + 500.12 + 500.09}{5} = \frac{2500.54}{5} = 500.108 \\[6pt]
\bar{y} &= \frac{300.20 + 300.18 + 300.22 + 300.19 + 300.21}{5} = \frac{1501.00}{5} = 300.200 \\[6pt]
\bar{z} &= \frac{100.05 + 100.10 + 100.02 + 100.08 + 100.04}{5} = \frac{500.29}{5} = 100.058
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Per-Run Distances $d_i$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_1 &= \sqrt{(0.008)^2 + (0.000)^2 + (0.008)^2} = 0.0113 \text{ mm} \\
d_2 &= \sqrt{(0.042)^2 + (0.020)^2 + (0.042)^2} = 0.0616 \text{ mm} \\
d_3 &= \sqrt{(0.028)^2 + (0.020)^2 + (0.038)^2} = 0.0510 \text{ mm} \\
d_4 &= \sqrt{(0.012)^2 + (0.010)^2 + (0.022)^2} = 0.0268 \text{ mm} \\
d_5 &= \sqrt{(0.018)^2 + (0.010)^2 + (0.018)^2} = 0.0270 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Mean Distance:**

$$\bar{d} = \frac{0.0113 + 0.0616 + 0.0510 + 0.0268 + 0.0270}{5} = \frac{0.1777}{5} = 0.0355 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Standard Deviation $\sigma$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum (d_i - \bar{d})^2 &= (-0.0242)^2 + (0.0261)^2 + (0.0155)^2 + (-0.0087)^2 + (-0.0085)^2 \\
&= 0.000586 + 0.000681 + 0.000240 + 0.0000757 + 0.0000723 \\
&= 0.001655
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{0.001655}{5-1}} = \sqrt{0.000414} = 0.0203 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Repeatability:**

$$R = \pm 3 \times 0.0203 = \pm 0.061 \text{ mm}$$

**Conclusion:** The SCARA robot has a repeatability of **$\pm 0.061$ mm**, which is typical for high-precision SCARAs and suitable for electronics assembly.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: ISO 9283 Repeatability Test Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Teach Target Pose P0] --> B[Command Robot to Return to P0]
    B --> C[Record TCP Position pi for Run i]
    C --> D{All N Runs Done?}
    D -- No --> B
    D -- Yes --> E[Compute Centroid p_bar]
    E --> F[Compute Per-Run Error Vectors e_i]
    F --> G[Compute Scalar Radial Distances d_i]
    G --> H[Compute Standard Deviation sigma]
    H --> I[Compute Repeatability Index R = ±3 sigma]
    I --> J[Report R in mm and Plot Error Sphere]
    J --> K[End of Test]

    style A fill:#1f77b4,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#ff7f0e,color:#ffffff
    style H fill:#2ca02c,color:#ffffff
    style I fill:#d62728,color:#ffffff
    style K fill:#9467bd,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Sources of Repeatability Error

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Commanded Pose]
        P0[Target Position P0]
    end

    subgraph ACTUATORS[Actuator and Transmission]
        J1[Backlash in Gears]
        J2[Encoder Quantization]
        J3[Cable Compliance]
    end

    subgraph LINK[Linkage and Environment]
        L1[Thermal Expansion]
        L2[Link Deflection]
        L3[Static Friction]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Achieved Positions]
        R1[Reached Position p1]
        R2[Reached Position p2]
        Rn[Reached Position pN]
    end

    P0 --> J1
    P0 --> J2
    P0 --> J3
    J1 --> L1
    J2 --> L2
    J3 --> L3
    L1 --> R1
    L2 --> R2
    L3 --> Rn

    R1 --> CENT[Centroid p_bar]
    R2 --> CENT
    Rn --> CENT
    CENT --> STATS[Repeatability R = ±3 sigma]
    STATS --> RESULT[Reported Specification]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Conceptual Map: Repeatability vs Accuracy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CLUSTER[Cluster of N Reached Points]
        T1[Point 1]
        T2[Point 2]
        T3[Point 3]
        T4[Point 4]
        T5[Point 5]
    end

    CENT[Centroid p_bar]:::centerNode
    CMD[Commanded Pose]:::targetNode
    R[R = ±3 sigma sphere]:::repNode
    A[Accuracy = |p_bar - Command|]:::accNode

    T1 --> CENT
    T2 --> CENT
    T3 --> CENT
    T4 --> CENT
    T5 --> CENT
    CENT -.->|defines| R
    CMD -->|deviation is| A

```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to Read the Diagrams:**
> - The first flowchart shows the **measurement procedure** mandated by ISO 9283.
> - The second block diagram shows the **physical sources** of variability that the test quantifies.
> - The third map shows the **distinction** between repeatability (spread around centroid) and accuracy (distance from centroid to commanded pose).
