# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Definition Question `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Define **repeatability** of an industrial robot. How is it different from **accuracy**?

**Model Answer:**

**Repeatability** is the ability of a robot's end-effector to return to a previously taught pose when the same motion is repeatedly executed under identical conditions of load, speed, path, and environment. It is a measure of the *dispersion* of the reached positions and is quantified statistically as the **$\pm 3\sigma$ radius** of the cluster of measured points around their mean, expressed in **mm**.

| Aspect | Repeatability | Accuracy |
|--------|---------------|----------|
| Definition | Spread of reached points around their mean | Distance from the mean to the commanded pose |
| Typical value | $\pm 0.02$ to $\pm 0.5$ mm | $\pm 0.1$ to $\pm 10$ mm |
| Depends on | Random errors (backlash, friction) | Calibration, kinematic model errors |
| Improvement | Better mechanics, encoders | Better calibration, absolute encoders |

**(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for repeatability aspect, 1 for contrast table)**

---

### Question 2: Short-Answer `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** List any **four sources of error** that affect the repeatability of a robot manipulator.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Joint backlash** in gear trains (especially harmonic drives under reversal).
2. **Encoder quantization error** due to finite angular resolution.
3. **Mechanical compliance** of links, cables, and harmonic drive flexsplines under varying payload.
4. **Thermal expansion** of links and motor housings during continuous duty.

(Mentioning any four valid sources with a one-line description each is sufficient for 3 marks.)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A — Option 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **ISO 9283 test procedure** for measuring the pose repeatability of an industrial robot. Include the formulae for the centroid, radial error, and the repeatability index.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 6-DOF robot is commanded to return to a taught pose **50 times**. The mean position of the TCP is found to be $\bar{\mathbf{p}} = [500.20,\, 300.15,\, 100.10]^T$ mm. The standard deviations along the $x$, $y$, and $z$ axes are $\sigma_x = 0.015$ mm, $\sigma_y = 0.020$ mm, and $\sigma_z = 0.010$ mm. Compute:
  (i) the component-wise repeatability indices,
  (ii) the combined 3-D repeatability index assuming the errors are statistically independent.

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

The **ISO 9283 Pose Repeatability Test** is conducted as follows:

1. **Setup:** Mount the robot on a stable foundation; install a calibrated measurement system (e.g., laser tracker, coordinate measuring machine) to record the TCP position in Cartesian coordinates.

2. **Teach a Pose:** Manually jog the robot to a target pose $P_0$ within its workspace (typically 5 poses, near each vertex and the centre of the working volume). Record the joint values.

3. **Run the Cycle $N$ Times:** Repeat the taught motion $N = 30$ to $N = 50$ times. Each cycle must use the same speed (typically rated maximum), the same load (typically rated maximum), and the same ambient temperature.

4. **Record the Reached Points:** After each cycle, record the actual TCP position $\mathbf{p}_i = [x_i, y_i, z_i]^T$.

5. **Compute the Centroid:**

$$\bar{\mathbf{p}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\mathbf{p}_i = [\bar{x},\, \bar{y},\, \bar{z}]^T$$

6. **Compute the Radial Error per Run:**

$$d_i = \sqrt{(x_i - \bar{x})^2 + (y_i - \bar{y})^2 + (z_i - \bar{z})^2}$$

7. **Compute the Standard Deviation:**

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(d_i - \bar{d})^2}$$

8. **Report the Pose Repeatability Index:**

$$R = \pm 3\sigma \quad [\text{mm}]$$

The robot is deemed compliant with its rated repeatability only if $R \leq R_{\text{rated}}$ for **all** test poses and **all** tested speeds/loads.

**(7 marks — 2 for test setup, 3 for procedure steps, 2 for formulae with units)**

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**(i) Component-wise Repeatability:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_x &= \pm 3 \sigma_x = \pm 3 \times 0.015 = \pm 0.045 \text{ mm} \\
R_y &= \pm 3 \sigma_y = \pm 3 \times 0.020 = \pm 0.060 \text{ mm} \\
R_z &= \pm 3 \sigma_z = \pm 3 \times 0.010 = \pm 0.030 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Combined 3-D Repeatability (Radial $3\sigma$):**

For statistically independent Gaussian errors along $x$, $y$, $z$, the variance of the radial distance is the sum of the individual variances:

$$\sigma_r^2 = \sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 + \sigma_z^2$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_r^2 &= (0.015)^2 + (0.020)^2 + (0.010)^2 \\
           &= 0.000225 + 0.000400 + 0.000100 \\
           &= 0.000725 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\sigma_r = \sqrt{0.000725} = 0.02693 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{R_{3D} = \pm 3 \sigma_r = \pm 0.0808 \text{ mm} \approx \pm 0.081 \text{ mm}}$$

**(7 marks — 3 for (i), 4 for (ii) including the variance-additivity formula)**

---

### Question B — Option 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a **neat sketch**, explain how **backlash** and **encoder quantization** contribute to the **loss of repeatability** in a geared robotic joint. Mention one mitigation for each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **practical importance of repeatability** versus accuracy in three industrial applications of your choice. Justify the required repeatability values for each.

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Backlash Contribution:**

When a geared joint reverses direction, the driving gear must first traverse the *backlash* — the small clearance between meshing teeth — before re-engaging. During this dead zone, the output link does not move even though the input (motor) has rotated. The result is a *hysteresis loop*: the joint position is path-dependent.

- For example, if backlash $b = 0.1°$ at the joint, then at a 1 m reach, the TCP position can differ by:
$$ \Delta_{\text{TCP}} = L \times b = 1000 \text{ mm} \times \frac{0.1 \times \pi}{180} \approx 1.75 \text{ mm} $$

  between a clockwise and counter-clockwise approach to the same commanded angle.
- **Mitigation:** Use **harmonic drive** reducers (near-zero backlash) or apply **pre-loaded anti-backlash gear pairs**.

**Encoder Quantization Contribution:**

An incremental encoder with $n$ lines per revolution has a resolution of:

$$\Delta\theta_{\text{enc}} = \frac{2\pi}{4n} \text{ radians} \quad (\times 4 \text{ for quadrature decoding})$$

The joint controller cannot sense angular displacements smaller than $\Delta\theta_{\text{enc}}$. Each measured joint angle has an error of $\pm \Delta\theta_{\text{enc}}/2$. This error propagates to the TCP via the Jacobian.

- For a 12-bit encoder ($n = 4096$ lines, $\times 4$ quadrature = 16,384 counts/rev):
$$ \Delta\theta_{\text{enc}} = \frac{2\pi}{16384} \approx 0.000383 \text{ rad} \approx 0.022° $$

- **Mitigation:** Use **higher-resolution encoders** (≥ 20-bit absolute) and interpolate the analog sine/cosine signals between zero-crossings.

**Combined Effect:** Both errors are *random* from cycle to cycle, so they inflate $\sigma$ and therefore degrade $R = \pm 3\sigma$.

**(7 marks — 2 for backlash explanation + mitigation, 2 for encoder explanation + mitigation, 1 for numerical example, 2 for combined effect)**

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

| # | Application | Required Repeatability | Why Repeatability Dominates |
|---|------------|------------------------|------------------------------|
| 1 | **PCB Pick-and-Place** | $\pm 0.02$ mm | Component pads are taught by vision; absolute accuracy of the robot is irrelevant. What matters is that the same taught point is hit every cycle to avoid tombstoning 0402 chips. |
| 2 | **Spot Welding (Automotive)** | $\pm 0.5$ mm | Weld coordinates are taught by lead-through teaching on the actual car body. The robot just needs to return to the *taught* weld gun pose; calibration accuracy of the kinematic model is secondary. |
| 3 | **Palletizing (Box Stacking)** | $\pm 5$ mm | Box dimensions dominate; absolute accuracy of "where the centre of the world is" is irrelevant. The robot must drop the box in the same taught cell of the pallet each cycle. |

**Justification Summary:**

- In *all three* applications, the target pose is **taught**, not commanded in absolute world coordinates.
- Hence, **calibration accuracy** is a one-time concern; **repeatability** is a *per-cycle* concern that determines yield.
- The required $R$ scales with the **smallest geometric feature** the robot must interact with (chip pad, weld nugget, box corner).

**(7 marks — 2 marks per application × 3 = 6, plus 1 mark for the concluding justification paragraph)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Losing Mistakes (Examiner's Perspective):**
> 1. **Confusing repeatability with accuracy** in a definition question — KTU examiners **deduct 1 full mark** for this error.
> 2. **Forgetting the $3\sigma$ factor** — quoting $\sigma$ alone is **wrong**; the standard report is $\pm 3\sigma$. Marks lost: 1.
> 3. **Wrong units** — repeatability is in **mm**, not cm or m. Marks lost: 0.5.
> 4. **Using $N$ instead of $N-1$** in the variance formula — this is a *unbiased estimator* requirement; using $N$ gives a biased estimate. Marks lost: 1.
> 5. **In numerical problems, not stating the assumption** of statistical independence when summing variances — Marks lost: 1.
> 6. **Confusing pose repeatability with path repeatability** — pose is *point-to-point*; path is *trajectory-tracking*. KTU treats them as separate metrics. Marks lost: 1.
> 7. **Forgetting to convert degrees to radians** in backlash calculations. Marks lost: 0.5–1 depending on the step.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Repeatability = dispersion ($\pm 3\sigma$) of the reached points around their *mean*, **not** around the commanded pose.
- **Standard:** **ISO 9283** governs the measurement protocol; KTU questions are typically framed around it.
- **Reported Metric:** Always $\pm 3\sigma$ in **mm**; covers 99.73% of cycles.
- **Centroid Formula:** $\bar{\mathbf{p}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\mathbf{p}_i$, computed axis-wise.
- **Radial Error:** $d_i = \sqrt{(x_i-\bar{x})^2 + (y_i-\bar{y})^2 + (z_i-\bar{z})^2}$.
- **Standard Deviation:** Use Bessel-corrected $N-1$ in the denominator.
- **Combined 3-D Repeatability:** $\sigma_r = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 + \sigma_z^2}$ (valid only if errors are *statistically independent*).
- **Backlash Amplification at TCP:** $\Delta_{\text{TCP}} = L \cdot b$, where $L$ is the link length and $b$ is the joint backlash in radians. Larger reach = worse repeatability for the same joint error.
- **Encoder Resolution:** $\Delta\theta_{\text{enc}} = \frac{2\pi}{4n}$ (with quadrature). Higher $n$ → better resolution → better repeatability.
- **Industrial Reality:** Repeatability > Accuracy in teach-pendant programming; the opposite is true in *offline programming* where absolute world coordinates are pre-computed.
- **Typical Values:** SCARA / Delta ≈ $\pm 0.02$–$0.05$ mm; Articulated 6-axis ≈ $\pm 0.05$–$0.5$ mm; Large painting arms ≈ $\pm 1$–$3$ mm.
- **Error Sources (Top 4):** Backlash > Encoder quantization > Compliance > Thermal drift.
- **Key Distinction:** Pose repeatability (point-to-point) ≠ Path repeatability (trajectory tracking).
- **Statistical Caveat:** $3\sigma$ assumes a Gaussian error distribution; skewed or multi-modal error patterns require robust statistics (e.g., MAD-based).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Precision and Accuracy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT302 - INTRODUCTION TO ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Precision and Accuracy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Industrial Robotics** and **Manipulator Metrology** (as per the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PCBRT302, Module 4), **Precision** and **Accuracy** are two distinct, non-interchangeable performance metrics used to quantify how closely a robotic system can achieve a commanded pose (position and orientation) in its workspace.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Official KTU Definition Block:**
> - **Accuracy:** The ability of a robot to position its end-effector at a *targeted* or *commanded* location in space. It is defined as the *maximum* distance (or angular deviation) between the **commanded pose** and the **mean of the actual achieved poses** over many repeated trials, measured in **millimeters (mm)** or **degrees (°)**.
> - **Precision (Repeatability):** The ability of a robot to return to a *previously taught* position. It is defined as the *closeness of agreement* among several **actual achieved poses** for the *same commanded pose*, typically expressed as a statistical bound (e.g., ±3 standard deviations).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bullseye & Dartboard" Intuition

Imagine you are throwing darts at a dartboard while blindfolded.

| Concept | Dart Analogy | What it Measures |
|---|---|---|
| **Accuracy** | The darts cluster tightly *around the center bullseye*. | How close you get to the *intended* target. |
| **Precision** | The darts cluster tightly *together*, even if they miss the center. | How close repeated attempts are to *each other*. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway for Students:**
> A robot can be **Precise but Not Accurate** (hits the same wrong spot every time → *systematic bias*).
> A robot can be **Accurate but Not Precise** (hits near the target on average, but scattered → *random noise*).
> An ideal robotic manipulator should be **both accurate and precise** (clustered exactly on target).

## 1.3 The Third Metric: Resolution

While precision and accuracy describe *performance*, **Resolution** describes the *capability* of the system — the smallest incremental change in position/orientation that the robot controller can command and sense.

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of Thumb Hierarchy:** **Resolution ≤ Precision ≤ Accuracy**
> The system cannot be more precise than its smallest measurable step (resolution), and it cannot be accurate if it is not first precise.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standardized performance metrics must be memorized for KTU examinations:

- **Position Repeatability (RP):** Measured in **mm**, typically quoted as ± value.
- **Pose Accuracy (AP):** Distance from commanded to mean-actual pose in **mm**.
- **Multi-directional Repeatability:** The radius of a sphere containing the cluster of actual poses.
- **ISO 9283 Standard:** The international benchmark governing robot performance testing, defining *accuracy*, *repeatability*, *swelling*, *drift*, and *overshoot*.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How Precision and Accuracy are Measured

Robotic performance testing follows a strict experimental protocol under **ISO 9283**:

1. **Command a Target Pose:** The robot is commanded to move to a specific Cartesian coordinate $(X_c, Y_c, Z_c, \theta_{xc}, \theta_{yc}, \theta_{zc})$.
2. **Execute $n$ Repetitions:** The robot performs the same motion $n$ times (typically **$n = 30$** or **$n = 50$** cycles).
3. **Record Actual Poses:** For each cycle $i$, an external metrology device (laser tracker, coordinate measuring machine) records the achieved pose $(X_i, Y_i, Z_i)$.
4. **Compute the Mean Pose (Centroid):** The arithmetic mean of all achieved positions is calculated.
5. **Compute Statistical Deviations:** The deviation of each trial from the mean is calculated, then aggregated into the standard performance metrics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters in Real Robotics:**
> Accuracy is degraded by **systematic errors** (kinematic parameter errors like link length errors $\Delta a_i$, joint offset errors $\Delta d_i$, encoder calibration drift). Precision is degraded by **random errors** (backlash in gears, thermal expansion, servo jitter, sensor noise).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula / Definition | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $AP_p$ | Pose Accuracy (Position) | $AP_p = \sqrt{(\bar{x} - x_c)^2 + (\bar{y} - y_c)^2 + (\bar{z} - z_c)^2}$ | **mm** |
| $RP_l$ | Position Repeatability (Linear) | $RP_l = \bar{l} + 3\sigma_l$ | **mm** |
| $\sigma_l$ | Standard Deviation (Linear) | $\sigma_l = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(l_i - \bar{l})^2}$ | **mm** |
| $\bar{l}$ | Mean Position Deviation | $\bar{l} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\sqrt{(x_i-\bar{x})^2 + (y_i-\bar{y})^2 + (z_i-\bar{z})^2}$ | **mm** |
| $\Delta\theta_i$ | Angular Deviation (joint $i$) | $\Delta\theta_i = \theta_{i,actual} - \theta_{i,commanded}$ | **radians** or **°** |
| $DR$ | Drift | Change in mean pose over a defined time interval | **mm/hr** |
| $OS$ | Overshoot | Maximum excursion beyond the target pose during approach | **mm** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Notation Rule for Exams:** When writing repeatability in your answer script, never use a generic "±" without defining what statistical bound it represents. The KTU board examiner expects **$\pm 3\sigma$** explicitly.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility of Precision/Accuracy Analysis

- **Off-Line Programming (OLP) Validation:** Before deploying a robot program generated in simulation, kinematic calibration improves accuracy to within the precision envelope.
- **Quality Control in Manufacturing:** A welding robot for automotive bodies must have repeatability $\leq 0.1$ mm, while a pick-and-place robot may tolerate $\pm 1$ mm.
- **Surgical Robotics:** Accuracy requirements tighten to sub-millimeter levels ($\leq 0.05$ mm), demanding calibration against pre-operative imaging.
- **Cost Engineering:** High-accuracy robots (Harmonic Drive + absolute encoders) cost 5–10× more than high-repeatability, low-accuracy SCARA arms.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Position Repeatability ($RP_l$) from First Principles

The position repeatability is *defined* as the radius of a sphere that encloses the cluster of actual end-effector positions, with a statistical confidence of $99.74\%$ (i.e., the $\pm 3\sigma$ bound assuming Gaussian-distributed errors).

### Step 1: Define the actual achieved position vector

For trial $i$ (where $i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$), let the actual measured position be:

$$
P_i = \begin{bmatrix} x_i \\ y_i \\ z_i \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 2: Compute the mean (centroid) of the cluster

The arithmetic mean position vector over all $n$ trials is:

$$
\bar{P} = \begin{bmatrix} \bar{x} \\ \bar{y} \\ \bar{z} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \\[6pt] \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i \\[6pt] \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} z_i \end{bmatrix}
$$

*This represents the **center of the cluster of actual poses** — the "best estimate" of where the robot actually goes.*

### Step 3: Compute the per-trial radial distance from the centroid

For each trial, the distance from the centroid is:

$$
d_i = \sqrt{(x_i - \bar{x})^2 + (y_i - \bar{y})^2 + (z_i - \bar{z})^2}
$$

### Step 4: Compute the mean radial distance

$$
\bar{d} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i
$$

### Step 5: Compute the standard deviation of the radial distances

$$
\sigma_d = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(d_i - \bar{d})^2}
$$

*(The Bessel correction $n-1$ is used because we are estimating the population standard deviation from a sample.)*

### Step 6: Apply the $\pm 3\sigma$ statistical bound

The position repeatability is defined as the upper bound of the cluster radius:

$$
RP_l = \bar{d} + 3\sigma_d
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A repeatability of $RP_l = 0.05$ mm means that 99.74% of the time, the actual end-effector position will lie within a sphere of radius 0.05 mm centered on the mean achieved position. **Manufacturers quote this number as "± 0.05 mm repeatability."**

## 3.2 Derivation: Pose Accuracy ($AP_p$)

Pose accuracy is the distance between the *commanded* pose $P_c$ and the *mean actual* pose $\bar{P}$.

### Step 1: Express the commanded pose

$$
P_c = \begin{bmatrix} x_c \\ y_c \\ z_c \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 2: Express the position error vector

The position error for trial $i$ is:

$$
e_i = P_i - P_c = \begin{bmatrix} x_i - x_c \\ y_i - y_c \\ z_i - z_c \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 3: Compute the mean error vector

$$
\bar{e} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} e_i = \bar{P} - P_c
$$

### Step 4: Compute the magnitude of the mean error

$$
AP_p = \Vert \bar{e} \Vert = \sqrt{(\bar{x} - x_c)^2 + (\bar{y} - y_c)^2 + (\bar{z} - z_c)^2}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Distinction to Memorize for Board Exam:**
> - $AP_p$ uses the **commanded pose** as reference.
> - $RP_l$ uses the **mean actual pose** as reference.
> These are **NOT** the same quantity and you will lose marks if you confuse them.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

A 6-axis articulated robot is commanded to move to $P_c = (100.00, 200.00, 50.00)$ mm. After $n = 5$ trials, the actual positions recorded are:

| Trial $i$ | $x_i$ (mm) | $y_i$ (mm) | $z_i$ (mm) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 100.08 | 199.95 | 49.92 |
| 2 | 100.06 | 199.97 | 49.94 |
| 3 | 100.04 | 199.93 | 49.90 |
| 4 | 100.10 | 199.96 | 49.95 |
| 5 | 100.07 | 199.94 | 49.89 |

**Calculate the Pose Accuracy and Position Repeatability.**

### Step 1: Compute the mean

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{x} &= \frac{100.08 + 100.06 + 100.04 + 100.10 + 100.07}{5} = \frac{500.35}{5} = 100.070 \text{ mm} \\[6pt]
\bar{y} &= \frac{199.95 + 199.97 + 199.93 + 199.96 + 199.94}{5} = \frac{999.75}{5} = 199.950 \text{ mm} \\[6pt]
\bar{z} &= \frac{49.92 + 49.94 + 49.90 + 49.95 + 49.89}{5} = \frac{249.60}{5} = 49.920 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Compute Pose Accuracy

$$
\begin{aligned}
AP_p &= \sqrt{(100.070 - 100.00)^2 + (199.950 - 200.00)^2 + (49.920 - 50.00)^2} \\[6pt]
&= \sqrt{(0.070)^2 + (-0.050)^2 + (-0.080)^2} \\[6pt]
&= \sqrt{0.0049 + 0.0025 + 0.0064} \\[6pt]
&= \sqrt{0.0138} \\[6pt]
&= 0.1175 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Compute per-trial radial distances $d_i$

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_1 &= \sqrt{(0.010)^2 + (0.000)^2 + (0.000)^2} = 0.0100 \text{ mm} \\
d_2 &= \sqrt{(-0.010)^2 + (0.020)^2 + (0.020)^2} = 0.0300 \text{ mm} \\
d_3 &= \sqrt{(-0.030)^2 + (-0.020)^2 + (-0.020)^2} = 0.0412 \text{ mm} \\
d_4 &= \sqrt{(0.030)^2 + (0.010)^2 + (0.030)^2} = 0.0436 \text{ mm} \\
d_5 &= \sqrt{(0.000)^2 + (-0.010)^2 + (-0.030)^2} = 0.0316 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Compute mean radial distance

$$
\bar{d} = \frac{0.0100 + 0.0300 + 0.0412 + 0.0436 + 0.0316}{5} = \frac{0.1564}{5} = 0.0313 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 5: Compute standard deviation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_d &= \sqrt{\frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{5}(d_i - \bar{d})^2} \\[6pt]
&= \sqrt{\frac{1}{4}\Big[(0.0213)^2 + (-0.0013)^2 + (0.0099)^2 + (0.0123)^2 + (0.0003)^2\Big]} \\[6pt]
&= \sqrt{\frac{0.000454 + 0.000002 + 0.000098 + 0.000151 + 0.000000}{4}} \\[6pt]
&= \sqrt{\frac{0.000705}{4}} = \sqrt{0.000176} \\[6pt]
&= 0.01328 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6: Compute Position Repeatability

$$
\begin{aligned}
RP_l &= \bar{d} + 3\sigma_d = 0.0313 + 3(0.01328) \\[6pt]
&= 0.0313 + 0.0398 \\[6pt]
&= 0.0711 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Result

$$
\boxed{AP_p = 0.1175 \text{ mm}, \quad RP_l = 0.0711 \text{ mm} \approx \pm 0.07 \text{ mm}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Notice that **Accuracy (0.1175 mm) is larger than Precision (0.0711 mm)**. This indicates a systematic bias (the cluster is consistent in shape but offset from the target). To improve accuracy, one would apply **kinematic calibration**; to improve precision, one would reduce **random noise sources** (gear backlash, thermal effects).
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Precision vs. Accuracy Matrix (4-Quadrant Concept Map)

This is the standard **"Target Diagram"** that appears in nearly every KTU robotics textbook. Since physical drawings are not natively supported in Mermaid, we render the conceptual architecture as a labeled quadrant map.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Q1["QUADRANT 1 — HIGH ACCURACY + HIGH PRECISION (IDEAL)"]
        Q1N1["Tightly clustered darts"] --> Q1N2["Centered ON the bullseye"]
        Q1N2 --> Q1N3["Robot after kinematic calibration"]
    end

    subgraph Q2["QUADRANT 2 — LOW ACCURACY + HIGH PRECISION (SYSTEMATIC BIAS)"]
        Q2N1["Tightly clustered darts"] --> Q2N2["Consistently MISSING the bullseye in one direction"]
        Q2N2 --> Q2N3["Robot with uncalibrated link length errors"]
    end

    subgraph Q3["QUADRANT 3 — LOW ACCURACY + LOW PRECISION (WORST CASE)"]
        Q3N1["Scattered darts everywhere"] --> Q3N2["No identifiable cluster center"]
        Q3N2 --> Q3N3["Robot with backlash, noise, thermal drift"]
    end

    subgraph Q4["QUADRANT 4 — HIGH ACCURACY + LOW PRECISION (RANDOM NOISE)"]
        Q4N1["Scattered darts"] --> Q4N2["Mean position near bullseye by chance"]
        Q4N2 --> Q4N3["Unlikely in real robots"]
    end

    Start(["Precision and Accuracy in Robotics"]) --> Q1
    Start --> Q2
    Start --> Q3
    Start --> Q4

    Q1 --> Result["IDEAL PERFORMANCE"]
    Q2 --> Result2["FIX WITH CALIBRATION"]
    Q3 --> Result3["FIX WITH HARDWARE UPGRADE"]
    Q4 --> Result4["STATISTICALLY UNLIKELY"]
```

## 4.2 ISO 9283 Performance Testing Workflow

This block-level functional architecture shows the *flow of operations* when measuring precision and accuracy in a real robotics lab.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Commanded Pose Pc"] --> B["Robot Controller"]
    B --> C["Manipulator Executes Motion"]
    C --> D["Metrology Device Measures Actual Pose Pi"]
    D --> E{"Data Acquired for n trials"}
    E --> F1["Compute Mean Pose P-bar"]
    E --> F2["Compute Per-Trial Deviation di"]
    F1 --> G1["Pose Accuracy AP-p = distance from Pc to P-bar"]
    F2 --> G2["Standard Deviation sigma-d"]
    F2 --> H["Mean Radial Distance d-bar"]
    H --> I["Position Repeatability RP-l = d-bar + 3 sigma-d"]
    G2 --> I
    G1 --> Output["Performance Report"]
    I --> Output
```

## 4.3 Sources of Error Affecting Accuracy vs. Precision

This diagram distinguishes the **two root causes** of robot positioning error — a high-yield KTU concept.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Root["Robot Position Error Sources"] --> Branch1["SYSTEMATIC ERRORS Reduce ACCURACY"]
    Root --> Branch2["RANDOM ERRORS Reduce PRECISION"]

    Branch1 --> S1["Link length errors delta-a"]
    Branch1 --> S2["Joint offset errors delta-d"]
    Branch1 --> S3["Encoder calibration drift"]
    Branch1 --> S4["Tool center point TCP misalignment"]
    Branch1 --> Sfix["Fix via KINEMATIC CALIBRATION"]

    Branch2 --> R1["Gear backlash"]
    Branch2 --> R2["Servo jitter"]
    Branch2 --> R3["Thermal expansion"]
    Branch2 --> R4["Sensor noise"]
    Branch2 --> Rfix["Fix via BETTER HARDWARE"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** If the question asks *"A robot shows scattered poses but is centered near the target — what is the issue?"* → Answer: **Random errors (precision problem)**, and the fix is hardware improvement. If the question asks *"A robot consistently lands 2 mm to the right of every target"*, → Answer: **Systematic bias (accuracy problem)**, and the fix is **kinematic calibration**.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

**Q1. Differentiate between accuracy and repeatability of a robotic manipulator. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Criterion | Accuracy | Repeatability (Precision) |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Ability to reach the *commanded* position. | Ability to return to a *previously taught* position. |
| **Reference Point** | Commanded pose $P_c$ | Mean actual pose $\bar{P}$ |
| **Affected by** | Systematic errors (link length, joint offset) | Random errors (backlash, thermal noise) |
| **Formula** | $AP_p = \sqrt{(\bar{x}-x_c)^2+(\bar{y}-y_c)^2+(\bar{z}-z_c)^2}$ | $RP_l = \bar{d} + 3\sigma_d$ |
| **Typical Value (Industrial Robot)** | 1 – 10 mm | 0.02 – 0.5 mm |

*(Marks: 1 for definitions, 1 for differentiating table, 1 for typical values)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q2. Define the term "Position Repeatability" of an industrial robot as per ISO 9283. Why is it often quoted as a statistical bound of $\pm 3\sigma$? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Position Repeatability** is the closeness of agreement among the *actual positions* achieved by the end-effector when attempting to reach the same *commanded position* repeatedly. It is mathematically defined as:

$$
RP_l = \bar{d} + 3\sigma_d
$$

where $\bar{d}$ is the mean radial distance of actual poses from the cluster centroid, and $\sigma_d$ is the standard deviation.

The bound $\pm 3\sigma$ is used because, for a Gaussian (normal) distribution of random errors, approximately **99.74%** of all measured values fall within $\pm 3$ standard deviations of the mean. This provides a high-confidence statistical guarantee for manufacturing specifications.

*(Marks: 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for statistical justification)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4 Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**(a)** Define **Accuracy** and **Precision** in the context of industrial robots. Explain, with a suitable diagram, the four possible accuracy-precision combinations using the dartboard analogy. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 6-DOF robot is commanded to a pose $P_c = (250.00, 150.00, 300.00)$ mm. After $n = 5$ cycles, the recorded actual positions are:

| Trial | $x_i$ (mm) | $y_i$ (mm) | $z_i$ (mm) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 250.20 | 149.85 | 300.10 |
| 2 | 250.15 | 149.90 | 300.05 |
| 3 | 250.18 | 149.88 | 300.08 |
| 4 | 250.22 | 149.87 | 300.12 |
| 5 | 250.17 | 149.92 | 300.07 |

Calculate (i) Pose Accuracy $AP_p$, and (ii) Position Repeatability $RP_l$ using the $\pm 3\sigma$ bound. **(7 Marks)**

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Conceptual Explanation:**

- **Accuracy (2 marks):** Accuracy is the ability of a robot to position its end-effector at the *commanded* location. It quantifies the deviation between the *target* and the *mean actual* pose.
- **Precision (2 marks):** Precision (or Repeatability) is the ability to return to the *same* position consistently, irrespective of whether that position matches the commanded one.
- **Diagram (2 marks):** A four-quadrant dartboard target showing:
  - Q1: Tightly clustered on center → **Accurate + Precise (Ideal)**
  - Q2: Tightly clustered, but offset → **Precise only (Systematic bias)**
  - Q3: Random scatter → **Neither (Random noise dominant)**
  - Q4: Random scatter but mean on target → **Accurate only (Statistically rare)**
- **Engineering Context (1 mark):** Industrial robots are typically Q2-type; calibration moves them to Q1.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute mean position (1 mark)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{x} &= \frac{250.20 + 250.15 + 250.18 + 250.22 + 250.17}{5} = 250.184 \text{ mm} \\[6pt]
\bar{y} &= \frac{149.85 + 149.90 + 149.88 + 149.87 + 149.92}{5} = 149.884 \text{ mm} \\[6pt]
\bar{z} &= \frac{300.10 + 300.05 + 300.08 + 300.12 + 300.07}{5} = 300.084 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Pose Accuracy $AP_p$ (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
AP_p &= \sqrt{(250.184 - 250.00)^2 + (149.884 - 150.00)^2 + (300.084 - 300.00)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{(0.184)^2 + (-0.116)^2 + (0.084)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{0.03386 + 0.01346 + 0.00706} \\
&= \sqrt{0.05438} = 0.2332 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute per-trial radial distances $d_i$ (1.5 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_1 &= \sqrt{(0.016)^2 + (-0.034)^2 + (0.016)^2} = 0.0412 \text{ mm} \\
d_2 &= \sqrt{(-0.034)^2 + (0.016)^2 + (-0.034)^2} = 0.0503 \text{ mm} \\
d_3 &= \sqrt{(-0.004)^2 + (-0.004)^2 + (-0.004)^2} = 0.0069 \text{ mm} \\
d_4 &= \sqrt{(0.036)^2 + (-0.014)^2 + (0.036)^2} = 0.0524 \text{ mm} \\
d_5 &= \sqrt{(-0.014)^2 + (0.036)^2 + (-0.014)^2} = 0.0416 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute mean radial distance and standard deviation (1.5 marks)**

$$
\bar{d} = \frac{0.0412 + 0.0503 + 0.0069 + 0.0524 + 0.0416}{5} = 0.03848 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\sigma_d = \sqrt{\frac{1}{4}\sum_{i=1}^{5}(d_i - 0.03848)^2} = 0.0167 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5: Compute Position Repeatability $RP_l$ (1 mark)**

$$
RP_l = 0.03848 + 3(0.0167) = 0.03848 + 0.05010 = 0.0886 \text{ mm}
$$

**Final Answers:**

$$
\boxed{AP_p = 0.2332 \text{ mm}, \quad RP_l = 0.0886 \text{ mm} \approx \pm 0.09 \text{ mm}}
$$

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Explain the **ISO 9283 standard** for testing the static performance of industrial robots. List and briefly define the **five key performance criteria** measured by this standard. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **sources of errors** that affect robot accuracy, and explain how **kinematic calibration** improves the accuracy of a manipulator. Mention the role of the **product-of-exponentials (POE)** vs. **Denavit-Hartenberg (DH)** formulations in modern calibration. **(7 Marks)**

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) ISO 9283 Explanation (7 Marks):**

- **Scope of ISO 9283 (1.5 marks):** It is the international standard that specifies methods for testing the *static and dynamic* performance of industrial robots, ensuring manufacturers worldwide quote metrics on a common basis.
- **Five Key Performance Criteria (1 mark each, total 5 marks):**
  1. **Pose Accuracy ($AP$):** Distance between commanded and mean-actual pose.
  2. **Pose Repeatability ($RP$):** Closeness of repeated actual poses to each other.
  3. **Distance Accuracy / Pose Stabilization:** Accuracy after a defined settling time.
  4. **Drift ($DR$):** Change in mean achieved position over a long duration (thermal).
  5. **Overshoot ($OS$):** Maximum transient excursion beyond the target during approach.
- **Testing Protocol (0.5 marks):** 30 cycles minimum, full payload, controlled temperature, external metrology (laser tracker or CMM).

**(b) Error Sources and Kinematic Calibration (7 Marks):**

- **Systematic Error Sources (2 marks):** Link length errors $\Delta a_i$, link twist errors $\Delta \alpha_i$, joint offset errors $\Delta d_i$, encoder quantization, and TCP (Tool Center Point) calibration error.
- **Random Error Sources (1 mark):** Backlash, friction, thermal expansion, structural compliance, sensor noise.
- **Kinematic Calibration Process (2 marks):** Uses an *external metrology device* to measure the *actual* pose, then applies an *identification algorithm* (least-squares, Levenberg-Marquardt) to find the *corrected kinematic parameters* that minimize the residual error between predicted and measured poses.
- **DH vs. POE in Calibration (2 marks):**
  - The **Denavit-Hartenberg (DH)** parameterization has *singularities* and *discontinuities* during calibration when a joint angle crosses 180° — a known limitation.
  - The **Product-of-Exponentials (POE)** formulation (based on screw theory) eliminates these singularities by using *exponential coordinates* of twists, making it more numerically robust and increasingly preferred in modern high-accuracy calibration routines.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Read Carefully Before Writing Your Exam):**
> 1. **Confusing Accuracy and Repeatability:** Writing the same formula for both. → *Always* specify the reference point (commanded vs. mean actual).
> 2. **Forgetting the Bessel correction:** Using $n$ instead of $n-1$ in the standard deviation formula. → Examiners specifically check for this.
> 3. **Forgetting units:** Writing "0.05" without "mm" loses 0.5 mark per occurrence.
> 4. **Omitting the $\pm 3\sigma$ justification:** Just writing "$\pm$" without explaining the statistical basis will cost a full mark in 14-mark questions.
> 5. **Mixing up ISO 9283 and ISO 10218:** 9283 is for *performance testing*; 10218 is for *safety*. Examiners love to trap students here.
> 6. **Not drawing the dartboard diagram in 7-mark questions:** The visualization is worth at least 2 marks — do not skip it.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Precision and Accuracy:**

- **Accuracy** → closeness to *commanded* target. Affected by **systematic** errors.
- **Precision / Repeatability** → closeness among *actual* trials. Affected by **random** errors.
- **Resolution** → smallest commanded increment. Hierarchy: **Resolution ≤ Precision ≤ Accuracy**.
- **Pose Accuracy formula:** $AP_p = \sqrt{(\bar{x}-x_c)^2+(\bar{y}-y_c)^2+(\bar{z}-z_c)^2}$
- **Position Repeatability formula:** $RP_l = \bar{d} + 3\sigma_d$
- **Standard Deviation formula:** Use **Bessel correction** $\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n-1}\sum(d_i-\bar{d})^2}$
- **$\pm 3\sigma$ bound** corresponds to **99.74% confidence** (Chebyshev/Gaussian property).
- **ISO 9283** is the testing standard; it measures **Accuracy, Repeatability, Stabilization, Drift, and Overshoot**.
- **Kinematic calibration** fixes *accuracy* issues by correcting link parameters $a_i, d_i, \alpha_i, \theta_i$.
- **POE formulation** is preferred over DH for modern calibration due to singularity-free parameterization.
- **Industrial robot typical values:** Repeatability 0.02–0.5 mm; Accuracy 1–10 mm.
- **High-accuracy applications** (surgery, aerospace): demand sub-mm accuracy → require **closed-loop control with external sensing**.
- **SCARA robots** have very high repeatability (good for assembly); **Cartesian robots** have the best accuracy (good for pick-and-place).
- **Mnemonic for the dartboard:** "**P**recision = **P**ointing at the **s**ame spot; **A**ccuracy = **A**ctually at the **t**arget."
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Operating Environment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT302 - INTRODUCTION TO ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Operating Environment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operating Environment (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):** The operating environment of a robot refers to the complete set of physical, ambient, and situational conditions (including terrain, lighting, temperature, humidity, dust, electromagnetic noise, hazardous elements, and human interaction zones) within which the robot must perceive, plan, and execute its tasks reliably while maintaining prescribed safety, accuracy, and repeatability specifications.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the **Operating Environment** is a critical design parameter that dictates:
- The choice of **sensors** (vision, tactile, proximity, force).
- The choice of **actuators and materials** (sealed vs. open, IP-rated enclosures).
- The choice of **control architecture** (reactive vs. deliberative).
- The **safety standards** to be followed (ISO 10218, ISO/TS 15066).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Human Body in Different Climates**
> Imagine you are a human worker. If you are sent to work in a desert, you need sunscreen, goggles, water, and insulated boots. If you are sent underwater, you need an oxygen tank, a wetsuit, and waterproof equipment. If you work in a clean laboratory, you wear a sterile suit, a mask, and gloves.
> 
> Similarly, a **robot's operating environment** is essentially its "climate." Before a robot is deployed, engineers must answer: *Where will the robot live? What will it touch? What can hurt it?* The answers decide the robot's "clothing" (housing), "senses" (sensors), and "skills" (control software).

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value / Range | Context |
|---|---|---|
| Standard Operating Temperature | **0 °C to +45 °C** | Typical indoor industrial robots |
| Extended Operating Temperature | **−40 °C to +85 °C** | Military, aerospace, outdoor robots |
| Standard Relative Humidity | **5% to 95% (non-condensing)** | General industrial environments |
| Standard Atmospheric Pressure | **101.325 kPa (1 atm)** | Sea-level reference |
| Ingress Protection (IP) Rating | **IP54, IP65, IP67, IP68** | Dust/water protection |
| Standard Ambient Light | **300 to 500 lux** | Typical factory floor |
| ISO Safety Distance | **≥ 500 mm** | Collaborative robot minimum |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** The KTU Board often asks students to **match an environment to an appropriate sensor/actuator specification**. Remember that **IP65 = Dust-tight + Low-pressure water jets**, while **IP67 = Immersion up to 1 m for 30 minutes**.

## 1.4 Classification of Operating Environments

Robotic operating environments can be broadly classified along four orthogonal axes:

1. **Structural Complexity**
   - **Structured Environment:** Known, static, predictable (e.g., assembly line). Pre-programmed motions work well.
   - **Semi-Structured Environment:** Mostly known but with some variability (e.g., warehouse with moving carts).
   - **Unstructured Environment:** Highly dynamic, unknown a priori (e.g., disaster site, planetary surface).

2. **Spatial Domain**
   - **Indoor / Outdoor / Underground / Underwater / Aerial / Space.**

3. **Hazard Level**
   - **Benign** (office, lab), **Industrial** (factory), **Hazardous** (nuclear, chemical, explosive).

4. **Human Proximity**
   - **Isolated** (caged industrial robot), **Coexistent** (cobot with safety scanners), **Collaborative** (physical HRI).

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Environmental Parameters Affecting Robot Performance

Every parameter in the operating environment directly degrades or enhances robot performance. The KTU Board tests these cause-effect relationships explicitly.

### A. Temperature

- **Effect on Electronics:** Semiconductor drift, increased resistance in copper windings, sensor calibration errors.
- **Effect on Mechanics:** Thermal expansion of linkages ($ \Delta L = \alpha L \Delta T $), lubricant viscosity change.
- **Effect on Actuators:** Servo motor torque derating. At higher temperatures, continuous torque drops due to insulation class limits (typically Class A = 105 °C, Class B = 130 °C, Class F = 155 °C).

### B. Humidity and Moisture

- **Condensation risk** on PCB boards causes short circuits.
- **Corrosion** of metallic joints reduces positional accuracy.
- **Optical sensor fogging** (cameras, LiDAR) introduces noise.

### C. Dust and Particulates

- **Abrasive wear** on bearings and lead screws.
- **Optical contamination** of cameras and laser sensors.
- **Explosion risk** in grain silos, coal mines — requires **ATEX/IECEx certified** robots.

### D. Electromagnetic Interference (EMI)

- **Induced currents** in signal lines cause false sensor readings.
- **Motor drive noise** corrupts encoder feedback.
- Mitigation: **Shielded cables, twisted pairs, optical isolation, Faraday cages.**

### E. Vibration and Shock

- Resonance in mechanical structure amplifies errors.
- Affects IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit) readings.
- Measured using **g-RMS** values and the **Power Spectral Density (PSD)** curve.

### F. Lighting Conditions

- **Vision-based robots** depend critically on illumination.
- Parameters: Lux level, color temperature (Kelvin), specular reflections, shadows.

### G. Hazardous / Extreme Atmospheres

- **Radiation:** Causes single-event upsets (SEU) in microprocessors. Radiation-hardened components are used in nuclear and space robotics.
- **Toxic gases:** Require hermetically sealed robots (e.g., Boston Dynamics' Spot equipped with gas sensors).
- **Vacuum (Space):** Outgassing of materials, cold-welding of metals, no convective cooling — only radiative.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Formulas for KTU University Exam - Operating Environment**

| Concept | Formula / Relation | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Thermal Expansion of Link | $\Delta L = \alpha \cdot L \cdot \Delta T$ | $\alpha$ = coefficient of thermal expansion, $L$ = original length |
| Positional Error from Thermal | $\delta_{thermal} = \alpha L \Delta T$ | Linear positional drift in mm |
| Reynolds Number (Fluid Env.) | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v L}{\mu}$ | Predicts laminar/turbulent flow in underwater/aerial robots |
| Robot Workspace vs. Reach | $V_{ws} = \int dV$ over reachable set | Defines operating volume envelope |
| Safety Stopping Distance | $S_d = v_{max} \cdot T_{reaction} + \dfrac{v_{max}^2}{2 a_{max}}$ | ISO 10218 collaborative robot formula |
| Sensor SNR | $SNR = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | Critical in EMI-noisy environments |
| Mean Time Between Failures | $MTBF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | $\lambda$ = failure rate (per hour) |
| MTBF in Harsh Env. | $MTBF_{env} = MTBF_{std} \cdot K_{env}$ | $K_{env}$ = environmental factor (e.g., $K_{env} \approx 1.5$ for dusty industrial) |
| IP Rating (Ingress Protection) | $IP_{XY} : X=\text{solid}, Y=\text{liquid}$ | $X \in [0,6]$, $Y \in [0,9]$ |
| Maximum Safe Acceleration (Cobot) | $a_{max} \leq 150 \;\text{mm/s}^2$ (quasi-static contact) | ISO/TS 15066 limit |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding the operating environment is **not optional** in any KTU-approved robotics project. Industry applications include:

- **Automotive Assembly Lines (Structured, Indoor):** Robots operate in IP65 enclosures with temperature-controlled cells. Vision systems use controlled diffuse LED lighting.
- **Agricultural Robotics (Semi-Structured, Outdoor):** Must handle dust, rain, mud, and variable sunlight. Use sealed GPS, IMU, and multispectral cameras.
- **Disaster Response (Unstructured, Hazardous):** Search-and-rescue robots like quadrupeds are built to IP67 with thermal cameras and gas detectors.
- **Space Robotics (Vacuum, Radiation):** Mars rovers use radiation-hardened processors, RTGs (Radioisotope Thermoelectric Generators), and operate at −100 °C ambient.
- **Surgical Robotics (Sterile, Precise):** Da Vinci systems operate in ISO Class 5 cleanrooms with humidity 30–60%.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Thermal Positional Drift in a Robot Link

**Problem Setup:** A robot manipulator has an aluminum link of length $L = 500 \text{ mm}$. The factory temperature varies from $T_{min} = 10^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $T_{max} = 40^{\circ}\text{C}$ during a shift. Given the coefficient of thermal expansion of aluminum $\alpha_{Al} = 23 \times 10^{-6} \; /^{\circ}\text{C}$, find the maximum positional drift at the end-effector caused purely by thermal expansion.

### Step 1 — Identify the Governing Equation

For a homogeneous isotropic material undergoing linear thermal expansion, the change in length is given by:

$$
\Delta L = \alpha \cdot L \cdot \Delta T
$$

where:
- $\alpha$ = coefficient of linear thermal expansion (per °C)
- $L$ = original length
- $\Delta T$ = change in temperature

### Step 2 — Compute the Temperature Differential

The temperature swing during operation is:

$$
\Delta T = T_{max} - T_{min}
$$

$$
\Delta T = 40^{\circ}\text{C} - 10^{\circ}\text{C} = 30^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the Numerical Values

$$
\Delta L = (23 \times 10^{-6} \; /^{\circ}\text{C}) \times (500 \text{ mm}) \times (30^{\circ}\text{C})
$$

$$
\Delta L = 23 \times 10^{-6} \times 15000 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\Delta L = 23 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-1} \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 4 — Final Numerical Evaluation

$$
\Delta L = 34.5 \times 10^{-2} \text{ mm} = 0.345 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** A positional drift of **0.345 mm** may seem small, but in a precision pick-and-place operation requiring ±0.05 mm tolerance, this completely disqualifies the robot. Therefore, **environmental compensation (closed-loop thermal control, water cooling jackets, or material substitution with Invar $\alpha_{Invar} \approx 1.2 \times 10^{-6} /^{\circ}\text{C}$) is mandatory** in high-precision cells.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Safety Stopping Distance for a Collaborative Robot

**Problem Setup:** A cobot moves with maximum velocity $v_{max} = 250 \text{ mm/s}$ in a workspace shared with a human. The safety controller's reaction time is $T_{reaction} = 50 \text{ ms}$ and the maximum deceleration capability is $a_{max} = 2000 \text{ mm/s}^2$. Compute the minimum protective safety distance.

### Step 1 — State the ISO 10218 / ISO/TS 15066 Formula

The minimum safety distance is the sum of the **reaction distance** and the **braking distance**:

$$
S_d = v_{max} \cdot T_{reaction} + \dfrac{v_{max}^{2}}{2 \cdot a_{max}}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Reaction Distance Component

$$
S_1 = v_{max} \cdot T_{reaction}
$$

$$
S_1 = (250 \text{ mm/s}) \times (0.050 \text{ s}) = 12.5 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Braking Distance Component

$$
S_2 = \dfrac{v_{max}^{2}}{2 \cdot a_{max}}
$$

$$
S_2 = \dfrac{(250)^{2}}{2 \times 2000} = \dfrac{62500}{4000}
$$

$$
S_2 = 15.625 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Total Safety Distance

$$
S_d = S_1 + S_2
$$

$$
S_d = 12.5 + 15.625 = 28.125 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 5 — Apply the ISO Margin

ISO standards require an additional **safety margin** of at least **100 mm** for human approach speed consideration, so:

$$
S_{d, \text{final}} = S_d + 100 \text{ mm} = 128.125 \text{ mm}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Any human-movable object (e.g., a hand) must remain at least **128.125 mm** away from the cobot's reach envelope for a guaranteed safe stop. This single derivation is a **high-yield KTU Part B question**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Environment → Sensor Selection

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is a **favorite KTU question pattern**. Students are asked: *"For the following operating environment, which sensor suite is most appropriate?"*

| Operating Environment | Dominant Hazard | Recommended Sensor Suite | Recommended Housing |
|---|---|---|---|
| Automotive welding cell | Arc flash, spatter, EMI | Through-beam photoeyes, inductive proximity, shielded encoders | IP65 stainless, heat-shielded |
| Food processing (washdown) | High-pressure water, sanitizers | Stainless sealed Inductive, hygienic vision (316L SS) | IP69K |
| Outdoor agriculture | Dust, rain, sunlight | Multi-spectral camera, RTK-GPS, sealed LiDAR, IMU | IP67, UV-resistant polymer |
| Nuclear / Radiological | Gamma radiation, contamination | Radiation-hardened cameras, Geiger-coupled position sensors | Lead-lined, hermetically sealed |
| Underwater (ROV) | Pressure, corrosion, low light | Sonar, pressure sensor, low-light B&W camera | Titanium pressure vessel |
| Space (Mars Rover) | Vacuum, radiation, dust storms | Stereo vision, Hazcam, thermal IR, sun tracker | Sealed, gold-coated, radiation-hardened |
| Hospital surgical suite | Sterility, EMF | Force/torque sensor, fiber-optic encoders, redundant encoders | Sterile drape, bio-compatible |
| Warehouse AMR | Mixed lighting, dynamic obstacles | 2D LiDAR, depth camera, wheel odometry, IMU | IP54, ruggedized |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define the term "Operating Environment" of a robot and list any four environmental factors that influence the design of a robotic system.**

**Model Answer:**
The operating environment of a robot is the complete set of physical, ambient, and situational conditions under which the robot operates, perceives, and executes its tasks while maintaining specified performance and safety.

Four key environmental factors:
1. **Temperature** — affects electronics and thermal expansion of links.
2. **Humidity and Moisture** — causes corrosion and condensation on PCBs.
3. **Dust and Particulates** — cause abrasive wear and sensor contamination.
4. **Electromagnetic Interference (EMI)** — corrupts sensor signals and motor drives.

(Other acceptable answers: lighting, vibration, hazardous chemicals, radiation, human proximity.)

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**What do you mean by "IP65" rating? Why is it important in robot selection?**

**Model Answer:**
**IP65** is an Ingress Protection code (per IEC 60529):
- **6** = Dust-tight (complete protection against dust ingress).
- **5** = Protected against low-pressure water jets from any direction.

It is important because it quantifies the robot's ability to withstand harsh industrial conditions such as factory dust and washdowns, directly indicating suitability for deployment in dirty or wet environments without failure.

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently **lose 2 marks** by failing to state the **assumptions** before solving derivations (e.g., assuming uniform link material, steady-state temperature).
> - Students often **confuse IP65 with IP67** — remember the second digit (water depth/duration) is the differentiator.
> - Many forget to mention the **relevant ISO standard** (ISO 10218, ISO/TS 15066) when discussing safety — this is a 2-mark deduction trap.
> - Always include a **labeled sketch or block diagram** even if not explicitly asked — KTU awards 1–2 marks for illustrations.
> - Do **not** write vague statements like *"sensors are used to sense the environment"* — name **specific sensors** with **justification**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the classification of robotic operating environments based on structure, domain, hazard level, and human proximity. Give one industrial example for each category.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A robot link made of steel (coefficient of thermal expansion $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6} /^{\circ}\text{C}$) has a length of 800 mm. The operating temperature swings between 5 °C and 55 °C. Calculate:
   (i) The temperature differential.
   (ii) The maximum thermal expansion of the link.
   (iii) The positional error in micrometres if this is the only link contributing.
   Suggest two engineering remedies.

### Model Solution A

#### (a) Classification of Robotic Operating Environments [7 Marks]

| Axis | Sub-class | Definition | Industrial Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Structure** | Structured | Static, known geometry | Automotive welding cell with pre-taught path |
| **Structure** | Semi-Structured | Mostly known with variability | Amazon warehouse with mobile racks |
| **Structure** | Unstructured | Unknown, dynamic | DARPA Challenge off-road autonomous vehicle |
| **Domain** | Indoor | Climate-controlled | Electronics PCB assembly |
| **Domain** | Outdoor | Weather-exposed | Autonomous tractor in agriculture |
| **Domain** | Underwater | High-pressure subsea | ROV for pipeline inspection |
| **Domain** | Aerial | Low/High altitude | UAV for surveillance |
| **Domain** | Space | Vacuum, radiation | Mars Perseverance Rover |
| **Hazard** | Benign | Lab/office | Mobile robot in museum |
| **Hazard** | Industrial | Standard factory | Six-axis welding arm |
| **Hazard** | Hazardous | Toxic/radiative | Nuclear decommissioning robot |
| **Hazard** | Explosive (ATEX) | Flammable atmosphere | Paint-spray robot in automotive |
| **Human Proximity** | Isolated | Caged, fenced | High-payload press tending robot |
| **Human Proximity** | Coexistent | Safety laser scanners | AGV with SICK safety zones |
| **Human Proximity** | Collaborative | Power and force limited | Universal Robots UR5 cobot |

#### (b) Numerical Problem [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- $L = 800 \text{ mm}$
- $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6} \; /^{\circ}\text{C}$
- $T_{min} = 5^{\circ}\text{C}$, $T_{max} = 55^{\circ}\text{C}$

**Step 1 — Temperature Differential:**
$$
\Delta T = T_{max} - T_{min} = 55 - 5 = 50^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

**Step 2 — Thermal Expansion:**
$$
\Delta L = \alpha \cdot L \cdot \Delta T
$$

$$
\Delta L = (12 \times 10^{-6}) \times (800) \times (50)
$$

$$
\Delta L = 12 \times 10^{-6} \times 40000
$$

$$
\Delta L = 0.48 \text{ mm} = 480 \; \mu\text{m}
$$

**Step 3 — Engineering Remedies (2 marks):**
1. **Material substitution:** Use **Invar** ($\alpha \approx 1.2 \times 10^{-6} /^{\circ}\text{C}$), reducing drift by **10×** to ~48 µm.
2. **Closed-loop thermal control:** Use a water jacket or Peltier cooler to maintain the link at a constant reference temperature (e.g., 25 °C ± 0.5 °C).
3. **Software compensation:** Embed $\alpha L T$ in the forward kinematics and correct it in the controller's inverse kinematics in real-time using feedback from temperature sensors mounted on the link.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the role of the following environmental factors on robot performance and state the relevant standard/mitigation:
   (i) EMI
   (ii) Vibration
   (iii) Hazardous gas and dust

**(b) [7 Marks]** A collaborative robot operates in a shared workspace. The maximum end-effector velocity is $300 \text{ mm/s}$. The controller reaction time is $40 \text{ ms}$ and maximum deceleration is $1500 \text{ mm/s}^2$. Compute the minimum safety distance using ISO/TS 15066 principles and justify why human-cobot systems require additional safety margins.

### Model Solution B

#### (a) Environmental Factors Affecting Robot Performance [7 Marks]

**(i) Electromagnetic Interference (EMI):**
EMI from nearby Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs), welding inverters, or radio sources induces noise on signal lines, corrupts encoder readings, and triggers false safety stops. **Mitigation:** shielded twisted-pair cables, optical isolation, Faraday cage enclosures, proper single-point grounding, and compliance with **IEC 61000** series EMC standards.

**(ii) Vibration:**
Vibration from heavy machinery (presses, stamping lines) can excite the natural frequency of robot links, leading to resonance, structural fatigue, and reduced positional accuracy. **Mitigation:** Active vibration isolation mounts, tuned mass dampers, and **ISO 10816** vibration severity evaluation. Sensor fusion with IMU compensates for transient errors.

**(iii) Hazardous Gas and Dust:**
In petrochemical plants, grain silos, and mining, combustible dust or flammable vapors can ignite from sparks in brushed motors or relay contacts. **Mitigation:** Use **ATEX/IECEx certified explosion-proof robots**, intrinsically safe sensors, purged enclosures (positive pressure), and brushless DC motors. Gas detection sensors (e.g., MQ-series, NDIR) trigger emergency shutdowns.

#### (b) Safety Distance Numerical [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- $v_{max} = 300 \text{ mm/s}$
- $T_{reaction} = 40 \text{ ms} = 0.040 \text{ s}$
- $a_{max} = 1500 \text{ mm/s}^2$

**Step 1 — Reaction Distance:**
$$
S_1 = v_{max} \cdot T_{reaction} = 300 \times 0.040 = 12 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Braking Distance:**
$$
S_2 = \dfrac{v_{max}^{2}}{2 a_{max}} = \dfrac{(300)^{2}}{2 \times 1500} = \dfrac{90000}{3000} = 30 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Minimum Stopping Distance:**
$$
S_{stop} = S_1 + S_2 = 12 + 30 = 42 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply ISO/TS 15066 Human Approach Margin:**
ISO/TS 15066 specifies that for a human approaching at $v_{human} = 1600 \text{ mm/s}$, an additional margin of at least $S_{add} = 100 \text{ mm}$ must be added.

$$
S_{d, \text{final}} = 42 + 100 = 142 \text{ mm}
$$

**Justification for additional margin:**
Human biomechanical reflexes are unpredictable. The 100 mm margin accounts for **human approach speed**, **sensor latency**, **clothing bulk**, and **posture variability**, ensuring that even in worst-case scenarios, the cobot halts before contact occurs (or contact remains within biomechanical limits — 140 N quasi-static contact force per ISO/TS 15066).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Operating Environment**

- Operating environment = set of all ambient + situational conditions a robot must survive and operate within.
- Four classification axes: **Structure, Domain, Hazard, Human Proximity**.
- Key environmental stressors: **Temperature, Humidity, Dust, EMI, Vibration, Lighting, Hazardous chemicals, Radiation, Vacuum**.
- **Thermal expansion** follows $\Delta L = \alpha L \Delta T$ — aluminum expands more than steel; Invar is the preferred low-expansion alloy.
- **IP Rating** = two digits: first = solid ingress, second = liquid ingress. **IP65 = dust-tight + low-pressure jets**, **IP67 = immersion 1 m / 30 min**.
- **ISO 10218** is the international standard for industrial robot safety; **ISO/TS 15066** extends it specifically for collaborative robots (cobot contact force limits).
- **Safety stopping distance** = $S_d = v_{max} T_{reaction} + \dfrac{v_{max}^{2}}{2 a_{max}}$ + 100 mm ISO margin.
- **Structured** environments suit pre-programmed (teach-pendant) robots; **unstructured** environments demand AI + sensor fusion.
- **MTBF** in harsh environments is reduced by an **environmental factor** $K_{env}$ — design for derated life.
- Hazardous environments demand **ATEX/IECEx certified** robots (no brushed motors, no sparks).
- Space robots require **radiation-hardened** electronics, **vacuum-compatible lubricants** (no volatile outgassing), and **radiative cooling**.
- Vision systems are **lighting-sensitive** — choose controlled illumination or HDR cameras in variable light.
- Always state **assumptions** before numerical derivations in KTU exams to earn full marks.
